Silver’s paired pieces supply the empirical post-mortem of the post-Callais redistricting wave plus a portable framework for evaluating constitutional hardball going forward — together the most operationally useful read in the cluster on the landscape after last week’s Berman/Atlantic Callais carry-forward and the open virginia-redistricting-frame thread. Goldberg’s San Antonio runoff dispatch is the same dynamic at the candidate-selection layer: a suspected-GOP dark-money PAC (“Lead Left,” registered April 24 at a Tallahassee Staples address) spending $43,000 to elevate a conspiratorial antisemite in a Democratic primary, exploiting a redrawn majority-Latino district where anti-establishment fury has outrun any party infrastructure capable of channeling it. The constitutional-architecture rewiring story and the candidate-vetting collapse story are different surfaces of the same dynamic.

On redistricting, Democrats are playing as the away team — Silver Bulletin, Nate Silver — 2026-05-11 · Claude’s Response → Responding to: The combined effect of three events that converted what Silver had read as a Democratic draw in the mid-decade redistricting war into a clear Republican win — the Supreme Court’s Louisiana v. Callais* decision weakening the Voting Rights Act, the Virginia Supreme Court invalidating the Democrats’ redistricting referendum on procedural grounds, and Republican-state redraws in Florida and elsewhere.*

Silver concedes his earlier thesis (that Democrats faced no structural barriers to holding serve, only insufficient willingness to be aggressive) was incomplete because it underweighted the courts. Per Nate Cohn’s NYT chart, the median House district has shifted from R+0.1 (a tick better for Dems than 2024) to somewhere between R+2.5 and R+3.9, with Democrats appealing Virginia to SCOTUS and considering a “fairly radical plan” to force state justices into early retirement (which Silver opposes — see his sister piece on constitutional hardball). Silver adds two technical corrections to Cohn: the analysis is deterministic where it should be probabilistic (DeSantis’s new Florida map creates districts that aren’t safe pickups in a blue-wave year), and Democrats outperformed presidential margins in 2024 House races by ~1 point due to better candidate quality, fewer uncontested districts, and lower-population/turnout Democratic districts producing more bang per vote. He then runs an LLM-mediated synthesis of state-court ideological leans (asked Gemini, Claude, ChatGPT to argue with each other) and finds Republicans hold 264 electoral votes worth of strong-or-leaning-R state courts vs. 252 for Democrats — not a structural advantage but a meaningful tilt, with Republicans owning the only court that really matters (the federal SCOTUS at 6-3). Operational conclusion: Democrats are playing as the “away team” — their gerrymanders will face more court scrutiny, close calls won’t break their way, they need to maximize aggressive redraws in New York and Colorado where blue state courts will pass them, but should accept short-run draws rather than escalate to Virginia-justice-retirement levels.

Nate’s 3 rules for constitutional hardball — Silver Bulletin, Nate Silver — 2026-05-15 · Claude’s Response → Responding to: Matt Glassman’s recent essay defining “constitutional hardball” (tactics technically legal under the Constitution but flouting democratic norms), with proximate triggers being Virginia Democrats’ abandoned plan to force the state supreme court into early retirement to overturn the redistricting-referendum nullification, and Kamala Harris’s “no-bad-idea brainstorm” floating DC/Puerto Rico statehood and Supreme Court packing.

Silver endorses Glassman’s golden-rule test (“would you favor this reform when the other side benefits, or only when you do?”) and adds two refinements: a categorical-imperative-style equilibrium test (if both sides reciprocate, does the system stabilize or oscillate out of control?), and a popularity test (does the public actually want this?). Applied: court packing fails all three — opposed by voters, would produce an unstable escalation toward thousand-justice Courts, and is asymmetric in motivation; mid-decade redistricting fails the equilibrium test less severely, since it just turns the House into “a version of the Electoral College” with state-level elections still mediating; statehood for PR is fine on equilibrium grounds and polls well, DC is a closer call. Silver’s substantive synthesis: Democrats should propose a “democracy agenda” combining gerrymandering reform, PR statehood, and SCOTUS term limits / mandatory retirement (all of which poll well), rather than presuming “the possibility of some sort of total partisan victory in an era of short-lived majorities.” He concedes Democrats aren’t in a symmetric position — Republicans got there first via Garland, Texas 2003 redistricting, Trump-era norm erosion — but argues the asymmetry cuts toward Democratic restraint precisely because they’ve branded themselves as the pro-norms party.

What’s Behind the Gerrymandering Arms Race — Eisler, Jacob, Compact — 2026-05-13 · Claude’s Response → Responding to: The April 29 Supreme Court decision in Louisiana v. Callais* (which curbed Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act and effectively condoned partisan gerrymandering), the rapid Republican redistricting wave it triggered across multiple southern states, and the May 8 Virginia Supreme Court decision voiding a Democratic-backed referendum that would have permitted partisan gerrymandering — along with the more aggressive Democratic response proposal to drop the mandatory retirement age for Virginia Supreme Court justices to 54, purging the bench to allow the gerrymander to proceed.*

Eisler — an election-law professor — concedes that partisan gerrymandering is a normal part of American politics and that Callais itself is a defensible “background tweak” to electoral procedure. His structural claim is that the alarming feature of the current moment is not the gerrymandering itself but the prioritization of procedural-machination contests over substantive governance: the focus of both parties’ state-level political discourse has migrated from “what policies attract voters” to “how do we engineer the formal terms of victory.” The Virginia age-reduction proposal is his canonical example — it would sacrifice judicial independence (a constitutional building block) for instrumental partisan gain, and represents a more extreme departure than even McConnell’s 2016 Garland blockade. Eisler treats the Democratic willingness to gerrymander as not-hypocritical-but-troubling: now that gerrymandering is standard, both parties pursuing it is expected; what’s troubling is that it has become the headlining policy of state parties rather than a procedural means to substantive ends. He closes on the broader implication that the focus on procedural machinations correlates with declining American state capacity (high-speed rail, naval production capacity vs. hegemonic rivals) — the political class has become “parasitic” by abandoning the welfare-of-the-people obligation in favor of formal power-pursuit.

Democrats Can’t Let This Antisemitic Sex Therapist Win Her Runoff — Goldberg, Michelle, NYT (via ProQuest) — 2026-05-11 · Claude’s Response → Responding to: The Texas 29th-runoff scenario in which sex therapist Maureen Galindo — who has publicly invoked “the Jews who own Hollywood,” cited Revelation 3:9’s “synagogue of Satan,” and claimed DHS is secretly based in Tel Aviv — is polling ahead of DCCC-backed sheriff’s deputy Johnny Garcia, with a suspected-GOP dark-money PAC (“Lead Left,” registered April 24 at a Tallahassee Staples address, WinRed metadata in its website) spending $43,000 to boost her.

Goldberg uses the Galindo case to make a two-part argument. First, the descriptive: Galindo’s combination of progressive populism, New Age therapeutic vocabulary, and conspiratorial mind-set is the left-wing analogue of the pandemic-era wellness-influencer pipeline to QAnon. She finished first in a four-way primary in a redrawn majority-Latino district that voted Trump +10 in 2024 but has soured since, suggesting genuine anti-establishment energy — but channeled through a candidate likely to lose a winnable seat. Second, the analytic: Goldberg distinguishes carefully between bad-faith antisemitism charges deployed to silence Israel criticism (her usual subject) and actual antisemitism, and locates Galindo in the latter via the Nation-of-Islam-derived “the people who identify as Jews today are imposters” frame. She cites the 19th-century formulation that antisemitism is “the socialism of fools” — anti-Jewish conspiracy theories deflect popular anger from unjust systems toward supernatural fantasies about demonic cabals. The closer makes the partisan-strategic case: Owens and Marjorie Taylor Greene are explicitly calling for a populist-left/populist-right alliance, and a Galindo would fit easily into it. The piece is also structurally a procedural-update story: GOP dark money is meddling in Democratic primaries to elevate the worst candidates, and party infrastructure (DCCC for Garcia, John Lira’s late-withdrawn endorsement) is in confused response.

The neutral-state critique returns: communitarianism, pop anti-racism, and the cultural-Marxism genealogy

This week produces the project’s strongest cross-orientation convergence to date on a single intellectual question: whether the post-1960s American liberal state’s posture of moral neutrality has structurally produced the present configuration of grievance politics. Michael Sandel’s long Noema interview with Nathan Gardels is the missing communitarian-pole reading the project’s Brooks-Mandel-Compact cluster has been running variations on for weeks — Sandel argues MAGA is the predicted “infusion of politics with moral substance” his 1986 conversation with Daniel Bell warned would inevitably follow from a liberalism-of-neutrality. The cluster is sharpened this week by Robert P. George’s eulogy of Jürgen Habermas — the German Frankfurt-School-second-generation philosopher whose ethics/morality distinction is the load-bearing continental version of Rawls’s comprehensive-doctrines/public-reasons cut, and which Sandel’s communitarian critique has long been directed against. Brooks, writing from inside the campus-leadership layer he visits, reports a parallel humanist revival in moral-formation, civic-thought, and “how to do life” programs. Andrews and Adubato form a deliberate Compact two-piece on the pop-anti-racism dilemma. Shullenberger excavates the LaRouchite origin of right-wing AND left-wing “cultural Marxism” theories and finds them descended from the same pamphleteering source. Sun Liping reads American “Left” from outside as Liberal-not-Left, asking Chinese readers whether the bai zuo (白左 — the Chinese-internet slur for Western progressives) framing actually fits their conditions. MacIntyre engages from the right with a frame the analysis flags as fundamentally flawed — a mono-causal female-economic-independence-causes-marriage-decline diagnosis whose implied prescription is held back. Six writers reaching the same diagnostic terrain from incompatible starting points (Catholic-natural-law right, communitarian center, post-liberal Compact, Burnham-Schmittian right, outside-China sociologist, mainstream-Atlantic moderate) is the week’s most-prized institutional finding; the disagreement migrates, as it has the last several weeks, to the prescription layer.

Culture Wars & The Myth Of The Neutral State — Noema interview with Michael Sandel, Noema — 2026-04-14 · Claude’s Response → Responding to: the 40-year arc since Sandel and Gardels’ 1986 dinner-table conversation with sociologist Daniel Bell about “the American Cultural Civil War” — and specifically the question Gardels opens with: now, in 2026, hasn’t MAGA done precisely what Sandel warned in 1986 would happen if liberalism continued to abdicate moral substance, by infusing politics with “the strong gods” of family, faith, and nation?

Sandel accepts the framing: yes, MAGA is the predicted outcome, because for 40 years the liberalism of neutrality — what Sandel calls “tolerance of avoidance” — created a moral vacuum in public discourse that “narrow, intolerant moralisms” (religious fundamentalism or hyper-nationalism) inevitably filled. He extends the diagnosis through his Tyranny of Merit framework: Reagan/Thatcher market faith was softened but not challenged by Clinton/Blair/Schröder/Obama, who embraced “the rhetoric of rising” (go to college, what you earn depends on what you learn) that contained an implicit insult to the 62 percent of Americans without four-year degrees — your failure is your fault. By 2016 (Brexit, Trump) the populist backlash arrived as politics of grievance against credentialed elites. Sandel’s prescriptive move is pluralism-of-engagement (the same-sex marriage debate as model: public mind-changing through substantive moral conversation, not court-mandated neutrality), backed by structural reforms — banning targeted digital advertising (because the social-media business model “coarsens and corrupts public discourse”), creating non-profit deliberation platforms, and using citizen assemblies chosen by sortition (lottery, the ancient democratic mechanism) to break credentialist prejudice in representative government. Sandel and Gardels float a radical extension: a bicameral legislature where one house is elected and one chosen by lot, citing the Irish Citizens’ Assembly precedent on abortion. The closing disagreement with Bell’s 1986 line (“no society can exist if we take non-negotiable moral and cultural issues and politicize them”) is Sandel’s load-bearing claim: we can’t know in advance what’s non-negotiable until we try to deliberate, and “listening is a democratic art” — the discipline this moment requires.

Jürgen Habermas: A Serious Man — Robert P. George, National Review — 2026-05-17 · Claude’s Response → Responding to: the death of Jürgen Habermas at 96 — Frankfurt School second-generation philosopher, lead theorist of “deliberative democracy” and “ethics of discourse,” and (per George) the continental analogue to Rawls in the Anglosphere.

Robert P. George (McCormick Professor of Jurisprudence, Princeton, and director of the James Madison Program) writes a generous-but-pointed eulogy: he names Habermas a serious man (closing with Don Corleone’s phrase) and credits the philosopher’s late-life intellectual honesty about Christianity’s load-bearing role in liberal-democratic values and his post-October-7 stand against antisemitism, while pressing hard on Habermas’s central theoretical move. That move is the ethics/morality distinction: “ethics” covers personal beliefs about how to live (where George places abortion and euthanasia in Habermas’s reading); “morality” covers public matters concerning “the interests of all.” George’s objection is that this distinction is functionally identical to Rawls’s untenable comprehensive-doctrines/public-reasons cut — both philosophers shifted abortion and euthanasia out of the scope of democratic deliberation without ever offering an argument that the unborn or terminally ill are not members of the human family whose interests count. The piece names John Finnis as the source of that critique and treats Habermas’s late dialogue with Ratzinger (2004) as the most productive engagement: Habermas conceded Christianity’s status as more than precursor or catalyst for liberal-democracy values and admitted that neither secularism nor postmodernism offered a serious alternative legacy. George closes by inviting Habermas’s disciples to honor the dialogue with the future Pope alongside the rest of the legacy. The piece is a natural-law-tradition reading of a liberal-secularist patriarch — register: serious, generous on the personal but unforgiving on the central theoretical disagreement.

Something Big Is Happening on Campus — David Brooks, The Atlantic — undated (May 2026) · Claude’s Response → Responding to: Public-trust collapse in U.S. higher education (Pew: 70% say universities headed in the wrong direction) and Trump’s attacks on the academy.

Brooks argues that the “humanistic” tradition — education aimed at character formation, ethical inquiry, and the “Great Conversation” rather than credentialing — is being rebuilt by a generation of dissident teachers and university presidents he encountered while visiting roughly two dozen campuses per year. He names the forces that hollowed it out (research specialization; the preprofessional turn measured by UCLA’s freshman-survey flip from 80% “meaningful philosophy of life” in the 1960s to 80% “very well off financially” today; politicization in a “Foucault-inspired monoculture” with a Michigan/Northwestern survey finding 88% of students admit lying to seem more progressive; crumbling humanistic self-confidence; loss of “national purpose” per Bill Readings’s The University in Ruins). He then catalogues three buckets of revival: moral formation (Purdue’s Cornerstone program scaling from 100 to 5,500 students; Wake Forest’s $35M Educating Character Initiative; Hadzi-Antich’s Great Questions Foundation at community colleges), civic thought (Yale’s Center for Civic Thought under Bryan Garsten; new state-funded civic schools at Tennessee, Ohio State, Florida, UT-Austin), and “how to do life” courses (Yale’s Santos happiness course; Notre Dame’s “God and the Good Life”; Yale’s “Life Worth Living”). His hinge claim is historical-pattern: universities reform after barbarism (Columbia’s Core was “born of trauma” after WWI; Maritain/Hutchins/Arendt after WWII), and the Trump era is now the catalyst. But the analysis flags a fundamental flaw: the evidence is selection-on-the-dependent-variable anecdotage from a writer with skin in the game (Brooks teaches in Garsten’s program at Yale and namechecks it favorably), and the survey data Brooks cites cuts strongly against his “tide is turning” thesis without acknowledgment.

Who Benefits from Urban Poverty? — Andrews, Helen, Compact — 2026-05-12 · Claude’s Response → Responding to: Beryl Satter’s new book Cash on the Block: The Broken Promise of Reinvestment in Black Urban Neighborhoods* (Harvard UP, 416 pp), which extends the thesis of Satter’s 2009 Family Properties (an influence on Ta-Nehisi Coates’s 2014 “Case for Reparations” essay) to a broader range of anti-poverty programs from the Great Society through the subprime mortgage crisis, arguing that anti-poverty money was systematically “funneled from government to elite intermediaries” with little reaching intended communities.*

Andrews’s structural complaint is that Satter’s earlier book contained “nuance” about white flight (the same facts looking different from competing perspectives) which has now disappeared into a “frankly conspiratorial analysis” in which programs failed because they were “designed to fail by malefactors.” Her empirical counters: (1) the Nixon “black capitalism” program (which Satter dismisses) increased minority-business federal contracts 7x in two years and SBA loans to minority businesses from 6% to 23% of loan dollars; (2) the Economic Opportunity Loan loss rate was 35% (10x the standard SBA loss rate), with two-thirds of Section 8(a) firms delinquent in 1973, despite the SBA’s own admission they put “11x as much energy” into helping minority firms; (3) the “poor pay more” finding (Caplovitz 1967) was investigated by the FTC, which found ghetto merchants made less money due to higher operating costs (6.7 cents per sales dollar in bad debts vs. 0.3 cents in affluent areas); (4) ShoreBank (the supposed exemplar of community-development banking, praised by Clinton in 1985) actually used bond-market investment returns to cross-subsidize its money-losing loans and was declared insolvent in 2010 with the FDIC citing “poor underwriting and credit administration.” Her core analytical move: doing business with the poor at market rates looks predatory; declining to do business with them looks like exclusion; subsidizing doing business with them produces subprime-style crises. Closing pivot: the activists Satter celebrates (NPA’s Gale Cincotta, ACORN, the SNCC “Black Manifesto” $500M demand) function as a bargaining strategy where extravagant demands are made to extract real but smaller settlements — and the beneficiaries of urban-poverty persistence include many on the racial-justice left, not just Satter’s named “white capitalists.”

Popping the bubble of pop anti-racism — Stephen G. Adubato, Compact Substack — 2026-05-15 · Claude’s Response → Responding to: Helen Andrews’s same-week Compact review of Beryl Satter’s Cash on the Block, Adubato’s own earlier Compact profile of Newark mayor Ras Baraka (son of radical poet Amiri Baraka), and CUNY sociologist John Arena’s research on the Newark public-school system documenting how anti-racist rhetoric gets co-opted to push through neoliberal economic policy.

Adubato — operating in the Compact Substack editorial-voice register, which is shorter and more polemical than the magazine itself — extends Andrews’s review into three claims: (1) the empirical impotence of DEI as practiced (it “permits enough minorities into elite spaces so as to soothe the consciences of those plagued by guilt of their ‘privilege’” rather than concretely alleviating minority working-class plight); (2) the on-the-ground evidence from majority-black Newark, where Mayor Ras Baraka — described as populist-leaning — explicitly dismissed police abolitionism as a “bourgeois-liberal” idea; (3) Arena’s structural finding that anti-racist rhetoric can be co-opted to distract from class-based politicking and thereby further neoliberal economic policy that maintains racial-minority disadvantage. The piece closes on the hope that “those determined to cling on to the sinking ship of wokeness will wake up already.” Functions as a short editorial pairing with the longer Andrews magazine piece rather than as a standalone argument.

The Frankfurt School Myth — Shullenberger, Geoff, Compact — 2026-05-11 · Claude’s Response → Responding to: A.J.A. Woods’s new book The Cultural Marxism Conspiracy: Why the Right Blames the Frankfurt School for the Decline of the West* (Verso, 245 pp), and the December 2025 publication of Gabriel Rockhill’s Who Paid the Pipers of Western Marxism? (the left-wing inversion of the same mythology), set against Paul Gottfried’s surprising acknowledgment in his 2009 memoir that he learned “true liberal intellectual exchange” from Herbert Marcuse at Yale.*

Shullenberger — Compact’s managing editor and the writer most associated with the magazine’s intellectual-history register — traces three historical layers of the Frankfurt School “cultural Marxism” myth: (1) the original Adorno/Horkheimer/Marcuse project in Dialectic of Enlightenment was actually an account of “how and why Western civilization almost destroyed itself from within” (Nazism, Stalinism, total war) — survivors of European near-self-immolation later being accused of perpetrating it; (2) the right-wing “cultural Marxism” narrative was constructed in the late Cold War by conservative think-tankers (William Lind, Paul Weyrich) as a way of keeping the communist threat alive after the Soviet collapse while explaining why the right’s economic victories had not produced cultural restoration — Woods’s key insight being that “precluding any acknowledgement that the market destabilizes traditional social structures created the need for an alternative explanation,” with foreign-born Jews as the convenient scapegoat in a revival of medieval antisemitic well-poisoning legends; (3) Woods’s most striking archival find — the LaRouchite organization (originally the National Caucus of Labor Committees, a late-60s SDS splinter) generated the most baroque elements of the cultural-Marxism narrative (Adorno composed the Beatles; Adorno-and-Marcuse subjected Angela Davis to “CIA zombie brainwash”), and these pamphlets later migrated into right-wing think tanks when LaRouche drifted rightward, with the New Left origins of the narrative being mostly forgotten. Shullenberger then notes that Rockhill’s December 2025 book has resuscitated the left-wing version (Frankfurt School as CIA front establishing a “compatible left” intelligentsia) — making explicit that both contemporary versions are inverted mirror images of each other and both descend from the same LaRouchite ur-text. He closes by reading Adorno/Horkheimer’s own framework against both versions: the antisemite “basically wants to imitate his mental image of a Jew,” and the projection of intellectual world dominion onto the Frankfurt School ghosts is itself the self-portrait of the projectors.

Why the American Left Is Liberal, Not Left — Sun Liping 孙立平, szhgh.com (Sun Liping mirror) — 2026-05-13 · Claude’s Response → Responding to: the Chinese-language discourse use of “white left” / bai zuo* (白左) — a derogatory term applied to Western progressive/liberal causes — and what Sun argues is the way the term smuggles Chinese readers’ distaste for Left (revolutionary socialism) into their reading of US left/right politics, producing a category error.*

Sun uses Yuval Levin’s The Great Debate: Edmund Burke, Thomas Paine, and the Birth of Right and Left as his anchor text. Levin’s two formulations: (1) today’s left-right debate is “a family quarrel within liberalism”; (2) it is not capitalism-vs-socialism but “a fundamental disagreement within liberalism over how best to realize freedom.” Sun extends these into a comparative-political claim: the American “Left” is in fact Liberal (modern liberalism, xinziyouzhuyi / 新自由主义) rather than Left (revolutionary socialism). The Sombart question (“Why no socialism in America?”) is treated as historically settled — material affluence, two-party constitutional democracy’s dissolution of radicalism, the immigrant-mobility tradition, and the westward “escape into freedom” frontier all made it impossible for Left to take root. The split Sun traces is internal to liberalism: 18th-19th century European liberalism limited royal power and championed free markets (classical liberalism = today’s right); the 20th-century split produced classical liberalism (now conservatism) vs. modern liberalism (xinziyouzhuyi, “new liberalism” — confusingly also the Chinese-language term for Anglo-American “neoliberalism,” though Sun is using it in the modern-American-liberalism sense). Three nodes structure the split: (1) the 1930s Great Depression and the New Deal as classical-liberalism self-correction; (2) the 1960s student/antiwar movement, when modern liberalism faced an assault from a real Left (revolutionary socialism) demanding system-level change rather than tax/court/regulation correction; (3) post-Cold-War globalization, when modern liberalism naively pushed globalization, pluralism-equality-inclusion (duo-ping-bao / 多平包, Sun’s compressed coinage for 多元-平等-包容), moralism, and contrition to excess — producing “social ills” and a corrective rightward turn. The post-2016 MAGA populist branch (anti-establishment, anti-globalization, anti-immigration, America First) replaced Reaganite establishment conservatism as the dominant Republican force, drawing on Rust Belt blue-collar / unemployed / red-state evangelical losers from globalization. Sun’s load-bearing rhetorical move is the parenthetical: he asks Chinese readers if they really think their own structural problems are excessive pluralism, dozens of genders, bathroom-label fuzziness, or refugee tolerance — making explicit that the bai zuo discourse is exporting US-conservative grievance into Chinese political imagination where it does not fit.

Conservatives are afraid to talk about the real marriage problem — MacIntyre, Auron, The Total State (MacIntyre) — 2026-05-13 · Claude’s Response → Responding to: The standard right-wing pro-marriage discourse that scolds young men (“get out of the basement, stop playing video games”) and, when more “adventurous,” cites economic burdens on family formation (housing, healthcare, childcare).

MacIntyre argues both standard moves dodge the real cause: female biological vulnerability historically forced women into economic dependence on husbands, marriage was the institutional encoding of that dependence, and the post-1960s package of contraception, abortion, legal hiring preferences, female-majority college enrollment, female-majority corporate workforces, and state welfare for single mothers severed the dependence — so women now have no structural reason to marry young or to stay married (he cites the stat that women initiate 70% of US divorces). Dwindling male career prospects, downstream of the same female-elevation policies that “systematically removed [men] from the pool of attractive marriage prospects,” compound the effect: women still won’t date men who earn less than they do, so a generation of downwardly-mobile young men has no marriage market either. He concedes both sexes bear responsibility but accuses conservatives of attacking only young men because they’re “the culturally approved target.” The piece is explicitly prescriptive-free (“there are no easy answers”) — the operative move is naming the sacred cow (female economic independence) the right won’t touch.

But the analysis identifies a fundamental flaw: the causal chain treats female labor-force participation as the prime mover of marriage decline while ignoring competing demographic and economic explanations (declining male wages predating major female workforce entry; cross-national marriage-rate data that varies independently of female employment; the comparable marriage decline among educated cohorts who could “afford” sole-earner households); the “hiring preferences elevated women” claim is asserted not evidenced; and the political prescription implied by the diagnosis — restoring female dependence — is left unstated, which is the move that would let the reader evaluate whether the diagnosis follows from the evidence or runs the other way.

The Sahel reorganization in three orientations

Three writers, three orientations, one finding: the April 25 Bamako attack and the FLA-JNIM rapprochement that produced it are not a counter-terrorism problem but suppressed-political-grievance finding new expression. Muto’s African Arguments piece extends last week’s Appiah-Mensah Azawad reading by anchoring it in the longer 2012-2024 institutional arc — France’s Operation Serval (January 2013), the 2015 Algiers Accord, the 2020-2021 coups, the 2023 MINUSMA expulsion, the January 2024 formal Accord withdrawal — each of which suppressed the Azawad question rather than resolving it. Aourid, writing in Al Jazeera Arabic, reads the same event through Ibn Khaldun’s pairing of ‘asabiyya (group solidarity, the cohesion of a kin- or tribe-based group acting as one political unit) and da‘wa (call or doctrine, the legitimating ideological mission that can graft onto an ‘asabiyya) — naming the FLA (Azawad Liberation Front, predominantly Tuareg, territorial-cultural) as supplying the ‘asabiyya and JNIM (Jama’at Nasr al-Islam wal Muslimin, Al-Qaeda-affiliated, transnational) as supplying the da‘wa, with the Khaldunian dynamic activated by a decayed central authority and made operative only when it gains external cover. Michalon and Mogaka fit the same reshaping to the bloc-architecture frame the project opened last week with Tabatabai’s bypass-network finding: France and Kenya — the Africa Forward summit’s two principals — are now articulating “the middle” position between the BRICS+/SCO bloc-builders and the Trump-administration UN-sidelining trajectory. The cross-orientation convergence is the cluster’s load-bearing feature.

Azawad Never Left: Reading Mali’s 2026 Crisis Through 2012 — Sachi Muto, African Arguments (publication) — 2026-05-13 · Claude’s Response → Responding to: the April 25, 2026 wave of attacks in Bamako, Kati, and across Mali — including the suicide attack the next day that killed Defence Minister Sadio Camara — claimed jointly by the FLA (Azawad Liberation Front, the predominantly Tuareg separatist movement) and JNIM (Jama’at Nusrat al-Islam wal-Muslimin, an Al-Qaeda-affiliated militant group), and to the international-coverage tendency to flatten this into a unified “terrorism + rebellion” story.

Muto’s central move is to separate the two actors that the security frame fuses. The FLA pursues territorial-political grievances rooted in northern Mali’s distinct Tuareg, Songhai, Arab, and Fulani communities; JNIM operates under a transnational ideological franchise (Al-Qaeda’s “global jihad” frame, which “severs the ties between Islamic practice and specific territories or communities”). Treating them as interchangeable, Muto argues, repeats the load-bearing mistake of 2012-2013: when the MNLA (National Movement for the Liberation of Azawad) declared independence in 2012 as a “secular nationalist movement with a stated willingness to negotiate,” France’s Operation Serval (January 2013) responded with the grammar of territorial integrity, sovereignty, and counter-terrorism — Le Drian’s National Assembly speech of 30 January 2013 described French forces as having “relations de bonne intelligence” with “le monde touareg,” a framing of “careful management” rather than political negotiation. The MNLA “received no meaningful diplomatic engagement from Paris.” The piece traces the path from there: the 2015 Algiers Accord (brokered by Algeria, France not a party) promised decentralisation and autonomy but didn’t resolve the political-status question; the 2020-2021 coups, the 2023 MINUSMA expulsion, the turn to Russian security support (Africa Corps), and the January 2024 formal withdrawal from the Algiers Accord all suppressed the question while ceasefires collapsed. The colonial-borders-cut-Tuareg-mobility frame (borders dividing Mali, Algeria, Niger, Libya across networks of trade and kinship) supplies the deeper structural argument: cross-border movement has been “read through the lens of security” rather than “understood on its own terms.” Muto explicitly resists the temptation to flatten Azawad to ethnic separatism — it is, in her telling, a sovereignty question about “who has the right to define territory, movement and belonging,” and it is “not a Tuareg question alone.” The political-grievance-rendered-invisible-does-not-disappear-it-finds-other-expressions closer is the load-bearing claim about why JNIM and FLA could converge despite incompatible ideological projects.

What Does Ibn Khaldun Say About What’s Happening in the Sahel Now? — Hassan Aourid حسن أوريد, Al Jazeera Arabic — 2026-05-11 · Claude’s Response → Responding to: the April 25, 2026 Bamako attack jointly claimed by the FLA (Azawad Liberation Front, predominantly Tuareg) and JNIM (Jama’at Nasr al-Islam wal-Muslimin, Al-Qaeda-affiliated), the seizure of Azawad including the symbolically charged city of Kidal, and the prevailing Western analytical view that this convergence is “unnatural” or “against nature.”

Aourid, a Moroccan political scientist, opens by stripping the prior conventional wisdom about the Sahel: that it had been “pacified” via French (Operation Barkhane / Serval) and then Russian (Africa Corps) interventions, that routine “mowing the grass” counter-terrorism policy was sufficient, and that Bamako’s alliance with Russia would let it extend control over Tuareg-distinct northern Mali. The April 25 attack shattered that frame. He notes the diplomatic significance of US Deputy Secretary of State Christopher Landau’s post-attack visits to Algiers and Rabat (with substantive content bypassed in favor of generalities), and that Washington, Moscow, and Paris all condemned the attack despite their conflicting interests — a sign the structural reading is shared across the great-power cluster. Aourid endorses Olivier Roy’s distinction: Middle East jihadist radicalism has been “beaten back” via transnational counter-terror; Sahel radicalism deploys radicalism as a “franchise” (the Arabic uses firanshiz / علامة تجارية, “brand name”) while proceeding from local considerations with tightly woven social fabric. The Tuareg dispossession across five states — language, mode of life, shared memory, “scars of subjugation, repression, and the drive to erase” — supplies the objective fact that lets JNIM (supra-national, caliphate-tied) and FLA (Tuareg-cultural, local) converge despite incompatible ideological projects. Aourid invokes the Tuareg-Kurdish comparison cautiously, anchors the historical reference points (Kidal subjected to “what resembles acts of extermination” in 1964; recurrent drought-driven Tuareg migration to neighbors and the Gulf), and reads Operation Serval 2013 as France protecting both the post-colonial framework and its tutelage over natural resources (gold where the Tuareg live in Mali, uranium in Niger’s Niger province). The piece’s load-bearing analytical move is invoking Ibn Khaldun’s pairing of ‘asabiyya (group solidarity / tribal cohesion) and da‘wa (call / ideological doctrine, usually religious in origin). Ibn Khaldun’s dynamic: when the central “state” (which Aourid carefully glosses as the ruling family in Khaldunian usage, not the modern nation-state) has weakened to “the stage of decay,” the meeting of local ‘asabiyya with a da‘wa lever becomes “a motor of history.” Western analysts who call this fusion “unnatural” are, Aourid argues, simply lacking the Khaldunian frame that has driven the Maghreb’s historical dynamics. The two caveats: (1) Ibn Khaldun’s frame was set in a context without intervening foreign powers, and several Maghreb dynamics have been aborted by foreign intervention (Crusader states, colonialism, post-colonial Western and Russian interventions); (2) the modern ‘asabiyya-da‘wa pairing becomes a real motor in the Sahel context only when it acquires international cover — becomes a lever for a foreign power and one of its instruments. The closer: what the Sahel has undergone cannot be reduced to “mowing the grass” — it signals a geopolitical reshaping of North Africa beginning from the Sahel.

In a world of blocs, France and Kenya chose the Middle — Noé Michalon and Stephen Mogaka, African Arguments (publication) — 2026-05-11 · Claude’s Response → Responding to: the May 11-12, 2026 Africa Forward summit in Nairobi — the first gathering of African heads of state convened by Emmanuel Macron since his 2017 election — and the prior commentary that has explained its Nairobi location through (i) French decline in francophone Sahel/West Africa and (ii) the trade/investment shift away from aid-based France-Africa relations.

The authors accept those two explanations as true but argue both are insufficient. The deeper rationale is geopolitical: France and Kenya are both “regional powers trying to consolidate their global position” whose interests depend on the continued functioning (and reform) of the post-WWII multilateral order. France inherited a permanent UN Security Council seat from its colonial-empire-era leverage; Kenya has been a multilateral-institutions host since UN headquarters arrived in Nairobi in 1972 (one of the UN’s four main global sites) and a peacekeeping/peace-mediation node. Both have practiced “summit diplomacy” — Paris and Nairobi hosting the One Planet Summit, Africa Climate Summit, Paris Peace Summit — building advocacy coalitions on global stages. Both share an “outsider-from-inside” leadership profile (Ruto and Macron also share a birthday, December 21). The piece situates the Africa Forward summit as preparation for the G7 summit France will host a month later, where Ruto has been invited and the Africa Forward deliberations on international-order reform will be highlighted. The choice of Nairobi over Lagos or Johannesburg (Nigeria and South Africa have stronger France economic ties) signals that Kenya — having held high-level engagements with China, India, Russia, UK, and Commonwealth in early 2026 — has positioned itself as a “strategic bargaining partner” in a global theatre where France refuses to be a spectator. The reformist agenda Paris and Nairobi share differs from both the Trump administration’s UN-sidelining / Board-of-Peace parallel architecture and the BRICS+/SCO sphere-of-influence building by Russia and China — it is “the middle.” The piece closes with structural pessimism: Macron has a year left in his term, his party will struggle in 2027, and the time window for the France-Kenya middle-position push to durably restructure international institutions before the order’s crumbling continues is short.

Hantavirus, vaccines, and the WHO-skeptic moment

A cross-orientation public-health disagreement crystallizes around the MV Hondius cruise-ship Andes-strain hantavirus outbreak. Tufekci textually audits the WHO assessment by phoning Gustavo Palacios, the senior author of the 2018 Epuyén-outbreak New England Journal of Medicine paper the WHO is citing, who tells her the agency is misreading his work — the paper’s “prolonged or close contact” phrasing was not meant to exclude respiratory/airborne transmission, and the calculated reproduction number of 2.1 is in early-SARS-CoV-2 range. Riley dismantles the theoretical substrate of HHS Secretary RFK Jr.’s 2021 The Real Anthony Fauci on its own internal logic, identifying that RFK confuses miasma theory (the 19th-century bad-air hypothesis disproven by Koch in the 1880s) with Antoine Béchamp’s terrain theory and slides between them — and traces the alleged Pasteur deathbed recantation (“the microbe is nothing, the terrain is everything”) to a 1946 fabrication first sourced from a self-published German anti-pharma book. The unsigned Free Press Apeel Sciences investigation is the cultural parallel: two April 2023 Facebook posts confusing a grape-derived produce coating (FDA- and EFSA-approved) with an unrelated UK floor cleaner of the same name, amplified by a MAHA-influencer pile-on (Bill Gates as bogeyman; Make America Healthy Again is RFK Jr.’s health-policy movement), destroyed a $640M-investment company in roughly 18 months. The three pieces collectively describe an information environment in which both regulatory institutions and their populist critics are now operating with credibility gaps wide enough to make ordinary public-health communication structurally impossible.

A New Viral Outbreak. The Same Mistakes All Over Again. — Tufekci, Zeynep, NYT (via ProQuest) — 2026-05-12 · Claude’s Response → Responding to: The MV Hondius cruise-ship hantavirus outbreak (at least seven confirmed infections), the April 25 St. Helena flight in which an infected passenger collapsed and died in South Africa with 97 traced contacts, and the WHO / Trump-administration messaging that the Andes strain is “much harder to catch” and spreads only through “close and prolonged contact.”

Tufekci takes the WHO and US public-health reassurances about Andes-strain hantavirus transmissibility apart by going to the source — interviewing Gustavo Palacios, senior author of the New England Journal of Medicine paper on the 2018 Epuyén, Argentina outbreak that the WHO is citing. Palacios tells her the WHO is misreading his paper: “prolonged or close contact” in the paper was not meant to exclude respiratory/airborne transmission, and airborne-transmission expert Linsey Marr told CBC the same data is “strongly suggestive of airborne transmission.” Tufekci marshals the empirical case: the 2018 outbreak started with one infected person at a 100-guest birthday party who left after 90 minutes, infecting five non-adjacent attendees, one of whom went on to infect six more including a spouse, and ten more were infected at the wake — a clear superspreading pattern. Palacios’s calculated reproduction number (2.1) is close to early-2020 SARS-CoV-2 (which the WHO also initially reassured about); the incubation period can be as long as 40 days (extraordinary, and makes contact tracing radically harder than the standard 5–14 day window); viral loads rise 48 hours before symptoms, suggesting presymptomatic transmission. The article’s structural argument is meta: officials are repeating the early-COVID error of reassuring on inadequate evidence rather than communicating uncertainty about modes of transmission, incubation period, and presymptomatic spread. The WHO has quietly updated its technical documents over the weekend to include “close proximity exposure” and “exposure in enclosed or shared spaces” — better but still rigid (still defines exposure as six feet for 15+ cumulative minutes, which doesn’t fit the Epuyén birthday-party data). The closer is the political payload: if the outbreak does become a pandemic, RFK Jr. is the US Health Secretary in charge of the response — a sentence that does an enormous amount of work for very few words. Pairs against Lileks’s NR “Don’t Buy into Hantapanic” piece this same week (which argues the opposite — that the WHO reassurances are correct and elite Covid-trauma is generating disproportionate alarm) and against Yglesias’s Slow Boring hantavirus piece.

The Bizarre Theory Behind RFK’s Attack on Vaccines — Riley, Alexander, Compact — 2026-05-14 · Claude’s Response → Responding to: RFK Jr.’s response to the 2026 hantavirus cruise-ship outbreak (“We have this under control”), his record at HHS (dropped recommendations on hepatitis A, meningitis, rotavirus and flu pediatric vaccines; cancellation of mRNA vaccine research; plummeting childhood vaccination rates), and specifically Chapter 12 of Kennedy’s 2021 book The Real Anthony Fauci, where Kennedy contrasts germ theory and what he labels “miasma theory.”

Riley’s structural move is to take Kennedy’s own framing seriously enough to identify the precise category-confusion: Kennedy labels his preferred framework “miasma theory” but the framework he actually defends is Antoine Béchamp’s terrain theory (susceptibility to disease comes from the host organism’s “terrain”; Béchamp’s “microzymes,” lacking any evidence, were the proposed mechanism). Riley’s analytical chain: (1) miasma theory historically posited bad-air-or-vapor causation and was disproven by Koch’s 1880s identification of cholera bacteria; (2) terrain theory is partially salvageable but Béchamp’s specific causal claim was wrong; (3) Kennedy’s apple-rot analogy and his “starving African child with measles” framing slide between the two; (4) the alleged Pasteur deathbed recantation (“the microbe is nothing, the terrain is everything”) was already debunked in 1946 by Pasteur’s grandson and is sourced by Kennedy to a self-published German anti-pharma book; (5) Kennedy’s pejorative framing (“germ theory’s proponents are domineering, imperialist, militarized”; vaccines as “poisons”) functions to turn readers against scientific consensus rather than to engage it. Riley closes by tracing the broader public-health-authority credibility collapse — the pandemic missteps spiked skepticism enough to make a scientifically ignorant cabinet secretary politically possible — and warns that the longer Kennedy holds the post, the worse vaccination outcomes will get.

This Company Was an American Success Story. Until MAHA Influencers Sank It. — The Free Press, Free Press — 2026-05-14 · Claude’s Response → Responding to: the near-total collapse of Apeel Sciences’ U.S. business between April 2023 and 2025 — from 60% of American avocados Apeel-coated at peak to zero now — driven by a Make America Healthy Again (MAHA) influencer disinformation campaign that started with two Facebook posts confusing Apeel Sciences’ grape-derived produce coating Edipeel with an unrelated UK floor cleaner of the same name.

The piece reconstructs the timeline: April 10, 2023, two Canadian Facebook posts claim Apeel “can’t be washed off” and link to a floor-cleaner ingredient list; the posts cascade through Facebook, Instagram, TikTok, YouTube, Telegram, Rumble, Reddit; MAHA-aligned influencers — Krysten Dornik (“Krysten’s Kitchen”), Robyn Openshaw (“GreenSmoothieGirl”), Alison Steinberg (then OAN, now LindellTV), actress Michelle Pfeiffer, and Indiana Republican congressman Marlin Stutzman (sponsor of the “Apeel Reveal Act of 2025”) — pile on, leaning on Bill Gates’s 2012 Gates Foundation grant as proof of malign intent. The FDA and European Food Safety Authority had separately approved Apeel; the product is monoglycerides and diglycerides from grape seed (the same chemistry as longstanding food-grade wax coatings), with only trace naturally-occurring heavy metals; nonetheless Limoneira (130-year-old citrus producer), Calavo, Costco, Kroger, Albertsons, and Driscoll’s all dropped Apeel under online-mob pressure. Apeel sued Openshaw under the 1946 Lanham Act (false advertising — not defamation, which is harder), settled for an apology video and undisclosed payment; Pfeiffer apologized after direct contact. Apeel’s European business (Edeka in Germany, Tesco in UK, plus Denmark, Netherlands, Norway) is thriving because EU law forces social-media platforms to remove illegal content under threat of fines. The piece is unsigned (bylined “The Free Press” — multi-author guard flag) and reads as long-form investigative work likely produced by an FP staff reporter or guest investigative contributor; the narrative voice is first-person (“I asked,” “told me”) throughout but supplies no name signal. Northeastern law-and-media professor Alexandra Roberts is the doctrinal anchor on why Lanham Act suits might generalize to other wellness-influencer cases.

Standalone pieces

These are pieces that don’t cluster with this week’s themes but each contribute distinct value. They are grouped lightly by domain for navigation: the continuing heterodox-macro/antitrust fault line, US state-capacity failures across immigration and infrastructure, the third week of the Klippenstein regressive-enforcement-architecture thread, the democratic-resilience question that Hungary’s Tisza victory has reopened, and a residual cluster covering Western-decline diagnoses, non-Anglophone reads, MR miscellany, and the rest.

Heterodox macro and antitrust — the Yglesias-Stoller fault line

The Yglesias mega-essay on the Spirit Airlines debate (13.6K-word podcast transcript) and Stoller’s two BIG pieces are a continuing project disagreement that should be tracked as such — last week the user flagged that the synthesis adopted Stoller’s “sabotage merger” framing of Spirit un-flagged, and this week Yglesias is making the counter-case directly. The Tooze and Cass pieces orbit the same fault line.

What the Spirit debate is really about — Matthew Yglesias, Slow Boring — 2026-05-14 · Claude’s Response → Responding to: Spirit Airlines’ Chapter 7 liquidation in the wake of the Iran-war-driven jet-fuel price spike; the conservative narrative (Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy, Blaze TV comedians, AI cartoon imagery) blaming Biden’s DOJ for blocking the JetBlue–Spirit merger; and the broader neo-Brandeisian intellectual project — Lina Khan, Matt Stoller (BIG), Ganesh Sitaraman (Vanderbilt Policy Accelerator), Phil Longman (Open Markets Institute), the Washington Monthly “Terminal Sickness” essay — arguing airline deregulation was a mistake. This is a long podcast transcript with Jerusalem Demsas, structured as Yglesias’s “tinfoil hat” argument that the anti-monopoly movement is really pro-central-planning in anti-monopoly clothing. The proximate cause of Spirit’s collapse is unambiguous: the war with Iran spiked jet fuel costs (jet fuel is ~20% of airline costs, prices roughly doubled with the Strait of Hormuz closed for ten weeks), and Spirit was already weakened by a Pratt & Whitney geared-turbofan-engine recall that disproportionately grounded its fleet plus legacy carriers copying its unbundled-fare model with “Basic Economy” defensive products. The counterfactual case: if JetBlue had been allowed to buy Spirit, the combined airline would almost certainly be bankrupt today anyway, so the conservative “Biden killed Spirit” narrative is literally wrong — but the antitrust-victory framing fails too, because the assets Spirit was trying to sell to JetBlue will now be auctioned to a mix of buyers including JetBlue at a lower price. The tinfoil-hat thesis: the pre-1978 Civil Aeronautics Board (CAB) regime was explicitly designed around a 1935 “ruinous competition” theory that justified restricting market entry to prevent price wars; the 1958 city-pair data shows 23 of the top 100 markets were monopolies and another 57 duopolies; the intra-California Los Angeles–San Francisco fare was less than half the regulated Boston–Washington fare for a similar distance; and the CAB-era extra revenue went into half-empty planes with piano bars rather than profits. The neo-Brandeisian discomfort with airline bankruptcy, refusal to push for the obvious pro-competitive moves (lifting cabotage restrictions, raising the 25%-voting / 49%-equity foreign-ownership cap so foreign carriers can compete domestically as Ryanair does in the EU), and explicit Longman essays calling for AI-enabled re-regulation of route allocations exposes that what the movement actually wants is the pre-Carter regulated economy, not more competition. Yglesias’s hinge: if the American Economic Liberties Project were called the Technocratic Regulators Can Improve on Market Outcomes Institute, the abundance-vs-antimonopoly dispute would stop being confusing. The piece also runs a “peer review” segment on a Gihleb/Giuntella/Morag NBER paper showing that male-disfavoring labor-market shocks shift household consumption toward female-coded goods, with Yglesias pushing back on Demsas’s reading by noting the study only tests one direction of shock.

Chartbook 448 Price controls, nationalization, financial repression, fiscal-monetary coordination - embracing the Four Horsemen of Heterodoxy — Adam Tooze, Chartbook (Tooze) — 2026-05-11 · Claude’s Response → Responding to: Gita Gopinath’s FT op-ed (warning that fiscally constrained governments may turn to “broad-based price controls, financial repression, nationalisations, and pressure on central banks to absorb fiscal risk”), Daniela Gabor’s amplifying tweet, and Tooze’s own Shutdown (2021) book argument about post-2020 crisis-management. Tooze takes Gopinath’s terse closing — that markets pricing the no-bailout future would produce synchronized stock-bond sell-offs — and reverses the polarity: rather than treating the heterodox measures as the disaster, treat them as the rational response to a permanent-crisis world (Meadway’s “permanent crisis”; Weber et al.’s “overlapping emergencies”). He sketches a four-step political-economy logic: (1) repeated shocks (COVID, Ukraine, Hormuz) force centrist politicians toward heterodox-Keynesian crisis-management against their will; (2) they retreat to fiscal rules at the first opportunity, producing the “Starmer charade of reformism” and exhausting both their political capital and the bond markets’ patience; (3) fiscal-space constraints then bite (the UK “moron premium” is the leading example); (4) constructive policy-thinking should turn to the Four Horsemen — price controls against profit-gouging, nationalization for capital discipline, treasury-central-bank coordination to absorb borrowing, and financial repression (negative real rates) to distribute the legacy burden. Tooze grounds the move in the 2019-2020 IMF/BIS “integrated policy framework” pivot, in which Gopinath herself helped formalize how emerging-market heterodox practice (FX intervention, capital controls, macroprudential measures combined with inflation targeting) had outrun the Washington-consensus textbook. His own ranking: hesitant on price controls, sees targeted nationalization or its threat as defensible, sees no alternative to financial repression and treasury-central-bank coordination.

Monopoly Round-Up: What Challenging a Bowling Monopoly Says About America — Matt Stoller, BIG (Stoller) — 2026-05-11 · Claude’s Response → Responding to: a class-action complaint filed days earlier against Bowlero, the private-equity-backed bowling chain now controlling 360 centers and 35% of US bowling industry revenue, brought by attorney Catherine Simonsen (formerly Lina Khan’s FTC merger team) of the new boutique Simonsen Sussman. Stoller’s piece is a Round-Up format, but the lead Bowlero argument carries the analytic weight. He places the case in the Bowling Alone (Robert Putnam) tradition — bowling is a load-bearing American civic institution (70 million annual bowlers, 28,000 leagues, a million league participants) being rolled up into “upscale entertainment” by financiers who explicitly disdain the sport (“I don’t think anyone takes bowling seriously” — Bowlero CEO Tom Shannon, 2011). The rollup mechanism is the standard PE playbook he tracks elsewhere: $450M Cerberus/Credit Suisse financing in 2012, 14 years of acquisitions including Lucky Strike and the Professional Bowlers Association, concentrated dominance in market after market (Northern Virginia 34%→95%), then dynamic pricing, hours cuts, gambling integration via the MoneyBowl app, and supplier-leverage advantages from exclusive deals with Kegel (lane-conditioning oil) and the PBA tournament circuit (which Bowlero owns and charges rivals to use). The legal innovation is the Clayton Act plus state unfair-practice laws, demanding court-ordered breakup of the chain and damages, before a jury — which Stoller reads as part of a “new constellation” of Khan-trained antitrust lawyers building creative remedies. The Round-Up’s other items (Mark Ruffalo Paramount-Warner op-ed; Colorado banning surveillance pricing; Oregon constraining corporate medicine ownership; Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy’s corporate-sponsored seven-month road-trip reality show; oligarch tax-as-racism rhetoric) sketch his thesis that populist anti-monopoly politics is “bubbling up” from multiple state-and-civic directions even as federal politics goes the other way.

Guess Who’s Willing to Pay? — Oren Cass, Commonplace — 2026-05-15 · Claude’s Response → Responding to: a New York Federal Reserve Bank blog post (“Same Shock, Different Roads? A K-Shaped Pattern at the Pump”) finding that the March 2026 gas-price spike led high-income households to maintain real gasoline consumption while low-income households were forced to cut back; the broader textbook-orthodox claim (Cass quotes Mankiw’s Principles of Microeconomics directly) that free markets “allocate the supply of goods to the buyers who value them most highly, as measured by their willingness to pay.” Cass uses the Fed finding to attack the textbook welfare claim head-on. The Mankiw passage — that ticket scalping is “one example of how markets reach efficient outcomes” — is offered as the explicit (not strawmanned) version of the orthodoxy he is rebutting. His argument: when low-income households cut gas consumption under a price shock, they aren’t revealing that they value the marginal gallon less; they’re revealing that their total budget is smaller. If anything the marginal gallon was more valuable to them, because they were already making harder tradeoffs before the spike. He coins this the “Learjet problem”: when gas prices spike, families take fewer trips to grandma while corporate executives don’t reduce private jets to Davos, and the resulting allocation is “efficient” in the Mankiw sense but obviously not welfare-maximizing. Cass concedes the usual reasons not to interfere (property rights, policymaker incompetence, productive incentives) but draws a sharp line: it’s unreasonable to pretend under inequality that the price system is welfare-maximizing rather than just rationing by ability-to-pay.

The piece then pivots into a roundup-style omnibus: Trump’s Beijing summit produced “mercifully much less” than feared (soybean/beef/Boeing buys, an H200 chip sale Cass dislikes, a Board of Trade), the Pentagon’s “Deal Team Six” critical-minerals push, the Connected Vehicle Security Act gaining traction with UAW and Teamsters backing, the Faster Labor Contracts Act four signatures short of a discharge petition, Mike Cassidy’s Senate primary potentially shaking up the HELP Committee, a David Valadao Supporting Newborn Parents Act baby-bonus, the third consecutive year of declining US overdose deaths, ChatGPT-driven grade inflation in college, US test-score generation-long decline, and Kalshi prediction-market parlay losses exceeding $100M.

US state capacity: immigration, infrastructure, regulatory loopholes

The Krikorian/Dickerson pair illuminates the immigration-enforcement architecture from two ends — the restrictionist program with its body-armor-vs-briefcase menu, and the receiving-end reporting from La Lima Honduras showing what the architecture produces at scale. Huq and Mulvaney expose two distinct US-state-capacity failures (mandatory critical-infrastructure cybersecurity standards never adopted; sports-betting regulatory arbitrage via CFTC jurisdiction).

Immigration Enforcement Needs ‘You’re Fired’ — Mark Krikorian, Commonplace — 2026-05-14 · Claude’s Response → Responding to: anxieties among immigration restrictionists (and Krikorian’s CIS colleagues) that the Trump administration is backing off mass-deportation promises after Tom Homan’s February 2026 de-escalation in Minneapolis and the dismissal of Border Patrol official Gregory Bovino following two protester deaths in confrontational enforcement sweeps; the 2024 RNC “Mass Deportation Now!” rallying cry; the Harvard-Harris poll finding majority support for deportation but Andrew Arthur’s gloss that voters “just don’t want to see or hear much about it.” Krikorian, head of the Center for Immigration Studies (CIS), argues the 14-15 million illegal aliens (roughly half admitted under Biden) cannot be removed through theatrical sweeps without provoking political backlash, but neither should the Trump administration retreat into amnesty. He divides the illegal population into “public safety threats” (handled with body-armor ICE enforcement plus detainer cooperation with police, complicated by sanctuary jurisdictions) and “everyone else” (handled with briefcase enforcement designed to make residence untenable — unemployable, unbankable, unlicenseable). The mechanism: mandatory E-Verify (currently used by ~50% of new hires voluntarily, possibly mandatable by executive regulation though Trump doesn’t favor it), targeted I-9 audits paired with SSA no-match letters now coupled with ICE response instructions and fines, periodic worksite raids on egregious employers, closing the matricula consular bank-account loophole, levying the civil fines for ignoring deportation orders (theoretically ~$1,000/day, never previously levied; waivable if illegals self-deport via the CBP Home app). On the inflow side, Krikorian credits the administration with reducing new unauthorized border crossings to “a lower number than any point since the nineteenth century” and warns against false security (smugglers boating up the California coast, visa overstays, port-of-entry fraud). The thesis: shrinking the illegal population is a process, not an event — fewer come, more leave on their own, the population declines over time without requiring the dragnets that alienate middle-of-the-road voters.

A New Kind of Family-Separation Crisis — Caitlin Dickerson, The Atlantic — undated · Claude’s Response → Responding to: The deportation regime under Trump’s second term — ICE holding 60,000 people in detention as of early April (71% with no criminal convictions); the Justice Department’s purge of immigration judges deemed too lenient; the policy guidance reported by DHS officials to Dickerson: “Nothing should slow down deportations.” Dickerson’s spent three days in late March at the La Lima, Honduras reception center run by the Italian Scalabrinian order under Sister Idalina Bordignon, watching five planes deliver 479 deportees in shackles. Of the 40 deportees she interviewed, 24 said they’d left children behind in the U.S. — 15 of those children were younger than 5, four were infants. Most parents said they were never asked about being a parent during their ICE processing, and one single mother reported an officer wrote on her documents that she was childless and told her it “doesn’t matter” she was being separated from her 3-year-old. ICE policy nominally requires officers to ask about minor children and reunite families before deportation, but it’s not codified in law and is hedged with “when operationally feasible” and “ICE reserves its right to make case-by-case removal decisions.” The texture is the argument: Claudia, 39, was deported leaving her 11-year-old in Atlanta; Jhonny, arrested at a fiber-optics job site despite a valid work permit, can no longer hold or kiss his 3-year-old; Edwin’s daughter leaves the phone on so he can watch over them until their mother returns from her overnight janitor shift; Denia, who’d lived 26 years in Texas, returned to find her mobile home being repossessed. The structural framing: Trump has deported people in similar annual numbers to Obama and Biden, but eliminated the safeguards (Obama and Biden both blocked ICE from deporting most people without serious criminal records and allowed sole caregivers of minor children to remain via check-ins). Three deportees have been murdered within days of arrival since last fall, per the Scalabrinians; gangs and bandits assume deportees’ families can pay larger ransoms. Honduras’s new Trump-aligned president has eliminated the cash-assistance program for deportees.

America’s Glaring Infrastructure Vulnerability — Yameen Huq, Commonplace — 2026-05-13 · Claude’s Response → Responding to: the November 2023 CyberAv3ngers (Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps-linked) attack on Aliquippa, Pennsylvania’s municipal water system; FBI Director Christopher Wray’s testimony on the People’s Republic of China Volt Typhoon group pre-positioning in US infrastructure; the 2024 Change Healthcare/UnitedHealth ransomware attack compromising 190 million Americans’ health records; and the EPA’s 2023 mandatory-cybersecurity-rule attempt being struck down by lawsuits one month before the Aliquippa attack. Huq frames US critical infrastructure cybersecurity as a textbook negative externality: the 50,000+ community water systems, 2,000 municipal electric utilities, and 6,000 hospitals cannot afford even one IT staffer (let alone a cybersecurity program), yet they’re now front-line defenders against foreign state cyber units. He traces the policy lineage: Clinton’s 1996 Executive Order 13010 and 1998 Presidential Policy Directive 63 established the voluntary public-private partnership; Obama’s 2013 PPD-21 and EO 13636 created the 16-sector framework still in use; of those 16 sectors only three (bulk power, pipelines/surface transport, nuclear) carry mandatory federal standards, all implemented in direct response to specific crises (2003 Northeast blackout, 2021 Colonial Pipeline attack, post-9/11). The 2023 EPA attempt to mandate water-utility cybersecurity assessments under the 1974 Safe Drinking Water Act was struck down in court; the Biden NSM-22 (April 2024) acknowledged voluntary limits but wasn’t binding; the Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure Act (CIRCIA, 2022) is delayed past May 2026 and would require only post-incident reporting, not prevention. The EPA’s 2024 Inspector General report found 97 drinking water systems serving 25M+ Americans had critical or high-risk vulnerabilities, with 70%+ of inspected utilities using default passwords, shared logins, and credentials for former employees. Health: 67% of healthcare organizations hit by ransomware in 2024, in-hospital mortality during attacks rising 33%. Huq’s three policy prescriptions: (1) mandatory minimum cybersecurity standards across all 16 sectors enforced by a central organization; (2) increased State and Local Cybersecurity Grant Program funding with flexibility to reach privately owned infrastructure; (3) Congress must act because creative regulatory interpretation produces second-best solutions that get struck down.

Washington’s End-Run Around Sports Betting Laws — Mick Mulvaney, Commonplace — 2026-05-10 · Claude’s Response → Responding to: the post-2018 Murphy v. NCAA regulatory regime in which states have spent years building sports-betting frameworks (age limits, licensing, advertising rules, problem-gambling supports); prediction-market platforms Kalshi and Polymarket now offering “event contracts” on football outcomes and individual player stats; 41 state attorneys general from both parties opposing the Commodity Futures Trading Commission’s jurisdictional claim; and the unusual CFTC counter-move of suing Wisconsin to assert its authority over what would otherwise be state gambling regulation. Mulvaney, former Trump chief of staff and now executive director of the “Gambling Is Not Investing” advocacy coalition, frames the prediction-market expansion into sports as a regulatory-arbitrage end-run around the federalism the Supreme Court established when it struck down the federal sports-betting ban in 2018. His core distinction: legitimate derivatives (oil futures letting airlines hedge fuel-cost risk) serve identifiable economic functions — hedging, price discovery, capital allocation — while a contract paying out based on the World Cup winner has no underlying asset, no hedging function, no productive connection. Calling such contracts derivatives, he argues, is a “linguistic trick” to swap state gaming regulators for the CFTC, whose regime has no gambling-age minimums, no state tax contributions, no responsible-gaming infrastructure, and no option for states (like Mulvaney’s home South Carolina) that have chosen not to legalize sports betting at all. He documents the underage-targeting evidence: Kalshi and Polymarket paying teenage influencers, sponsoring college clubs, marketing in spaces where younger audiences gather, while their default 18-and-up access bypasses the 21-and-up state rules. He quotes Trump’s own discomfort (“the whole world has become somewhat of a casino…I’m not happy with any of this”), citing the Commodity Exchange Act’s requirement that futures contracts provide societal value as a CFTC tool the chair Mike Selig has refused to use. He addresses the “prediction markets are different from sportsbooks because traders bet against each other” defense by citing the Wall Street Journal’s investigation finding the overwhelming profit on prediction markets accrues to a tiny fraction of traders with sophisticated data and software — the house may not win, but Wall Street shops are now harvesting amateur sports bettors. (Mulvaney’s note: a former professional poker player and statistician told WSJ “you’re going to find that the easiest money is going to be in sports.”) The path forward: CFTC should rule sports event contracts outside legitimate derivatives; Congress should reaffirm gambling is a state matter; states should keep litigating; voters should pressure representatives.

Iran-war-enabled executive coercion: Klippenstein (week 3) + McCarthy from the right

This week’s strongest within-standalones convergence: Andrew C. McCarthy (NR’s former-federal-prosecutor legal analyst, generally an Iran-war supporter) and Ken Klippenstein (independent-left national-security reporter) arrive at the same finding from opposite orientations — the Iran war has accelerated the Trump DOJ / NSC use of state coercion against the press and against right-wing critics. McCarthy’s piece is the procedural-legal case: the DOJ subpoenas of WSJ, Axios, and WaPo reporters violate the AG-approval-and-narrow-drafting norms that have historically held federal press-shield legislation at bay. Klippenstein extends the regressive-enforcement-architecture thread the project has been tracking — the cross-coalition redefinition mechanism (Gorka on Tucker Carlson) and the duplication-by-design mechanism (Pentagon 211 innovation orgs). Cross-orientation convergence on the same diagnostic frame from a Garland-critic-but-process-conservative ex-prosecutor and a Substack national-security reporter is the project’s highest-prized signal.

The Trump DOJ Goes to War with the Press — Andrew C. McCarthy, National Review — 2026-05-16 · Claude’s Response → Responding to: the May 12 WSJ report that the Justice Department issued March 4 grand-jury subpoenas to the Wall Street Journal (and parallel ones to Axios and the Washington Post) over February 23 leak-sourced WSJ reporting that General Dan Caine, Joint Chiefs chairman, had warned Trump about the risks of attacking Iran six days before the war began. McCarthy (longtime NR legal-analyst, former federal prosecutor, Ball of Collusion author, generally an Iran-war supporter and a Garland critic) makes a procedural-legal argument the central voltage of which is institutional rather than ideological: he supported reversing Garland’s blanket no-subpoenas-for-newsgathering policy on the merits, but the DOJ guidelines (28 CFR §50.10 and DOJ Manual §9-13.400) require AG-level approval, serious crime grounds, significant information, exhaustion of alternative routes, and narrow drafting — all enforced in good faith — for any subpoena to a reporter. He argues the precondition for press-shield legislation having failed in Congress is that the DOJ has historically been a responsible arbiter, and once that assumption goes, the press-shield case becomes irresistible. The substantive critique is sharper: the February 23 WSJ piece did not expose any vital defense secret. The “upshot” of the reporting was that Trump knew the risks (Iranian attacks on US bases, Israeli population centers, Arab partners; depletion of air-defense interceptors) before launching the campaign and proceeded under the delusional conviction that aerial bombing would topple the regime in days. McCarthy includes an unusual on-the-record self-positioning — he supported the military action against Iran but has consistently maintained that Trump doesn’t grasp Iran’s apocalyptic ideology and that the regime would fight rabidly for survival. He distinguishes the present subpoenas from the Obama-era James Rosen and AP-records episodes and the first Trump administration’s WaPo/NYT/CNN records sweeps as a continuation of a politicization-of-DOJ pattern that has accelerated and that, on present trajectory, will exhaust the historical norm that has held press-shield legislation at bay. Closes warning that “harder battle lines are drawn” once subpoenas fly. Register: ex-prosecutor, in-house counsel to the political right, writing on national-security/press tension with the procedural authority of someone who has used these guidelines.

New Counterterror Strategy Eyes Tucker Carlson — Ken Klippenstein, Ken Klippenstein — 2026-05-13 · Claude’s Response → Responding to: Sebastian Gorka’s recent Breitbart interview (with editor-in-chief Alex Marlow) and his Dean Cain interview promoting the new National Counterterrorism Strategy that Klippenstein previously leaked. The Strategy formally identifies only three priority threats — narco-terrorists, Islamist terrorists, and “Violent Left-Wing Extremists” — and explicitly does not list right-wing extremism. Klippenstein’s lead is the operative finding: asked by Marlow whether there’s any “right-wing terror” the administration considers a threat, Gorka named former Fox News host Tucker Carlson and streamer Nick Fuentes — both of whom recently broke bitterly with Trump over the Iran War — and argued they aren’t conservatives at all. The mechanism Klippenstein names is what he calls the “terrorism two-step”: the administration first redefines a right-winger as not-really-a-right-winger (Gorka’s specific charge that Carlson “lauds Sharia law” is fabricated — Carlson described Gulf monarchies as self-confident in a way that made them welcoming to Christian visitors, not as model societies) and then targets them under the official “we don’t go after the right” rubric. Klippenstein names this as the No True Scotsman fallacy with state enforcement teeth. The piece’s secondary finding is that Gorka himself, in a Dean Cain interview, gloated about how thin negative media coverage of the Strategy has been (“delicious”), with only one Politico story registering. Klippenstein notes the obvious motive: Carlson called the Iran war “the single biggest mistake Trump, or any American president, has made in my lifetime,” called the strikes “reprehensible and immoral,” and said the war happened “at the behest and then the demand of Israel”; Fuentes posted “ISRAEL IS DRAGGING US INTO WAR. AMERICA FIRST.” That, not extremism, is the offense being prosecuted.

The Pentagon Shakedown No One Noticed — Ken Klippenstein, Ken Klippenstein — 2026-05-15 · Claude’s Response → Responding to: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth’s $1.5 trillion Pentagon budget request, an increase of more than $400 billion year-over-year, and Hegseth’s framing of the increase as enabling a “business model” replacement for “bureaucratic” acquisition. Klippenstein’s argument is fundamentally one of counting. He notes that congressional scrutiny has focused on the comparatively small question of whether the Iran War cost $25 billion or $29 billion (a “drop in the bucket” against the headline number), while the structural story — that DoD has never passed an audit and has no functional business model — goes uncontested. He then operationalizes the “innovation bloat” critique by enumerating 211 distinct Pentagon organizations created since the second Iraq War dedicated to “rapid,” “innovative,” or “future” procurement — not including legacy research labs or normal materiel commands. The list (which he publishes in full) ranges across every service branch and combatant command: AFWERX, SOFWERX, NavalX, ARCWERX, DEFENSEWERX (and at least eight other -WERXs), multiple “Rapid Capabilities Offices,” “Tech Bridges,” “Innovation Labs,” “Software Factories,” “Battle Labs,” and named projects (Kessel Run, Replicator, Project Convergence, Golden Dome, Beholder’s Gaze). Each organization, he argues, exists because its predecessors evidently failed at the same task; each accumulates funding while its peers continue to do similar work; together they constitute “duplication at an industrial scale.” Hegseth, despite his anti-bureaucracy rhetoric, will not slash these organizations — he’ll add a 212th to oversee distribution of the new $400 billion. The piece is investigative rather than analytic in form: the list is the evidence, and the auditing it implicitly invites is the policy ask.

Ukraine after the war

Kraiev (Foreign Policy Council “Ukrainian Prism,” Kyiv) and Umland (European Policy Institute, Kyiv) write the strongest policy-architecture piece on post-war Ukraine in the corpus — a multidimensional read across security, economy, institutions, demographics, and political cohesion. The piece is institutional-Ukrainian-policy register, committed to EU integration; its load-bearing finding is demographic.

When the Guns Fall Silent: Ukraine After the War — Oleksandr Kraiev and Andreas Umland, National Review — 2026-05-17 · Claude’s Response → Responding to: the spring 2026 stalemate in Ukraine (Russia’s May 13-14 1,400-drone air attack as the war’s largest, ongoing “balance of death” on the front line), and Western planning gaps around the “day after.” Kraiev (Foreign Policy Council “Ukrainian Prism,” Kyiv) and Umland (European Policy Institute, Kyiv) argue that a cease-fire would not be a recovery period but the start of a multidimensional new struggle — security, economy, institutions, demographics, and political cohesion all tested simultaneously.
