Mark Follman’s Mother Jones investigation closes the safety loop: ChatGPT walked him through tactical advice on AR-15 selection, hollow-point ammunition, rooftop-attack vantage points, and body-cam livestreaming in twenty minutes, despite OpenAI’s claimed strengthened guardrails. The AI deployment environment is losing political ground on three flanks simultaneously — buildout-localities, demand-side scaling, deployment-safety — and the discourse has not yet caught up.

Constitutional hardball moves from policy to power-acquisition

Nate Silver’s two-piece set this week sharpens the Virginia-redistricting question the project has been tracking: Republicans won the structural mid-decade redistricting argument and lost the war, the median House district has shifted from R+0.1 to R+2.5–R+3.9 per the post-Callais redraws, and Silver’s LLM-mediated synthesis of state-court ideological leans puts Republicans at 264 electoral votes worth of strong-or-leaning-R state courts versus 252 for Democrats — meaningful tilt, with Republicans holding the only court that really matters (federal SCOTUS at 6-3). Democrats are playing as “the away team”; Silver’s operational prescription is to maximize aggressive redraws in New York and Colorado where blue state courts will pass them, accept short-run draws rather than escalate to the Virginia-justice-early-retirement level some Democrats are considering.

Jacob Eisler’s Compact piece is the deeper diagnostic the Silver framework points toward. The structural finding is that contested politics has migrated from “contesting governance outcomes” to “contesting the formal terms of obtaining power” — and the migration correlates with the broader decline in state capacity. The Virginia Democratic age-reduction proposal (purging the state Supreme Court to allow the gerrymander to proceed) is Eisler’s canonical example, more extreme than McConnell’s 2016 Garland blockade because it sacrifices a constitutional building block for instrumental partisan gain. The Silver-Eisler convergence is the strongest cross-orientation finding on this thread: from different starting points, both arrive at the same conclusion that what looks like “the redistricting fight” is in fact the politics of the period. Michelle Goldberg’s column on the San Antonio Democratic runoff (a conspiratorial antisemite poised to win because Democratic radicalization absent ideological discipline curdles into Owens-style paranoia) is the third evidence point — the same dynamic in the candidate-vetting layer Democrats can no longer impose discipline on.

Iran-war-enabled executive coercion of the press, read from opposite poles

The week’s strongest unexpected-aligned-poles convergence: the Iran war has crossed the threshold at which the executive’s coercive instruments against the press, formally constrained by procedural norms that held across both parties since at least the Reagan era, have begun operating against them. Andrew McCarthy at NR — former federal prosecutor in the Southern District of New York, a Garland critic, and an Iran-war supporter — and Ken Klippenstein from the independent national-security left arrive at the same diagnostic through different operational levers, neither citing the other, the agreement independent.

McCarthy’s lever is the DOJ procedural architecture. The May 12 WSJ disclosure of March 4 grand-jury subpoenas issued against Wall Street Journal reporters — with parallel ones to Axios and the Washington Post — over the February 23 leak-sourced WSJ piece on Joint Chiefs Chairman Dan Caine’s pre-war warnings to Trump is the precipitating event. The technical argument is load-bearing: 28 CFR §50.10 and DOJ Manual §9-13.400 require AG-level approval, serious crime grounds, significant information, exhaustion of alternative routes, and narrow drafting for any compulsory legal process directed at journalists, enforced in good faith such that prosecutors usually don’t even ask. The structural-claim observation is that the reason no federal press-shield legislation has cleared Congress is that the DOJ has historically been good enough, often enough, that no congressional majority has been built — once that breaks, the shield-law case becomes politically winnable. The reporting in question disclosed no vital defense secret; the war was being telegraphed openly on Truth Social. The “upshot” the subpoenas are targeting is that Trump went in fully informed about the risks (Iranian retaliation against US bases, Israeli population centers, Arab partners; air-defense interceptor depletion) and proceeded under the conviction that aerial bombing would topple the regime in days. McCarthy strengthens rather than weakens the procedural argument with the on-the-record self-positioning that he supported the military action but consistently said Trump doesn’t grasp Iran’s apocalyptic ideology — the responsible-arbiter-as-precondition observation is doing the work, not partisan animus.

Klippenstein’s lever is the counter-terrorism designation architecture. Sebastian Gorka, the NSC counter-terrorism czar, named Tucker Carlson and Nick Fuentes as right-wing terrorism subjects in a Breitbart interview by first redefining them as non-conservatives. The mechanism Klippenstein names — the “terrorism two-step” — is the No True Scotsman fallacy with state enforcement teeth: the National Counterterrorism Strategy formally targets only narco-terrorists, Islamist terrorists, and “Violent Left-Wing Extremists” and explicitly excludes right-wing extremism, while the operational application targets anyone who breaks publicly with the administration’s Israel-and-Iran posture by first stripping them of the category-membership the Strategy’s formal text would have protected. Both Carlson (calling the strikes “the single biggest mistake Trump, or any American president, has made in my lifetime”) and Fuentes (“ISRAEL IS DRAGGING US INTO WAR. AMERICA FIRST.”) broke with Trump over the Iran war specifically — the motive is the operative fact the formal Strategy text was designed to launder.

Two different operational instruments — DOJ subpoena power and counter-terrorism designation discretion — pointed in two different directions (mainstream press and right-MAGA-dissenter), one cross-orientation diagnostic on the Iran-war-enabled press-prosecution architecture itself. The open question is whether this is one-cycle escalation or the equilibrium shift McCarthy’s framework predicts; the test is whether the 119th or 120th Congress moves federal press-shield legislation in response, with McCarthy’s structural-claim implication that the historical bargain holding shield laws back has now broken regardless of how the specific Iran subpoenas resolve. The McCarthy pairing also extends the Klippenstein regressive-enforcement-architecture thread into a third week — the architecture is now visible to the institutional-conservative right and the independent national-security left simultaneously, via different instruments, with the Iran war as the common enabling event.

The neutral state returns: Sandel as the missing communitarian pole

The strongest cross-orientation convergence of the week is on a longer arc the project has been tracking through the Brooks-Mandel-Compact cluster without quite naming. Michael Sandel’s Noema interview with Nathan Gardels is the missing communitarian-pole reading the cluster has been running variations on, and Sandel accepts the framing directly: MAGA is the outcome 1986-vintage critics of liberal neutrality predicted, because for forty years the “tolerance of avoidance” version of liberalism created a moral vacuum that “narrow, intolerant moralisms” inevitably filled. Sandel’s Tyranny of Merit framework supplies the mechanism: the Clinton-Blair-Obama “rhetoric of rising” (go to college, what you earn depends on what you learn) contained an implicit insult to the 62% of Americans without four-year degrees, and the populist backlash is the corrective. The prescriptive move is pluralism of engagement — the same-sex marriage debate as model — backed by structural reforms including banning targeted digital advertising, citizen assemblies chosen by sortition, even a bicameral legislature with one house lottery-selected on the Irish Citizens’ Assembly model. The Roe v. Wade example is the sharpest articulation: the court announced neutrality on the moral status of the fetus and then imposed a three-trimester framework that was itself a moral determination under cover of neutrality, producing “bad faith” working-class voters articulate as “the elites are pretending they don’t have a worldview while imposing it.”

Robert P. George’s NR eulogy for Jürgen Habermas — the Frankfurt-School second-generation philosopher and Sandel’s continental analogue in defending the neutralist project, who died at 96 — supplies the intellectual-landscape map Sandel’s critique presupposes. George (Princeton, natural-law tradition) presses on the same structural move: Habermas’s distinction between morality (public, “the interests of all”) and ethics (personal beliefs about how to live) functioned identically to Rawls’s comprehensive-doctrines/public-reasons cut, removing abortion and euthanasia from democratic deliberation by category-shift rather than by argument — exactly the Roe v. Wade “neutrality as cover for substantive imposition” move Sandel anchors his case to. (George compresses Habermas’s late post-secular position — religious traditions as semantic resources liberal democracies cannot fully replace — into a stronger claim than the texts support, but the observation about the category-shift move is independent of the compression.) The load-bearing finding is that the intellectual sphere is interrogating liberal neutrality simultaneously from every available position; the camp still defending the neutralist project is the one whose patriarchs are eulogized rather than the one whose voices are emerging.

What makes this a cross-orientation finding rather than a Sandel restatement is the convergence from genuinely different positions. David Brooks in the Atlantic reports a humanist-revival on campuses — Purdue’s Cornerstone (100 → 5,500 students), Wake Forest’s $35M Educating Character Initiative, 14,000 students across 142 Life-Worth-Living classes at 35 institutions — though the piece carries a fundamental-flaw flag for selection-on-the-dependent-variable (Brooks teaches in one of the programs he namechecks favorably). Helen Andrews and Stephen Adubato’s Compact two-piece dismantles Beryl Satter’s “ghetto as conduit” thesis and adds Newark reporting on pop anti-racism collapsing on the ground. Geoff Shullenberger’s Compact essay traces both the right-wing and left-wing “cultural Marxism” conspiracy theories to a shared LaRouchite ur-text — Adorno-composed-the-Beatles, Marcuse-and-Adorno-CIA-zombie-brainwashed-Angela-Davis pamphlets generated by an SDS splinter that drifted rightward and seeded the conservative think-tank version — concluding both contemporary versions are inverted mirror images of the same source. Sun Liping reads the same configuration from outside and supplies the diagnostic redirection: the American “Left” is in fact Liberal (modern liberalism, xinziyouzhuyi) rather than Left (revolutionary socialism), and the Chinese-language bai zuo (白左) slur smuggles Chinese readers’ distaste for Left into US politics, producing a category error that does not describe Chinese reality. Auron MacIntyre’s Blaze piece on marriage decline as caused by severed female economic dependence is the right’s mono-causal-restoration move on the same terrain and carries a fundamental-flaw flag — the causal chain treats female labor-force participation as prime mover while ignoring declining male wages predating major female workforce entry, cross-national variation independent of female employment, and comparable marriage decline among educated cohorts. Two consecutive weeks with fundamental-flaw flags for MacIntyre is now a tracked pattern.

The Sahel reorganization and the hantavirus three-way

Three writers from three orientations converge on the same Sahelian finding this week: the FLA-JNIM rapprochement and the April 25 Bamako attacks are not a counter-terrorism problem but a suppressed-political-grievance dynamic finding new expression. Sachi Muto’s African Arguments piece extends last week’s Appiah-Mensah Azawad reading — Operation Serval, the 2015 Algiers Accord, the 2024 junta repudiation are all the same suppression failing in different costumes. Hassan Aourid uses Ibn Khaldun’s pairing of ‘asabiyya (group solidarity) and da‘wa (call/doctrine) to read the same event from Arabic political theory: a “revolutionary focus” reactivated against an exhausted central state, becoming a real motor of change only when it acquires foreign-power cover. Noé Michalon and Stephen Mogaka fit the local event into the macro frame — Macron’s choice of Nairobi for the Africa Forward summit as a France-Kenya alignment around defending multilateral institutions against the BRICS+/SCO and Trump-Board-of-Peace alternatives. The four-piece longitudinal pattern is the kind the project’s roster could not have produced without the African Arguments + Arabic-language + Courrier-adjacent expansion of the discovery layer.

Zeynep Tufekci’s NYT column on hantavirus is the strongest single piece of audit-the-citation reporting in this week’s corpus. Tufekci phones Gustavo Palacios, senior author of the 2018 Epuyén Argentina outbreak paper the WHO is citing for its reassurance, and Palacios tells her the WHO is misreading his paper — “prolonged or close contact” was not meant to exclude respiratory/airborne transmission. Airborne-expert Linsey Marr reads the same data as “strongly suggestive of airborne transmission.” Palacios’s calculated reproduction number is 2.1 (close to early-2020 SARS-CoV-2 figures the WHO also initially reassured about); incubation can be 40 days; viral loads rise 48 hours before symptoms. The Epuyén birthday-party superspreader pattern (one infected person at a 100-guest party leaves after 90 minutes, infecting five non-adjacent attendees) survives mode-shift. Officials are repeating the early-COVID error of reassuring on inadequate evidence; if the outbreak becomes a pandemic, RFK Jr. is the US Health Secretary in charge of the response. Matthew Yglesias’s Slow Boring piece accepts the substantive epidemiological reassurance but treats the “don’t panic” framing as evasive, and James Lileks at NR holds the WHO-is-correct position. The empirical question is now sharp and resolvable within 6-12 weeks: the St. Helena flight contact-tracing (97 contacts), the Hondius cruise-ship cohort (40+ Americans in 42-day quarantine), and the Canadian Vancouver Island positive test reported by BBC this week will resolve toward one or the other. James Riley’s Compact piece on RFK Jr.’s 2021 book dismantles the Béchamp/Pasteur “terrain theory” confusion at the conceptual layer (Kennedy mislabels his preferred framework as “miasma theory” but actually defends Béchamp; the alleged Pasteur deathbed recantation was debunked in 1946 by Pasteur’s grandson and is sourced to a self-published German anti-pharma book), and the unsigned Free Press investigation of how two Facebook posts and an MAHA-influencer pile-on destroyed FDA-approved produce company Apeel Sciences in eighteen months supplies the parallel coda: the public-health information environment is structurally exposed to coordinated-influencer pressure regardless of how the specific transmissibility question resolves.

What’s missing

Wire check. Three significant developments appear nowhere in roster coverage. The DR Congo Ebola outbreak the WHO declared an international emergency this week (246 suspected cases, 80 deaths in Ituri, two confirmed in Uganda, Bundibugyo strain with no approved drugs or vaccines) drew zero roster engagement — suggesting pandemic engagement is calibrated to whether the affected population is on a Western cruise ship. The Hezbollah fiber-optic FPV-drone evolution against the IDF (BBC Verify geolocated 35 strike videos since March 26; IDF acknowledges it cannot develop effective countermeasures) is the same Ukrainian-doctrine-as-transferable-asset dynamic Tabatabai documented for the Gulf states last week, now operating against Israel from Lebanon, and no roster writer connected it. The Uyghur foreign fighters in Syria (NPR’s Emily Feng’s on-the-ground reporting on the Uyghur militants who were elite forces in HTS’s 2024 Aleppo offensive and are now embedded in al-Sharaa’s Syria) is the first first-person operational reporting on a population whose existence Beijing reads as one of its sharpest external-security problems, with zero roster engagement.

Perspective check. Still no one operating from inside an AI-displaced workforce (four consecutive synthesis weeks; the structural roster gap is hardening). No Middle East operational reporting from inside the post-Hormuz energy-emergency cohort. No reporting from inside the data-center-impacted-locality cohort that goes beyond Stone-Banks’s Perry-Village interview register; the project knows the politics but not the texture.

What the mainstream press is getting wrong

The Beijing summit was narrated by the press as a continuation of Trump-Xi personal diplomacy when it is the data point at which foreign leaders began treating the United States, not just Trump, as term-limited. NBC’s Llamas/Rubio sit-down, CBS’s Face the Nation with USTR Greer, NPR’s “Politics chat: Takeaways from Trump’s trip to China,” and the Guardian’s coverage all treat the summit as bilateral diplomacy with transactional deliverables (the partial Boeing/GE pledges, the Iran-sanctions reversal, the Taiwan rhetoric). The roster cluster names the structural reassessment specifically: Gulf states buying Chinese air defenses, the bypass network forming around Washington, the AI-deployment race being won where the US is not running, Iran having rebuilt 30 of 33 Hormuz missile sites and ~90% of its underground storage. None of the seven press outlets connects these to the summit. The press treats Xi as Trump’s interlocutor; the roster treats Xi as the calmly waiting executor of a multi-decade strategic plan whose central wager is that American institutional decline is structural.

The redistricting story is being narrated as a partisan-procedural win when it is a constitutional-architecture rewiring. NPR Politics, NBC, CBS’s Face the Nation redistricting segment, and the Guardian’s “Jim Crow 2.0” South Carolina coverage all frame the post-Callais wave as a Republican gain measurable in House seats. The deeper finding — Silver’s “Republicans won the structural argument” + Eisler’s “from contesting governance outcomes to contesting the formal terms of obtaining power” — is roster-only. The press has the empirical material (the Louisiana primary mid-vote suspension, the Virginia Supreme Court ruling, Trump’s “if they have to vote twice, so be it” pressure on South Carolina) and treats it as one more partisan-skirmish news cycle. The roster treats it as the operating layer of the constitutional rewiring that will structure the next decade’s politics regardless of which party holds Congress in 2027.

Press absence: the Hezbollah FPV-drone learning curve. BBC Verify’s piece is the only major-outlet engagement of an evolving capability that crosses every existing radar-and-EW assumption, and it appears nowhere else in the press scan. The implications for the Iran-war financial-pressure architecture, for Israeli operational planning, and for the Taiwan-as-recipient-of-Ukrainian-doctrine dynamic Tabatabai named are all material. Both subcultures filtered it out.

Carry-forward findings

Three new entries, three refinements, one new open disagreement.

  • china-power-axis-disagreement (new) — Which metric of Chinese power is load-bearing for 5-10 year forecasting is itself the contested object. Douthat’s demographic-and-GDP measure predicts a faster Beijing move (closing window); the industrial throughput + AI deployment + diffusion measure (Stoller, Chan, Smith, Foer, Schuman, Bordoff, Zhou) predicts a slower one. Test: AI-deployment-and-diffusion benchmarks over 24 months; PLA Taiwan-window exercise/posture pressure; Beijing’s two-five-year-plan budget allocations as urgency-versus-patience signal.

  • datacenter-buildout-resistance (new) — Local buildout-resistance is the binding equilibrium constraint on the US AI buildout, not a frictional cost. Three weeks of converging evidence describes an environment in which absentee-capital projects systematically bypass local democratic process and the bypass itself generates the opposition. Test: ≥3 additional $1B+ cancellations in 12-24 months; state-vs-federal preemption fight reaching SCOTUS; meaningful buildout shift offshore.

  • doj-press-prosecution-pre-shield-equilibrium-shift (new) — The Iran war has produced executive coercive instruments against the press operating outside the procedural norms (28 CFR §50.10, DOJ Manual §9-13.400) that historically prevented federal press-shield legislation. McCarthy from the institutional-conservative right and Klippenstein from the independent national-security left converge on the diagnostic via two different instruments (DOJ subpoena power against WSJ/Axios/WaPo over Iran-leak reporting; counter-terrorism designation against Carlson and Fuentes by reclassification-as-non-conservatives). The historical bargain holding shield laws back is the responsible-arbiter-as-precondition; once that breaks, the structural case becomes politically winnable. Test: whether the 119th or 120th Congress moves a federal press-shield bill in 12-36 months; whether additional cross-coalition press subpoenas issue without resignation-level DOJ pushback; whether the Gorka-Carlson designation mechanism advances to operational moves (FBI investigation, financial-mapping, no-fly placement) rather than remaining rhetorical.

  • bypass-network-as-post-american-architecture (refined) — Now extends into civilian fiber-optic-cable infrastructure (Iran’s cable-tax legislation) and Indo-Pacific institutional absence (Samaan: Hormuz a “dry run, and a failed one” for the Taiwan-coalition substrate). Itani-Arayssi add partner-state internal coherence as a third binding constraint (LAF 27% Shia).

  • iran-war-center-of-gravity-financial (refined) — Bordoff supplies the energy-system causal mechanism behind the China-wins finding (billion-barrel stockpile, 50%-EV sales, pre-war Five-Year-Plan autarky paying off). Varoufakis supplies the US-side cui-bono mechanism (Permian frackers, 12-day stock-market recovery). Cable-tax operationalization extends Hormuz leverage from oil to bandwidth.

  • european-center-exhaustion (refined) — Tooze 449 adds the monetary-mechanism layer: the Bank of England is the unspoken structural cause of Labour’s gilt-market paralysis, with the post-Draghi Italy comparison and Robin Brooks’s BoE-versus-ECB data as supporting evidence.

  • regressive-enforcement-architecture (refined, week 3 of the Klippenstein thread) — The categorical-designation move now operates against the internal dissent it was nominally pointed away from: Gorka named Tucker Carlson and Nick Fuentes as right-wing terror targets by redefining them as non-conservatives (“terrorism two-step”), explicitly because both broke with Trump over the Iran War. The McCarthy NR piece on DOJ subpoenas to WSJ/Axios/WaPo over Iran-war-leak reporting (now also tracked under the doj-press-prosecution-pre-shield-equilibrium-shift entry above) is the cross-orientation pairing — the procedural-norms erosion is now visible to the institutional-conservative right and the independent national-security left simultaneously, via different instruments pointed in different directions, with the Iran war as the common enabling event.

Meta-finding

The mode that did the most analytical work this week was audit-the-citation — Tufekci phoning the original paper’s senior author and finding the WHO is misreading him; Riley dismantling Kennedy on Béchamp/Pasteur (Pasteur’s grandson debunked the deathbed-recantation in 1946; Kennedy sources it from a self-published German anti-pharma book); Shullenberger tracing the cultural-Marxism conspiracy to its LaRouchite ur-text. Citation-auditing is rare in commentary; this is the week when it produced the most load-bearing findings. Convergence-across-orientations held up second-best, with the Sandel-Brooks-Andrews-Adubato-Shullenberger-Sun-George read on neutral-state liberalism the cleanest cluster of the project to date — six positions across communitarian center-left, Atlantic establishment-liberal, post-liberal Compact right, contrarian-historian, Chinese sociological outside-view, and Catholic natural-law philosophy, one diagnosis. The McCarthy-Klippenstein convergence on Iran-war-enabled press prosecution is a close second — institutional-conservative right and independent national-security left arriving at the same diagnostic via two different operational instruments (DOJ subpoenas; counter-terrorism designation discretion), neither writer citing the other, the agreement independent. Silver-Eisler on procedural-power-acquisition migration and Muto-Aourid-Michalon-Mogaka on Sahel round out the cluster. The pattern recent weeks have established holds: when writers from genuinely different positions arrive at the same diagnosis through different rhetorical paths, the disagreement that should be open is on the prescription, not the description.

Interpretive framework mode produced one important standalone result: the bypass-network-as-post-American-architecture frame Tabatabai opened last week has become this week’s working vocabulary for what is happening in the Hormuz fiber-optic layer, the Indo-Pacific institutional absence, the Lebanese partner-state coherence, the Sahelian convergence, and the Bordoff energy-security finding. A framework offered as analysis one week being used the next as the implicit lens through which writers in different regions and disciplines articulate their own findings is the meta-meta-evidence that it names something real. Historical-analogy mode produced two productive uses (Tooze’s Italy-Trichet-vs-Draghi comparison; Aourid’s Ibn Khaldun) and one flagged-as-mono-causal one (Aurelien’s Thatcher-causation chain on Western tactile collapse — the substantive observation about credentialized facsimile-of-life is real but the channel-confused causal account weakens the broader argument).

Full Articles

Xi Jinping Was Only Humoring Trump

Franklin Foer · ” · Original →

Spare a moment, please, for the lame-duck superpower. It calls itself the leader of the free world, but the free world no longer believes it. When it extends its hand, nobody rushes to accept. When it threatens, nobody trembles.

After President Trump arrived in Beijing this week, Xi Jinping showered him with pomp befitting a summit of great powers. Yet the Chinese leader permitted potshots at his guest to go viral on his country’s internet rather than suppressing them, as some observers expected he would during a state visit. Xi answered Trump’s lavish praise by sternly lecturing him about meddling with Taiwan. In the end, Xi offered nothing of great substance—no solutions to the war in Iran, no sweeping trade deals, no promises of access to rare earth minerals. Xi used the visit to humor the lame-duck president, waiting for his time to pass.

[Michael Schuman: A checkers player meets a three-dimensional-chess master]

During the first Trump administration, foreign leaders flattered and accommodated the president out of deference to American power. They feared it; they relied on it. During the second administration, and especially since the beginning of the Iran war, their calculus has quietly shifted—not because the strategy of obsequiousness has failed, but because it’s no longer worth the trouble. Like many of his counterparts around the world, Xi has begun to assume that it’s not just Trump who is term-limited; it’s also his nation.

Trump’s war in Iran was meant to showcase American power. It did the opposite. In the course of failing to remove a much weaker regime or eliminate its nuclear threat, the United States blew through its arsenal—so much so that allies in the Pacific reasonably wonder whether enough munitions remain to protect them. According to The Wall Street Journal, the Pentagon is now worried that it lacks the firepower to execute contingency plans for defending Taiwan.

Supporters of the war argued that it would deal China a severe blow by eliminating one of its most potent allies. But the Gulf nations most threatened by Iran have actually turned to China. As first reported by The Washington Post, an intelligence assessment prepared for the chair of the Joint Chiefs of Staff warned that those countries have begun acquiring from Beijing the systems needed to protect their oil infrastructure and bases. Trump didn’t just fail to weaken China’s position in the Middle East. He strengthened it.

Without exerting itself much, Beijing has profited from America’s self-immolation. China’s petroleum reserves and its investments in renewable energy have allowed it to offer Thailand, the Philippines, and Australia relief from the energy crisis that the United States instigated. Instead of applying diplomatic pressure on Iran to cut a deal, China has let the conflict linger, so that the United States continues to bear the blame for the disruptions to shipping. Meanwhile, China poses as the faithful steward of the rules-based order—the cooler head, the power on which even the U.S. must now rely.

By patiently waiting out this moment, by letting the United States exhaust itself, China has bought time to pursue what Xi calls “national self-reliance”—time to catch up with the West technologically and to fortify itself for the point when competition takes a harsher turn.

That very same strategy is guiding Iran. Trump repeatedly signals his desire for a deal to end the war, by wishfully exaggerating how close he is to reaching one. But Iran keeps responding to his offers with outrageous demands, including for reparations for the destruction the United States wrought.

[Robert Kagan: America is now a rogue superpower]

In the meantime, Iran has been able to dig out weapons systems buried in the rubble caused by American strikes on bunkers and caves. According to intelligence assessments, The New York Times reports, the Iranians have restored access to 30 out of 33 missile sites along the Strait of Hormuz. Across the whole of the country, Iran has regained roughly 90 percent of its underground missile storage. Without having to purchase a rocket or launcher, it has bounced back.

American history is rife with the perils of lame-duck leaders. As their time in office grinds to a close, presidents grow eager to write a final chapter worthy of their saga. They reach for the grand gesture; they attempt to solve the intractable problem. But in their mad dash to assert their relevance, they manage merely to prove how little they matter to the rest of the world. Trump is now living that fate, and the consequences extend far beyond his presidency. Every failed deal, every summit that yields nothing, every boast that goes unfulfilled, confirms what adversaries already suspect. A lame-duck superpower exhausts itself in full view of the world, and the world moves on.

Claude’s Response

Foer’s central move is to invert the standard “Trump is reckless / Xi is patient” frame from a personality diagnosis into a structural one: foreign leaders’ deference doesn’t track who occupies the White House but what they assess the U.S. is now structurally able to deliver. The argument is straightforward Atlantic-establishment declinism, but it lands harder than usual this week because it converges with material data the cluster has been accumulating for a month. The CSIS munitions-depletion finding, the WSJ Taiwan-contingency story, the Post Joint-Chiefs assessment, the NYT Iran-missile-recovery numbers, and Ariane Tabatabai’s WOTR “bypass network” piece from last week (Saudi/UAE/Qatar signing 10-year security agreements with Ukraine; Taiwan-Czechia-Japan-Poland defense-industrial linkages) are all pointing at the same configuration. Foer is doing the Beijing-summit-specific rendering of what the broader cluster has been calling the post-Pax-Americana bypass architecture.

The strongest contribution is the Gulf states bought Chinese air defenses finding, which is the single hardest piece of evidence in the cluster that the Iran war reversed the very strategic effect its supporters claimed it would have. Zineb Riboua’s Hudson Institute piece (which Foer links) argued the strike would “deal China a severe blow by eliminating one of its most potent allies.” The Post’s reporting that the opposite happened — that the partners China was supposedly threatened by are now turning to Beijing because Washington can’t protect their infrastructure — is the kind of cleanly falsifying empirical finding that should reset the debate but won’t, because the people who advocated the war have nothing else to fall back on.

The weakness is the “lame-duck nation” frame itself, which collapses two separable claims under one rhetorical roof. The first claim — Trump is increasingly powerless on the world stage because his counterparties have priced in his patterns — is well-supported. The second claim — the United States is structurally a lame-duck superpower whose decline is independent of who governs it — is the much bigger claim and is asserted, not demonstrated. The munitions story actually supports the smaller claim (the U.S. cannot simultaneously prosecute a Middle East war and posture for Taiwan because the industrial base wasn’t designed for two-theater capacity), but Foer extends it to the nation level without engaging the obvious counter — that the same industrial-base constraint would, with serious investment over years, be solvable. The Tooze / Dalio cluster from last week converged on the decline diagnosis, but disagreed about whether it’s reversible; Foer just takes the decline as given. Pair with Schuman in this week’s roundup for the same argument with operational-China-affairs texture rather than declinist mood-music.

Back to summary

A Checkers Player Meets a Three-Dimensional-Chess Master

Michael Schuman · ” · Original →

In the centuries when dynasties ruled China, kings and chieftains across Asia sent “tribute missions” to the imperial court to pay homage to the emperor in exchange for access to the empire’s riches and favors. Donald Trump’s trip to Beijing this week recalled those missions. The United States president arrived hat in hand, seeking money and promises from China’s latter-day emperor, Xi Jinping. The visit, meant to establish stability after a decade of trade wars and acrimonious one-upmanship, instead highlighted how the balance of power is tipping away from Washington. Despite America’s economic, military, and diplomatic heft, Trump’s missteps have put him and the country on the back foot in dealings with the far more disciplined Xi.

Trump opened the proceedings with his usual kowtowing. “You’re a great leader. I say it to everybody,” Trump told Xi at a welcoming ceremony yesterday at the Great Hall of the People. “Sometimes people don’t like me saying it. But I say it anyway because it’s true.” The fawning didn’t get him very far. In the meeting that followed, Xi promptly issued a stern warning about Taiwan, which Beijing claims as its own. Stressing that the “Taiwan question is the most important issue in China-U.S. relations,” Xi warned the U.S. to handle the matter with “extra caution,” according to a summary of his comments from China’s Ministry of Foreign Affairs. If not, Xi said, “the two countries will have clashes and even conflicts.”

Secretary of State Marco Rubio later told NBC News that the administration’s policy on Taiwan has not changed, but Trump himself—who still needs to sign off on plans to sell $14 billion in weapons to Taiwan—seems less committed. Trump said today that when Xi asked him whether he would send troops to defend Taiwan, he did not offer an answer. Washington’s position on defending Taiwan has long been ambiguous, but Trump added to reporters that “The last thing we need right now is a war that’s 9,500 miles away.”

Trump’s sycophancy didn’t change Xi’s mind on Iran either. Trump had delayed his trip to China by six weeks for fear that the Iran war would overshadow what he hoped would be a big diplomatic win. But the unresolved conflict still intruded on the dealmaking. The U.S. has been troubled by China’s support for Iran through supplies of weapon components and as the top buyer of the country’s oil. Shortly before the summit, Trump’s team turned up the pressure on Xi to curtail this aid by sanctioning refiners and companies in China and Hong Kong involved in these deals. Yet Xi ordered the refiners to ignore Trump’s edicts, uncowed by a president who often folds under pressure.

[Vivian Salama and Jonathan Lemire: The hippocratic summit]

In a Fox News interview yesterday, Trump crowed that Xi had promised not to arm Iran. But Trump had said in April that Xi had already assured him that Beijing wasn’t sending arms to Iran, yet the findings of U.S. intelligence officials suggest otherwise. Instead of pressing Xi on Beijing’s arms sales or oil purchases, Trump said that he was considering lifting sanctions on the Chinese oil companies in question. He even seemed to defend Xi’s position. “Look, he’s not coming in with guns. He’s not coming in with rifles. They are not coming in shooting,” Trump said. “He’s been very good.”

Perhaps Trump has merely recognized that expecting Xi to help solve his Middle East mess is a nonstarter. Tuvia Gering, a fellow at the Atlantic Council who tracks China in the Middle East from Jerusalem, told me that Xi’s geopolitical vision “imposes a definitive ceiling on China’s willingness to facilitate Trump’s objectives in Iran.” China’s goal seems to be to weaken U.S. power in the region, so helping “to secure a decisive U.S. victory would be strategically self-defeating.”

Trump made only slightly more progress on trade. He came to Beijing as a traveling salesman, hawking American products in pursuit of his long-running goal of closing the U.S. trade deficit with China. Though he didn’t get the firm purchase commitments he wanted, he did not leave empty-handed. Trump said that Xi pledged to purchase about 400 General Electric jet engines and 200 “big” Boeing airliners, though the details remain hazy and no formal agreement seems to have been set. This is far less than the deal for 500 737 Max jets that Trump had been touting, but if the orders do come through, they’d be Boeing’s first major order from China in about a decade.

These pledges have allowed Trump to spin this summit as a success, but Xi has an emperor’s appreciation of the role a few choice gifts can play in securing leverage over a foreign power. What Xi offers, he can threaten to take away. Xi has already exploited American dependence on Chinese rare earths and supply-chain components to keep Trump in line. Last year, he halted purchases of soybeans from American farmers, a key Trump voting bloc, to pressure the president to stand down from his trade war. Getting more American businesses and constituents hooked on Chinese cash promises to be yet another way to assert China’s power over the U.S.

These tough tactics seem to have taught Trump that China has become too powerful to push around. “The U.S. has realized that China has achieved mutually assured deterrence status,” Wang Huiyao, the president of the Center for China and Globalization, a Beijing-based think tank, told me. He argues that this has brought about “a big paradigm shift” in Washington’s approach to China, and has curbed the hawkishness of Trump’s messaging. Wang suggests that a new pragmatism may now prevail between the U.S. and China, one in which U.S. leaders no longer try to get China to adopt Western values but “respect the differences and find a way to work together.”

Trump seems to have embraced this change of heart. “Having a good relationship is a good thing, not a bad thing,” Trump told Fox yesterday. “It’s great when you have good relationships with very powerful countries.”

The disputes over Taiwan, Iran, and trade suggest that a more stable U.S.-China relationship rests mainly on Trump’s reluctance to press Xi too hard. Trump has duly brushed aside a number of contentious issues that have soured relations, such as China’s continued support for Russia’s war in Ukraine and its export-heavy economic policies that threaten U.S. industry. This could prove politically risky. China hawks in Washington still advocate for a tougher line on Beijing to protect American interests, and the midterms could usher in a more hawkish Congress. But watching Trump swan around Beijing with an entourage of prominent American CEOs, including Elon Musk and Nvidia’s Jensen Huang, left the impression that the American president sees China as a business opportunity rather than as a security threat.

Despite being at war with a China partner, Trump seems content to simply sell some Boeings and beans. The Trump administration doesn’t have “any great ambition for this relationship,” Bonnie Glaser, the managing director of the Indo-Pacific program at the German Marshall Fund, told me. Trump has set his priorities to merely “keeping the relationship from going off the rails” and “ensuring that America’s needs are met,” at least when it comes to trade.

[Michael Schuman: Trump’s head-scratching turn toward China]

Xi, however, has great ambitions. Trump may now see China as a mutually beneficial economic partner, but Xi’s policies are designed to change the world order at America’s expense. Beijing is working to engineer China’s technological and industrial dominance, backing Russia in a destabilizing war in Europe, and generally setting the stage to achieve global supremacy when the United States flames out. Trump, with his disdain for global alliances and liberal values, doesn’t seem interested in contesting Xi on these fronts. “Xi Jinping has the long plan, about dominating the world and putting the United States in its right place,” Joerg Wuttke, a partner at the consulting firm Albright Stonebridge Group, told me. “Donald Trump doesn’t look that far.”

A couple of trade deals have apparently made Trump happy enough to step aside and let Xi pursue his global agenda. Like the Chinese emperors of old, Xi has used the lure of Chinese wealth to reinforce China’s power. Beijing has sought to find “the minimum price point to keep Trump invested in the process,” Jonathan Czin, a foreign-policy fellow at the Brookings Institution, told me. This is how a U.S. president who has long insisted on American strength and a tough line on China consigns the country to a weaker future.

Claude’s Response

Schuman’s piece does what Foer’s doesn’t: it gives the same diagnosis operational texture, sourced to China-affairs analysts who track the language of the Chinese Ministry of Foreign Affairs summaries (Schuman is reading the actual MFA readout, not just U.S. coverage). The contrast in registers is the value of running both in the same roundup. Foer’s frame is declinist mood-music; Schuman’s is mechanism-tracing: the specific concessions Trump made (Taiwan ambiguity, lifting the refiner sanctions, accepting four-month-stale promises on Iran arms), the specific things Xi extracted (no movement on Russia-Ukraine support; no movement on export-heavy economic policy threatening U.S. industry; explicit deference on Taiwan), and the specific commercial fig leaves Trump took home (Boeing/GE orders large enough to brand a summit success, small enough to be “the minimum price point”).

The Czin “minimum price point” frame is the load-bearing analytic contribution. It’s the cleanest articulation of how Xi is playing this: not maximalist demands designed to humiliate Trump (which would politically blow up the relationship and bring China hawks back to the table), but the smallest possible cluster of trade-deal-shaped wins that lets Trump declare victory and walk away from the harder questions. The implication is that Xi has correctly read Trump’s value function — Trump cares about the appearance of a deal, not the content — and is gaming it with precision. This is the kind of finding that ought to embarrass the China-hawk faction of the administration but probably won’t, because they don’t have leverage to force a different posture.

The Wang Huiyao “mutually assured deterrence” quote deserves scrutiny. Wang runs a Beijing-based think tank that is explicitly designed to translate Chinese state preferences into the language of Western policy debates, so his characterization of a “big paradigm shift” toward “respecting differences and finding a way to work together” is the Chinese-side-of-the-table framing, not a neutral observation. Schuman uses it without flagging that provenance, which is a slight against the reader. The Glaser and Wuttke quotes are more reliable as outside observation.

The shared weakness with Foer: both writers treat the Trump-flames-out / Xi-plays-long-game frame as a structural finding when it is at least partly contingent on choices the next U.S. administration will make. Schuman gestures at the contingency (“a more hawkish Congress could push back”) but doesn’t sustain it. The piece’s strongest claim is the narrow one — at this specific summit, on these specific issues, Trump conceded substantively and Xi extracted operational wins for show concessions — and that claim is well-supported. Pair with Foer for the same diagnosis at higher altitude and (in the context of this week’s roundup) with the Bordoff/Meyer Hormuz transcript for the energy-side mechanism that’s giving Xi the freedom to play this hand.

Back to summary

Trump and Xi: what's behind the friendly words?

euro|topics · ” · Original →

US President Trump and Chinese President Xi have held several rounds of talks behind closed doors. Although little is known about what was discussed, both parties later emphasised their constructive cooperation. Trump spoke of friendship and a “great future”. Xi said that the two countries could strengthen each other. Europe’s press tries to figure out what is going on behind the scenes.

Le Monde columnist Alain Frachon examines the fierce competition between China and the US: “Both countries know what is crucial in the race for global supremacy: quantum computing and AI. In these areas, the two superpowers must reconcile the reality of their mutual dependence with their quest for autonomy. Beijing and Washington are cautiously imposing controls on particularly sensitive exports as a weapon: the Americans have the most advanced chips, the Chinese have rare earths. When it comes to technological supremacy between China and the US, the jury is still out.”

China is benefiting from the US’s mistakes, writes Rzeczpospolita: “The Chinese believe that time is on their side, not just economically and technologically but also from a geostrategic perspective. In their view, the best proof of this theory is the disastrous Iran conflict, from which the Americans are currently unable to extricate themselves. Admittedly, this conflict is also hitting China’s economic interests hard because it depends on oil imports from the Persian Gulf. But in the long term, the tide could turn, because unlike Washington, Beijing has invested in renewable energies and aims to be a global leader in this field.”

The visit will do nothing to change the US’s waning influence on the global stage, writes the Frankfurter Allgemeine Zeitung: “Trump may believe that the glow of China’s strength will reflect on him, but he fails to recognise that China is using this trip to increase its own power. Xi specifically addressed the Taiwan issue and warned that American support for the island republic could lead to a conflict with China. The White House, by contrast, made no mention of Taiwan. … Beijing is patiently and deliberately positioning itself to advance to the centre of the global order – a position it feels entitled to occupy. During his first term in office, Trump already identified China as the main threat to the United States, but instead of focusing on this, he is getting sidetracked.”

Corriere della Sera explains one of the metaphors used by Xi: “Yesterday, Xi warned of the ‘Thucydides Trap’. … He was referring to the Athenian historian Thucydides’ account of the Peloponnesian War between Athens and Sparta (431–404 BC), which was triggered by the rise of Athens and ultimately unleashed the conflict that devastated the civilisation at the time. … Xi used this metaphor to warn the US against crossing the red line on the Taiwan issue and obstructing the ‘historical inevitability of the island’s reunification with mainland China.’ This implies that China would resort to military force, if necessary, to regain Taiwan, even though it is aware of the devastating consequences of a conflict between superpowers.”

Commenting on the Chinese economy, Dagens Nyheter notes: “China’s economic miracle was abruptly halted by a property crisis five years ago. Since then, deflation has taken its toll on the country. Although strong exports are sustaining growth, they are based on low prices and wages, which reduce consumption and demand, and thus exacerbate deflation. China finds itself in a difficult situation. If we look at the causes of this problem, they lie not only in the enormous and substandard investment in housing construction, but also in Xi Jinping’s thirst for control. … Both domestic and foreign private investors and consumers are unsettled.”

El Periódico de Catalunya sees signs of improved relations: “Everything suggests that the visit will end in agreements that restore trust and stability between the two countries. … There are unlikely to be any historic breakthroughs on the two main points of contention – trade relations and the status of Taiwan. However, their positions are closer when it comes to the war in Iran and the Strait of Hormuz. … Both leaders are moving closer on the subject of reopening the Strait of Hormuz and the need to avoid Iran possessing nuclear weapons. These outcomes would ease political tensions and economic pressures worldwide.”

The EU has many reasons to be worried about the meeting in Beijing, warns Delo: “Should the presidents and negotiators reach an agreement, we can be sure that the Americans will expect preferential treatment regarding Chinese exports of rare earths. Europe, and the EU in particular, are likely to be relegated to the bottom of the list of buyers for these 21st-century raw materials. However, should Trump’s team decide not to open the doors to Chinese electric cars, the European market can expect a flood of hundreds of millions of beautifully designed, high-quality and unbeatably cheap Chinese-made electric vehicles intended for export because the overcrowded domestic market can’t absorb them. … Neither Trump nor Xi see European interests as important.”

Claude’s Response

This Euro|topics aggregation is doing two distinct kinds of work, and the more interesting one is the editorial-selection layer. The summit is the week’s largest single news event; every major European newspaper had a take; Euro|topics’ decision to surface these seven (Le Monde, Rzeczpospolita, FAZ, Corriere, Dagens Nyheter, El Periódico, Delo) and skip the more euphoric Atlanticist takes that exist (in the FT, Spectator, certain Polish liberal outlets) constitutes an argument the project should name explicitly: European centrist press converges on a China-rising read that US commentary mostly does not surface, and the convergence is broad enough that calling it idiosyncratic is wrong.

What’s structurally informative about the cluster is the prediction asymmetry. Where US roster takes on the summit (Foer, Schuman, Mandel, Douthat-Chan) are reading the same summit through the lens of Trump’s domestic position, MAGA’s China hawks, or US negotiating posture, the European cluster is reading it through the lens of European strategic exclusion (Delo’s “neither Trump nor Xi see European interests as important”), Taiwan-deterrence credibility (FAZ’s “patiently and deliberately positioning itself to advance to the centre of the global order”), and historical-cycle inevitability (Rzeczpospolita’s “time is on their side”). The European cluster’s frame is closer to Tooze’s “Chinamaxxing” (Chartbook 446) than to most of the US right-coded roster pieces’ framing — even though the European outlets here include conservative voices (FAZ, Rzeczpospolita). This is itself a data point for the European-center-exhaustion carry-forward the synthesis opened 5/9: even establishment European centrist outlets now read US strength as structurally past-peak.

The Corriere piece on the Thucydides-Trap citation is the most operationally useful single contribution because it surfaces the specific Xi rhetorical move that US press largely missed. Xi citing Thucydides in summit talks — referencing the Athenian-Spartan dynamic that destroyed the civilization of the time — is a strong signal that Beijing reads itself as the rising power facing the declining one and is prepared to use that frame publicly with the US president in the room. The fact that this was the metaphor chosen, with Taiwan as the explicit subtext (“historical inevitability of the island’s reunification”), is closer to a coded ultimatum than a goodwill gesture, and the European press got the citation; the US press largely did not.

The El Periódico anomaly is the single most interesting piece in the aggregation because it cuts against the consensus, and the project should pay attention to what specifically it cuts against: not the Taiwan question (where it agrees there will be no breakthrough), not the trade question (same), but specifically on Iran/Hormuz, where it reads US-Chinese alignment on avoiding Iranian nuclear weapons and on reopening the strait. This is a testable prediction (a meaningful Hormuz cooperation framework would be one of the most consequential possible outcomes of the summit), and if it materializes it would significantly disturb the cluster’s other readings. The synthesis composer should track whether El Periódico’s read holds up over the next two weeks; if it does, the cluster’s pessimistic frame about US weakness is incomplete in an important specific dimension.

One structural caveat: Euro|topics’ selection methodology is curated but not transparent, and the project should be modest about treating “European centrist press converges” as a claim — what’s converged is “the seven outlets Euro|topics chose this week.” That said, the pattern is strong enough (six of seven on broad China-rising trajectory; one anomaly on a specific cooperation dimension) that the editorial-selection argument doesn’t need to lean on representativeness — the selection itself is the argument Euro|topics is making, and the project’s interest is in what that argument adds to the US-led roster cluster.

Back to summary

Is America in Decline and China Rising? Not for Long.

Douthat, Ross · 2026-05-16 · Original →

A grand summit in Beijing is a natural time to assess the state of the U.S.-China competition, the dynamics of great power conflict, the balance of forces in this new Cold — or maybe just Cool? — War.

It’s also a good time to revisit my own predictions. Six years ago, in the early days of the coronavirus, I argued that rather than a “Chinese century” we might be looking at a “Chinese decade,” a window when China’s power would hit a peak and the American position would be maximally endangered — but with a more favorable balance awaiting the United States in the later part of the century, if we could get through the Chinese maximum unscathed.

One part of that analysis was simply wrong. I was writing at a moment when the American response to the pandemic seemed much more shambolic than Beijing’s efficient containment strategy, and I assumed that there could be a kind of Covid dividend for China from that difference. In hindsight, America’s stumbling approach actually proved more effective than China’s in the long run, because the People’s Republic eventually found itself in a permanent-lockdown trap that yielded all kinds of social and economic damage.

But in other ways, the 2020s have proceeded somewhat as I expected. The American imperium has been hard pressed on every front, and our leadership — slumping and senescent in the last presidency, obnoxious and bullying in this one — has gifted China a reputation for relative stability, notwithstanding Xi Jinping’s own aggressive and repressive moves.

There is a lot of talk about rebuilding American manufacturing, and the Trump era has seen a partial decoupling of the United States and China, a clear shift away from the “Chimerica” model that defined the 2010s. But the decoupling is taking place in the shadow of a profound Chinese industrial advantage and continuing Chinese scientific and technological success. We can debate what it means that China lags just behind Silicon Valley in the artificial intelligence race (that’s the topic of this week’s episode of my podcast, “Interesting Times”), but our edge in frontier models doesn’t feel like a definite hard-power advantage as long as China is radically outpacing us in building machine tools, robots, ships and drones.

Six months ago, I was telling myself an optimistic story about the national security balance, where the United States maintained an edge in battlefield experience — with our support for Ukraine against Russia and our interventions in Iran and Venezuela serving as a testing ground for new weapons and A.I.-enabled tactics. But watching the American military stockpile collapse under the pressure of a regional war against Iran this year should make everyone skeptical that our advantages are adequate for a sustained conflict in East Asia. Fighting Iran to a stalemate seems like the kind of thing that happens just before you fight the Chinese and lose.

So the world of the 2020s does seem to have gone China’s way in very important respects. To the extent that comparisons to the actual Cold War are relevant, China is a more powerful material competitor than the Soviet Union ever managed to become, and our so-far-unsuccessful Iranian gamble has left the American hard-power position looking as parlous as it’s ever been.

But what about the world of the 2040s or 2060s? Six years ago I wrote that China’s growth rate might be slowing, making it less likely to achieve either the living standards of its East Asian neighbors or eclipse the United States as the world’s largest economy. Since then, the Chinese attempt to lock in a broad economic sphere of influence through its Belt and Road Initiative has hit repeated setbacks. And as it turned out, 2021 was the point of greatest nominal-G.D.P. convergence with the United States, and since then America has grown faster while China has struggled with its Covid hangover and various internal problems — raising the possibility that there will never be a moment when the Chinese economy is the biggest in the world.

Or maybe we should call it a likelihood rather than a possibility, since it’s incredibly difficult to generate high rates of growth under conditions of rapid population aging — and the other great trend of the last six years is that China’s demographic situation now looks much, much worse.

The end of the one-child policy in 2016 was supposed to boost birthrates. Instead the Chinese fertility rate has been crashing, hitting an average of 1.0 births per a woman’s lifetime in 2025, half the replacement level; it was the country’s fourth consecutive year of population decline. The grim social trends that attract justified attention in the United States — the alienation of the sexes from each other, a loss of interest in marriage and family — seem to have advanced much more rapidly in China. A new paper on attitudes among Chinese young people finds that 32 percent of those aged 18 to 24 reported “no desire for children,” up from 5 percent in 2012.

These patterns make a striking counterpoint to the evidence of increasing Chinese confidence, even arrogance and hubris, about the inevitability of American decline. Just how confident can Chinese people really be in their culture’s future if the rising generation is so disinclined to reproduce? Just how confident should China’s leaders be that they can outlast the United States if their population could be cut in half over the next few generations? How much power, hard or soft — and China’s global cultural influence remains notably limited — can a rapidly aging civilization expect to project?

At the very least, any scenario where Chinese power doesn’t ebb has to involve radical technological disruption. As in, a world where robots and A.I. take over an extraordinary share of economic and creative labor. Or a world where radical health improvement makes population aging far less economically significant. Or a world where technology revolutionizes human reproduction, making it possible for authoritarian states to engineer repopulation, like the World State with its hatcheries in Aldous Huxley’s “Brave New World.”

In any of these scenarios, a potential Chinese age in human history will look like no era or imperium we’ve ever seen before. Whereas if you imagine a future that remains at least somewhat normal, somewhat human, it still seems reasonable to short the Chinese century and bet that Beijing’s power is peaking now or soon.

Then the great question becomes whether Xi Jinping sees the world this way. To the extent that we believe in the narrative of Chinese confidence, a comfortable expectation in the Middle Kingdom that America’s problems are part of a long arc of Western decline, we should be hopeful that we can pass through this moment without a deadly confrontation.

Chinese hubris, in this sense, might be the best guarantor of world peace, ensuring that Beijing will wait and wait to test its power against ours, wait and wait to claim Taiwan … and find, in waiting, that its best chance has passed it by.

But presumably Xi and his circle can see all the trends that I’ve just described. And if they don’t have perfect confidence in technological revolution, if they aren’t self-deceived about the prospects for American collapse, then I would expect them to have a plan for potential confrontation very, very soon.

Claude’s Response

Douthat is doing the thing he does well: holding himself accountable to a prior forecast, marking the part he got wrong (COVID dividend) before claiming credit for the part he got right (Chinese peak window). This is unusually clean intellectual hygiene for a columnist and is worth flagging as a strength. The demographic data is also genuinely dispositive on the long-horizon question — a country whose 18-24 cohort reports 32% “no desire for children” up from 5% in twelve years is in a different category from a country with garden-variety low fertility, and the absolute numbers on China’s population trajectory are not seriously contested.

But the piece has a fundamental flaw of scope. Douthat collapses “Chinese power” into “Chinese demographic and GDP trajectory,” and the cluster of writers reading the same summit — Foer’s Atlantic piece on the Beijing display, Stoller’s Efficiency Moat thesis, Tooze’s seven Chinamaxxing vectors, Mandel in Commentary, Schuman in The Atlantic — are explicitly arguing that the relevant power measure has shifted to industrial throughput in machine tools, batteries, EVs, drones, ships, and grid-scale energy build-out, none of which Douthat addresses. He gestures at it (“China is radically outpacing us in building machine tools, robots, ships and drones”) but treats it as a near-term tactical concern rather than as a structural redefinition of what twentieth-century-style “power” even means in a fully decarbonized, AI-saturated production stack. If Tooze and Stoller are right that the relevant power vector is industrial capacity to make physical things, China’s demographic crunch matters less than Douthat assumes, because automation absorbs the labor shortfall and the existing industrial base compounds over decades.

The “Chinese hubris guarantees world peace” framing is also unusually self-undermining. Douthat is in effect arguing: if Beijing sees what I see, they should act soon; if they don’t see it, they’ll wait. The Kyle Chan podcast (this week’s other Douthat-China piece) explicitly addresses this and concludes Beijing is not “AGI-pilled,” does not see itself as racing against a closing window, is running a long-horizon diffusion strategy — which on Douthat’s own logic is the good outcome from a war-avoidance standpoint. The two pieces are in productive tension Douthat doesn’t resolve. The most useful project pairing is therefore Douthat-against-Douthat: read the column and the podcast as a single Douthat-China-week and note that the column’s hawkish-implied conclusion is partly disarmed by the podcast’s interlocutor.

The closer (“very, very soon”) is a Douthat tic — gesturing at imminence without specifying a mechanism or a year — and lands less heavily once you notice that he made the same call six years ago about the 2020s and is now revising the danger window forward into the next administration. The forecast may still be right; the rhetorical move is suspicious.

Back to summary

China’s Not the Problem. We Are.: Interesting Times (Douthat podcast with Kyle Chan)

Douthat, Ross · 2026-05-14 · Original →

The difference in perspectives between superpowers is shaping the race for A.I. dominance.

The United States and China are really the only two countries that matter right now in shaping the artificial intelligence future. As President Trump and President Xi Jinping meet in Beijing, there’s a kind of Cold War atmosphere, with people talking about an A.I. arms race. But who is winning? Are we even in a race at all? Kyle Chan, a foreign policy fellow at the Brookings Institution, says it’s hard to call it a race because the U.S. and China have very different A.I. goals.

Below is an edited transcript of an episode of “Interesting Times.” We recommend listening to it in its original form for the full effect. You can do so using the player above or on the NYTimes app, Apple, Spotify, Amazon Music, YouTube, iHeartRadio or wherever you get your podcasts.

Ross Douthat: Kyle Chan, welcome to “Interesting Times.”

Kyle Chan: Great to be here.

Douthat: So at the moment, there are really only two countries that matter for the A.I. future: the United States and China. Their leaders are meeting in Beijing this week, and the atmosphere is sort of similar to a kind of Cold War atmosphere, where people think and argue and talk about them being in a kind of arms race.

You are an expert on China and A.I., and we’re going to talk about that race: who’s winning, what winning even means, whether it even makes sense to talk about the U.S. and China in terms of a race.

But I want to start with a basic question. How is China’s current approach to A.I. different from the American approach?

Chan: It’s quite different, actually. In the U.S., there’s a particular focus on A.G.I. — artificial general intelligence — and to create something approaching an artificial superintelligence, some kind of almost machine god that can do virtually everything that any human can do ——

Douthat: And more!

Chan: And more. That’s right.

Douthat: You want to get more. That’s the “super” part.

Chan: [Chuckles] Absolutely. And you can see that the amount of spending, the amount of investment, the amount of effort that the American big tech companies and their quote-unquote “start-ups,” like OpenAI and Anthropic — which are now close to $1 trillion each — are pouring into this is an indication that they’re making a big bet that they can get there at some point, maybe in the near future. That’s the race to A.G.I. in the U.S.

China is running a different kind of race. I would argue they’re running multiple races. On the one hand, they are trying to produce better and better A.I. models. They do want to try to keep pace with their American competitors, but that’s not all they’re focused on. They’re also focused on efficiency, making these models smaller, cheaper to run, easier to deploy. That’s one area.

Another area they’re focused on is diffusion — trying to get A.I. into the hands of as many users as possible — and part of that strategy involves open source. This involves kind of giving away your models for free. And that allows other people around the world, including in Silicon Valley, to download Chinese models and to also customize them and tweak them based on their own data, and to make them work in a way that’s more tailored to their own needs. That’s the advantage of open source.

Another major area that China’s focused on is applications. Specifically, robotics is a huge area of focus, both for the government and for Chinese A.I. companies.

But you don’t really hear so much about A.G.I. You might hear some of the Chinese tech founders talk about this, and they sometimes sound a little similar to their counterparts in the U.S. But overall, they’re much more focused on the nuts-and-bolts uses and applications of A.I. in people’s daily lives. That’s the key priority.

Douthat: So if I went to Shanghai or Beijing right now and spent a couple of weeks there interacting with physical reality and digital reality, do you think I would notice a big A.I.-driven difference versus life in the United States? Just describe the everyday experience of this strategy, to the extent that it makes a difference in how people are living.

Chan: Yeah. So in the larger cities in China, you might see autonomous delivery robots dealing with package deliveries, food deliveries. In a restaurant, you might see a waiter robot bringing your food. This is not super, super widespread yet, but it’s starting to come about. Hotels, rather than having room service be delivered by a person pushing a cart coming up the elevator, it might be a delivery robot. You have, of course, self-driving cars. You might even have drone delivery for coffee or food.

But it would be a subtle but probably surprising difference to what most Americans experience in terms of their interaction with A.I. in the physical world.

Douthat: Let’s just pause for context, because you talked about the government versus the Chinese A.I. companies. I think most viewers and listeners are accustomed to the American situation, where you have a set of big companies, they have been extremely, lightly regulated by Washington, D.C., and just in the last year, we’ve started to get into dynamics where the Pentagon especially seems concerned about their national security implications. There’s talk about regulation, screening of models and so on, but basically it’s been a very traditionally American capitalist environment. Not a Manhattan Project or anything like that.

To what extent is China similar or different just in the relationship between the companies and what is obviously a much more powerful and often repressive state?

Chan: In China, the state is in charge. Or specifically, I should say the party state. The Chinese Communist Party and the various government agencies that they oversee, they’re the ones who set the rules. They’re the ones who ultimately are shaping the trajectory of China’s A.I. industry.

They have quite strict regulations — for example, requiring A.I. models to be registered in advance. They have certain content and censorship rules that must be followed. They have a whole host of ways to enforce their rules and have leverage over Chinese A.I. companies. And there are echoes back to a previous era where Chinese regulators cracked down on Chinese internet companies, for example.

That’s the overarching relationship, but that doesn’t mean that the Chinese A.I. labs themselves are just in lock step following whatever Beijing says. Ironically, China tried a more top-down model to technology in a previous era, and that failed miserably. It did not produce the kind of innovation and flexibility and agility in the marketplace that you would need to have cutting-edge technology.

Douthat: What era are we talking about with the more top-down approach?

Chan: That was, I would argue, going back to the Mao era. This is the classic ——

Douthat: Pre-Deng, roughly pre-1980s?

Chan: Exactly, yeah. That almost Soviet command-economy-style approach.

So what you have is sort of a hybrid model in China, if I could characterize it in a single word — a broader direction and guidance and certainly support from the central government in China as well as local governments on the one hand, but then also trying to create space for competition and innovation from the Chinese A.I. labs themselves, whether you’re talking about China’s equivalent of the Big Tech companies, like Alibaba or Tencent, the maker of WeChat, the popular super app, or you’re talking about China’s own A.I. start-ups, like Z.ai or Moonshot AI, which have actually become quite popular around the world.

Douthat: What are the Chinese equivalents of an Anthropic or an OpenAI right now?

Chan: That’s a good question. So maybe DeepSeek would be the closest. And then you have the smaller start-ups. And by smaller I mean like on the order of $40-to-$50-billion market cap. And those are some of the more successful ones.

But it’s hard to find that kind of middle ground. DeepSeek now is preparing to take in outside investment. Remember, they were actually not originally an A.I. company. They were part of a hedge fund, actually, that was trying to use A.I. to develop more sophisticated financial models. So they’re sort of a category unto themselves.

Douthat: And all of these companies, though, are operating under some basic constraints that don’t apply to U.S. companies right now, mostly around chips. Can you describe the landscape of constraint in China and what it means?

Chan: Yeah. I had mentioned earlier that Chinese A.I. companies are trying to run different races, and one of those was efficiency. Part of that is in response to the constraints that they’re under, in particular around compute and chips.

So remember, right now the U.S. has export controls on our most advanced semiconductors, basically made by Nvidia, and we stopped those from officially being sold in China. We allow the sale of watered-down versions, but the idea is that we keep the best and the most advanced chips for American A.I. companies in the United States and for allies and partners.

For China, that means that they don’t have access to the most cutting-edge A.I. chips. They have some Chinese domestic alternatives — and this is a big part of the story. One of the leading players in the space is Huawei, the heavily sanctioned Chinese tech giant that rose first in the telecom space, branched into smartphones and is now in pretty much every other industry — electric vehicles, clean technology and certainly now A.I. and chips. So China’s trying to build up their own capacity for developing A.I. chips on their own, not just designing them, but actually producing them.

The problem is, they’re just not quite as good as the Nvidia chips. And without that, it does put a lot of constraints on what they can do. So they’re trying to squeeze more out of very limited compute resources.

Douthat: Why aren’t their chips as good? I know this is a simple-minded question. Is it just that Nvidia is so awesome at engineering and China’s engineers, even if they have a Nvidia chip, can’t quite get there themselves? Talk to me like a non-chip specialist.

Chan: This is the $5 trillion question, which is currently, I think, roughly the market cap of Nvidia today.

There are a couple different aspects to this. One is actually the chip fabrication that is producing the chips. Remember, Nvidia doesn’t make their own chips. TSMC in Taiwan, they’re the ones that make the chips.

Douthat: Conveniently located not that far from China.

Chan: [Chuckles] That’s right. To the consternation of probably a lot of folks in Washington and maybe other folks dependent on those supply chains.

But TSMC has been pushing the boundaries for increasingly advanced semiconductors in a whole range of areas, and that includes A.I. And Nvidia, by partnering with TSMC, can combine some of the best design work out there with some of the best production capabilities.

For example, ASML, a Dutch company that maybe some people have heard of, it’s actually one of the biggest tech companies in Europe now. They make these extremely precise, extremely expensive lithography machines for printing chips, basically. And they’re the only ones in the world that can make this kind of machine.

They sell those to TSMC. TSMC can use that cutting-edge technology, combined with their own cutting-edge manufacturing processes, and work with Nvidia to produce these incredible state-of-the-art chips that keep getting better and better.

Douthat: So essentially, when we talk about the U.S. not allowing Nvidia to sell to China, we’re effectively talking about the U.S. cutting China out of a larger supply chain that runs through Taiwan, through the Netherlands, all around the world?

Chan: Absolutely.

Douthat: OK. That’s interesting and very helpful. What does China have going for it then, in terms of A.I. build-out, that the U.S. doesn’t have?

Chan: Energy is absolutely huge in China. If you’re thinking about the broader A.I. stack — that is, not just the chips or the models themselves, but deeper down on the layer — energy is perhaps the most important and least talked about.

For the U.S., this is a major bottleneck. It’s very hard now for data centers to build out the power capacity to power all those chips that they’re putting together.

In China, interestingly, they’ve been building out energy at a very rapid pace — clean energy like solar, wind, batteries. They’re trying to leverage that ongoing energy build-out to feed into their compute build-out, which then feeds into their A.I. development.

So you see really interesting sort of strategies that the Chinese are taking. For example, they have this effort to try to build data centers out in the western provinces, away from the high-population urban areas in China.

At first, that might not make any sense. Don’t you want to have your data centers close to where people are actually using them? Don’t you want to have that low latency, high response time?

What China’s trying to do is they’re trying to leverage a lot of their renewable energy resources out in those further-off regions.

They’re also trying to just do sort of good old-fashioned geographical redistribution, always concerned about having these poorer provinces remain poor while the high-tech Shenzhens and Shanghais speed on ahead.

So this is another area where they’re trying to leverage some of their strengths to feed into areas where they’re maybe weaker.

Douthat: China is, to simplify, imagining a future where they’re only a little bit behind the U.S. and — actually, say what that means. People talk about the best Chinese models being three months behind the U.S. or six months behind the U.S. How far behind are they, and what does that mean in practice?
