The LaRouche section of The Cultural Marxism Conspiracy reads like a Thomas Pynchon novel, and had me laughing out loud at several points. As Woods recounts, around the same time Students for a Democratic Society was fracturing into rival factions before spiraling into terrorist violence, LaRouche positioned himself as a pseudo-Leninist guru in the New York City radical scene. Pitting himself against the various SDS splinter groups, he promised to train a revolutionary vanguard capable of overthrowing “Rockefeller Fascism,” his memorable shorthand for the American establishment.

LaRouche and his followers were fixated on Marcuse, accusing him of being a CIA-funded plant subverting the left from within. Marcuse’s most famous student, Angela Davis, had been subjected to a “CIA zombie brainwash program” by Marcuse and Adorno, LaRouche alleged. As the 1970s wore on, the LaRouchites’ sci-fi epic became ever more baroque. It was their prolific pamphleteering, Woods shows, that gave rise to some of the more outlandish elements of the “cultural Marxism” narrative, such as the claim that Adorno was somehow responsible for popularizing rock music. Another, less remembered accusation is that the painfully highbrow philosopher had “undermined the morale of the American people through the promotion of soap operas.” As Woods notes, the irony is that these allegations can be read as a degraded imitation of the Frankfurt School’s own critique of the deadening effects of the culture industry.

Eventually, the LaRouche organization drifted to the right, and began circulating its publications to right-wing think tanks; it was at this point that Weyrich, Lind, and others in that realm began to embrace the Frankfurt School myth and deploy it for their own purposes. Eventually, the New Left origins of the anti-Frankfurt School narrative were mostly forgotten, even as elements of the original version (the LaRouchites’ weird confabulation about Adorno and the Beatles, for example) continue to surface in meme form decades later. “The history of Cultural Marxism/s,” as Woods writes, “has always been a story of revisions, borrowings, and recontextualizations.”

This story had a new twist last December, when Villanova professor Gabriel Rockhill published a j’accuse directed at the long deceased philosophers of the Frankfurt School under the title Who Paid the Pipers of Western Marxism? In effect, Rockhill has resuscitated the old LaRouchite “CIA zombie” strain of Frankfurt School mythology, albeit in a slightly less bizarre form. He alleges that Adorno, Horkheimer, and company were a CIA front charged with waging an “intellectual world war on the socialist alternative” by establishing a “‘compatible left’ intelligentsia” in the West to undermine the real socialists in the Soviet Union, China, and Cuba.

Woods doesn’t address this latest twist, but the origin story provided by the book makes it seem like a homecoming. As one review notes, to support his insinuation that Marcuse remained on the CIA’s payroll in the 1960s (the exiled philosopher’s wartime work for the OSS, the CIA’s precursor organization, is well-established), Rockhill cites the New Left magazine Progressive Labor, the outlet of the eponymous splinter group of SDS. It was from this same publication’s anti-Marcuse diatribes, as Wood shows, that LaRouche cribbed his anti-Frankfurt School narrative. Many early LaRouchites had previously been members of PL.

The left-wing Frankfurt School demonology Rockhill is reviving, in other words, has the same origins as the much more popular right-wing version. The result, as I have written previously, is that we now have Frankfurt School conspiracy theories of both the right and the left that are perfect inverted mirror images of each other, with the first telling us that this small group of philosophers subverted the capitalist West on behalf of the communist East and the second alleging the opposite.

It is no accident that the absurdities of the sectarian left are gaining new traction alongside the grotesqueries of the antisemitic right. “There is no antisemite,” wrote Adorno and Horkheimer in Dialectic of Enlightenment, “who does not basically want to imitate his mental image of a Jew.” The “portrait of the Jews” as all-powerful puppet masters, in other words, is the “self-portrait” of all those who “long for total possession and unlimited power.” Something like this applies to those who seize today on Frankfurt School demonology: They project their delusional fantasies of intellectual world dominion onto their spectral nemeses. This was blatant enough in the case of LaRouche, who accused Adorno and Marcuse of brainwashing their followers at precisely the moment he was turning his organization into a Scientology-like cult; it is no less true of ideological entrepreneurs trying to game the attention economy today.

There are plenty of reasonable grounds on which to criticize the Frankfurt School. Adorno and Horkheimer’s gloomily deterministic account of the self-destructive tendencies of bourgeois civilization was understandable given the horrors that had engulfed the world in which they came of age, but it left them incapable of perceiving the resilience of the democratic culture and traditions of the nation where they spent their exile. Marcuse remained in America much longer, but also remained largely blind to its virtues, even as he proved overly susceptible to the nihilistic radical chic of the New Left.

Nevertheless, these thinkers’ intellectual legacy in the United States is far more rich and varied than their facile detractors on the right or left have been capable of appreciating. What makes their work compelling was that they were neither simplistically for or against “the West”: They believed that realizing the emancipatory possibilities of Western cultural and intellectual traditions also required acknowledging its internal contradictions and self-destructive potential. This rigorously critical stance is what has made them a ripe target both for right-wing chauvinists and for neo-Third Worldists like Rockhill.

One irony of the Frankfurt School’s reception, as Gottfried briefly notes in Encounters, is that its “attacks on bureaucratic structures and on Enlightenment rationalism … have profoundly conservative implications.” The historian Christopher Lasch, a supporter of the New Left in his early years, credited the Frankfurt School with contributing to his positive revaluation of the family as “the last defense of a rich and autonomous inner life” over and against the market and the bureaucratic state. Similarly, the critical theory journal Telos, with which Gottfried and Lasch were both associated, used the Frankfurt School’s frameworks to critique the managerial liberalism of the “New Class.”

Woods—who otherwise seems broadly supportive of progressive social justice causes—acknowledges the continued relevance of this line of critique. Toward the end of The Cultural Marxism Conspiracy, Woods refers to “the absorption of left-wing concerns into a range of managerial procedures”: that is, the “corporate wokeness” of DEI initiative and HR departments. This is one front, as Lasch saw decades ago, on which the Frankfurt School’s account of how market and state have hollowed out autonomous subjectivity still has much to teach us. Also prescient are Adorno and Horkheimer’s warnings about what happens when “thought … becomes a commodity, and language the means of promoting that commodity”—phrases that resonate anew in the age of Large Language Models, when tech CEOs inform us that “intelligence” will soon be a metered utility.

The “prime cause of the retreat from enlightenment into mythology,” Adorno and Horkheimer wrote in Dialectic of Enlightenment, is to be found less in our seduction by irrational myths than “in the Enlightenment itself when paralyzed by fear of the truth.” That is, when modern societies refuse to confront the contradictions generated by economic and technological progress, ad hoc mythologies emerge to disguise these contradictions. The persistence of the Frankfurt School myth vindicates this essential and disturbing insight.

Geoff Shullenberger is Compact’s managing editor.

Claude’s Response

This is Shullenberger’s analytical strongest mode — using a single book review as the occasion for a longer genealogical argument that puts Compact’s house concerns (managerial liberalism, the corporate absorption of left-wing language into HR-and-DEI procedures, the contradictions of laissez-faire-with-cultural-traditionalism) onto a coherent intellectual-history map. The central analytical move (that left- and right-wing Frankfurt-School conspiracy theories are inverted mirror images descending from the same LaRouchite source) is genuinely novel even for readers familiar with the cultural-Marxism discourse; the LaRouche-to-conservative-think-tank migration is the kind of archival finding that does specific evidentiary work.

Strengths: the Paul Gottfried opening (paleoconservative editor of Chronicles, mentor of Richard Spencer, coined “alt-right,” and yet gratefully credits Marcuse as the teacher of “true liberal intellectual exchange”) is the perfect frame — it lets Shullenberger establish that the contemporary right-wing dismissal of the Frankfurt School is not even consistent within the right’s own intellectual-genealogy. The closing pivot to AI (“‘intelligence’ will soon be a metered utility”) and the Adorno/Horkheimer quote about thought becoming a commodity is rhetorically effective and substantively justified — the Frankfurt-School critique of culture-industry-and-commodification really does have specific 2026 traction.

The piece’s structural strength is what Shullenberger does not do: he refuses to defend the Frankfurt School in toto. He acknowledges Adorno and Horkheimer’s “gloomily deterministic” account left them unable to see American democratic resilience; Marcuse’s susceptibility to “nihilistic radical chic of the New Left.” Including these concessions makes the central argument (that the contemporary conspiracy theories are degraded imitations of a substantive intellectual project that deserves engagement on its own terms) harder to dismiss as defensive special pleading.

Weaknesses: the piece’s analytical reach is sometimes farther than its evidence requires. The claim that the medieval antisemitic well-poisoning legend is the structural template of the cultural-Marxism narrative is rhetorically powerful but underargued — Shullenberger relies on a single Adorno/Horkheimer quote (“antisemitism is a deeply imprinted schema, a ritual of civilization”) rather than engaging with the historiographic literature on whether all foreign-subversion conspiracy theories really do descend from this single template. The Christopher Lasch / Telos / “Frankfurt School has profoundly conservative implications” tag-on at the end is interesting but feels like a paragraph-and-a-half that wanted to be a separate piece — the connection between the LaRouche-to-think-tank migration and the Lasch / managerial-liberalism critique isn’t fully traced, and Shullenberger could have given either more space.

For the project: this is a Compact piece that earns its space — Shullenberger is doing intellectual-history work the roster doesn’t otherwise cover, and the specific archival finding (the LaRouchite origins of the right-wing narrative) is the kind of evidence-based reframe of contemporary discourse that even readers who disagree with Compact’s broader frame should find useful. The piece pairs with the Andrews “Who Benefits from Urban Poverty” critique as the week’s Compact-intellectual-history register, but where Andrews is doing direct policy-critique, Shullenberger is doing meta-discourse-genealogy. The cross-orientation pattern (Compact running both an evidence-based attack on RFK Jr.’s anti-vaccine framework and an evidence-based attack on right-wing Frankfurt-School mythology in the same week) is itself a useful signal about Compact’s editorial range that the synthesis composer might note.

Back to summary

Why the American Left Is Liberal, Not Left

Sun Liping 孙立平 · 2026-05-13 · Original →

English translation:

In The Great Debate: Edmund Burke, Thomas Paine, and the Birth of Right and Left, Yuval Levin makes two very clear statements:

One: today’s debate between left and right is a family quarrel within liberalism.

The other: today’s debate between left and right is not a contest between capitalism and socialism, but a fundamental disagreement within liberalism over how best to realize freedom.

These two sentences ought to clarify, for us, the dispute between the American left and right today.

This lets us understand a problem: today’s American Left is Liberal, not Left. But one term — “white left” (白左) — has thrown the Chinese-language discussion into a tangle. Why? Because the term “white left” lumps together, in one stewpot, the very different Lefts that have appeared at different times in different contexts. In particular it misleads people into using their distaste and wariness toward Left to understand and intervene in the Western dispute between left and right, thereby leading to a misreading of our own fundamental problems and causing splits that shouldn’t have existed. (That’s why I keep asking: do you really think the issue we face is being too pluralistic, too egalitarian, too inclusive? Do you really think the issue is dozens of genders? Do you really think it’s gender transition or fuzzy bathroom labels? Do you really think it’s being too welcoming to immigrants or refugees?)

The first thing to make clear: there has never been any Left of consequence in American history.

There was once the Sombart question — why is there no socialism in the United States? From the historical record this is in fact the case. In moments of acute economic and social conflict, like the Great Depression of the 1930s, the United States did produce something resembling a Left, and it had real influence. But once things returned to normal, that influence vanished into thin air.

According to Sombart’s explanation, the reasons were: the relative material affluence, the way the two-party constitutional democracy dissolved radical thought, the mobile social structure formed by the immigrant tradition, and the “escape into freedom” provided by the vast westward frontier — all of which made it very hard for Left to take root in American society.

So what is the American “left”? Where does it come from?

To put it most simply: it comes from a split within liberalism — or, as Levin puts it, a family quarrel inside liberalism. In people’s plain-language intuition, liberalism has always been on the right side of the political spectrum, and historically that was so. In the Europe of the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries, liberalism meant limiting royal power and championing free-market economics. That is the liberalism normally classified as right. As for the United States — it had no monarchy, and its founding ideas (individual liberty, democracy, capitalism) were themselves classical liberalism. You could say the entire starting point of America was liberalism.

The trouble lies in the later split within that liberalism.

This split, in Levin’s account, can be traced back to the Burke–Paine debate. But its hardening into two great political camps was a twentieth-century affair. In the twentieth century, against the prevailing reality of the time, liberalism underwent a profound internal transformation and began to split into two branches: classical liberalism, and modern liberalism (or new liberalism). The former became today’s right-wing classical liberalism or conservatism; the latter became today’s left-wing new liberalism or modern liberalism. Gradually right-wing liberalism stopped wanting to call itself liberalism, and so handed the name “liberalism” over to the left-wing liberals.

That is the origin of today’s American left being Liberal, not Left.

What is the real point of disagreement? Perhaps one could put it this way: the question behind it is what social conditions are needed for individual freedom to develop. Should the state actively create the necessary material foundation and social conditions for individual freedom? The two sides answer that question differently. Against the backdrop of economic crisis and intensifying social conflict, this question becomes even more salient. Hence the shift from “negative freedom” (freedom from) to “positive freedom” (freedom to). We can leave aside, for now, who is right and who is wrong; but a premise is that the disagreement of the two sides is about how to realize freedom — neither side is a demon. (The reason “white left” caught on in the Chinese-speaking world is precisely that it conveniently smuggles people’s posture and feelings toward Left into this left/right dispute, thereby intensifying the polarization and tearing.)

The above split runs roughly through three nodes:

The first node is the Great Depression of the 1930s. In that unprecedented economic crisis, people began to doubt the market-fundamentalist classical liberalism. The New Deal made large-scale use of government power to intervene in the economy and built a social-welfare system. Because these policies were seen as a self-correction of liberalism, they were named “new liberalism.”

The second node is the student and antiwar movement of the 1960s. If in the Great Depression new liberalism faced doubts from the conservatives who represented classical liberalism, this time new liberalism faced an assault from a real Left. New liberalism held that the system could be progressively corrected through taxation, the courts, and regulation; Left held that the economy and social system needed to be fundamentally changed.

The third node is the globalization process after the Cold War. After the upheavals in the Soviet bloc and the dissolution of the USSR, the entire West was bathed in an atmosphere of optimism and naivety. From here, new liberalism naively pushed globalization, naively pushed pluralism-equality-inclusion (多平包), with moralism running rampant and contrition running to excess. From this point onward new liberalism began to play out of tune, even to lose its mind, and produced all sorts of social ills.

In response, as a corrective, the world began turning rightward.

But here one must note one point: the conservatism that faced new liberalism in this moment was composed of two forces: an Establishment conservatism, and a Populist conservatism. Over the past several decades, what dominated the Republican Party was the establishment force — what is usually called Reaganite traditional conservatism.

But from 2016 onward the populist branch of conservatism suddenly broke out, its core program being anti-establishment, anti-globalization, anti-immigration, America First. The forces behind it were principally the losers from globalization: the blue-collar workers of the Rust Belt, the unemployed, and also the populations of the economically lagging regions where evangelical believers are concentrated (the red states of America). In other words, the rise of populism has a deep social foundation.

This trend thoroughly ended the dominance of Reaganite traditional conservatism within the party and turned MAGA’s populist conservatism into the dominant force inside the Republican Party. This in fact is the process by which the far right was formed.

Original text (中文):

尤瓦尔·莱文（Yuval Levin）在《大争论：左派和右派的起源》（The Great Debate: Edmund Burke, Thomas Paine, and the Birth of Right and Left）一书中说了两句很明白的话：

一句是，现在左与右的争论，是一场自由主义内部的家族争论。

另一句是，今天左与右的争论，并非资本主义和社会主义之争，而是自由主义内部对于如何更好地实现自由的根本分歧。

这两句话，对于我们理解今天美国左与右的分歧和争论，应该是说得很明白了。

这样，我们也就可以理解一个问题，现在美国的左是Liberal，而不是Left。但一个词，白左，在中文世界把这个问题搅得一窝乱。为什么？因为白左这个词把不同时期不同背景下出现的含义不同的左，都给一勺烩了。尤其是误导人们用对Left的反感和警惕，来理解和介入西方意义上的左右之争，从而导致对自己根本问题的误解并造成本不应有的撕裂（所以我多次问，你真的以为我们是过分多元过分平等过分包容的多平包的问题吗？你真的以为我们是几十种性别的问题吗？你真的以为我们变性或厕所男女模糊的问题吗？你真的以为我们是对移民或难民过分包容的问题吗）？

这里，首先要说清楚的，美国历史上根本就没有过能形成气候的Left。

历史上曾经有个桑巴特命题，即美国为什么没有社会主义？从美国的历史看，确实是这样。在经济和社会矛盾比较尖锐的时候，如三十年代大萧条时，美国确实出现过Left，并且有一定影响。但一到正常时期，Left的这种影响也就烟消云散了。

按照桑巴特的解释，其原因是，物质生活水平的相对富裕、两党制主导的民主宪政体制对于激进思想的消解，由移民传统形成的流动性社会结构以及广大疆域提供的向西而行的”逃入自由”的社会流动机会，都使得Left很难在美国这个社会中扎下根来。

那美国的左翼是怎么一回事？它来自哪里？

用最简单的方式来说，就是源于自由主义内部的分裂，或者如莱文所说的自由主义内部的家族争论。我们知道，在人们朴素的印象中，自由主义一直是作为政治光谱中的右端存在的。在历史上，也确实如此。在18-19世纪的欧洲，自由主义是主张限制王权，主张实行自由市场经济的。这就是一般归类为右的自由主义。就美国来说，它没有王权的历史，它的建国理念，比如个人自由、民主、资本主义，本身就是古典自由主义的。可以说，整个美国的起点就是自由主义。

事情出在后来这个自由主义的分裂上。

这种分裂，按照莱文的说法可以追溯到伯克与潘恩之间的争论。但真正成为政治的两大阵营，则是20世纪的事情。在20世纪，因为当时的现实背景，自由主义本身经历了一场深刻的内部嬗变，开始分裂为古典自由主义和现代自由主义（或新自由主义）两大支脉。前者即为今天作为右翼的古典自由主义或保守主义，后者即为今天作为左翼的新自由主义或现代自由主义。慢慢地，右翼自由主义也不愿意继续把自己叫作自由主义了，于是就把自由主义这个名头送给了左翼自由主义。

这就是美国今天的左是Liberal，而不是Left的由来。

真正的分歧点是什么呢？也许可以这样说，背后的问题是个人自由的发展需要怎样的社会条件？国家是否应当积极为个人自由创造必要的物质基础和社会条件？是对这个问题的回答不同。在经济危机和社会矛盾激化的背景下，这个问题会更加凸显出来。于是就有了从”消极自由”（freedom from）向”积极自由”（freedom to）的转向。这当中的是非对错我们暂且不论，但一个前提是，双方的分歧是如何实现自由，对立的双方都不是妖魔（白左这个词在中文圈之所以流行，就是它方便地把人们对Left的立场和情绪带入这场左右之争，从而加剧对立与撕裂）。

上述的分裂过程大体经历了如下三个节点：

第一个节点是30年代的大萧条。在这场空前的经济危机中，人们开始质疑市场万能的古典自由主义。在罗斯福新政中，大规模使用政府力量干预经济、建立社会福利制度。由于这些政策被视为自由主义自身的矫正，因而被称之为新自由主义。

第二个节点是60年代的学生和反战运动。如果说在大萧条中，新自由主义面对的是代表古典自由主义的保守派的质疑，而这一次，新自由主义面对的，则是真正左翼（left）的围攻。新自由主义认定制度可以通过税收、法院、监管来逐步纠偏的，左翼（Left）则认定需要从根本上改变经济和社会制度。

第三个节点是冷战后的全球化过程。在苏东巨变、苏联解体之后，当时整个西方洋溢在一种乐观、天真的氛围中。于是，新自由主义着开始天真地推进全球化，天真地推行多平包，道德心泛滥，忏悔心过度。正是从这个时候开始，新自由主义开始荒腔走板，甚至走火入魔，由此造成种种的社会弊端。

于是，作为一种矫正，世界开始向右转的过程。

但这时需要注意到一点，此时面对新自由主义的保守主义，是由两股力量构成的，一个是建制派的保守主义（Establishment），一个是民粹派的保守主义（Populists）。在过去几十年间，在共和党中占支配地位的主要是建制派的力量，即通常所说的里根式的传统保守主义。

但从2016年开始，保守主义中的民粹派突然异军突起，其核心主张是反建制、反全球化、反移民、美国优先。其背后的力量主要是全球化的受损者，包括铁锈地带的蓝领、失业者，也包括经济比较落后的福音派信徒比较集中的地方的民众（美国的红州）。也就是说，民粹派的崛起，实际上是有着深厚的社会基础的。

这一趋势彻底终结了里根式传统保守主义在党内的主导地位，并使MAGA的民粹保守主义成为共和党内的主导力量。这实际上也就是极右翼形成的过程。

Claude’s Response

Sun is doing the same diagnostic move he’s done in prior weeks (his “structural power” / 结构力 framework, his JD-vs-local-stalls dispatch from third-tier Chinese cities, his Iran-oil-shock-doesn’t-break-China-deflation analysis): use a Western frame as a lens, redirect attention to Chinese internal dynamics. This week’s redirection target is the bai zuo (白左, “white left”) slur and the broader practice of importing American conservative discourse into Chinese political reading via clickbait translation. The move is unusually direct for Sun — most of his pieces work indirectly, but here he names the audience and the cost explicitly (“the reason ‘white left’ caught on in the Chinese-speaking world is precisely that it conveniently smuggles people’s posture and feelings toward Left into this left/right dispute, thereby intensifying the polarization and tearing”).

The Levin frame is well-chosen and the genealogy he constructs is broadly defensible at the high level of resolution he’s working at. The taxonomy holds: classical liberalism → modern liberalism split, with the latter capturing the “liberalism” label as the former adopts “conservatism”; the three nodes (Depression, 1960s, post-1989 globalization); the populist-conservative break in 2016. These are conventional enough at the level a comparative-political essay needs that the load-bearing question is not whether the taxonomy is right but what Sun is using it for.

What Sun is using it for is the bigger find. The parenthetical aimed at Chinese readers — “do you really think the issue we face is being too pluralistic, too egalitarian, too inclusive? do you really think the issue is dozens of genders? do you really think it’s gender transition or fuzzy bathroom labels?” — is doing important work. It names the way Chinese-language conservative-coded discourse has been parasitically borrowing from American culture-war framing without owning that the borrowed framing belongs to a US-specific liberalism-vs-new-liberalism family quarrel that does not map onto Chinese political conditions. Sun is functioning here as a Chinese-language version of the function that, say, the project would value in a US writer who pushed back on imported Continental frames being applied to American conditions: he is arguing that diagnostic categories don’t transfer for free across political-cultural contexts.

The piece’s main analytical limit: Sun’s three-node history treats the 1960s real-Left assault and the post-1989 modern-liberalism overreach as broadly continuous with each other, but the cluster of “social ills” he attributes to post-1989 modern liberalism is the cluster American conservative discourse calls progressivism or the cultural Left, and Sun doesn’t quite name whether he agrees with that diagnosis or merely treats it as exogenous data. There’s an asymmetry: he is careful to insist “neither side is a demon,” but his recital of the modern-liberal excesses (moralism running rampant, contrition running to excess, globalization naively pushed) is one-sided enough that the reader can plausibly conclude he agrees with the conservative critique. This isn’t a fundamental flaw — the piece is openly about the bai zuo taxonomy, not about adjudicating the US culture war — but the per-theme writer should note that Sun is operating with a Chinese-conservative-coded frame even as he argues the bai zuo slur should be dropped.

For the project, Sun’s outside-view of US politics fits cleanly into the Democrats’-strategy / Working-class-plight cluster. Mandel (Commentary, Saudi-drove-Iran-war), Bowles (Free Press), and the broader Democratic-failure-mode cluster are working the inside-the-discourse version of the same diagnostic question; Sun supplies the comparative-and-translated-into-other-language perspective on what the US left actually is. The synthesis composer should note that this is the second consecutive piece (Sun + the implicit complement of the Persuasion Grzymala-Busse piece) tracking the limits of conventional categories like “liberal” and “democratic” — both writers, from very different vantage points, are doing definitional clarification work for a discourse that has lost its terms.

Anchor on first use: 新自由主义 (xinziyouzhuyi) here = American modern liberalism (FDR-and-after), NOT the Anglo-American “neoliberalism” the same Chinese phrase usually translates; 白左 (bai zuo) = the Chinese-internet pejorative for Western progressives/cultural left; 多平包 (duo-ping-bao) = Sun’s contraction of 多元-平等-包容, “pluralism-equality-inclusion”; 结构力 (jiegouli, “structural power”) = the third-power category Sun has been developing through his recent writing, distinct from hard and soft power. The project should add 多平包 to data/vocabulary.md as a Sun-specific compression worth tracking.

Back to summary

Conservatives are afraid to talk about the real marriage problem

MacIntyre, Auron · 2026-05-13 · Original →

An honest discussion about marriage needs to include a discussion about feminism and its consequences.

The fact that marriage in America is on the decline is concerning, but few people understand how deep the problem goes.

Recent data shows the dismal marriage numbers in the United States. The percentage of married couples, average age at which couples are marrying, and number of children are all in catastrophic decline. The numbers represent a dramatic collapse in the institution that serves as a foundation for successful civilizations.

Conservatives will often place the blame on a lack of individual virtue, and there is plenty of truth to that claim, but the most important factors are baked into the structure of our society in a manner that Republicans are terrified to address.

When a movement refers to itself as “conservative” you would think the preservation of marriage would be its top priority. However, when marriage does receive any attention from conservative pundits and politicians, it is in the form of glib advice directed at young men telling them to get out of their parents’ basement and stop playing video games.

Part of the marriage problem is certainly the lack of initiative on the part of young males, but this is also the easiest and most cowardly attempt to explain away the issue. Our culture encourages placing the blame on young men, who are one of the few groups that can be attacked without consequence. The real answers require taking on far more sacred cows.

When conservatives are feeling a little more adventurous, they will admit that some aspects of our economy are antithetical to family formation.

The fact that the average age of first-time homeowners is pushing past 40 signals how difficult achieving stability for young families has become. College is now required for even the most entry-level jobs, and the cost keeps exploding, consuming the capital that once went into a starter home.

The costs of health care and food continue to skyrocket so that most households require two incomes, forcing mothers to work, while the price of child care also increases rapidly. The economic issues are real and important, but even they do not tell the whole story.

Love, duty, and honor are all factors that hold our social bonds together, but it is dependence that makes them necessary in the first place and continues to undergird them when everything else falls away.

Modern people do not like to hear this fact because they believe that maximizing freedom and independence is the ultimate good, but there is a point at which autonomy rips society apart.

Men are, of course, dependent on many things, but women, due to their biological vulnerability, were always directly dependent on men.

Women are physically weaker, less aggressive, become pregnant, must care for children, and regularly need the direct intervention of others to ensure their safety and security. Women could not work outside the home while pregnant or raising children, so they remained dependent on their husbands’ income.

People will have sex — we are hardwired to pursue it — so the reality was that women and men needed to get married early to secure the safety of mothers and their children. Family formation was part of the rhythm of life. It was largely unavoidable, and this kept marriage rates among young people relatively high.

Once birth control and abortion made pregnancy a choice instead of an inevitability, everything shifted. Single women became a larger part of society, and the state expanded its reach to provide them with broader physical and economic protections.

The newly emancipated woman needed her own stream of income, and corporations were more than happy to provide it. Women doubled the labor pool, driving down wages. In large bureaucratic organizations, where compliance is key, the more agreeable nature of women is considered an asset in a way that it would not be in a more entrepreneurial economy.

Advantages were built into every level of our system to help elevate women due to the perceived biases that existed when mothers were expected to stay home. Universities gave priority to women, who now earn more degrees than men. Corporations gave hiring priority to women, who now make up a majority of their workforce.

Government assistance and scholarship programs were established to ensure that working mothers did not fall through the cracks. It is not that women stopped getting married — every female must be married at some level — they simply became dependent on the men running the government and corporations instead of traditional husbands.

Women do not date men who earn less than they do, even if they think of themselves as independent. Deep down, females know that in the modern world, income signals status and status means protection.

Even in our hyper-feminist culture, women still know they could become mothers at any time and naturally seek out protection and stability. The push to elevate women in education and hiring meant that the percentage of well-paying jobs going to men shrank. This resulted in men being systematically removed from the pool of attractive marriage prospects.

Dwindling prospects for both education and career have already set young men on the path to downward mobility, but the lack of prospects for marriage was the nail in the coffin. These changes, coupled with the one-sided nature of divorce and child custody law, means that many men no longer see a reason to bother dating.

Women initiate 70% of divorces in the United States. The truth is that women who do not need men do not marry when they are young and are far more likely to divorce if they do.

To be clear, this is not to absolve men of their responsibility. Both sexes made this mess, both sexes are to blame, and both will need to do hard work to fix the problem.

Men must have the drive and vitality to make something of themselves, no matter what situation they find themselves in. But in the rare instances where conservatives are even willing to address the marriage crisis, they save all their criticisms for young men because they are the culturally approved target.

There are no easy answers to the chaos that modernity has visited on the dating and marriage landscape, but the structural issues are real, and telling men to “get it together” does nothing to change that. Until conservatives are willing to be as honest with women as they are with men about our situation, nothing will improve.

Claude’s Response

This is MacIntyre operating in his characteristic register — diagnosing a feminist-modernity etiology for a social problem the mainstream right is too cowardly to name, with the conclusion that “structural” change is required but the structural change goes unspecified. The rhetorical move is the same one Mary Harrington runs in her “rules are for suckers” anarcho-tyranny pieces: name a sacred cow the official discourse can’t touch, attribute the dysfunction to it, leave the prescription as homework. The aesthetic of bracing honesty does most of the work; the actual argument is thinner than it presents.

The core empirical claim — that contraception plus female workforce entry caused marriage decline by removing the structural necessity of male provision — is plausible as one ingredient among several but is presented as the load-bearing mechanism with no engagement of alternatives. The collapse in male real wages for the bottom two quintiles since 1979, the collapse in marriageable-male wages relative to female wages in the same cohort, the housing-affordability collapse MacIntyre himself names but treats as secondary, the secular decline in religiosity (which his frame should care about), and the cross-national variation in marriage rates among countries with similar female labor-force participation — none of these are addressed. The bare claim that “every female must be married at some level” and now they’re “married to the state” is the kind of rhetorical totalization that, if true, would also be true in Scandinavia, where it isn’t.

The “women initiate 70% of divorces” stat is real but ambiguous as evidence for his frame — it’s also consistent with women bearing disproportionate marital labor and exiting at higher rates because of that, which would imply the opposite policy direction. The “women won’t date men who earn less” claim is empirically supported in mating-preference literature but doesn’t establish the causal direction MacIntyre wants (preference may be downstream of the very wage-gap reversal he’s describing, not the cause of marriage decline). And the “advantages were built into every level of our system to help elevate women” claim — universities preferring women, corporations preferring women — is asserted in passing as if uncontroversial; the actual evidence on hiring discrimination in the post-2010 corporate environment runs both directions and the gap between female degree attainment and labor-force participation is itself unexplained on his frame.

The genuine strength of the piece is its refusal to participate in the standard conservative consolation ritual of blaming Gen Z men’s character. The genuine weakness is that the implied alternative — re-creating female economic dependence to restore marriage’s structural necessity — is the thing MacIntyre needs to name and defend if his diagnosis is going to do any work, and he won’t. Naming the sacred cow is only half the move; declining to say what should replace it lets the piece keep all its rhetorical posture while owing the reader nothing. This is the second consecutive week MacIntyre has run a piece where the diagnostic frame is presented as bracing structural honesty and the operative claim it would license is held back. The pattern itself is worth flagging for the synthesis composer.

Back to summary

Azawad Never Left: Reading Mali’s 2026 Crisis Through 2012

Sachi Muto · 2026-05-13 · Original →

On 25 April 2026, Mali appeared to enter a new and dramatic phase of crisis. Explosions and gunfire were reported around Bamako and Kati, while attacks struck several parts of the country. In the north, Kidal once again became a symbol of state fragility and armed contestation.

Yet this was not an isolated incident. It was the eruption of accumulated grievances rooted in a much longer history of marginalisation. To understand why Mali is once again facing such a crisis, we need to return not only to the 2012–2013 war, but further back — to the colonial and postcolonial arrangements that made northern Mali a periphery to be governed, secured, and repeatedly abandoned. This article argues that the crisis of April 2026 is not a rupture from Mali’s recent past, but the renewed visibility of a question that never disappeared: Azawad.

Entry of Kidal, a city in northern Mali. On the left, Kidal is written in Tifinagh script.

The situation in Mali is still unfolding and much is unclear. Some facts, however, are stark: on 26 April, Mali’s Defence Minister Sadio Camara was killed in a suicide attack on his home — one of the most consequential political assassinations the country has seen in years.

Two distinct forces have claimed responsibility for the broader wave of attacks: the Azawad Liberation Front (FLA), a predominantly Tuareg movement seeking independence for northern Mali, and JNIM (Jama’at Nusrat al-Islam wal-Muslimin), an Al-Qaeda-affiliated militant group that identifies with Al-Qaeda’s strategy of armed ‘global jihad’: a transnational ideological current, that claims to severs the ties between Islamic practice and specific territories or communitiesTreating the FLA and the JNIM as a single actor is a mistake — yet the dominant security framing in international coverage risks doing exactly that. Their alliance of convenience obscures fundamentally different, and in many ways competing, agendas. Still, the image of a collapsing state besieged simultaneously by separatists and jihadists has made it easy to reach for a familiar narrative: Mali is falling apart, and terrorism and rebellion have joined hands.

That framing is not new, and that is precisely the problem. In 2012, the National Movement for the Liberation of Azawad (MNLA) — a predominantly Tuareg political movement rooted in decades of marginalization declared independence for northern Mali. For a brief moment, Azawad existed as a political question demanding a political answer.

That moment did not last. As militant Islamic groups, including Ansar Dine and what would become JNIM, swept across the north and effectively sidelined the MNLA, the international reading of the crisis shifted decisively. The Tuareg independence movement was no longer the main focus of the Malian government, but rather background noise in a counter-terrorism operation against other actors in the north of the country.

France’s military intervention in January 2013 — Operation Serval — crystallised this transformation. An analysis of French government documents from this period reveals a striking pattern: the language of territorial integrity, sovereignty, and terrorism dominates, while the underlying political grievances of northern communities are systematically absent.

This is not merely an analytical observation. In a speech to the French National Assembly on 30 January 2013, Defence Minister Jean-Yves Le Drian described the situation in Kidal in telling terms: French forces had maintained, he said, ‘relations de bonne intelligence’ [well-informed relations] with representatives of ‘le monde touareg’ [the Touareg community] — a phrase that reveals much. The phrasing suggests a relationship less of political negotiation than of careful management.

The MNLA, despite being a secular nationalist movement with a stated willingness to negotiate, received no meaningful diplomatic engagement from Paris. The war on terror had its own grammar, and political dialogue did not fit.

The consequences were structural. By framing the crisis as a security emergency rather than a political dispute, the international response foreclosed the very conversations that might have addressed Azawad’s status. The question was not resolved — it was suppressed.

The 2015 Algiers Accord, which was brokered by Algeria and signed between the Malian government and a coalition of northern armed groups, including the MNLA, promised decentralisation, integration of former fighters, and greater autonomy for the north. France was not a party to the Accord; the diplomatic framework had shifted from Paris to Algiers.

The political status of northern Mali — who governs it, under what arrangement, and with what degree of autonomy — was never genuinely resolved. The Accord managed the conflict without addressing its roots. As the Malian state grew increasingly unstable through successive coups in 2020 and 2021, and as the military government expelled the UN peacekeeping mission MINUSMA in 2023 and turned instead to Russian security support, relations between Bamako and northern armed groups deteriorated sharply. The juntas that came to power framed their seizure of authority in the language of sovereignty and anti-colonialism — presenting France and the international community not as partners but as obstacles to Malian self-determination. On 25 January 2024, the military government formally withdrew from the Algiers Accord altogether. By 2025, the fragile ceasefire had effectively collapsed.

What happened in April 2026 is not a new crisis. It is the return of an old one — the same question, in a more explosive form.

Imposed Lines

To understand Azawad, one must look further back than 2012. The colonial borders dividing Mali, Algeria, Niger and Libya cut across Tuareg networks of movement, trade and kinship. What became international borders were, for many Saharan communities, imposed lines across older social worlds.

Modern states have consistently struggled to accommodate this mobility. Since independence in 1960, successive Malian governments failed to deliver basic infrastructure, services, or economic investment to northern communities — grievances that Tuareg leaders cited repeatedly as the root cause of each successive rebellion. When the state did intervene, it did so with force rather than governance. Movement across borders — a historical practice and economic necessity — has repeatedly been read through the lens of security: as smuggling, as insurgency, as threat. The Tuareg relationship to the Saharan space has thus been governed, surveilled and criminalised rather than understood on its own terms.

This is why Azawad cannot be reduced to ethnic separatism. At its core, it is a question of sovereignty — of who has the right to define territory, movement and belonging. It is also, crucially, not a Tuareg question alone. Northern Mali is home to Songhai, Arab and Fulani communities whose relationships to the state, to armed groups, and to the idea of Azawad itself are distinct and often contested. Any political solution must reckon with this complexity — not flatten it.

The temptation, in moments of acute crisis, is to reach for the security frame. It is familiar, it is actionable, and it offers the clarity that politics rarely does. But applying a single counter-terrorism logic to what is happening in Mali today risks repeating the same mistake made in 2013.

JNIM and the FLA are not the same actor — one transnational and ideological, the other rooted in the specific territorial grievances of northern Mali. Treating them as interchangeable — as a unified threat to be defeated — obscures precisely what needs to be understood. When political grievance is rendered invisible, it does not disappear. It finds other expressions.

The history of northern Mali since 2012 is, in many ways, a history of security operations that ended battles without ending the conflict. Each intervention bought time; none addressed the question of what northern communities are owed — in terms of political recognition, economic inclusion, and land rights.

The Azawad question has not returned because it never truly disappeared. It was renamed as terrorism, postponed, and ultimately denied as a political question. The events of April 2026 show the limits of that denial.

The post Azawad Never Left: Reading Mali’s 2026 Crisis Through 2012 appeared first on African Arguments.

Claude’s Response

This is the African Arguments cluster’s second consecutive week on Mali — last week Appiah-Mensah argued the same JNIM-FLA convergence had invalidated both the junta’s sovereignty narrative and the Russian-paramilitary security model; this week Muto extends the analysis by anchoring it in the longer 2012-2024 institutional history. The piece’s strongest move is the methodological one: it takes the Western security-frame habit (collapse two actors into one threat, defeat the unified threat) and shows how it produces the very convergence it claims to disrupt. JNIM and FLA do not actually share an ideological program (transnational caliphate vs. territorial autonomy for Saharan communities); what they share is an objective dispossession — “the cultural-identity dispossession of Tuareg groups distributed across five states” — that the security frame consistently refused to engage as political. The Le Drian “relations de bonne intelligence… le monde touareg” archival quote is doing real work: it documents that France’s Operation Serval treated the Tuareg politico-cultural community as something to be managed administratively rather than negotiated with, even when the MNLA was the secular-nationalist negotiable interlocutor France could have engaged before Ansar Dine and JNIM swept across the north.

The pairing with Aourid’s Al Jazeera Arabic piece this same week is the project’s most important cross-orientation convergence of the window. Two writers from radically different vantage points — Muto, working in the African Arguments academic-policy register, foregrounding colonial-postcolonial state formation and the failure of the Algiers Accord; Aourid, working in the Arabic-language Maghreb political-science register, foregrounding ‘asabiyya (group solidarity) and da‘wa (call/doctrine) as Ibn Khaldun’s frame for understanding how local-tribal cohesion can fuse with ideological mobilization when the central state has reached “the stage of decay” — converge on the same diagnosis: the April 25 attack is a structural event whose causes are not the convergence of two named groups but the failure of any framework to absorb the underlying dispossession. They differ on prescription (Muto wants political recognition of Azawad; Aourid is more historicizing, treating the moment as one in a long pattern), but the empirical agreement is striking. This cross-orientation convergence is exactly the project’s most-prized institutional finding and deserves explicit framing in the synthesis.

Two minor critiques. First, Muto’s structural-grievance frame, while correct, underweights the immediate-actor analysis the piece would benefit from at the policy layer: who within FLA and JNIM coordinated the April 25 attack? What does the assassination of Defence Minister Camara on April 26 tell us about JNIM-FLA tactical command relationships? The piece is so committed to the long-arc structural story that it skips the recent operational story — which Appiah-Mensah’s earlier piece did engage. A reader who comes to Muto without having read Appiah-Mensah will not get a clear picture of the actual tactical event. Second, the prescription is essentially absent — “political recognition” of Azawad and “economic inclusion” and “land rights” are correct generalities, but the piece doesn’t engage what political mechanism could plausibly deliver them given the junta’s incentives, the Algiers Accord’s failure, and the Russian Africa Corps’s ongoing role. This is a critique of scope rather than of analysis: Muto’s piece does what it sets out to do (correct the security-frame flattening) and leaves the prescription to a follow-up.

For the project, Muto’s piece is the keystone of the Sahel cluster forming this week (Muto + Aourid + last week’s Appiah-Mensah). The Sahel is now a third structural theme alongside European-center exhaustion and the Iran impasse, and the cluster’s distinctive feature — different rhetorical registers converging on the same finding — is what makes it load-bearing.

Back to summary

What Does Ibn Khaldun Say About What’s Happening in the Sahel Now?

Hassan Aourid حسن أوريد · 2026-05-11 · Original →

English translation:

The prevailing view of the Sahel region was that it had been “pacified” and “cleansed” of jihadist groups and pockets of terrorism, and that it sufficed to pursue routine policies of what in counter-terrorism literature is called “mowing the grass.”

The alliances struck by Mali’s new government with the Russian Africa Corps appeared to be helping extend control over northern Mali — the heart of the insurgency, where the Tuareg have a deeply rooted identity particularism.

The Sahel was viewed as a flank in northwest Africa where extremism took refuge, and the imposed calm — secured after a string of foreign military interventions (first France, then later Russia via its Africa Corps) — gave the impression that the fuse of insurgency had been extinguished and the unrest cut off at the root.

It is therefore why the attack that targeted Bamako on April 25 — carried out by both the “Azawad Liberation Front” and “Jama’at Nasr al-Islam wal-Muslimin,” and by which the Azawad region together with its symbolically charged city Kidal were seized — shattered that approach.

The readings of the event’s nature did not err, whether at the level of international or regional powers or among specialists — security people, analysts, and reporters following the file. All of them saw in it an inflection point, a transformation with regional consequences, especially for the Sahel, and for North Africa more broadly.

It was no coincidence that U.S. Deputy Secretary of State Christopher Landau touched down in both Algiers and Rabat in the aftermath of the attack, although nothing of what emerged from the meetings was leaked beyond the general statements that revealed nothing of the substance of those meetings — substance bypassed in favor of focusing on marginal points or sticking to generalities.

What is striking is that Washington, Moscow, and Paris all condemned the attack despite the divergence of their interests — not only because it threatens Mali’s unity but because of what it carries in terms of the possibility of destabilizing the region, or what gets reduced to the two great evils: “terrorism” and “secessionism,” and their occasional twinning.

The view of some experts — among them the researcher Olivier Roy — distinguished between the radical tendencies in the Middle East, which were tied to a jihadist internationalism and have been beaten back, and the radical tendencies in the Sahel, which deploy the radical orientation as a franchise (a brand name), proceed primarily from local considerations, and enjoy a tightly woven social fabric.

Ideological affiliations here are variable; what is constant are their organic ties — of a tribal or local character — and their political, social, and cultural conditions. They proceed from a concern to defend their identity, or their space, or their wealth, and so they rest on a community network and stand on an exhausted memory that has been subjected to oppression, indeed extermination, at several junctures of their modern history.

This situation, and that unifying memory, made possible the rapprochement between the jihadist “Jama’at Nasr al-Islam wal-Muslimin,” with its supra-national dimension, and the local “Azawad Liberation Front” — despite the difference in reference frameworks. To many analysts this appeared unnatural (or “against nature,” as some analyses translated from the French).

The “Azawad Liberation Front” is predominantly shaped by local and cultural considerations, and is one of the Tuareg components in Mali. The “Jama’at Nasr al-Islam wal-Muslimin,” by contrast, is jihadist in nature and is in principle tied to the caliphate project.

Yet the two currents meet by departing from an objective fact: the cultural-identity dispossession of Tuareg groups distributed across five states, who are bound together by cultural and identitarian ties — language, mode of life, modes of organizing life, shared memory, and shared tragedies. Identity here cannot be separated from the scars of subjugation, repression, and the drive to erase.

The Tuareg situation is not unique among peoples who have been torn apart in Africa and elsewhere, and some researchers often compare the Tuareg with the Kurds in the Middle East. I do not know how apt the comparison is, but what is fixed is that the Tuareg have a distinctive identity, branching out of the Zanaga human group known in Arabic as Sanhaja.

Those who paved the way for independent states in the post-state arrangements opted to respect the borders inherited from colonialism, and that may have been a correct choice if the concerned communities were given the means to manage their own affairs — that is, to obtain a form of self-rule, and to have their distinctiveness respected.

But things did not unfold this way in Mali. The Tuareg suffered the two evils under the rule of the nascent Malian state, and the collective memory testifies to recurrent tragedies at several junctures since 1964, when Kidal was subjected to something resembling acts of extermination, in addition to years of drought that pushed many to migrate to neighboring countries and some of the elites to the Gulf states.

Relations remained strained between Mali’s successive governments and the Azawad region, until 2012 — in the wake of the “Arab Spring” and the movement of arms from Libya into the Sahel — when the “Azawad Liberation Front” seized the opportunity to declare its rebellion against the central authority, prompting the French intervention well-known as Operation Barkhane, then Serval.

The concern of the French authorities was not only to protect the post-colonial framework but to guarantee its tutelage in a region rich in natural resources — among them gold, where the Tuareg are located in Mali, and uranium in Niger’s Niger province — and the Sahel became an arena of great-power competition, its surface being the fight against terrorism, its substance being the laying of hands on the wealth.

The recent events return the clock to zero — that is, they make of the Sahel once again a revolutionary focus. The idea of the “revolutionary focus” has not died out, and it had previously been adopted by organizations of a nationalist and leftist persuasion, and used to threaten the organizations tied to the French protective authority.

But the political choices made by those movements, and their drift away from a revolutionary option in favor of secession in a circumscribed space, smothered the revolutionary direction; they were affected by the recession that Arab nationalism and socialism in general experienced, and by the weakening of the cover they had received from nationalist regimes — among them Qaddafi’s Libya, which was the first to embrace the “revolutionary focus.”

In North Africa, Islamist tendencies have always collided with local tendencies — and to be more precise, with Amazigh tendencies.

The latter aligned themselves with what is called the modernist tendencies, and were seen as standing at the opposite pole from the Islamist tendencies — which some analyses described as a “coalition against nature,” or unnatural. In the Sahel, however, the two tendencies have known a rapprochement, even if a circumstantial one, and this is what gives them their momentum when they meet, and their retreat when they collide.

Ibn Khaldun’s analysis helps us understand this dynamic — that is, the meeting of ‘asabiyya (group solidarity) and da‘wa (call/doctrine), or of a local orientation with an ideological lever of religious provenance. What appears unnatural to Western analysts is what Ibn Khaldun considered the dynamic that drove North Africa, or the Maghreb.

This pairing of ‘asabiyya and da‘wa is what makes them a motor of history — on the condition that the ruling structure (or the “state” in Ibn Khaldun’s expression, by which he means the ruling family, not what we today understand by the state) has been weakened and reached the stage of decay.

We should of course put things in perspective — Ibn Khaldun’s analysis was set in a context that did not involve foreign powers as participants. Several dynamics in the Maghreb have previously been aborted because of the intervention of foreign powers — from the Crusader states in antiquity, to colonialism more recently, and the multiple forms of foreign intervention in the post-colonial context, including the recent interventions the region has known, whether from France or Russia.

Conversely, the pairing of ‘asabiyya and da‘wa in the Sahel context becomes a motor when it gains an international cover, and becomes a lever for a foreign power and one of its instruments.

The transformation the Sahel has undergone cannot be reduced to “mowing the grass” — it signals a geopolitical reshaping of the North African region, beginning from the Sahel.

The situation in the Sahel obviously cannot be addressed with the logic of “mowing the grass.”

The views in this article do not necessarily reflect the editorial stance of the Al Jazeera Network.

Original text (العربية):

كان المنظور العام الساري حول منطقة الساحل أنه قد تمت “تهدئتها” و”تطهيرها” من الجماعات الجهادية وبؤر الإرهاب، وأنه يكفي انتهاج سياسات روتينية تدخل فيما يسمى في أدبيات التصدي للإرهاب بـ”جز العشب”.

وكانت التحالفات التي أجرتها حكومة مالي الجديدة مع فيلق أفريقيا الروسي، تسعف في بسط السيطرة على أقاليم شمال مالي، بؤرة التمرد، حيث الطوارق ذوو خصوصية هوياتية متجذرة.

كان ينظر إلى منطقة الساحل كخاصرة في شمال غرب أفريقيا، يرتع فيها التطرف، وكان الهدوء الذي فرض فرضا، بعد سلسلة من التدخلات العسكرية الأجنبية، منها فرنسا، ثم روسيا لاحقا عبر فيالق أفريقيا، يوحي بأنه تم إطفاء فتيل التمرد وقطع شأفة الشغب.

ولذلك أتى الهجوم الذي استهدف باماكو من قبل كل من “جبهة تحرير أزواد”، و”جماعة نصرة الإسلام والمسلمين”، يوم 25 أبريل/نيسان- وتم الاستيلاء بمقتضاه على إقليم أزواد ومدينته ذات الرمزية كيدال- ليعصف بتلك المقاربة.

لم تخطئ القراءات حول طبيعة الحدث، على مستوى القوى الدولية والإقليمية والمختصين، من الأمنيين والمحللين والصحفيين المتابعين، إذ رأوا فيه، جميعا، منعطفا، وتحولا له تداعيات إقليمية، على مستوى منطقة الساحل خاصة، وشمال أفريقيا عموما.

ولم يكن من قبيل المصادفة أن يحل نائب وزير الخارجية الأمريكي كريستوفر لاندو في كل من الجزائر، والرباط، في أعقاب الهجوم، غير أنه لم يرشح شيء مما تمخضت عنه اللقاءات، إلا ما ورد في بيانات عامة لا تسفر عن جوهر هذه الاجتماعات، التي تم تجاوزها بالتركيز على النقاط الهامشية، أو بالوقوف على العموميات.

اللافت أن كلا من واشنطن، وموسكو، وباريس، أدانت الهجوم، رغم تضارب مصالح هؤلاء، ليس فقط لأنه يهدد وحدة مالي، ولكن لما ينطوي عليه من إمكانية زعزعة المنطقة، أو ما يختزل في الشرَّين المستَطيرَين: “الإرهاب”، و”الانفصال”، وتلازمهما أحيانا.

كانت نظرة بعض الخبراء، ومنهم الباحث أوليفي روا، تميز بين الاتجاهات الراديكالية في الشرق الأوسط تلك التي ترتبط بأممية جهادية وتم دحرها، وبين الاتجاهات الراديكالية في منطقة الساحل والتي توظف التوجه الراديكالي كفرانشيز (علامة تجارية)، وتنطلق بالأساس من اعتبارات محلية، وتحظى بنسيج اجتماعي مترابط.

الانتماءات الأيديولوجية هنا متحول، والثابت هو علاقاتها العضوية ذات الطابع القبلي، أو المحلي، وأوضاعها السياسية والاجتماعية والثقافية، فهي تنطلق بهاجس الدفاع عن هويتها، أو مجالها، أو ثرواتها، وهي لذلك تستند إلى شبكة مجتمعية، وتقوم على ذاكرة منهكة تعرضت للعسف، بل للإبادة، في محطات عدة من تاريخها الحديث.

هذه الوضعية، والذاكرة الموحدة أتاحتا التقارب ما بين “جماعة نصرة الإسلام والمسلمين” الجهادية، ذات البعد ما فوق الوطني، مع “جبهة تحرير أزواد” المحلية، رغم اختلاف المرجعية، وهو ما بدا لكثير من المحللين أمرا غير طبيعي، (أو مناف للطبيعة، كما جاء في بعض التحليلات في ترجمة من الفرنسية).

إن “جبهة تحرير أزواد”، تغلب عليها الاعتبارات المحلية والثقافية، وهي واحدة من المكون التاركي (الطوارق) في مالي، أما “جماعة نصرة الإسلام والمسلمين”، فذات طبيعة جهادية، وترتبط مبدئيا بمشروع الخلافة.

بيد أن الاتجاهين يلتقيان بالانطلاق من واقع موضوعي، وهو استلاب الهوية الثقافية لجماعات من الطوارق، موزعين على خمس دول، تجمعهم وشائج ثقافية وهوياتية، منها اللغة، ونظام العيش، وتنظيم الحياة، وذاكرة مشتركة، ومآسٍ. لا تنفصل الهوية عن ندوب القهر والقمع والسعي في الطمس.

ليست حالة الطوارق بمنفردة عن شعوب أخرى تم تمزيقها في أفريقيا وغيرها، وكثيرا ما يقارن بعض الباحثين وضع الطوارق بالأكراد في الشرق الأوسط، ولا أدري مدى وجاهة المقارنة، ولكن الثابت أن للطوارق هوية خاصة، متفرعة عن المجموعة البشرية الزناكية المعروفة في اللغة العربية بصنهاجة.

ارتأى الممهدون للدول المستقلة في ترتيبات ما بعد الدول الوطنية، احترام الحدود الموروثة عن الاستعمار، وقد يكون ذلك خيارا صائبا إن أتيح للمعنيين تدبير شؤونهم؛ أي الحصول على نوع من الحكم الذاتي، واحترام خصوصياتهم.

ولكن الأمور لم تسر بهذا الشكل في مالي، وعانى الطوارق الأمَرين، تحت حكم الدولة المالية الناشئة، وتشهد الذاكرة الجمعية بمآسٍ متواترة في محطات عدة منذ 1964، حيث تعرضت كيدال إلى ما يشبه أعمال إبادة، فضلا عن سنوات من الجفاف دفعت الكثيرين إلى الهجرة إلى بلدان الجوار، وبعض النخب إلى دول الخليج.

ظلت العلاقات متوترة بين حكومات مالي المتعاقبة وإقليم أزواد، حتى عام 2012 في أعقاب “الربيع العربي”، وانتقال السلاح من ليبيا إلى منطقة الساحل، وانتهزتها “جبهة تحرير أزواد” فرصة لتعلن تمردها عن السلطة المركزية، مما استدعى تدخل السلطات الفرنسية، كما هو معلوم في عملية برخان، ثم سرفان.

لم يكن هاجس السلطات الفرنسية حماية المنظومة ما بعد الاستعمارية فحسب، بل ضمان وصايتها، في منطقة تزخر بموارد طبيعية، منها الذهب، حيث يوجد الطوارق بمالي، واليورانيوم في إقليم ناير بالنيجر، وأصبحت منطقة الساحل مجالا لصراع القوى، ظاهره محاربة الإرهاب، وباطنه وضع اليد على الثروات.

تعيد الأحداث الأخيرة العقرب إلى نقطة الصفر، أي تجعل من الساحل، بؤرة ثورية. لم تندثر فكرة “البؤرة الثورية”، وسبق أن تبنتها تنظيمات من منطلق قومي ويساري، وكانت تتهدد التنظيمات المرتبطة بالسلطة الحمائية الفرنسية.

ولكن الخيارات السياسية التي أجرتها تلك الحركات، ونأيها عن خيار ثوري لفائدة الانفصال في حيز محدود، وأدَا التوجه الثوري، وتأثرت بالانحسار الذي عرفته القومية العربية والاشتراكية عموما، وضعف الغطاءات التي كانت تحظى بها، من أنظمة قومية، منها ليبيا القذافي الذي كان أول من احتضن “البؤرة الثورية”.

في شمال أفريقيا، اصطدمت دوما الاتجاهات الإسلامية بالاتجاهات المحلية، وحتى نكون أكثر وضوحا بالاتجاهات الأمازيغية.

كانت الأخيرة تصطف فيما يسمى الاتجاهات الحداثية، وكان تُرى على طرفي نقيض من الاتجاهات الإسلامية، وهو ما نعتته بعض التحليلات بتحالف ضد الطبيعة، أو غير طبيعي، أما في منطقة الساحل، فقد عرف الاتجاهان تقاربا ولو ظرفيا، وهو ما يمنحهما زخما حين يلتقيان، وتراجعا حين يصطدمان.

ويسعفنا تحليل ابن خلدون في فهم هذه الدينامية، أي التقاء عصبية ودعوة، أو توجه محلي، برافعة أيديولوجية من مرجعية دينية. وما يبدو غير طبيعي لمحللين غربيين، هو ما اعتبره ابن خلدون بالدينامية التي كانت محركا لشمال أفريقيا، أو بلاد المغرب.

هذا التلازم بين العصبية والدعوة هو الذي يجعل منهما محركا للتاريخ، على شرط أن تكون البنية الحاكمة (أو الدولة حسب تعبير ابن خلدون ويعني بها الأسرة الحاكمة، وليس ما نفهمه نحن اليوم من الدولة)، قد اعتراها الوهن، وبلغت مرحلة الاضمحلال.

ينبغي طبعا أن ننسّب الأمور، ذلك أن تحليل ابن خلدون كان يندرج في سياق لم تكن فيه قوى أجنبية متدخلة، وقد سبق لديناميات عدة، في بلاد المغرب، أن أُجهضت بسبب تدخل قوى أجنبية، من الدول الصليبية قديما، ثم الاستعمار حديثا، والأشكال العدة لتدخلات أجنبية، في سياق ما بعد الاستعمار، إلى التدخلات الأخيرة التي عرفتها المنطقة، سواء من قبل فرنسا، أو روسيا.

وبالمقابل، يصبح التلازم ما بين العصبية والدعوة، في سياق منطقة الساحل، محركا إن حظي بغطاء دولي، وغدا رافعة لقوة أجنبية وإحدى أدواتها.

التحول الذي عرفته منطقة الساحل لا يمكن أن يختزل في “جز العشب”، وينبئ عن إعادة تشكيل جيوسياسي لمنطقة شمال أفريقيا، انطلاقا من منطقة الساحل.

طبعا لا يمكن التصدي للوضع في منطقة الساحل بمنطق “جز العشب”.

الآراء الواردة في المقال لا تعكس بالضرورة الموقف التحريري لشبكة الجزيرة.

Claude’s Response

Aourid’s piece is the project’s single most analytically distinctive piece of the week and pairs directly with Sachi Muto’s African Arguments piece on the same April 25 event. The two writers converge on the same empirical finding (the JNIM-FLA fusion is not “unnatural” but the predictable expression of a long-suppressed Tuareg dispossession question) through radically different intellectual frames: Muto through colonial-postcolonial state formation and a careful tracing of the 2012-2024 Algiers Accord failure; Aourid through Ibn Khaldun’s ‘asabiyya-da‘wa framework, which gives the convergence its native conceptual grammar rather than treating it as a Western analytical puzzle to be solved. This is exactly the cross-orientation convergence the project’s coordinate map is designed to surface.

The Ibn Khaldun anchor is the load-bearing intellectual move and should be carefully glossed for English readers in any downstream theme fragment. ‘Asabiyya (عصبية) in Khaldunian usage names the cohesive solidarity of a kin- or tribe-based group that lets it act as a coherent political unit; da‘wa (دعوة) names the call or doctrine (in Khaldun’s medieval Maghreb context, usually religious) that can graft onto a tribal solidarity and give it a legitimating ideological mission. The pairing is the engine of dynastic founding in Khaldun’s Muqaddimah — and Aourid’s claim is that the Sahel today is replaying this pairing, with the FLA supplying the ‘asabiyya (Tuareg cohesion across five states, drawing on shared language and shared trauma of dispossession) and JNIM supplying the da‘wa (transnational jihadist legitimating claim). The Khaldunian model also explains the condition under which this pairing becomes a real motor: the central state must have decayed to the point of vulnerability. Aourid is naming the Bamako junta as exactly such a decayed central authority — coup-installed, lacking legitimacy, dependent on Russian mercenaries — and reading the April 25 attack as the ‘asabiyya-da‘wa pairing claiming the opening.

What Aourid adds beyond Muto is the geopolitical-mechanism caveat: the Khaldunian pairing only becomes operative as a transformative motor when it “gains an international cover” and becomes “a lever for a foreign power and one of its instruments.” This is the piece’s most original analytical claim and the one most worth carry-forwarding. It implies that the post-April-25 Sahel trajectory will depend less on the FLA-JNIM relationship per se (which can rise and fall on local dynamics) than on whether a major external power (Russia, China, Algeria, possibly Turkey) supplies international cover for the pairing as a usable instrument against the Bamako junta or its Russian sponsors. If no such cover emerges, the Sahel insurgency stays at the “revolutionary focus” stage Aourid describes; if it does, the geopolitical reshaping he predicts becomes operative.

The piece’s main analytical limits are two. First, Aourid doesn’t engage what makes the Bamako junta specifically vulnerable in Khaldun’s sense beyond its post-coup illegitimacy and Russian dependence — there’s no engagement with whether the junta’s economic-policy or fiscal-distributional choices have created the “decay” Khaldun’s model requires. Second, the Khaldunian frame is descriptively powerful but predictively underspecified: it tells us when the dynamic is possible but not whether the specific pairing of FLA ‘asabiyya and JNIM da‘wa will hold up under the centrifugal pressures their incompatible ideological projects (Tuareg territorial autonomy vs. transnational caliphate) will eventually exert. Both Aourid and Muto correctly note this incompatibility; neither resolves the question of how long the convergence can persist if Bamako’s central authority is even partially restored.

For the project’s standing themes register, this piece + Muto’s establishes the Sahel as a thread worth formalizing. The two pieces together suggest a structural finding: when the Western counter-terror frame strips away the political grievance that initially organized a separatist movement, the movement does not disappear — it acquires a da‘wa (legitimating call) that the Western frame would have considered incompatible with its prior orientation. This is a transferable analytical move worth checking against other suppressed-separatism cases the project might track.

Anchor inline for any per-theme writer: ‘asabiyya (عصبية) = tribal/group solidarity, Ibn Khaldun’s technical term for the cohesion that lets a kin-group act as one political actor; da‘wa (دعوة) = call/doctrine, the legitimating ideological mission (usually religious) that can graft onto an ‘asabiyya. The Khaldunian dynamic of ‘asabiyya + da‘wa + decayed central state = dynastic transition is the Muqaddimah’s core model and well worth a brief gloss when the synthesis invokes it. Aourid is also a roster regular whose Khaldunian frame should be familiar to repeat readers but cannot be assumed for one-off readers.

Back to summary

In a world of blocs, France and Kenya chose the Middle

Noé Michalon and Stephen Mogaka · 2026-05-11 · Original →

Kenya’s president William Ruto and France’s Emmanuel Macron in Nairobi at the opening of the Africa Forward Summit, 11 May 2026 (Courtesy: State House, Nairobi)

Why Nairobi? A lot of ink has already been spilled to explain the choice of Kenya to host the upcoming Africa France summit, the first gathering of heads of state from the continent Emmanuel Macron is convening since his election in 2017. But a broader, more geopolitical explanation has largely been overlooked.

The origin of mzungu, the word in Kiswahili to designate Westerners, is quite an odd one: it refers to the person roaming and circling around. Over the last decade, France and its president Emmanuel Macron wamezunguka sana in Africa, with multiple trips and initiatives in several parts of the continent.

Over the past decade, most Sahelian countries severed ties with Paris and other West African capitals took some distance too. France has managed to maintain strong relationships with a couple of countries in Francophone Africa, namely Côte d’Ivoire, Gabon, Benin, Cameroon or Congo. The hosting in Nairobi of the Africa-France summit – shrewdly named Africa Forward without any mention of France – on 11th and 12th of May serves as a powerful indicator of a shift in France’s policy in Africa with a broadening towards Non-francophone countries.

To explain the choice of the Kenyan capital, various explanations emerged. It has been argued multiple times that this decline of the French influence in francophone African countries pushed Paris to find new opportunities in the English speaking sphere. This is, indeed, true: no French president has travelled beyond their country’s African sphere of influence, the so called-pré-carré, as much as Emmanuel Macron. Since his election in 2017, he has travelled to Ethiopia, Nigeria, South Africa, Angola, Kenya and Mauritius, sometimes severally – and this list is not exhaustive, nor does it include the numerous visits of these countries’ officials to Paris. To this extent, the hosting is both a confirmation and acceleration of this trend.

It has also been argued that the choice of Nairobi as the venue of the summit is a powerful signal of the shift in France’s policy towards African countries from one of aid to one of trade, investments and partnerships. The event, with its first day dedicated to the private sector, is to showcase Kenya’s vibrant economy, with a robust banking ecosystem and a dynamic tech startup scene – both sectors Macron, a former banker, particularly likes. This is also true: the number of French companies in Kenya has nearly quadrupled in a decade, reaching around 140 in 2026, while France boasts about being the fifth-largest investor in the country. The French development agency has a regional office in Nairobi and claims to have injected €2 billion since the late 1990s.

Both reasons are true: the search for a diplomatic reset from an English-speaking country without any colonial history with France, and the focus on a business and trade-first approach partially explain the choice of Nairobi to host the summit.

But, still, why Nairobi, and not Lagos or Johannesburg? After all, Nigeria and South Africa entertain stronger economic ties with France than Kenya, and Emmanuel Macron has also tried to strengthen his relationship with Presidents Bola Tinubu and Cyril Ramaphosa.

There’s more than meets the eye: William Ruto and Emmanuel Macron share more than a birthday (21st December for those who are curious) and an outsider-from-inside profile in their respective political stages. As both regional powers trying to consolidate their global position, France and Kenya have a common interest in defending various features of the current international order and architecture, including multilateralism and the rule of law. The defence of this global framework also implied, however, the admission of its flaws and the advocacy for its reform.

The French president seeks to use the Africa Forward event both as a platform for transforming Africa-France relations and a venue to support the continued push for reforms of the international order. France will be hosting the G7 summit a month later, where the Africa Forward summit and its deliberations about reforms of the international order will be highlighted by President Ruto who has been invited to the event.

Indeed, Paris keeps a strong position within the current world order, which it inherited from the Second World War: despite coming out bruised from the conflict, France’s then massive colonial empire secured her a seat among the five permanent members of the United Nation’s Security Council. While obviously a member of the Western bloc, the French foreign policy always made sure to keep its autonomy towards it – an attitude that still transpires in Paris’ current positions vis-à-vis Iran, Israel or the US administration.

Nairobi, on the other hand, has been a strong proponent for multilateralism since its independence, hosting UN headquarters – today one of the four main sites in the world – since 1972 and supporting various peacekeeping missions as well as peace mediation initiatives. During the Cold War, Kenya was one of the only allies of the Western bloc in a region dominated by leftist or Marxist regimes. This did not prevent the successive Kenyan regimes from building some links with China, the USSR and the Eastern bloc. In short, both countries tried to maintain a balance in their ties with the world’s various blocs, and the current global institutions were instrumental for them to keep this fragile equilibrium running.

This world order is rapidly changing. Bigger powers are expanding their spheres of influence, each on their own. The potential collapse of these institutions could easily weaken Kenya’s and France’s positions, which are protected by the (relative) checks and balances that prevailed with international laws, on trade, environment and sovereignty.

This is one of the reasons why presidents Ruto and Macron are among the key proponents of a sweeping reform of existing institutions, to bring more non-Western countries to the UNSC and give them more voting rights in multilateral funders. Both presidents are outspoken about multilateralism and developed a “summit diplomacy” that saw Nairobi and Paris host various global events (One Planet Summit, Africa Climate Summit, Paris Peace summit, among many others) that they tried to use to build advocacy coalitions on the global stage.

As East Africa’s main diplomatic hub hosting the world’s biggest UN headquarters, Nairobi has become a significant theatre of influence, in which France does not want to become a spectator. The Kenyan government’s recent high-level engagement with China, India, Russia, the UK and the Commonwealth – which all happened since the beginning of the year, positioned Nairobi as a strategic bargaining partner for France. Kenya has particularly positioned itself as a major regional and global convening hub including through its hosting of the first Sustainable Blue Economy Conference in 2018 and the first Africa Climate Summit in 2023.

The reformist agenda pushed by Paris and Nairobi, with a strong emphasis on combating climate change, significantly differs with the American one under the Trump administration, which has largely sidelined the UN, broke with trade rules and diplomatic practices, and started building a parallel organisation with the Board of Peace – which mostly seems dormant or little active so far. While Russia and China advocate for a reform of the UN, they also started strengthening their own sphere of influence through the BRICS+, the Shanghai Cooperation Organisation.

Exactly a year to the end of his term, it will be difficult for Emmanuel Macron to inspire a whole new vision of the Africa-France relations that can survive him, at the same time his political party will struggle to remain in power after the 2027 elections. As a consequence, his shared position on international matters with William Ruto, offers them one of the last occasions to push for a progressive reform of international institutions, in the hope to stabilise an already crumbling world order. Otherwise, the one running into circles quickly ends up spiralling down.

The post In a world of blocs, France and Kenya chose the Middle appeared first on African Arguments.

Claude’s Response

This is a smart, locally-anchored piece doing two things at once: explaining why Nairobi (not Lagos or Johannesburg) hosted the Macron summit, and naming the bloc-architecture question the summit is positioned inside. The “France and Kenya chose the middle” frame is the load-bearing analytical move — it names a third bloc-position (committed to reforming, not abandoning or replacing, the post-WWII multilateral order) distinct from both the BRICS+/SCO architecture-builders and the Trump-administration UN-sidelining trajectory. The mzungu (~“the one roaming and circling around”) and Africa Forward (without “France” in the name) etymologies are doing rhetorical work the piece earns: this is a France-out-of-its-pré-carré story written by writers who know the language and the diplomatic context.

The piece fits cleanly into the project’s bypass-network-as-post-american-architecture carry-forward opened in the 2026-05-09 synthesis. Tabatabai’s WOTR piece documented Saudi-Ukraine, UAE-Ukraine, Taiwan-Czechia-Japan-Poland security-cooperation agreements as a partner-to-partner architecture bypassing Washington as hub; the France-Kenya Africa Forward axis is a parallel architecture in a different domain — diplomatic/multilateral reform rather than defense-industrial.
