The cross-orientation convergence here is again the project’s most-prized finding: a Continental French and a Kenyan analyst, an Iranian-American Middle East scholar, a Stanford political scientist (Grzymala-Busse), and a Moroccan Maghrebi political scientist (Aourid) are all reaching for the same diagnosis from different vantage points — the post-1995 institutional order is exhausted and what’s emerging is a multi-bloc world in which the productive question is whether middle-powers can build partner-to-partner architectures fast enough to absorb the collapse.

What the piece doesn’t engage well: the harder question of whether the France-Kenya “middle” is in fact a viable third bloc-position or merely a transitional waystation. The authors note Macron has a year left, his party will struggle, and the time window is short — but they don’t seriously engage what survives Macron’s exit. Kenya’s middle-position is more sustainable (Ruto’s diplomatic strategy isn’t dependent on a French-domestic election cycle), but the France-Kenya axis the piece names is structurally fragile if either side loses domestic legitimacy. The piece treats this as a closing note rather than the central analytical problem. A reader looking for the structural-test version of the argument has to supply it themselves.

A small linguistic flag for downstream agents: the piece uses Kiswahili anchoring (mzungu, wamezunguka sana) and the French pré-carré (sphere of influence) idiom; both should be glossed inline for English-speaking readers when invoked in the per-theme fragment or synthesis.

For the project’s coordinate map, this is exactly the kind of non-Anglophone-but-English-language piece (Kenyan + Franco-Kenyan authors, anchored in Nairobi diplomatic context) the discovery layer was expanded to catch. Continues to validate the African Arguments addition.

Back to summary

A New Viral Outbreak. The Same Mistakes All Over Again.

Tufekci, Zeynep · 2026-05-12 · Original →

There’s no question that another pandemic will strike, but no one knows when or which virus will be the cause. What we can determine with pretty good clarity is how ready we’ll be, how well we’re constructing obstacles to slow the path of emerging threats and how fast we’re learning lessons from painful experience.

As the final remaining passengers disembarked from the MV Hondius cruise ship, on which at least seven people were confirmed to have been infected with hantavirus traveled, the answers are becoming increasingly clear: We’re still leaving a lot to chance, crossing our fingers and hoping for the best.

Consider the history of the Andes strain of the hantavirus. According to a paper in The New England Journal of Medicine, in 2018, a hantavirus outbreak with this strain — the same strain linked to the Hondius cruise — began in Epuyén, Argentina. It started after one infected person attended a birthday party with about 100 guests. He had a fever and was feeling tired, and he left after about an hour and a half. Five people who were in the room — but not necessarily all even sitting right next to him — later sickened.

One of those five partygoers most likely went on to infect six more people, including his spouse, and died 16 days after he became ill. During his wake, 10 more people became infected, from the spouse. It was about then that the public health authorities, realizing how dangerous the situation was, started enforcing strict quarantine measures. That seems to be how it finally died out.

Yet in recent days, the World Health Organization has reassured the public that hantavirus can be transmitted only through “close and prolonged contact” and that, as a result, it is unlikely to spread widely among the population at large? “The one thing with this one is that it’s much harder to catch,” President Trump said on Monday, echoing W.H.O. and U.S. public health officials. “It seems like it is not easy to spread.”

We know fairly little about the Andes strain of the hantavirus, with an estimated 3,000 human cases over three decades. How could that assertion about its not being easy to spread be true given what we know about the 2018 superspreading event?

I reached out to Gustavo Palacios, the senior author of the study about the Epuyén outbreak. He seemed as baffled by these pronouncements as I was. He told me that the paper he and his fellow researchers wrote used the phrase prolonged or close contact but he explained that, as they had written in their article, they didn’t mean solely physical or bodily contact. He told me that they believed that the virus spread via respiratory secretions. Looking at the same study, an airborne transmission expert, Linsey Marr, told CBC/Radio Canada that “it’s strongly suggestive that airborne transmission is happening.”

Dr. Palacios also said that he and his co-authors had calculated the median reproduction number of the Andes virus to be 2.1 — meaning that one sick person infected about two other people. That’s more than enough for sustained human transmission. That reproduction number is not much lower than the initial strain of SARS-CoV-2, the virus that causes Covid-19, as calculated in February and March of 2020, by the way, so I’m not feeling great about the reassurances from health officials that this will not become a pandemic. How do they know?

Dr. Palacios was also worried about the differences between the previous Andes strain outbreak setting and the current one. Containing an outbreak in a tiny isolated rural village in Patagonia, Argentina, during the dry season is a different prospect than containing one on a cruise ship with ocean humidity conditions or with people traveling onward in planes.

At the same time, authorities keep insisting that only symptomatic people can spread the virus. In Dr. Palacios’s study, the transmission events that the researchers could trace had indeed occurred while people were displaying symptoms. But he’s also said that 48 hours before the onset of symptoms should be considered a high-risk period as well. He told me that people’s viral loads rise before symptoms break out, so it’s reasonable to assume there is some risk earlier. Besides, with a single study done after the fact, he and his team hadn’t been able to pinpoint every exact moment that a person passed the virus to another — many unknowns remained from that outbreak.

The last twist was that his paper shows that the incubation period (the time between virus exposure and symptoms) can be as long as 40 days. Some people get sick more than a month after being exposed, which is an unusually long stretch of time. That’s a big deal because it makes managing the outbreak much more challenging.

On April 25, a Dutch cruise passenger took a flight from St. Helena to South Africa while ill, collapsed at the airport after arrival and died soon after. While W.H.O. officials have claimed the risk of spread during the flight or on the ship was low, that incident was just 17 days ago — if incubation can be as long as 40 days, there are 23 days before we’ll know if all her contacts are in the clear. As of Monday, South Africa’s health minister said that the authorities had identified 97 possible contacts in the country exposed to the hantavirus and that 90 of them had been reached and advised that they were being monitored. Based on South Africa’s guidelines, this meant asking people to perform daily temperature and symptom checks and to contact authorities immediately if they get sick. It’s unclear if everyone on the plane has been reached, and we can only hope this is sufficient.

Meanwhile, photographs of crew members while still on the ship show many of them hanging around together in a hallway while waiting to be interviewed by health authorities, covering their mouths and noses with only flimsy masks. In photos that have circulated, one person who had just left the ship can be seen on a bus, still clad in protective gear, but having removed their mask, seen dangling over one ear.

After the Covid pandemic, after the 2002 SARS epidemic, after the Epuyén hantavirus outbreak, we really have learned too little. One key lesson from both SARS and Covid was how much superspreading can play a role. Early on, many infected people spread the virus to few people, which generated comforting statistics on average. But when the circumstances aligned, it turned out that a single person could infect a large number of people all at once, setting off chains of transmission that were difficult to control.

We still don’t fully understand why some people superspread and others do not. But if it can happen once, as it did in Epuyén, it can happen again.

During a press conference last week, a W.H.O. official addressed people who had disembarked, asking them to present themselves to health care authorities if they were developing symptoms. W.H.O. officials also kept defining transmission as happening through close prolonged contact — intimate partners, household members. Encouragingly, over the weekend, the W.H.O. published new technical documents to clarify its definition of the type of contact that could cause the spread of hantavirus. It now includes “close proximity exposure” as well as “exposure in enclosed or shared spaces.”

But even these definitions still suffer from a lack of learning from the Covid experience, such as limiting exposure to being within about six feet for a cumulative period of more than 15 minutes. We know from the study of airborne transmission that that guidance may be too rigid and fail to capture the full risk profile of the virus. The Epuyén outbreak doesn’t seem to fit that framework. Still, I would say this is better than nothing, and much better than how slow things were in 2020 and onward. But these guideline changes were done too quietly.

How are people who may have been exposed supposed to protect themselves if they are not told of the modes of transmission and the stakes accurately and loudly, including when the definitions evolve? Public health officials, from the W.H.O. to U.S. officials, would be more helpful if they stopped constantly reassuring people about the likelihood of future events they can’t accurately calculate — like the odds of a pandemic occurring or how long this outbreak could last — and just told us more details about the things that matter: mode of transmission, lengthy period of incubation and the inevitable uncertainty of something for which there is little actual knowledge.

If we’re lucky, this hantavirus outbreak will peter out, or resemble the 2002 SARS outbreak: It dies out with the help of safety measures and because the virus doesn’t adapt fast enough. If we are unlucky? It should be unthinkable, but here we are. And this time Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. will be in charge of the U.S. response.

Claude’s Response

This is Tufekci at her recognizable strongest — institutional-failure reporting grounded in source-shoe-leather. The methodological move is the load-bearing one: she doesn’t argue with the WHO from her own authority, she gets the senior author of the paper the WHO is citing on the phone, and the senior author tells her the WHO is misreading his paper. That kind of citation auditing is rare in commentary and is the column’s structural strength. The Marr quote (“strongly suggestive of airborne transmission”) adds an independent expert voice, and the R₀ comparison to early COVID is dispositive — an Andes-strain R₀ of 2.1 is in the same range as early SARS-CoV-2, and the WHO’s reassurance language is structurally identical to the 2020 reassurance language Tufekci was right about and they were wrong about.

The Epuyén outbreak case is the kind of evidence that survives mode-shift: one person at a 100-guest party infecting five non-adjacent attendees in ninety minutes is hard to explain on a “close prolonged contact” model and easy to explain on an aerosol-respiratory model. The 40-day incubation period is the most important under-noticed fact in the piece — it means the standard 14-21-day contact-tracing window misses many cases, which has direct policy implications the WHO has not yet addressed.

The piece’s two limitations are worth flagging. First, Tufekci’s structural argument — that officials are repeating early-COVID reassurance errors — is correct as a pattern-recognition claim, but she does not engage seriously with the asymmetric-error problem from the other direction: early-pandemic warning fatigue, the political cost of premature alarm to public-health credibility (which Lileks at NR is pressing the opposite side of), and the question of how an Alex-Jones-saturated information environment metabolizes premature alarm relative to premature reassurance. She gets the empirical-uncertainty case right but glides past the meta-question of how public-health communication should work in a low-trust environment, which is the harder question. Second, the RFK Jr. closer is doing too much work — it’s a rhetorical move that lets her drop a political fact (he is the Health Secretary) without engaging with what that fact would actually mean for the response (vaccine policy, FDA authorization, CDC messaging). The sentence is correct but underdeveloped, and a fuller piece would have spent a paragraph on the specific decision points where RFK Jr.’s posture would matter — that’s where the column’s policy bite lives.

For project pairing: this is the cleanest available counterweight to Lileks’s NR “Hantapanic” piece (which argues from absence-of-evidence; Tufekci argues from misread-evidence), and the strongest evidence case in the cluster. Together with Yglesias on the same outbreak, the three pieces are the project’s best-available cross-orientation read on whether the public-health community has learned anything from COVID. Tufekci has the strongest source case; Lileks has the strongest meta-case about communication asymmetries; Yglesias is the middle position. The synthesis composer should treat this as a live disagreement worth surfacing, not a settled one.

Back to summary

The Bizarre Theory Behind RFK’s Attack on Vaccines

Riley, Alexander · 2026-05-14 · Original →

Asked about the hantavirus cruise ship outbreak this week, Secretary of Health and Human Services Robert F. Kennedy Jr. expressed confidence about facing down the threat: “We have this under control and we’re not worried about it.” Unfortunately, his track record on infectious disease should give one no confidence in his confidence.

We have had a bit more than a year now of a Department of Health and Human Services headed by RFK, Jr. The evidence is growing by the day that this was a disastrous appointment. Kennedy has dropped recommendations for several children’s vaccines (hepatitis A, meningitis, rotavirus and the flu), and children’s vaccination rates are plummeting to levels below those needed to prevent transmission of a number of serious childhood illnesses that were previously well under control or nearly eradicated. Research on mRNA vaccines has been the target of a cancellation effort by Kennedy’s HHS, which will handicap our ability to respond to new viral threats.

What is even more concerning than Kennedy’s well-known anti-vaccine agenda is his profound scientific ignorance. When making the case against his nomination, the vaccinologist Paul Offit alleged that Kennedy rejected the germ theory of infectious disease, a cornerstone of modern medical science. We can verify this charge with reference to Kennedy’s 2021 book, The Real Anthony Fauci. It is too late now for the discrediting material found there to prevent his ascent to the top health post in the country, but it is worth examining it in detail, just to be aware of the dangers still posed by Kennedy’s appointment.

In The Real Anthony Fauci, Kennedy contrasts two theories about the cause of disease: The germ theory and the miasma theory. The ensuing discussion suggests that he does not understand either of them well. The miasma theory, Kennedy writes, “emphasizes preventing disease by fortifying the immune system through nutrition and by reducing exposures to environmental toxins and stresses”; germ theory, by contrast, “h[olds] that germs alone cause…disease.”

Kennedy’s description of miasma theory is inaccurate. This disproven view, which posited that people got ill because of “bad air” or vapor emitted by rotting organic matter, emerged in the mid-nineteenth century. Proponents observed, for instance, that cholera seemed concentrated in areas where foul-smelling sewage water was present; but when sanitation systems were improved and foul odors reduced, cholera rates went down. Miasma theory did not posit that something carried by the air (microbes) caused disease, but the air itself.

Then, in the 1880s, Robert Koch identified cholera bacteria. It had already been demonstrated that microscopic organisms in the air caused putrefaction, and that they could provoke infection if they were introduced into open wounds by hands bearing the microbes. Germ theory—the idea that disease is caused by certain microbes—was soon confirmed experimentally, and miasma theory was tossed into the mountainous scrap heap of disproven scientific theories.

It is not clear why Kennedy juxtaposes an obviously incorrect theory, completely rejected in the contemporary scientific community, to germ theory and suggests there is something to be learned from both. He does not even seem to know that the definition he provides for miasma theory does not fit that theory historically. What he describes as miasma theory actually fits another theory, conceived by Antoine Béchamp, a scientific contemporary and competitor of Louis Pasteur, himself one of the central figures in the consolidation of scientific consensus around germ theory. Béchamp’s effort at explaining the cause of disease is known as terrain theory.

Terrain theory is not as thoroughly discredited as miasma theory. It argues that susceptibility to disease comes primarily from the state of the individual organism—the “terrain.” Greater overall health protects an individual from disease, and disease strikes individuals who are already in a poor state of health. Germ theory is not in contradiction with the ideas, advanced by terrain theory, that not all microbes produce disease—indeed, only a very small minority do, even potentially—and that even those microbes that do so do not make all individuals ill. Kennedy labors mightily to make it seem that germ theory denies or overlooks any role of the dietary and health robustness of individuals in protection against disease—another mischaracterization.

Although elements of terrain theory can be salvaged, Béchamp went radically wrong in a number of ways. He posited the existence of “microzymes” in human cells that were triggered into action by unhealthy “terrain” conditions such as a bad diet or pollutants in the environment. This, he posited, was the actual cause of disease. But there is no evidence of the existence of microzymes.

The presentation of what Kennedy mislabels miasma theory suffers from some of the same problems as Béchamp’s theory. He offers his readers the analogy of an apple in explaining the causal operation of miasma theory in producing disease. If the apple’s skin is not pierced, it can remain edible for a long time. If the skin is punctured, however, this “triggers systemic rot…as the billions of opportunistic microbes…colonize the injured terrain.” But is it the injury to the terrain, or the microbes, that cause the disease? Kennedy follows the apple analogy with a contrast to the germ-theory view that “microscopic pathogens” cause disease. The implication appears to be that it is not the microbes that are doing the important work, but the injury.

Kennedy pushes still farther in this direction, arguing it is “malnutrition and inadequate access to clean water” that cause infectious disease in poorer parts of the world, and not pathogens, “When a starving African child succumbs to measles,” he writes, “the miasmist attributes the death to malnutrition; germ theory proponents…blame the virus.” Therefore, he goes on, “the miasmist approach to public health is to boost individual immune response.” No germ-theory proponent denies that malnutrition weakens the immune system, which makes underfed people more susceptible to a host of diseases. But when a starving child gets measles, the cough, the fever, and the rash accompanying that disease are caused by the measles virus. Likewise for subsequent complications that often follow for those infected with measles, such as pneumonia, diarrhea, and encephalitis. It is straightforwardly incorrect for a “miasmist” to claim that addressing hunger will cure the patient. Well-nourished children, especially those who are unvaccinated, get measles too, and some of them get very sick from it, and some of them even die from it.

Even as he tries to give the impression that he is just describing two competing theories, Kennedy makes clear that he is a partisan of what he calls miasma theory, which is in fact a version of terrain theory. When he describes the consolidation of a scientific consensus in favor of germ theory, he doesn’t present it as a clear victory for evidence and truth, writing: “For better or worse, the champions of germ theory…proved victorious.” He then suggests germ theory might have won out over miasma theory only because it “mimick[ed]…demon possession” in its mode of explanation, and this played well to the prejudices of the public at the time. Germ theory’s proponents are “domineering,” “imperialist,” and “militarized,” in Kennedy’s reading.

It is here that Kennedy’s views on vaccines come into the picture. Germ theory, he claims, mandates introducing “poison”—that is, a vaccine—into the body to kill pathogens. But a poison is a chemical agent that is harmful or lethal to living organisms, and vaccines do not directly kill virions. Typically, what they do is to imitate them, though in a weakened form or, in the case of the mRNA Covid vaccines, they temporarily program the individual’s own cells to make copies of a protein in the virus, so that the human immune system can learn to recognize them and successfully attack them when they are subsequently encountered in the body. Once we recognize this, Kennedy’s contrast between germ theory and miasma theory collapses, because normal human immune response absent vaccines acts in precisely the same manner.

Louis Pasteur was a central proponent of germ theory, but Kennedy leads his readers to believe Pasteur later recanted and acknowledged the superiority of terrain theory. “The microbe is nothing, the terrain is everything,” Kennedy tells us Pasteur said on his deathbed. But Pasteur’s grandson and biographer, Louis Pasteur Vallery-Radot, put paid to this lie in the Bulletin de l’Académie nationale de medicine in 1946, describing the phrase attributed to his grandfather as the “pure invention of [a famous novelist].” He further emphasized that that even though Pasteur recognized the contribution of “the terrain,” he also understood the ultimate causal origin of disease in pathogens. Kennedy’s source for the Pasteur misattribution is, incidentally, a self-published book-length rant by a German journalist titled Virus Mania: How the Medical Industry Continually Invents Epidemics, Making Billion-Dollar Profits at Our Expense.

Kennedy concludes by claiming he has refuted the “central dogma” of germ theory and provided “stark support for miasma’s approach to medicine” with a series of graphs showing mortality rates for various infectious diseases declining alongside progress in nutrition and sanitation. Vaccines, he claims, had much smaller impacts on declines in diseases including influenza, measles, and polio. But no proponent of germ theory denies improved nutrition and sanitation have massively contributed to health, and nothing in its explanation of disease is challenged by that fact. Indeed, the case for vaccination has to do with strengthening immune response, and improved nutrition also has a role to play. Cleaner water and improved sanitation likewise contribute to the prevention of disease precisely by making it less likely that people will come in contact with the microbes in water and human waste that produce illness.

In the closing passage of this section of his book, Kennedy endeavors to make it seem he is being even-handed and recognizing “the best” in both germ and miasma theory, but the overall tenor of these pages is unmistakable. He is hostile to the germ theory’s explanatory logic and he apparently believes that public health interventions based on it—such as antibiotics and vaccines—are at best minimally effective and at worst harmful. The pejorative and factually incorrect language he uses to describe how germ theory informs public health—the supposed introduction of “poisons” into the body, the ostensible total rejection of environmental and nutritional factors—is designed to turn the reader against the scientific consensus.

How did we arrive at a point where someone as ignorant of basic science as RFK Jr. was able to occupy the top public health post in the country? The understandable frustration of parts of the American public over the significant missteps of public health authorities on guidance during the pandemic—this despite the fact that the lightning-fast production and performance of the mRNA vaccines is one of the most remarkable public health successes in recent decades—spiked skepticism of medical authorities in many segments of the society. It is not clear in the short term how to restore enough confidence from the American public to, for instance, get us back to the vaccination rates we need for mass protection. One thing is clear, though. The longer Kennedy is in a key decision-making position on public health, the worse things are going to get.

Alexander Riley is a professor of sociology at Bucknell University.

Claude’s Response

This is a textbook example of the disciplined-empirical critique register that Compact occasionally publishes when it lets non-house-style writers in. Riley is a sociology professor (not a virologist or epidemiologist), which gives the piece a useful structural property: he’s not defending the medical establishment from a position of professional self-interest, and he’s engaging the philosophical-historical substrate of Kennedy’s argument rather than just citing the CDC. The miasma-vs-terrain-theory distinction is the analytically load-bearing move and Riley makes it carefully — the fact that Kennedy mislabels his own preferred theory is genuinely diagnostic of an underlying confusion, not just a vocabulary slip.

Strengths: the piece’s strongest single move is the Pasteur-deathbed-quote tracing, which Riley sources to a 1946 Académie nationale de médecine bulletin and identifies as a fabrication from a self-published German conspiracy book (Virus Mania). This is the kind of citation-trail forensic work that makes the conspiracy-genealogy visible. Riley also handles the apple-rot analogy carefully — he doesn’t strawman Kennedy by ignoring it, he engages with what the analogy is actually trying to do (locate causation in the injured terrain rather than in the microbes) and shows why even on its own terms it doesn’t work (the microbes still do the disease-producing work; injury just provides the entry point). The piece is also honest about the prior public-health failures (it explicitly names the pandemic missteps as the political-economy explanation for Kennedy’s confirmability) rather than treating institutional credibility as if it were never damaged.

Weaknesses: the piece is structurally a long takedown of one chapter of a 2021 book by a now-cabinet-secretary, which means it underweights the institutional-political question — what is the policy mechanism by which Kennedy’s theoretical confusion translates into the specific 2026 outcomes Riley names (the hepatitis-A/meningitis/rotavirus/flu rollbacks, the mRNA research cancellation, the plummeting vaccination rates)? Riley names the outcomes but doesn’t trace the bureaucratic path. The piece also stops short of the harder political-economy question: what would actually restore vaccine confidence, given that the pandemic-era public-health authority collapse Riley acknowledges was a real institutional failure, not just paranoid misinterpretation? “It is not clear in the short term how to restore enough confidence” is honest but is also where the most interesting analysis would begin.

For the project: this is a clean piece of evidence-based critique on a topic where Compact’s house style sometimes drifts toward giving heterodox public-health views more credit than the empirical record supports. The choice to commission Riley — a Compact-aligned sociologist who is nonetheless prepared to call Kennedy’s framework “obviously incorrect” — is a useful institutional signal about Compact’s editorial range. The piece pairs naturally with any roster coverage of the 2026 public-health story (childhood-vaccination-rate collapse, hantavirus outbreak response) and supplies the theoretical-substrate critique most other writers won’t do.

Back to summary

This Company Was an American Success Story. Until MAHA Influencers Sank It.

The Free Press · 2026-05-14 · Original →

This Company Was an American Success Story. Until MAHA Influencers Sank It.

All it took was two Facebook posts to turn an online mob against Apeel Sciences and its booming business of keeping food fresh longer. James Rogers, the former CEO of Apeel Sciences, is seen at its headquarters in Santa Barbara, California. (Yuri Hasegawa/Redux)

The eureka moment for James Rogers arrived while driving past some California farmland in 2011. He was a doctoral student at the University of California, Santa Barbara, focusing on world hunger, and the drive got him thinking about a huge problem with fresh produce. Growing it wasn’t the problem. Keeping it fresh was.

Working out of his garage, Rogers devised a fix. He used materials found in the skins, peels, and seeds of fruits and vegetables—especially grapes—to create a protective covering that slowed down the water loss and oxidation that cause fresh produce to spoil after being harvested. The early results won him a $100,000 grant in 2012 from the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation. Rogers soon hired two classmates as employees and launched a company called Apeel Sciences.

What happened over the next decade was an only-in-America triumph of entrepreneurial ingenuity and hard-earned recognition among consumers and celebrities alike. Time hailed Apeel as one of its “Genius Companies” in 2018 for keeping fruits and vegetables fresh “two to three times longer.” Apeel-coated apples, avocados, lemons, limes, mangoes, and oranges were sold in thousands of supermarkets across the United States, from Kroger to Costco to Trader Joe’s. Germany’s largest supermarket chain, Edeka, found that spoilage of imported avocados from Chile and Peru fell 50 percent—and sales jumped 20 percent. Apeel raised over $640 million from huge investment firms. Celebrities like Oprah Winfrey and Katy Perry also invested in the company. An avocado is shown with and without Apeel after 10 days. (Apeel Sciences)

“We got through seven years of demonstrating its value to retailers,” Rogers told me. Apeel-coated cucumbers were about to become the next big thing. Walmart had begun selling some that no longer had to be cocooned in plastic shrink-wrap to stay fresh.

And then came April 10, 2023. On that day, at exactly 10:21 a.m. ET, two Facebook posts falsely claimed that Apeel’s product “can’t be washed off” and warned viewers not to “eat anything with the Apeel sticker on it.” The posts linked to the ingredients of a floor cleaner also called Apeel but made by a UK company. Within hours, the posts ricocheted across Facebook, Instagram, TikTok, YouTube, Telegram, Rumble, and Reddit.

The posts ignored the fact that the Food and Drug Administration and the European Food Safety Authority had separately evaluated Apeel, and found no safety concerns. Or that most of the produce sold in stores is coated with a thin layer of wax. This was an online mob, wielding disinformation and threats. And no one could stop it.

Soon after those first two Facebook posts, which came from Canada, similarly baseless claims were repeated by Make America Healthy Again (MAHA) influencers, including some who saw the worst in Apeel’s connection to Bill Gates. For many in the MAHA movement, Gates is a bogeyman, often accused of using his wealth to unduly influence global health policies, favoring pharmaceutical interests over public well-being, and even controlling the food supply. MAHA influencers took issue with Bill Gates’s past involvement with Apeel. (Patrick van Katwijk/Getty Images)

Typical of the online attacks was a 1,000-word post by Krysten Dornik, who runs a Substack newsletter called Krysten’s Kitchen, with the headline: “The Dangerous Chemical Bill Gates Is Coating Your Organic Produce With.” Bots helped the posts mushroom.

“It caught us totally off guard,” said Harold S. Edwards, the CEO of Limoneira, one of the largest avocado and lemon producers in the U.S. The 130-year-old company had just signed a licensing agreement with Apeel, which Edwards hoped would help his business. “I’m lucky if 30 percent of what we sell doesn’t rot,” he told me. Instead, he became a target, too.

His email and cellphone number were published online. A voicemail from a male caller warned: “If you are going to poison us with Apeel, you better watch yourself.” Edwards also started getting messages from Limoneira’s retail customers, who said they were being hounded by followers of influencers who had published their names and phone numbers and were urging consumers to boycott their stores. Costco and other food retailers told Limoneira they would no longer buy its produce unless the company could guarantee there was no Apeel on it.

“It doesn’t take a lot,” said one purchaser at a large grocery chain in the U.S. “No one wants the risk of losing sales. It’s easier to abandon Apeel than it is to defend them.”

After Costco, Albertsons, Kroger, and other supermarket chains buckled under the pressure, Limoneira decided to back out of its agreement with Apeel before using it on a single avocado or lemon.

This was an online mob, wielding disinformation and threats. And no one could stop it.

By the fall of 2023, retailers stopped asking Calavo Growers, a large producer of avocados and tomatoes, to use Apeel’s coating, said Mike Browne, a former executive vice president of sales and operations at Calavo. He told me that he never questioned the “wholesomeness” of Apeel but realized that its obvious benefits were nothing compared with the power of people “saying really nasty things.” His conclusion: “Social media killed Apeel.” It isn’t surprising anymore that influencers can ruin companies with TikTok posts, podcasts, and blog posts based largely or even entirely on conspiracy theories, conjecture, vibes, and feelings. But no one I spoke to could recall another example where the destruction was so swift or complete.

At Apeel’s peak, 60 percent of the avocados sold in American grocery stores were coated with Apeel. The number is now zero. Almost all of Apeel’s revenue in the U.S. has disappeared, and the company was forced to lay off most of its employees. Rogers was replaced as CEO in 2024.

“I love the product. It’s a pity it’s not out there,” said Debora Langston, a consultant who spent months testing Apeel for Limoneira, comparing it with the wax coating used for decades. Friends in the food industry warned that Apeel was dangerous and could cause cancer. She expected poor results but reached the opposite conclusion. Her customers in Europe still use Apeel. Scientists from Apeel Sciences point out three of the most prolific molds that attack and shorten the lifespan of berries, avocados, and citrus at the company’s lab. (Mathew Scott/The New York Times via Redux)

There was nothing Apeel could do to restore the confidence of consumers who fell for the lies and spin—or win back skittish supermarkets. What it could do was try to track down those responsible for bringing Apeel down—and make them pay.

This article is featured in Tech and Business. Sign up here to get an update every time a new piece is published.

Robyn Openshaw, a wellness influencer who markets herself as GreenSmoothieGirl, published over 60 anti-Apeel posts on social media and her own website from July 2023 to May 2025. She wrote that Apeel’s coating was made with heavy metals and solvents, including chemicals found in gasoline, and encouraged her “Green Smoothie Girl Army” to protest by phone and email and in person.

In June 2024, Openshaw called Edwards, the Limoneira CEO, on his personal cellphone, asking him if the company was selling Apeel-coated products. He replied by text: “Hi Robyn, We tested Apeel but never sold any products (avocados or lemons) with Apeel on them. We stopped when the market told us it doesn’t want Apeel.” She published a blog post with the headline: “GreenSmoothieGirl Gets Apeel Shut Down at Billion-Dollar Produce Company.” A video on YouTube boasted that she got Limoneira to “quit using Apeel products.”

Openshaw also reached out to executives at Costco. Produce suppliers to Costco told me that its instructions to stop using Apeel came after Openshaw and her followers inundated Costco with messages and phone calls. When I asked Openshaw about this, she said Costco told her that the company stopped using Apeel because it was too expensive.

The anti-Apeel posts drove traffic to Openshaw’s website, where she sells nutrition supplements and food products. She also found a way to profit directly from her opposition to the company: a “genius guide” listing stores that did and didn’t carry Apeel-coated produce, selling for as much as $19.97. Michelle Pfeiffer attacked Apeel on social media but later apologized. (Samir Hussein/WireImage via Getty Images)

In July 2025, the actress Michelle Pfeiffer joined the chorus of influencers posting about Apeel. “Organic produce is no longer safe,” she wrote. “Bill Gates’s Apeel just got approved for USDA-certified organic.” The approval, she said, was “very concerning.” Pfeiffer’s post credited My Health Forward, another frequent Apeel critic.

Later that month, Marlin Stutzman, a Republican congressman from Indiana, proposed the “Apeel Reveal Act of 2025” to require disclosure of certain product coatings used on fruit and vegetables. “The red flags went up for me when I saw that the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation was behind the development of this product,” Stutzman, a MAHA-friendly farmer, said in a Facebook post. He added in a post on X: “If there is a chemical on our foods in American grocery stores and Bill Gates is associated—COUNT ME OUT.” The congressman said Apeel was using a “mystery coating.”

Almost all of Apeel’s revenue in the U.S. has disappeared, and the company was forced to lay off most of its employees.

But there was no mystery at all about the product, known as Edipeel. It is made from monoglycerides and diglycerides, which are extracted from grape seed, its only active ingredient. Edipeel is distributed as a powder, mixed with water, and applied to foods before packaging. The manufacturing process uses no solvents.

The only heavy metals found in Apeel-coated fruits and vegetables are trace amounts that occur naturally and are common in all crops due to absorption from surrounding soil. Apeel uses heat and vacuum to purify the plant oils, reducing the levels of those naturally occurring heavy metals.

Apeel decided to contact Pfeiffer. Twenty days after her anti-Apeel post on Instagram, she apologized in a new post. “Ugh! For any of you who reposted or shared my story about Apeel, it turns out that I unintentionally posted inaccurate and outdated information, and I’m very sorry for that,” she wrote. She acknowledged that Apeel’s organic-certified product was first approved in 2017 and never sold commercially. She also said that Gates had nothing to do with Apeel after the Gates Foundation made a second grant to the company in 2015.

Apeel’s business in Europe was still thriving because the kind of attacks allowed against the company in the U.S. by Facebook, Instagram, X, and other social media platforms are strictly prohibited in Europe. There, social media companies risk steep fines if they fail to remove illegal posts once they are notified.

Edeka, the German supermarket chain, sells avocados, lemons, mandarins, and oranges that are coated with Apeel. Apeel’s European customers also include the British giant Tesco and stores in Denmark, the Netherlands, and Norway. The European Union has confirmed the safety of Apeel, but it isn’t permitted on products with edible skins. An Edeka spokeswoman said it is trying to change that, which would cause “the potential impact of reducing food waste” to be “many times greater.”

Last August, Apeel sued Openshaw, who became a wellness influencer after appearing in the reality television series Wife Swap. In addition to accusing Openshaw and her company of defamation, a legal long shot, Apeel alleged violations of the Lanham Act, a 1946 law that prohibits false advertising. Companies can use the law to challenge the veracity of ad claims made in the media.

Apeel blamed Openshaw’s statements and direct outreach to retailers and produce suppliers for causing the loss of deals worth at least $28.1 million that Apeel hoped to finalize with Costco, berry supplier Driscoll’s, and Limoneira. Apeel claimed that over $60 million in expected revenue vanished after she mobilized the “Green Smoothie Girl Army” to “be the resistance” and show up at stores.

The firm that Apeel hired, Clare Locke, is one of those that represented Dominion Voting Systems in its lawsuit against Fox Corp. Dominion accused Fox News of knowingly broadcasting false claims that Dominion’s voting machines were involved in a conspiracy to rig the 2020 presidential election. Fox settled the case for $787.5 million in 2023.

Alexandra Roberts, a professor of law and media at Northeastern University, said there are few examples of the Lanham Act being used to hold influencers accountable for what they post and say online. Roberts thinks that they should be legally responsible, especially if they make money from their content.

“Advertising laws should apply to influencers,” she said. “Otherwise, they don’t feel obliged to make truthful representations. And people trust what influencers say as fact, especially in health and wellness marketing.”

Openshaw could have said that Apeel “ruins the taste of my fruit,” which likely would have averted any legal peril, Roberts said. Instead, “she made claims like it’s ‘toxic’ or ‘poisonous,’ and that becomes a representation of fact.” It was even more legally dangerous to encourage followers to buy her “genius guide,” which brought in money from “provably false claims.”

Fearing similar lawsuits, other Apeel critics admitted they were wrong or pulled their content down. Alison Steinberg, a former One America News Network host who now works for LindellTV, posted an apology for describing Apeel on air as “literal poison.” In June 2024, she said it was made with “hazardous chemicals . . . known to seriously damage internal organs.”

Openshaw’s lawyers have argued in court filings that her statements were “opinions and commentary “that consist of “loose, figurative, or hyperbolic language that no reader would believe presented facts.”

When I spoke to her last month, she put it differently. “Apeel needed someone to blame,” she said. Several other influencers who railed against Apeel had far more followers than she did, she added, but they had taken down their inflammatory posts by the time she was sued.

But what about all the things she said? In early April, Openshaw told me that she still believes Apeel contains “synthetic preservatives” and is made with toxic chemicals. “I will not make a hostage video admitting I was wrong,” she said defiantly. “I am just a pawn and a scapegoat on which to blame all their failings.” But two weeks ago, Openshaw posted a 53-second clip that in fact looked a lot like a hostage video. Reading from a prepared statement, she said this about the trashing of Apeel: “Quite a bit of it is not true.” Without referring directly to her own anti-Apeel posts, she added: “Influencers and others appear to be holding Apeel responsible for all the ills of the food world, and that is not fair.”

Luiz Beling, Apeel’s CEO, told me that the suit was settled, with the agreement calling for Openshaw to apologize in videos on Instagram, X, and YouTube, and pay an undisclosed amount. Her company’s insurer recommended and paid for the law firm that represented her in the lawsuit.

Beling believes the settlement might give Apeel another chance in the U.S. Since he arrived, he has overseen new formulations that can be shipped globally and sprayed onto fruits and vegetables. The product’s cost has declined sharply and is in line with food-grade wax. Instead of supermarkets, Apeel is trying to sell to growers, and it managed to persuade most of its former retail customers to lift their restrictions prohibiting Apeel. The legislation targeting Apeel has gone nowhere. Still, retailers said they doubt Apeel will ever make it back into American grocery stores.

Apeel still doesn’t know who was behind those first two Facebook posts that sank the company. Some people in the industry speculate that makers of food-grade wax and other traditional food coatings are to blame. “They charge the packing houses millions a year and are extremely protective of their business,” said Langston, the consultant who tested Apeel for Limoneira. Openshaw said that she and other influencers she knows “were never paid” for their posts, and “none of us coordinated with each other.”

Beling said Apeel has gotten smarter about using artificial intelligence to follow everything about the company that pops up online. The attacks haven’t ended. “We are trying to figure it out,” he told me. “There is still someone out there trying to get us.” The Free Press is for free thinkers. Send this to someone who values open debate and curiosity.

Claude’s Response

This is sustained-reporting investigative work — the kind Free Press does sporadically and does well — and it lands a genuinely important argument: the wellness-influencer / MAHA disinformation economy now has the demonstrated capacity to destroy a regulator-approved, science-defensible business in roughly eighteen months, and the only legal counter-weapon that survived contact with the courts is the 1946 Lanham Act false-advertising statute, weaponized against influencers who monetize false claims (Openshaw’s $19.97 “genius guide” to which stores carried Apeel was load-bearing for the suit). That is a load-bearing finding for the broader RFK/MAHA discourse this week. It pairs directly with Alexander Riley’s Compact piece on the “bizarre theory” behind RFK’s anti-vaccine position (also in this week’s roundup) and with the Atlantic’s Tom Bartlett piece from the prior week documenting MAHA’s quiet political eviction from federal health policy (Casey Means withdrawn as Surgeon General nominee, replaced by mainstream radiologist Nicole Saphier). The three pieces together describe a movement that has lost institutional leverage at the federal level but retains a destructive private-sector veto via influencer mobs — which is arguably the worse outcome, because federal political contestation is at least subject to electoral pushback while a Costco purchasing decision driven by an inundation of GreenSmoothieGirl emails is not.

The reporting is methodical and the sourcing is multi-sided: Apeel CEOs past (Rogers) and present (Luiz Beling), Limoneira CEO Harold Edwards, Calavo’s Mike Browne, a Costco supplier who speaks anonymously, the consultant Debora Langston who actually ran the comparison tests, and Openshaw herself in two separate interviews (the second of which the piece dramatizes as a moment when she pivots from defiance to settlement). The Pfeiffer redemption arc — apology twenty days after the Apeel team contacted her, acknowledgement that Gates exited involvement after a 2015 grant — is the piece’s clearest demonstration that the disinformation is correctable when contested in good faith. The Stutzman “Apeel Reveal Act” detail (a sitting congressman legislating off “red flags went up for me when I saw the Gates Foundation”) is the piece’s clearest demonstration that even MAHA-friendly elected officials are operating on the same vibes-and-bogeymen substrate as the wellness-influencer base, which complicates any clean influencer-versus-establishment framing.

The piece’s weakness is at the edges. It treats the FDA and EFSA regulatory determinations as effectively dispositive of safety — which is the conventional administrative-state position, but the piece never engages why an audience predisposed to distrust those bodies (e.g., post-Covid trust collapse, the documented capture of FDA advisory committees by regulated industries, the EFSA’s own history of opacity on glyphosate) would find them dispositive either. The honest version of the argument would concede that the Apeel product genuinely is opaque to consumers in the sense that there’s no on-package disclosure, that “monoglycerides and diglycerides extracted from grape seed” is informationally not much better than “mystery coating” for a layperson reading a fruit sticker, and that the Stutzman bill’s disclosure requirement is therefore not crazy on its merits even if its champions are operating on bad chemistry. The piece’s framing — sober regulators and Bill Gates philanthropists on one side, dumb conspiratorial mob on the other — works as media criticism but undersells the real epistemic problem that the food system asks consumers to trust institutional verification they have plausible reasons to distrust. Still, on the narrow question of whether Apeel was destroyed by lies — yes, and the documentation here is solid — the piece is unimpeachable.

Back to summary

What the Spirit debate is really about

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-05-14 · Original →

Spirit Airlines is dead, and the right, true to form, is blaming Joe Biden.

But the proximate cause is very clear: The war with Iran spiked jet fuel costs, and the already-battered airline couldn’t absorb the hit to its bottom line. Spirit also had fundamental problems—a Pratt & Whitney engine recall that grounded a huge share of its fleet, legacy carriers copying its unbundled-fare model and capturing some of its customer base. The odds are good, in fact, that if JetBlue had purchased Spirit the combined airline would be bankrupt today. In a pure business sense, Biden probably did the industry a favor.

As an antitrust analysis, though, the fact that Spirit went bust calls into question the idea that the airline industry is uncompetitive and full of monopolies that need busting.

Members of the neo-Brandeisian movement, however, are undeterred. Which is what I think makes this episode such a useful skeleton key for understanding what this movement is really about — not fighting monopolies or promoting competition, but promoting a return to an economy with a much higher level of central planning and state control. In the latest episode, Jerusalem and I talk through the specific circumstances of Spirit’s failure and the debates around the JetBlue merger. But I also don a literal tinfoil hat to discuss the real history of the “anti-monopoly” movement and its goals.

The transcript will be after the paywall in this post for paying subscribers.

WATCH THE EPISODE HERE

New episodes post every Thursday.

For an ad-free version and full transcript, subscribe at TheArgumentMag.com.

Time stamps:

0:00-Spirit Airlines is dead

7:07-The blocked JetBlue merger

14:54-1960s airline nostalgia

21:12-Matt’s tinfoil hat theory

26:29-Pre-Carter airline monopolies

35:26-The dawn of deregulation

47:17-Anti-monopolists want less competition

54:19-America’s protectionist policies

58:22-Peer review: Marriage and gender-coded purchases

Corrections:

  • At 0:55:00, Jerusalem says the EU began creating its single aviation market starting in 1992 and going through 1997. While the full package of legislation did take effect in 1997, the first legislative package was passed in 1987.
  • At 0:55:24, Jerusalem says that the five largest European aircraft control 45% of the European market while the four largest U.S. aircraft control 80% of the U.S. market. Those figures were accurate, but slightly more recent analysis shows the markets have shifted somewhat. A 2018 analysis showed the four largest European airlines controlled 40% of the EU market, while a 2025 analysis showed the four largest U.S. airlines controlled 74% of the U.S. market.

Show Notes:

  • Coverage of the Strait of Hormuz, through which 20% of the world’s oil and natural gas travel, being closed since Feb. 28: Brookings article, CBS News article
  • Coverage of jet fuel composing 20% to 30% of airlines’ costs: OpenAirlines article,Seeking Alpha article, IATA article
  • Coverage of Spirit attempting Chapter 11 bankruptcies to save itself before shutting down under Chapter seven: Law.com article, OAG article, Las Vegas Sun article
  • Coverage of Spirit blaming rising fuel costs in bankruptcy filing: AirlineGeeks article, Time article
  • Podcast hosted by conservative comedians Stu Burguiere and Dave Landau that blamed Spirit’s failure on the Biden administration’s decision to block Spirit and JetBlue’s merger: BlazeTV podcast episode
  • Coverage of Transportation Sec. Sean Duffy blaming Spirit’s failure on the Biden administration’s decision to block Spirit and JetBlue’s merger: Fox News article, ABC7 article
  • Coverage of JetBlue losing money: Reuters article, Quartz article, The New York Times article
  • Coverage of Pratt & Whitney’s recall of its geared turbofan engine, which impacted more Spirit aircraft than all other airlines combined: Simple Flying article, FlightGlobal article
  • Coverage of Spirit receiving over $150 million in compensation from Pratt & Whitney due to recall: Aerospace Global News article
  • Coverage of a Reagan-era judge blocking JetBlue’s merger with Spirit Airlines after President Biden’s DOJ sued to stop it: AP article, CNBC article
  • Coverage of Spirit boosting premium offerings after its merger was blocked: Reuters article, Upgraded Points article, Spirit Airlines release
  • Coverage of Delta president grudgingly introducing Basic Economy fares to compete with Spirit, even though “People don’t really want the product when they see exactly what it is.”: Conde Nast Traveler article, CNBC article, AJC article
  • Photos shown depicting 1960s and 1970s air travel: Getty image, Getty image
  • Coverage of plans at low-cost European airlines, including Ryanair, to offer short flights with small, vertical, stool-like seats that resemble leaning more than sitting. Prices for these flights could be as low as five Euros: EuroWeekly News article, Thrillist article
  • Coverage of 1978 airline deregulation changes, initially under Jimmy Carter: Journal of Transport Geography article, Air and Space Museum article, EBSCO article
  • Coverage of Trump Shuttle’s operations and failure: The Washington Post article, AeroTime article
  • “Amazon’s Antitrust Paradox,” article by Lina Khan on Amazon’s e-commerce domination: The Yale Law Journal article
  • “Terminal Sickness,” article by Phillip Longman and Lina Khan attacking airline deregulation: Washington Monthly article
  • “Why Flying Is Miserable: And How to Fix It,” book by Ganesh Sitaraman arguing that airline deregulation was a mistake: Goodreads page, Amazon page
  • “The Anti-Bigness Ideology,” article by Matt Bruenig about why bigness isn’t a huge concern for his socialist ideology: Mattbruenig.com article
  • Federal Aviation Report of 1935, which defended the lack of competition created by strict airline regulations, saying “To allow half a dozen air lines to eke out a hand-to-mouth existence where there is enough traffic to support one really first-class service and one alone would be a piece of folly.”: Govinfo page
  • Article profiling former Sec. Mayor Pete Buttigieg’s conversion to the anti-monopoly cause: Politico article
  • “How Airline Markets Work… or Do They? Regulatory Reform in the Airline Industry,” paper by Severin Borenstein and Nancy L. Rose arguing that the price volatility in airlines can be explained by normal macroeconomic conditions: NBER chapter
  • Abundance, book by Ezra Klein and Derek Thompson: Goodreads page, Amazon page
  • “Airline Deregulation, Revisited,” essay by former Justice Stephen Breyer expressing regret about the effect airline deregulation had on workers: Bloomberg Businessweek article
  • History of the United Fruit Company’s actions in Latin America: Harvard Review of Latin America article, Retrospect Journal article, The World article
  • “Why airlines are always going bankrupt,” article by David Oks about competition in the airline industry: David Oks article
  • “We Fly Congress: Market Actions as Corporate Political Activity in the U.S. Airline Industry,” Temple University study showing airlines increase flights to districts where members of Congressional transportation committees live: Organization Science article
  • “The Secret to Reindustrializing America Is Not Tax Cuts and Tariffs. It’s Regulated Competition,” 2025 article by Phillip Longman, cited by Matt: Washington Monthly article
  • Red Plenty, book by Francis Spufford about communist abundance: Goodreads page, Amazon page
  • 49 USC § 40102(a)(15), U.S. law that states domestic airlines must have 75% of voting stock and a majority of equity owned by U.S. citizens: GovInfo U.S. code
  • Fact sheet on EU air transport rules to form a single aviation market, which began in 1987 and were implemented through 1997: EU fact sheet
  • 2015 analysis showing the five largest European airlines (Ryanaid, EasyJet, Lufthansa, Air France-KLM, and IAG) control 45% of the European market: European Commission page
  • Slightly more recent analysis (2018) showing that the four largest European airlines (Ryanair, Lufthansa, Air France-KLM, and IAG) control 40% of the European market: Global Finance article
  • 2025 analysis showing the four largest U.S. airlines (United, American, Delta, and Southwest) control about 74% of the U.S. market: Spherical Insights analysis
  • “Do Airlines in Chapter 11 harm their rivals?: Bankruptcy and pricing behavior in U.S. airline markets,” paper by Severin Borenstein and Nancy Rose showing that bankruptcies can cause prices to dip briefly: NBER working paper
  • Peer review: “Gender-Specific Economic Shocks and Household Bargaining Power,” paper by Rania Gihleb, Osea Giuntella, and Dor Morag: NBER working paper

Transcript:

Matthew Yglesias

This is why I’m so in on my tinfoil hat. Because I think that a lot of people are genuinely confused as to what this debate is about, because if the American Economic Liberties Project was called the Technocratic Regulators Can Improve on Market Outcomes Institute, then nobody would be confused. It’s a debate about what it means to be a center left political party should be to be a capitalism and welfare state party or it should be a central planning party.

Jerusalem Demsas

Hi, my name is Jerusalem Demsas.

Matthew Yglesias

I’m Matthew Yglesias.

Jerusalem Demsas

And welcome to The Argument. It’s a show where two friends argue about politics, policy, and whatever else we’re interested in that week. This week, Matt and I are gonna talk about Spirit Airlines.

Matthew Yglesias

Yes, Spirit Airlines.

Jerusalem Demsas

Not great.

Matthew Yglesias

It’s bankrupt. This is interesting. I mean, I think we want to talk about what actually happened with this airline, but also, I think that some of the political arguments about this are like a skeleton key for understanding really big ideological divides in America in a way that people don’t totally understand.

Jerusalem Demsas

Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias

But first, we had an airline.

Jerusalem Demsas

No longer exists. It’s bankrupt.

Matthew Yglesias

It’s gone. So, Spirit for — I don’t know if those don’t know — it was what we call an ultra-low-cost carrier (ULCC). It’s a cheap airline. ULCCs focus mostly on price-sensitive leisure travelers, tourists, things like that. They do point-to-point service.

Spirit is one of the older ones in the United States. So its elimination is sort of significant.

Jerusalem Demsas

Yeah. And the proximate cause of this is obviously the Iran war.

Matthew Yglesias

Well…

Jerusalem Demsas

The Strait of Hormuz has been closed for, what, like six weeks now? How long has it been?

Matthew Yglesias

It’s been a while since I last took a supertanker through it.

Jerusalem Demsas

It’s been a while; it’s 10 weeks, actually. And then, 20% of the world’s oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz. Obviously, that’s going to raise jet fuel costs. Jet fuel is like 20% of the costs of airlines’ largest expense after labor. And the cost of jet fuel is roughly doubled at this point.

So, it seems bad. That’s what they’re citing in their bankruptcy documents.

Matthew Yglesias

Right. I mean, to back it up: So, Spirit had been going on for a while. Its business model had been ailing for a while. JetBlue, which is a different airline, tried to buy them when Joe Biden was president. The Justice Department argued that this would be an antitrust problem. They got a federal judge to agree the acquisition was blocked.

Spirit then went bankrupt pretty soon after. They did what’s — there’s multiple kinds of bankruptcy. There’s two main ones for companies: chapter 11 and chapter seven. And in chapter 11, bankruptcy, you shrug off your old debts. You can rewrite leases —this is important for airlines. You can screw over your workers on things you’d promise to pay them.

Airlines are filing for chapter 11 bankruptcy all the time because it’s one of the only ways they can reduce their cost structure. So Spirit did a chapter 11, they emerged from chapter 11. They then did another chapter 11. They emerged from that. And then jet fuel prices spiked.

Jerusalem Demsas

So this is a huge debate happening right now about what actually is the cause of Spirit’s bankruptcy, which is what we’re kind of getting into here. Spirit, as I said, is nominally citing — and it is, like the last knife was definitely the jet fuel prices.

They were very exposed to this. They were already in a bad position. But this was the final nail in the coffin.

But now there’s all this discourse, like I was just listening to a podcast on The Blaze this morning. So it was just two comedians — it’s a comedy podcast, but they were on the right and they were basically entirely attributing the demise of Spirit to the DOJ suit to prevent Spirit from merging with JetBlue under the Biden administration.

And the argument that’s really burgeoning both on the right — you know, Sean Duffy, transportation secretary for the Trump administration, is really saying this — but also, lots of people are saying this online. And elected officials are arguing this, too, that the Biden administration made an anti-capitalist, anti market, anti-competitive decision to allow for Spirit Airlines to — or to prevent Spirit Airlines from being able to merge with JetBlue — and there’s this, like, “Well, look what happened.”

“So you didn’t let them merge, but now it doesn’t even exist. So the benefits that you were supposed to get of Spirit Airlines continuing and continuing to be this pressure to prevent price increases no longer exists.”

Matt Yglesias

Right. Well, so, that second argument I think makes a lot more sense than the first, right? But what they’re literally saying, like I saw, you know, there was an AI cartoon and it’s like Joe Biden and Elizabeth Warren are the pallbearers at the Spirit Airlines funeral. They’re jumping around.

Jerusalem Demsas

There’s just two of them?

Matt Yglesias

I think so.

Jerusalem Demsas

Pretty strong.

Matthew Yglesias

Yeah, they’re strong guys.

So, in a literal sense, clearly, it’s the increase in jet fuel is why Spirit is liquidating. That’s the difference between today and two months ago. In a counterfactual sense, I mean, it seems likely, actually, that if the purchase had gone through, right… JetBlue has been losing money since this time anyway. Spirit is also losing money.

If they had merged, JetBlue would have taken on Spirit’s debts, and it also would have incurred costs because when you buy an airline, you need to repaint all the planes, so on and so forth.

They would have made this purchase not knowing that Trump was going to start a war with Iran that was going to spike the price of jet fuel. And so the combined entity would almost certainly be bankrupt now.

Jerusalem Demsas

Yeah, I mean, that’s a very plausible argument people are making. I do not feel like I have the necessary business expertise to know if that’s true or not. But I do think that it’s — one thing that’s relevant is that Spirit also made a bunch of interesting business decisions.

So after it wasn’t… You know, the JetBlue merger was also super controversial at the time, like even within Spirit, there were concerns that JetBlue’s merger was just definitely not going to be allowed and that they should just not… The shareholders should not accept the merger, but they were drawn in by the massive potential payout that they were going to get an exit for accepting that.

But I think that this point was that after the merger was blocked by a Reagan-era judge, Spirit decided to try to not just be a budget airline anymore. They were like, “OK, we need to now expand our premium offerings.” I forget what they were called, like, the big front seats or whatever, like some kind of premium offerings that they started doing.

And they started really trying to break up the main carrier. And, obviously, anyone who’s ridden Spirit — I don’t think Matt or I have ever had the privilege. I guess we’ll never have the privilege at this point — the whole point was it’s really ultra-low. It’s not meant to have any kind of premium offerings.

And it becomes, it creates this weird dynamic, though, in the arguments from the DOJ that were like, “OK, we want to keep Spirit around because they’re this ultra-low-cost carrier,” but then they block the merger with JetBlue, and then you get a situation where Spirit thinks its only pathway out of this is to no longer be just an ultra-low-cost carrier.

So even if the war with Iran doesn’t happen, and jet fuel prices don’t spike, it seems unclear to me whether Spirit would have remained this very low-cost carrier into the long term. That doesn’t seem like the strategy they were going for.

Matthew Yglesias

Well, and this is, I think, the correct critique of the Biden administration policy. It’s not that they caused Spirit to go bankrupt, but that Spirit’s bankruptcy — not just the chapter seven liquidation that’s happening now, but the two prior chapter 11s — it just, it calls into question what they were trying to accomplish here.

Because Spirit was a failing business, right? But it had assets. It had airplanes. It had slots and gates, primarily in Florida. JetBlue was interested in buying that stuff.

The argument that was made to block the merger was, “No, no, no, we need this independent, cheap airline with lots of flights in and out of Florida, rather than consolidation.” But Spirit was losing money.

And so the alternative to them being purchased by JetBlue was to be purchased by somebody else who would also want that stuff, or for them to change their business model to be more comparable to these higher-priced airlines, or what ultimately happened, which is that the airline is going to be dissolved and the planes and the gates will be auctioned off.

So, somebody else will buy them. Probably in the end, JetBlue is going to get a lot of what it wanted out of this — like the material objects — at a lower price because they’re not going to need to pay a control premium.

So, in some sense, right, some of the brainiacs who are involved in this are like, “Well, so we were correct to block it because this whole merger was a bad business idea,” which I sympathize with.

A lot of times — I think it’s underrated — that a lot of times, CEOs want to do mergers simply because it’s cooler and more fun to be the CEO of a bigger company rather than a smaller one. And it doesn’t really make business sense. And it’s not an evil monopoly plot. It’s just a bad idea.

Like when AT&T bought Warner Bros. years and years ago, and it’s like they had this story that they were telling their shareholders. But at the end of the day, being the CEO of a cellphone infrastructure company is kind of boring. And being the CEO of a movie studio is awesome; you get to hang out with movie stars, you go to the Oscars. They just lost all this money and it was unwound ultimately.

So, I feel, bottom line, the specific allegations being leveled at the Biden people about this are not literally true. But, also, it’s really hard to say that this was an example…

Jerusalem Demsas

Of success?

Matthew Yglesias

…of this amazing new era of antitrust policy that was supposed to give us all these benefits. All that happened is the failing airline that wanted to sell itself to JetBlue is now failed and will be sold off for parts.

Some of the parts will be bought by JetBlue, some by, I guess, Breeze. I mean, there’s a bunch of airlines out there.

Jerusalem Demsas

I also think that it’s like, when I was looking into this, it just is clear that there was also some very bad luck at Spirit Airlines that it was facing. I mean, I don’t actually know what this is, but there’s this type of jet engine called a geared turbofan engine. And Pratt & Whitney are the manufacturers of this product, and they had to do a recall.

And Spirit Airlines was the single most exposed airline for this recall. And so, they were able to… They had to pay, I think it was like $130 million a year, or something like that, in order to compensate the airlines.

But that doesn’t actually fix the problem. Like you’re not able to sell people…

Matthew Yglesias

Well, I mean, airlines are a tough business, right? So, the mainline carriers fly these very complicated fleets with lots and lots of different types of planes on it, and typically a low-cost carrier will only get one kind of plane, right?

And so there’s a lot of cost savings for that because you can, you don’t need as many spare parts on hand because it can come in. Your pilots only need to train for one thing, but you are left more vulnerable to something going wrong because you only have one supplier, right?

So, airlines is a difficult business. Airlines, a lot of airlines, have gone bankrupt over the years. And Spirit had a nice long run as a business that helped maybe cause some other airlines to have financial difficulties over the years. And that’s what competition is supposed to be.

Jerusalem Demsas

Yeah. And I mean, I was a kid before this period, but the 2010s is… Like, right now, it’s really normal when you go online and you’re like, “Let me go buy a flight,” right? And you’re like, “Oh, there’s all these different tiers.” I don’t remember all the names.

Like there’s Basic economy. There’s Super Basic Economy. There’s Loser Basic Economy. And then there’s Premium Loser Basic, and there’s all these different ways, and there’s…

You can, once you’ve actually bought your Premium Loser Basic Economy ticket, then you can decide if you want to spend another $35 on a seat with more leg room. And then, you can also spend another $15 if you want to board early and you don’t actually have loyalty points with that airline.

And then on top of that, if you want to — maybe on some airlines — if you want to bring more than just one checked bag, you have to pay. Anyway. There are all of these, there’s been a bunch of unbundling that’s happened in the airline space and not just on the Spirit and the Frontier, but also on the legacy airlines and on the airlines that are supposed to be these premium products.

Matthew Yglesias

But it was the Spirits of the world that introduced that model.

Jerusalem Demsas

That did this, exactly. And I was too young to remember the time before this, but this is in the 2010s really. And Spirit is older than this, but this is when Spirit really starts, and also Allegiant and Frontier really start actually pioneering this new business model.

And it’s funny, the Delta president at the time, when all this was happening, and they had to compete with Spirit and Allegiant and all these kind of very-low-cost carriers, ultra-low-cost carriers.

The president was basically just like, “We’re calling it a Spirit match fare.” Like, said specifically, that Basic Economy is not something we want to grow. As we’ve outlined before, it’s a defensive product against the ultra-low-cost carriers. Like it’s really, really explicit that the actual effect of all of these airlines, these ultra-low-cost airlines, it’s to genuinely produce lower prices for consumers.

And it’s difficult to tease out because when we think about Delta or United or JetBlue or any of these other airlines lowering their prices in response, and, therefore, Spirit can no longer compete, and, therefore, Spirit has to go out of business. It’s like, is that a win for…?

That sounds like a win for consumers. But then, obviously, there’s this question of: When Spirit goes away, what’s going to happen? And I think that’s a big part of the conversation today.

Matthew Yglesias

Well, and there’s also, I mean, some of what drives discourse around this is that ultra-low-cost carriers came into the market, they offered people cheaper airfares, but they did also offer a worse product, right? Like what the innovation was…

Jerusalem Demsas

Typically cheaper things are worse.

Matthew Yglesias

Right, well, I mean, it just, it turns out that for not everybody, but most people, most of the time, what they basically want is to get to their destination as cheaply as possible, right? And so there’s been this long-term trend toward creating lower airfares, in part by decontenting the product. And so you’ll sometimes see these nostalgia posts and they’ll show you what an airplane was like in the ‘60s…

Jerusalem Demsas

Oh my God, we should put that up on the screen. It’s like men and women in the 1960s garb, and they’re lounging in these large chairs, like bigger than this chair. There’s no one near them. And there’s alcohol being served on this giant platter and like a roast turkey or whatever.

Matthew Yglesias

And the thing is, is that those fares cost roughly what a business class fare would cost today, right, in a country that was poorer. So you just used to have a situation where many fewer people flied. Airfares were much higher. They were particularly much higher relative to incomes. But the experience was really, really nice.

That has now been reinvented as the premium cabin in a mainline airline. And then, first the ULCCs came in and they invented this cheapo mode of flying. And then the mainline carriers started responding so that, now, you can get economy, which is kind of crappy, but then Basic Economy, you give up even more and it’s cheaper, cheaper, cheaper.

And so, the standard neoliberal “thing to say about this” is that this is…

Jerusalem Demsas

Way more people get to fly now. I just think it’s…

Matthew Yglesias

Right!

Jerusalem Demsas

It’s one thing, like the top — I don’t even, I wish I could look up this number — but a very small slice of American society was flying commercial in the 1960s and 70s. This was just, the normal flight was a normal flight for a very rich person.

I find this is very analogous to the college debate sometimes. Like, the composition of people who are attending college today, who are considered college-educated, are just a completely different composition of people. Way more people get to be a part of this. And so, it drives a lot of weird confusion when you see the, you know, there’s pictures of the 1960s and people are like, “Well, why is it so much worse now?”

It’s like, no, the thing you should be comparing it to was driving across the country. You were not, you didn’t have a flight option because you could not afford to fly.

Matthew Yglesias

But it depends, because one aspect of this that I do think is relevant is that when I was a kid in the ‘80s — and that was the early days of this transformation of the industry — my mother worked at Newsweek. She was mostly a graphic designer-photography person rather than a writer.

But Newsweek journalists, like journalists today, traveled for work fairly frequently. And journalists then, like today, were not treated to the super premium fares on these things. They had to book regular economy-class tickets. But because the worst ticket that you could buy in the ‘70s and ‘80s was just way better than an economy fare today, things have gotten way worse for people like that, right?

If you travel a lot for work — which means you’re probably in the upper strata of American society, but you’re not in the real upper strata of American society — you are now kind of saddled with this product that has been developed to optimize for the needs of the majority of people who fly infrequently, and when they fly, it’s to visit family or to go on vacation.

And for them, the upside is that an airplane is much faster than a car. And so you can get where you’re going and you can do it affordably. And now, all kinds of people fly, at least sometimes, just for fun and it’s cheap.

And in Europe — where people are poorer on average but also have more vacation days on average, and also there’s just more interesting places to go — the ULCC segment is huge on Ryanair, Jet2, EasyJet, things like that. Because you have lots of people who just would really like to get to Budapest or Warsaw or whatever it is for a long weekend as cheaply as possible. And it’s crappy planes, but you get there.

In America, the market is structurally different. The mainline airlines invented Basic Economy to defend, and…

Jerusalem Demsas

It just sets up a bunch of interesting trade-offs, too, because, at some level — obviously, whether it’s these upper middle class people who are now having to buy Basic Economy because they’re trying to be cost cutting — people often have very little loyalty to the airline. When they go on Google Flights, or whatever it is that they do, they just pick the cheapest thing often. Sometimes they’ll discriminate on nonstop or something like that. But, largely, they’re just like, “I want the cheapest flight possible. I don’t really care if it’s United, I don’t really care if it’s Delta” Some small segment of the population cares a lot because they…

Matthew Yglesias

I care so much. I’m clinging to my Star Alliance Silver status for dear life.

Jerusalem Demsas

But I don’t have — I don’t really care about that sort of thing. I think, like many Americans, I just sort of… And maybe I know I’m leaving money on the table, but it’s just one of those things where I just go for the cheapest thing. I’m a woman of the people, Matthew, unlike you.
