I think the thing that’s important about that is that there’s almost a “I want it to be much cheaper than it’s ever been, more so than the technological progress or the increased competition would — if you’re just looking at the marginal cost of these airlines — that it would justify, and also I want it to be this very luxury product that’s available for everyone.”

And I also think that that would be great. Like it would be a good situation if we got higher-quality airline tickets or airline experiences and things were very, very cheap, and hopefully with more technological progress, more competition, you can actually get access to those sorts of things.

But there’s almost a weird dynamic where people both are really mad at how expensive air travel is, and that’s their primary focus, but so much of the policy discourse is like, “Focus on this: Isn’t it really annoying that there are all these fees?” or “Isn’t it really annoying that there was this proposal from — I forget what it was, but it was some airline in Europe — to have short standing flights.”

And it was really treated as this horrible thing that companies are doing to you. And the CEO is like, “I just think you can get more people on, it’s a cheaper flight for everyone, and you can opt into a higher quality thing if you want to.”

And so, it just becomes this question of whether you view companies as largely entities that are trying to make a profit in any way possible, or if you think that they’re actively just exploitative entities, regardless of what’s going on. But we’re talking about all of this…

Matthew Yglesias

Can I do my bit now?

Jerusalem Demsas

You can.

Matthew Yglesias

You know, we gotta go because I got to put on my tinfoil hat here and talk about what we’re really talking about here.

Jerusalem Demsas

This is, how much work…? You didn’t put any work into this hat. This is not even a hat.

Matthew Yglesias

I’m not a crafty person, but I need to, I’m getting paranoid. No, here’s the thing.

Jerusalem Demsas

How long are you gonna keep that on? Just the whole podcast.

Matthew Yglesias

We’ll see what happens.

Jerusalem Demsas

OK.

Matthew Yglesias

For a long time now, there has been factional controversy inside the Democratic Party. And one side in this controversy likes to label itself anti-monopoly. Or they will sometimes talk a lot about antitrust policy. And Lina Khan is probably the most prominent figure in this sort of movement. But they have multiple policy organizations: Open markets institute. American economic liberties project. They seemingly dominate the Washington Monthly’s journalism and have influence over The American Prospect, The New Republic…

And these people would like you to think that they are really interested in competition and that that’s the point of all of this. But you see their commentary, specifically on the airline industry — which is not the most important industry in the world — but their beef with the airline industry is that there is too much competition.

There was a big deregulation of the airline industry that took place in Jimmy Carter’s administration that unfolded into the early 1980s, and that has unleashed this series of changes where flights got cheaper, the low end tickets got worse, there have been many airline bankruptcies, there have been a lot of chapter 11 processes in particular that have not been great for the workforce at the labor unions, at the airlines. And sometimes an airline liquidates — goes out of business.

There was a guy named Donald Trump, ran an airline called Trump Shuttle when I was a kid. It would take you from D.C. to New York. It’s not in business anymore. I don’t know what happened to that guy, and it’s this very different world. And Khan herself, you know, she, before the Amazon antitrust paradox piece, she had this piece with Phil Longman called “Terminal Sickness” that said airline deregulation was a mistake.

And they’re blaming all kinds of things on this, right? Ganesh Sitaraman has a book out fairly recently about how airline deregulation was a mistake. He’s one of the big movers and shakers behind Vanderbilt Policy Accelerator. He’s old friends and was a key adviser to Sec. Mayor Pete at the Department of Transportation.

Pete, when he was there at DOT, brought one of these people over from FTC to do competition policy there. And, it’s just a huge — it’s not the most important thing in the world, but it’s a huge tell that the vision that they actually have for the American economy is not that we’re going to use antitrust enforcement to make markets be competitive. It’s that we’re going to use the idea that real-world markets don’t exhibit total textbook frictionless competition as a pretext to impose pre-Carter regulatory frameworks on everything.

Jerusalem Demsas

OK. Let’s just back up for a second because, so, this is obviously a bit of a different episode than we… Hat’s coming off?

Matthew Yglesias

Taking off the hat, getting normal.

Jerusalem Demsas

It was hard to take you seriously, I’ll be honest with you. I was not fully grokking even what you were saying as a result of that, so… tin pot hat wearers out there, really reflect on your choices.

I want to take a step back, because usually we have an episode where Matt and I are beefing over something specific. This segment has an argument that both of us have general thoughts about Spirit Airlines. But, now, Matt has a specific argument about how… Well he has a meta point about how this specific episode shows that there’s actually this shady plot underlying a lot of the rise of anti-monopoly discourse in the public.

And just situate people in this who are not so online, there has been a rise of a movement, like the neo-Brandeisian movement, you might hear a lot about Lina Khan, who was FTC chair under Joe Biden, but there are lots of people who have been interested in the space; Matt detailed a few of their names. And many of the things they’ve been known for are things I think that we would support, things like banning, noncompetes, which I think is actually just neoliberalism. But other things they’ve been really focused on are around blocking big companies from getting too big — like a real fear of bigness.

Matt Stoller, who’s a big person in this space, his newsletter is called BIG. I mean, again, they’re very, very concerned about big entities existing. Matt Bruning, who writes for The Argument Magazine, he wrote this whole piece about how this kind of concern with bigness is not really, you know, that cogent from his perspective as a, as a socialist.

But then, that entire thing kind of feels very weird in the airline space, which I think is why you think it’s such a skeleton key, right? Because in the airline space, what’s actually happening under the pre-airline deregulation moment — before Carter, before Nader really takes this on — is it was a system that privileged legacy airlines. It was literally trying to maintain an oligopoly of airlines that were closely regulated by something called the CAB: Civil Aeronautics Board.

And it’s kind of funny because I didn’t actually realize this for a while, but the entire conception of the Civil Aeronautics Board — the entire conception, like the Roosevelt-era imposition of this direct regulation of prices and all these other factors that go into airline businesses — is that they were worried about there being too much competition.

There’s a theory of ruinous competition that you would have all of these people entering the airline market, all of these companies entering into it, and in order to compete, they would all lower their prices too much such that they wouldn’t be able to sustain a business. That these new entrants would drive legacy airlines out of business. And the whole theory is around this belief that the actual market for airlines can’t function normally.

Matthew Yglesias

Right, yeah. You know, the operation of the old Civil Aeronautics Board was complicated, but the key thing is that, if I looked and I said, “Wow, the guys flying planes from D.C. to Chicago are making a lot of money. I’d like to take one of my planes that’s currently flying around Idaho or something and fly it from D.C. to Chicago instead,” I would have to go to the CAB and say, “I would like permission to start flying planes from D.C. to Chicago.” And then they would do a market analysis that was designed to ascertain whether or not that market was large enough to support a third airline, or in many cases, a second airline. And, you know, oftentimes the answer would be “no,” because the purpose of the CAB framework was to prevent the airlines from engaging in price wars that would drive them out of business.

And, you know, this was good, obviously, if you were a shareholder in one of those airlines. It was very good if you worked at one of the airlines because it meant that you never had the experience that many more contemporary airline pilots, flight attendants, and staff have had of the airline saying, “Oh crap, we’re going out of business,” right? So you had these…

Jerusalem Demsas

And to be clear, this is like, not me — I mean, I know we had a tinfoil hat earlier — but this is not just me and Matt just theorizing. The Federal Aviation Report of 1935 explicitly argues, “To allow half a dozen air lines to eke out a hand-to-mouth existence where there is enough traffic to support one really first-class service and one alone would be a piece of folly.”

So this is an intentional decision. And in 1958, 23 of the 100 largest city-pair markets were effectively monopolies. Another 57 were effectively duopolies, in only two did the largest three carriers have less than 90% share. And the thing that we’re talking about here — this kind of regulation was not, this is not pro-competitive in any real sense.”

Matthew Yglesias

No, I mean it was the opposite, right? And so, you know, if you look, there’s a Politico piece in which they are profiling Secretary Mayor Pete’s conversion to the anti-monopoly cause. And he says in there “Well, you know, aviation isn’t like a regular industry. You know, there’s these high capital costs, significant barriers to entry, etc.,” which is true, there are high capital costs and there’s significant barriers to entry, right?

Like, in the deregulated market, you know, I was saying an airline could shift its planes from Idaho to Chicago, but Jerusalem and I, we can’t just start an airline, right? Like, we have both started websites at different times, and it’s hard work, but nobody’s going to stop you, right?

Jerusalem Demsas

They can keep trying.

Matthew Yglesias

Whereas, for safety reasons, you can start up an airline, it’s not illegal, but it’s quite challenging, right? There’s not free entry and exit.

So, it’s true. It’s not a perfect textbook marketplace. And so, this idea from the 1930s was, well, given that competition would work imperfectly, it’s far too dangerous to allow it to work at all. And so, you would have, in many European countries, they just had state-owned airlines, right? That’s why the names of the airlines are like British Airways, Air France, right? Because they were run by the government.

In America, we “believed in capitalism,” so these were all private companies, but the government was telling them what they were allowed to do. But there was this exception, which is that the Constitution was seen as limiting the CAB’s authority to interstate aircraft.

Jerusalem Demsas

Before we get into it, I think it’s really important; I just want to point out here, I don’t want to fully, implicitly buy this idea that airlines or the airline industry is just like necessarily broken in this specific way.

So, there’s this great paper by Borenstein and Rose. We’ll link it in the show notes, but they want to show that you don’t need any exotic “this industry is structurally broken”-type ideas in order to explain why it is that like airline profits swing wildly and that they just use kind of an ordinary, you know, economic macroeconomics model, and they find that just the fact of the volatile demand, which is, you know, no recessions, 911 happens, like, stuff occurs; holidays shift around, fuel shocks, etc.

But then, that plus the sticky costs that you already mentioned, labor contracts, whatever it is, and fuel shocks, which we’re witnessing right now. These three things perfectly explain this within the normal macroeconomic neoclassical model without… So, even the presumption that like, “OK, there is something specifically so weird about this market that it’s impossible to imagine us regulating it in a normal way is like, I don’t think that…

Matthew Yglesias

Well, right. And that’s why I think the exception to the CAB is relevant. America has — some of our states are quite large, and so there is meaningful air travel demand between the cities of Texas and between the cities of California, right? And so, in the 1970s, reformers started noting — because it was this theoretical controversy, right?

Jerusalem Demsas

As all are.

Matthew Yglesias

Right, exactly. But is it really so different in a relevant way? And they said, well, you can look at flying from LA to San Francisco and compare it to flying D.C. to Boston because these are very similar distances. It’s similar affluent big metro markets. And the intra-California route was way cheaper.

Jerusalem Demsas

And just to put numbers on it. So, the lowest fare between Boston and Washington that was served only by CAB-certified trunk carriers was $24.65. I mean, that sounds really cheap anyway, but the Pacific Southwest Airlines, using the same equipment, carried passengers between Los Angeles and San Francisco, which is only 59 miles closer, for $11.43. The jet fare was only $13.50, so that’s a huge difference.

I mean, this is why the argument for airline deregulation was so strong, is that you could just look at what was happening inside of these states and just go, “Clearly this regulated market is not providing cheap airfare.”

And to be clear, they knew that. That was the point, they were raising prices for things that they thought were worth it. I mean, way above market. And it’s so interesting, like the fares, they were rising. It was about 20 — The 1972 estimate was that airfares were 20% to 100% above competition cost. And you’d expect: Are all these airlines raking it in? Were they all just making massive profits?

They weren’t. Which is kind of weird. And they look into it. And basically what’s happening is that they’re trying to — they can’t compete on cost. And so they’re trying — they can’t control most of the dimensions that the airlines normally get to control, because the CAB was regulating all of those.

So instead, they’re competing in other weird ways. So, carriers are trying to grab market share by flying more flights than the route justifies. They buy bigger, fancier planes. They serve real meals. They have piano bars on 747s all that stuff. But the cost…

Matthew Yglesias

Right, this is why the planes were so nice.

Jerusalem Demsas

Yeah, and that costs money. The craziest part of it is that by the early 1970s, planes were flying with average load factors below 50%. They were half empty. And today, it’s roughly 85%. So, I mean, I don’t know, I guess we didn’t care about climate change then, but that seems quite hard.

Matthew Yglesias

Right right right right. So, you know, this is why deregulation happens, right? People got concerned about inflation in the 1970s.

Jerusalem Demsas

Nader was a big part of this.

Matthew Yglesias

And, so, people started looking at different things. Ralph Nader at the time was a big proponent of airline deregulation. Stephen Breyer, who became a Supreme Court justice later was Ted Kennedy’s policy guy working on this. Jimmy Carter was converted to the cause. A guy named Alfred Kahn was a regulatory economist and did a lot of modeling work on why this would be better. And he was put in charge of the CAB with a mandate to dismantle it.

Bipartisan law was passed. And this was one of a number of different things that happened in Carter’s term that sort of inaugurate the era of neoliberalism. And so many of the disputes that we have… I mean, it’s not — it would be wildly overstated to say that everything everybody says about Amazon is like just secretly about airline deregulation.

It’s that understanding… I think that when the Abundance book came out, I think that a lot of people were surprised that that text took so much fire from the anti-monopoly movement, because on its face, like a book about abundance and growth seems like it has an obvious potential conflict with at least certain segments of the environmental movement that are avowedly degrowth.

A lot of people, I mean, every talk, every conference I’ve seen about this, people like “labor unions!” you know, because they see attention in economic policy. But antitrust enforcement to not have monopolies is just supposed to be pro-growth policy, right?

Jerusalem Demsas

It’s also, to be clear, removing the barriers for building new housing typologies and reducing the cost to entry of that market is pro-competition behavior. In California, when they made it really legal to, when they made it legal to build ADUs after like 30 years of laws, you saw a bunch of new firms enter the market that are, like, ADU firms. And that’s pro-competition.

Matthew Yglesias

Exactly. If you’re using these words in a normal way, it’s not just tha there’s no tension between these goals, it’s just the same, right? All the abundance-y deregulatory stuff: The purpose is to promote competition.

Jerusalem Demsas

Yes.

Matthew Yglesias

All the regulatory anti-monopoly stuff in normal universe is to prevent illicit cartels from privately blocking competition. But the reason — But it’s not like this is just some big misunderstanding, or, you know, when I interviewed Ezra and Derek about this, I think Ezra was saying like, “Well, it’s like a competition over attention…” He’s really into attention…

Jerusalem Demsas

I think that’s part of it.

Matthew Yglesias

Sure. But like there is a genuine, big ideological disagreement, which is, like, a lot of things happened downstream of airline deregulation that a person could plausibly construe as undesirable. Like Justice Breyer said, “I feel sort of bad about the extent to which workers in the industry ended up taking it on the chin as a result of this.”

I think a lot of journalists wish that their economy airfares got them nicer flights. If the plane was half empty, that would be really cool; you get some more elbow room, things like that.

Longman and Kahn, in their article, they outline this theory that, I think there’s something to it, which is that like in a CAB universe, you would not have been allowed to just pull metal out of these industrial Midwestern cities and reallocate flights to the growing Sun Belt. And that could have helped prop up the economies of cities like Saint Louis and Cincinnati and Cleveland that have been in a state of decline.

Now, they put like a ton of interpretive weight on this as a causal factor. But it’s just definitely true that if you have this regulator who is literally telling every airline in America where they can and cannot fly airplanes, that gives the federal government a lot more ability to do “industrial,” you know, to shape the economy. And is that good?

Jerusalem Demsas

But I think this is really important because this, actually, is part of — this obsession with regional inequality and this obsession with specific places needing to be propped up…

Matthew Yglesias

Oh, you hate the Midwest.

Jerusalem Demsas

I don’t hate the Midwest, don’t say that! The Khan piece you’re referencing begins with this anecdote of the CEO of Chiquita Brands International — like the banana… used to be United Fruit Company. It’s kind of funny that this is their hero given the sordid history of the United Fruit Company in Latin America and beyond.

But, basically, it’s a sad story. He loves Cincinnati. They’d been there for a while. It was no longer a hub. It was difficult to attract talent. It was difficult to get around business travelers. So they moved to Charlotte. And this is the entire intro of this piece, and like…

Matthew Yglesias

The whole idea of a banana company being headquartered in Ohio is borderline absurd.

Jerusalem Demsas

I don’t know, but the guy was really obsessed with Cincinnati, and then he didn’t really like NASCAR and that was, like, sad and whatever. And I thought this is like such a fundamentally conservative position. Like small-c conservative position to be like, “We think that there are certain places… The places that used to have airline hubs must continue to have them, and that’s proper.

Because it’s not like there’s a principled thing here, where any city that’s the size of X should have an airline, because that would just be insane. And right now we have this system — after the airline deregulation happened, we created these EAS subsidies, Essential Air Service program, in order to make sure that places would not just immediately lose all of their airline access.

And it’s like, the eligibility is basically based on places that had service over 40 years ago when the industry was deregulated. And so you have, like, Ogdensburg, New York, with 10,000 people.

Matthew Yglesias

Well, the EAS is about really small towns going to zero air service… The Longman point is just that certain primarily Midwestern cities that were — in the 1940s, among the biggest cities in America — used to be airline hubs, a lot of those hubs have been dismantled. And now Charlotte, Miami, Phoenix, Dallas are hubs and I guess I’ve…

The collapse of population in the city of Saint Louis is a genuine kind of American tragedy. That being said, it’s just, like, why should the purpose of national aviation policy be to prevent people from moving from Saint Louis to Phoenix?

Jerusalem Demsas

I do think that the problem…

Matthew Yglesias

Because I believe in human freedom.

Jerusalem Demsas

I mean, I agree, I think it’s also that the limiting principle here is actually what gets at what’s weird about this is just sort of, like, what is our level at which we’re willing to do this because it’s not just costless, right? When the CAB had those prices that were 20% to 100% over what the competitive price likely would have been, that’s being paid by people.

So the question is not like, “Oh, don’t we think it would be great if someone in Cincinnati had direct flights to everywhere in the country and international markets as well,” it’s “How much do you think we should be willing to pay for it?”

And I don’t know, I think there’s a weird level of disdain that a lot of these people have for the cosmopolitan elite, despite they themselves being a part of it. Like, in the Khan article, there’s this line that says, “If you are in the business of making and trading stuff beyond derivatives and concepts, you probably have to go to places like Cincinnati, Pittsburgh, Memphis, Saint Louis, or Minneapolis, and you know firsthand how hard it has become to do business these days in such major heartland cities, which are increasingly cut off from each other and from the global economy?”

And it’s like, first of all, your job is trading concepts, too. So, I don’t really know where this is coming from. But I think there’s also this weird sense again, like, real America is in this place. And like everyone else who lives in these, like high-population-density, whether cities or suburbs, like everywhere else in the country, these are like fake Americans. This is just a fundamentally conservative argument.

Matthew Yglesias

Yes. But also, I mean, I just find, in particular, the discourse here never spotlights the actual trade-off, which is not just… OK, some Midwestern cities lost service, and then what? So it’s like, there are more flights than ever before in America. The American population has grown, and the per capita use of airplanes has also grown.

Jerusalem Demsas

The decline in prices of, like, roughly 60% could be attributed to deregulation.

Matthew Yglesias

But also, I mean, if you go back to 1970, whenever it is that, you know, Chiquita Banana was in Cincinnati, there were way fewer flights to Austin, way fewer flights to Las Vegas, way fewer flights to Tampa, you know. And so it’s like, I don’t know. You know, the economy evolves.

Jerusalem Demsas

But this is part of why they’re so hostile to NIMBYism. Because a lot of, when you look at the NIMBY original economics literature, it’s focused on the innovative capacity of agglomeration, right? Part of what’s going on here is that folks like Ed Glaeser and other economists, Moretti, etc., [are] looking at the agglomeration of people in places like Los Angeles or these superstar cities, and that’s a positive thing.

Innovation happens in these places, new firms get developed, new ideas, cities are really great engines of opportunity and economic growth, and all of these cures for things come out of these hubs or whatever. And that’s not because there’s something special in the water in Silicon Valley or whatever. It’s that a lot of people are together and that just creates much more potential for new ideas to flourish.

It also lets people start new firms in a place where there’s like a thick labor market for the types of people that they are trying to hire for and from, and all of this hostility to this attitude is kind of a sense that that’s actually bad. That everyone… I did this panel once, and it was like a Chatham House rule, so I can’t be very specific about it. But I was just talking broadly, actually it was an interview. I was just talking about all the benefits that kind of accrue, doing my normal shtick about YIMBYism and agglomeration and economic growth and there were just like a lot of these kinds of people in the audience, and they were just like, they didn’t care about the lower cost. They didn’t care about the innovation and the benefits.

It was like, “No, it’s fundamentally bad that there are all these people agglomerating in Seattle and D.C. and New York. That’s just bad.

Matthew Yglesias

But I just also read some of this in the other direction, right? Which is that part of the whole argument around the CAB, right, was, well, this technocratic analysis will give us a better outcome than the slightly messy… David Oks has a good article about competition in the airline industry. And what’s true is that, essentially because planes are so big, right, you can only compete in these giant chunks, right? It’s not like — it’s hard to compete on the margin.

So there’s a tendency for an individual market to sort of lurch between “somebody’s reaping big profits here” to “now everybody’s losing money.” And it’s odd. It’s kind of jarring. And so, you could have an expert regulator just make everything be smoother. But, would that happen in real life, right?

And I think just an obvious thing that might happen is some airline might say “Way more people live in Austin today than used to, but fewer people live in Saint Louis than used to. So instead of my plane going from Chicago to Saint Louis, I want to send it to Austin.” And then the senator from Missouri is going to be like, “fuck no.” And so then you can’t allocate the airplanes correctly.

Jerusalem Demsas

I mean, this literally happens right now with EAS subsidies. Like there’s a study…

Matthew Yglesias

It happens constantly!

Jerusalem Demsas

But there’s a study that shows that the subsidies are the biggest in cities represented by elected congresspeople on the House transportation committee.

Matthew Yglesias

Yes, I mean, it happens all the time when you have this kind of regulated function. So now I want to quote Phil Longman’s 2025 article about this. And he says, “it’s not hard to imagine, for instance, politicians from both parties who represent ‘flyover’ cities banding together to demand more equitable, affordable and practical air service through a new regulatory regime, perhaps one that takes advantage of AI and other modern data processing tools to make pricing and other regulatory functions more precise than under the old CAB system.”

I think that’s incredibly naive, whatever, whatever. But, to be clear, the guy who has this take about how we need to do central planning, right, and that we’re going to use AI to make the central planning really good, his job title is that he’s policy director at the Open Markets Institute.

And this is why I’m so in on my tinfoil hat. Because I think that a lot of people are genuinely confused as to like what this debate is about, because if it was called, if Phil Longman’s organization was called the Central Planning Institute and the American Economic Liberties Project was called the Technocratic Regulators Can Improve on Market Outcomes Institute, then nobody would be confused why they would be like…

Jerusalem Demsas

Why they were beefing with abundance, yeah.

Matthew Yglesias

Why do the advocates of central planning dislike this mild-mannered like zoning change? And we could say, like, listen, we are like having a debate, right? It would also help explain why this is a debate inside Democratic Party politics, right? It’s a debate about whether what it means to be a center left political party should be to be a capitalism and welfare state party or it should be a central planning party.

Jerusalem Demsas

As an aside, I do actually wonder whether AI makes like the Hayekian argument that, like, you can’t actually have a regulator that knows enough to centrally plan properly, whether that actually really undermines that.

Matthew Yglesias

I mean, I think that is a really important debate that we should discuss at some point. And this is like, do you know Francis Spufford’s book Red Plenty.

Jerusalem Demsas

Oh, yeah.

Matthew Yglesias

Because that’s kind of what it’s about. It’s like “Can we make central planning work with better computers, right?”

Jerusalem Demsas

But like I want to be clear, there’s so many normative assumptions baked in. It’s not even just central planning, which I think is also part of what’s going on here. It’s also: You could do central planning for the purpose of maximizing efficiency and output.You could have a goal of saying, “OK, we actually would want these planes to route two places where there’s high population centers. We do like the fact that there’s like this growing industry in Silicon Valley. And we do think that Charlotte is clearly a growing hub for whatever reason, like biotech and the research triangle or whatever it is. And we’re going to do central planning for the purpose of maximizing growth and output.”

But they have a specific normative judgment, right? That actually there is a specific, I don’t know if it’s a nostalgia for the way that the economy looked at this particular point in time under New Deal prominence? And it’s not even just the specific policies, it’s also the fact that Cincinnati was a major city, it’s that Detroit was a major city.

These things are very important to them for some reason. And there’s so many normative judgments about that that I think that are really unstated, which is like, “Hey, efficiency is not just an annoying technocratic term. It speaks to the fact that everyone gets to pay less.

And I just think it’s really weird that in this moment of affordability — where inflation is the driving concern that people have in the electorate on both the right and the left — that this movement, which is quite literally, objectively about raising prices for the purpose of paying for things that they think are worth it, is trying to cast itself as this affordability hero. And I don’t know, like…

Matthew Yglesias

I mean, again, again, this is why I think the tinfoil hat is necessary because the whole watchword of affordability is interesting, right? Because in a literal sense, right, like, can I afford, you know, this laptop, right? That’s a function of the price of the laptop and my income.

So, affordability is just economic growth, right? Like, when there’s economic growth, there is more affordability. So why do we have to talk like — Why do we have to obsessively invoke the word affordability, right?

And it’s like, “Well, because if you get people to not think about economic growth and you just get them on some other track, that’s like affordability, affordability, affordability, then maybe price controls is the answer, right?

Jerusalem Demsas

Well, I think, also, that there’s a short-term problem. Like, economic growth, correct, is the fundamental driver of affordability, but it does so, like, not tomorrow.

Matthew Yglesias

Yeah yeah yeah, right. Well, right. But I mean, it just means we’re not having a conversation anymore about the long-term and the short-term and inflation and blah, blah, blah. We’re letting all these other ideas in through the backdoor, right? So, theoretically, the idea of the CAB price control regime could be to prevent people from exploitation at the hands of airlines, right?

When there happens to be an oil price shock, that’s not the case. But again, like Longman’s article is full of this idea that people are getting gouged and that passengers are suffering, and none of it’s true because he is asserting that the failures of the CAB system are down to the amount of computing power that exists there, rather than that this is a fundamentally unsound way to run an economy.

And this practice that Buttigieg was invoking in the Politico article of just “Spot the deviation from total undifferentiated commodity,” and then be like, “Now we’re bringing in the central planning.” Like, it’s a really bad way to make economic policy, and…

Jerusalem Demsas

We aso haven’t talked…

Matthew Yglesias

I know that I sound crazy, but I really need people to understand that these are not disputes about “Are monopolies good?” or “Should the government enforce antitrust rules?” It’s disputes about “Do we want an open, competitive market in which companies fail, in which companies try to reduce labor costs and other things and succeed? Or do we want to create this regulated economy that, yes, lets us put forward alleged social goods, like we want to prop up the economy of Cincinnati,” and even things that I would agree are good, right? Like you could steer toward, like greener aviation if you were controlling every aspect of the system. It’s just, it’s incredibly costly.

Jerusalem Demsas

I also think that we… I mean, to me, the real tell of all of this is when I started doing some research on what happens in other regional markets. Like the U.S. is one of a handful of major aviation markets that combines two of the most protectionist policies that are obviously anti-competitive. One is a ban on cabotage, which is like foreign carriers can’t fly U.S. points — fly between two U.S. points and…

Matthew Yglesias

Right, so Emirates can’t go Dubai to New York, then New York to LA, and then back around…

Jerusalem Demsas

Exactly. And, also, there’s a hard cap on foreign ownership of U.S. airlines: Twenty-five percent voting stock, 49% equity. And, you combine that with the fact that chapter 11 lets weak U.S. carriers restructure rather than die, and you get a system that’s really, really good at keeping the existing four players.

And I looked to see if there was any evidence that any of these people in the anti-monopoly wing of this party have really come out against cabotage and for allowing foreign airlines to compete, and it just doesn’t exist. Like, in the EU, for instance, starting in 1992 through like 1997, they’ve created a true single aviation market.

So, like 20 years after we do it, they do a bunch of liberalization of their own airline industries. And then that creates real competition. So Ryanair, EasyJet, Wizz, Vueling can base aircraft and run domestic routes anywhere in the EU bloc. And the result is that the top five European airline groups control about 45% of the market versus 80% for the top four in the U.S.

And I’m just sitting here like, “OK, I like to take people at face value. I don’t like to read a lot into what they’re saying here,” but it is very weird to me that perhaps the single biggest unlock, not just for lowering prices for consumers, but also for raising competition, is basically unspoken by the anti-monopoly wing, I think in large part because they’re really allying themselves with very much more nationalistic elements of U.S. policy and politics.

But it’s just bizarre. And I don’t know, like, I think, also, when you look at the European market, it’s just, at some level I’m like, “You know, there’s this belief that, you know, the fact that Spirit goes bankrupt, the fact that they’re bankruptcies in U.S. airline industry all the time is really bad.”

It’s like, “OK, I don’t really know, if I have this thing in my head about how much bankruptcy is OK. Like, why? How much… Does it hurt consumers is my question — I don’t really care if a company goes bankrupt generally.”

But airline death in Europe is really routine in the last few years: Alitalia. Flybe, twice. Norwegian’s near collapse. Monarch. Thomas Cook. Air Berlin. WOW. Primera. Cobalt. Fly PLAY. Braathens. I mean, these are all going bankrupt….

Matthew Yglesias

Because if you think about a competitive market, right? If I was to be like “Wow, over the past 10 years, a lot of restaurants in America have closed.” You’d be like, “Sure. And a lot of restaurants open,” right? And in a recession, more restaurants close than open, and in a growth cycle, more open than close. But that’s what competitive markets are like. That’s what anti-monopoly is.

Jerusalem Demsas

And there’s a legit paper about bankruptcy and pricing behavior in U.S. airline markets from AER.

Matthew Yglesias

Ooh, fancy.

Jerusalem Demsas

I’ve got all my papers, Matt. I did my research. Matt does not want me to factmaxx so much. He wants less factmaxxing.

Matthew Yglesias

I don’t approve of facts. I’m all theory.

Jerusalem Demsas

But they look into what actually happens when a carrier enters chapter 11; they find that fares dip briefly, healthy rivals often don’t follow, and when bankrupt airlines abandon routes, competitors fill the slots quickly. They find no statistically discernible service decline at small or large airports when a carrier with operations there enters bankruptcy.

So if the whole point of anti-monopoly is to be focused on the consumer — and consumers having lower prices, better experiences, whatever it is — if that’s literally what’s happening here, then why do they care so much about whether there’s bankruptcies? And I think that, to me, this brings back my point about this fundamentally conservative nature of this movement, because it’s there’s this sense that the existing institutions have to be protected, the specific things that are going on right now or the 1970s or the 1950s or the 1930s or whatever, that was good. And like we have to get back to that. And it’s just like, I don’t know, I’m not sure if it’s better that more people live in Detroit than live in Washington, D.C. I don’t, I don’t know. I feel like it’s like: Freedom is good. People can make decisions.

Matthew Yglesias

Shots fired. Should we move on to peer review?

Jerusalem Demsas

Yes. Peer review. So peer review is a segment of this podcast where Matt and I look at an interesting new white paper.

Matthew Yglesias

You have. You kept wanting to quote academic research in the main segment and it ruins it! This is when we’re talking about it.

Jerusalem Demsas

You got to factmaxx all the time. Theory doesn’t take you all the way.

This is an interesting paper. So this paper is about whether intra household bargaining power shows up in what couples buy, and whether gender specific labor market shocks shift consumption toward whatever spouse got, the better outside option.

They do these two —they’re like two natural experiments they’re looking at. One is the industrial robot adoption, which disproportionately displaced men in routine manufacturing. And then they also look at the fracking boom, which also disproportionately raised wages for less educated men. And they find that a standard deviation increase in robot exposure narrows the male female spending gap by $20, 4% of the mean, shifting away from male-coded goods and toward female-coded goods.

So, basically, as men are getting fucked over by the economy or male-specific jobs or male exposed jobs or labor markets that are more likely to be messed up, like, in the household, spending shifts towards like female-coded goods. I guess, the theory here is, obviously, like the literal market power within the household, but is shifting towards women.

Matthew Yglesias

It’s not just, you know, people say “coded” all the time now, but what they actually do is they look at what do single men and what do single women buy, right? And so then they show that when…. Then they look at married couples, right? And so, within a couple, if men’s earning power declines, the household consumption starts to look more like the consumption of a single woman.

And then with fracking, it’s the opposite.

Jerusalem Demsas

And, I mean, you can read this a lot of different ways. One thing that’s interesting about the paper is they find that the effect is stronger in college-educated couples, which indicates to me this is an egalitarian mechanism when you’re looking at the power of women going up. And so, you could just imagine, right? Like, a world where there’s, you know, you’re talking about 1950s, 60s; last week our episode was about Betty Friedan’s The Feminine Mystique. And you have a male earner and he controls the money, and he has a bank account, and the woman maybe has specific access to parts of the budget.

So more of the budget is literally controlled by the male spending decisions. So even if, at the grocery store, you’re making choices between two different things, maybe the guy is buying, making those decisions more so.

But in a world where women are also earning and now maybe earning more in some cases, or at least are earning more relatively to their previous market positions, they now have more power. So it’s potentially an interesting story. I think it’s interesting from an AI perspective too.

Matthew Yglesias

Yeah. I mean, I guess my quibble, doubt, I don’t know, something about this, right, is when I think about intra-household bargaining over this kind of thing, I feel like, I get why they did the paper this way. Because, you know, always when you’re trying to do empirical research, you need a tractable question, right?

But this kind of shift from boy stuff to girl stuff I think is more, it’s probably smaller than the question of who is actually making decisions around genuinely joint expenses, right? So, like, so much stuff isn’t gendered in that way. Single men, single women have refrigerators, they have stoves, they have cars, right? We’re spending, you know, all spending money on this stuff. And then couples buy that stuff together. And, you know, we reason together and we reach a mutually agreeable decision.

Jerusalem Demsas

Hopefully.

Matthew Yglesias

Hopefully. But like, clearly, I mean, on an informal basis, somebody is getting more say than somebody else over these kinds of calls. And I feel like that’s where the bargaining is, to some extent, most intense. Precisely because it has to be bargained, right? I mean, if the thing is….

Jerusalem Demsas

You’re not going to have two fridges, yeah.

Matthew Yglesias

And you know, different people just manage…

Jerusalem Demsas

I don’t know; rich people have that garage fridge, which I learned about.

Matthew Yglesias

You can always have, I have two fridges.

You can always manage these things in different ways, right? But if you have a two earner household, maybe both people just have some of their own money and then they have some pooled money. And so, sure, if one person in the household starts making more money, maybe they’re totally discretionary expenses.

You know, I don’t know, what do men buy? I’m like buying more scotch or something. But, you know, the bargaining is like, you actually do need to reach an agreement about your couch, your fridge, which house do you buy?

Jerusalem Demsas

I do think, too, the part that was perhaps most concerning to me was the impact on marriages and on fertility. So, shocks favoring women shift consumption and they also destabilize marriages, and shocks favoring men shift consumption weakly and they raise fertility within existing unions.

And there’s a not-creepy way of interpreting that, which is that men get wealthier, maybe the woman’s able to now stay at home and have a kid without having some kind of problem with their earning, right? Like a shock favoring men, increasing the number of children.

Matthew Yglesias

Yeah, sure.

Jerusalem Demsas

Yeah. If the shack favoring men ends up increasing the fertility, that could be a positive. Like, “Oh, great, honey, you got a raise. Now we can have a kid, right? Like, that could be good.

But it indicates to me that, also, that the fact that shocks favoring women destabilized marriages indicates that there are unhappy women in marriages who, without money, are not able to leave their husbands.

The other interpretation is just that, in a marriage, men and women need to bring things to the couple for stable relationships to form. One of which is: You should be employed and have a job and that’s important to take care of family. And so, it’s really bad that when someone loses their job, or maybe, you know, the fracking boom, etc. like that sort of thing will break up households because the number one financial stressor in marriage is finances. So there’s lots of ways to interpret this stuff.

Matthew Yglesias

Well, but wait, wait, wait. Let’s give due credit to everybody for just being normal here because the.

Jerusalem Demsas

Was that not normal?

Matthew Yglesias

No no no no no. Just because the shocks that they had, right, the male-favoring shock is a positive shock. It’s like, because of fracking, now working class men can earn higher pay. The female skewing shock was a negative shock, right? They don’t have in the study the question “What happens if something occurs that raises women’s…”

Jerusalem Demsas

Well it’s like shocks favoring women relatively.

Matthew Yglesias

We don’t know what would happen, right, if you had the opposite. Like, fracking for ladies, you know, so that women’s earnings just go up, right? Now, one thing that could happen is that could destabilize marriages. Maybe with their greater earning power, these wives are like, “Fuck it, I don’t need to put up with this guy and his bullshit anymore.”

But it also could be that it’s like the male earnings boost. And they say, “Now we can afford a nicer stroller or more car seats or babysitting, and so, we should have more babies. We just honestly don’t know. The research doesn’t answer it.

Jerusalem Demsas

Well, it’s in the table. It’s in Table A8 of the paper. Oh no

Matthew Yglesias

Oh no.

Jerusalem Demsas

I don’t know if you went to the table Matthew.

Matthew Yglesias

I didn’t see the table.

Jerusalem Demsas

You didn’t see the table.

Matthew Yglesias

What happened in Table A8?

Jerusalem Demsas

You’re not factmaxxing enough.

Matthew Yglesias

I’m not.

Jerusalem Demsas

Basically, there’s — a one standard deviation increase in robot exposure produces a 3% decline in the marriage rate, a 13% increase in divorce, and a 6% decline in the number of children.

Matthew Yglesias

Right, but I’m saying that’s a negative shock to men, it’s not a positive shock to women.

Jerusalem Demsas

Oh, I see what you’re saying. OK, yeah.

Matthew Yglesias

So we just don’t know, right?

Jerusalem Demsas

But, I mean, this model implies some stuff.

Matthew Yglesias

Uhhhh.

Jerusalem Demsas

You don’t think so?

Matthew Yglesias

I’ve been pushing to do the gender slop argument with you for a long time.

Jerusalem Demsas

This isn’t gender slop. These are facts.

Matthew Yglesias

Yes, these are facts. I mean, these are — this is the way of the world.

Jerusalem Demsas

All right. If you guys want to hear a gender slop podcast — no, we’re not going to give you any more information about what that means — shoot us a note.

Matthew Yglesias

No no no no no no, wait wait wait wait. Because I just, I want to go back to enjoying purchases, though, because, you know, we talked about Feminine Mystique last week. And, you know, one of the things Friedan is really geared up about is how much products are marketed toward housewives, right? Because at least her perception of the 1950s economy is that, even though women had, like, very little financial control under that paradigm, they were the people making the purchasing decisions about all these appliances and things like that. And that’s why there was so much marketing focused on them.

Which is just to say, it’s why I think that, without looking at the balance of decision-making over genuinely joint purchases, we’re not really plumbing the depths of what’s occurring here. We’re really just seeing that, if people have more money as an individual, they do more individual discretionary spending rather than fully sharing it equally with their spouse, which is interesting, but I don’t know.

Jerusalem Demsas

Matt wants to be really faithful to what we can know, and I’m trying to go by beyond that, to the theory. What happened to theory?

Matthew Yglesias

I love theory. I just think we already knew from theorymaxxing that, you know, “I don’t want no scrubs,” and so, if industrial robots render you unemployable, like…

Jerusalem Demsas

It’s going to be a problem.

Matthew Yglesias

You’re gonna have problems in life, yeah.

Jerusalem Demsas

All right, well, that’s another episode of The Argument. I will see you next week.

Matthew Yglesias

Well, please like, subscribe, share the podcast, tell the world we need to purchase discretionary consumer goods here.

Jerusalem Demsas

Yes we do.

Matthew Yglesias

Bye.

Jerusalem Demsas

Bye.

Claude’s Response

This is the rare Yglesias mega-essay where the genre actually fits the argument — the case he’s making requires both the historical sweep (1935 Federal Aviation Report through 1978 Carter-era deregulation through the 2010s neo-Brandeisian revival) and the close textual reading of contemporary movement documents (Longman’s “AI-enabled” CAB-revival fantasy, Buttigieg’s Politico conversion-narrative, Sitaraman’s book), and bundling those into a single conversation lets him do a kind of forensic political-economy work that a 1,200-word column wouldn’t sustain. The core empirical claim — that the CAB regime was explicitly an anti-competition framework justified by a “ruinous competition” theory and that the price comparisons inside vs. outside CAB jurisdiction make the deregulation case essentially open-and-shut — is correct and load-bearing. The Borenstein-Rose finding that airline price volatility is fully explained by ordinary macroeconomic models without recourse to “this industry is structurally broken” theorizing is the cleanest single rebuttal to the neo-Brandeisian case, and Demsas is right to feature it.

The strongest moment is the cabotage point. If your stated goal is more competition in U.S. aviation, the largest available lever is letting Ryanair fly Chicago–Miami or Emirates fly Dubai–New York–Los Angeles–Dubai, and the fact that the anti-monopoly movement has not embraced this — Yglesias and Demsas can’t find a single major figure on the record for it — really is diagnostic. It suggests that the operative commitment is to a particular kind of American airline industry with particular labor and ownership characteristics, not to competition per se. That’s a defensible commitment, but it’s a different one from what’s being marketed, and forcing the distinction is genuinely useful.

The piece’s most contestable move is the cleanness of the “central planning, not competition” framing. There’s a real middle position the discussion doesn’t engage: that some industries with high capital costs, network effects, and natural-monopoly tendencies (railroads historically, broadband today, possibly aviation) do exhibit market failures that pure deregulation doesn’t fix, even if the CAB framework was a bad answer. Yglesias and Demsas treat the choice as binary — either you accept that airline markets work like restaurant markets, or you want to revive Civil Aeronautics Board central planning — and that excluded middle does a lot of work in their argument. A serious neo-Brandeisian would say the goal is targeted competition policy (anti-cartel enforcement, slot-allocation reform at major airports, gate-control unbundling at hubs, mandatory interlining) that doesn’t require either pre-Carter price-setting or the cabotage liberalization Yglesias likes. The piece doesn’t engage that version because it’s targeting the maximalist Longman/Sitaraman wing, which is fair enough, but the argument’s apparent force depends on letting the maximalists stand in for the whole school.

The Stoller dimension is also worth flagging given cross-week continuity. Last week’s synthesis adopted Stoller’s “sabotage merger” framing on Spirit, and this week’s Yglesias piece is essentially a 13,000-word reply arguing the opposite — that the Biden DOJ’s merger block didn’t kill Spirit but also didn’t accomplish what it was supposed to accomplish, and the deeper Stoller-Khan-Sitaraman intellectual project this episode is part of is not what it claims to be. The synthesis-composer reading this week should treat the Yglesias/Stoller disagreement as a genuine open dispute the project is tracking rather than collapsing to either side.

The “peer review” segment is the loosest part. Yglesias’s methodological objection to Demsas’s reading of the household-bargaining paper — that the study only contains negative-shock-to-men data and doesn’t actually test what happens with positive-shock-to-women — is technically correct but Demsas’s Table A8 catch (the marriage-rate decline and divorce-rate spike under robot-exposure shocks) is the more interesting datum and the conversation never really resolves what to make of it. The segment reads like padding to fill the podcast format rather than a peer-of-the-main-argument finding.

Back to summary

Chartbook 448 Price controls, nationalization, financial repression, fiscal-monetary coordination - embracing the Four Horsemen of Heterodoxy

Adam Tooze · 2026-05-11 · Original →

If the current state of the world economy is marked by stark cognitive dissonance, if what is not sustainable cannot be sustained, then what comes next?

One answer was given at the weekend in a piece in the FT by Gita Gopinath who recently returned to Harvard after stints as first deputy managing director and chief economist of the IMF:

Policymakers would do well to use this period in which stock markets seem disconnected from heightened risk to craft a new playbook for crisis support that is both fiscally sustainable and supportive of long-term growth. The experiences of the pandemic and Ukraine war provide a valuable lesson in what that should look like: support targeted to the vulnerable; bailouts only to companies that are liquidity constrained but otherwise viable — and whose failure poses systemic risks; and co-ordinated fiscal and monetary policy so they do not work at cross-purposes. If a new course is not charted, governments constrained by fiscal space may rely on heterodox measures, including broad-based price controls, financial repression, nationalisations, and pressure on central banks to absorb fiscal risk. None of this would be good for the economy with synchronised sell-offs across stocks and bonds as markets realise that the backstop they were counting on is no longer there.

Yesterday I read Gopinath in terms of the pervasive cognitive dissonance of our current moment. Today I want to draw attention to Gopinath’s terse denouement. To repeat:

If a new course is not charted, governments constrained by fiscal space may rely on heterodox measures, including broad-based price controls, financial repression, nationalisations, and pressure on central banks to absorb fiscal risk.

What Gopinath sees as being at risk are the last guard rails of what used to be called the “Washington consensus”. But should we be anathematizing these options, or embracing them?

I am encouraged to return to Gopinath’s op ed by this tweet by macrofinance comrade Daniela Gabor. Daniela, too, highlights Gopinath’s final warning.

As Daniela asks: against the backdrop of our current disorder, what is the alternative What is the scenario?

Say we are in a new age of “polycrisis” or “permanent crisis” (Meadway), or “overlapping emergencies” (Weber et al) compounded by the disintegration of US hegemony. In other words it is not by accident that we face shock after shock - COVID, Ukraine, Straits of Hormuz etc etc.

Say that, in the face of such crises, centrist politicians are driven, against their will, to the realization that such shocks can only be managed by large-scale and coordinated monetary and fiscal policy of the kind argued for by “heterodox” left-Keynesians (the argument of my book, Shutdown). It is not by accident, therefore, that debts are rising in stepwise shocks.

Say that centrist politicians believe they have no alternative to such experimentation, not because they actually embrace the radical political potential of such policies, but because they are threatened by the rise of right-wing populism and feel they need to “do something”.

But assume also, that they lack any real agenda of progressive politics. At the first whiff of trouble they retreat to “fiscal rules” and subservience to conservative central bankers. Then, in political terms, we will be caught in the nasty charade of reformism that is epitomized by the UK’s government under PM Keir Starmer.

To make matters worse, as Gopinath argues, the financial markets will likely begin to anticipate such policy reactions and the markets will consequently become increasingly blind to mounting systemic risks. An additional measure of financial risk will then be overlayed on top of the emergencies of the polycrisis and the blowback from the meltdown of US hegemony.

This sequence of crisis & crisis-management - emergency intervention & recovery - recovery & half-hearted retrenchment can continue for a while. But, increasingly, governments when facing the next shock and the next round of rescue measures will face fiscal constraints. This at least is what Gopinath imagines. Alarmists may argued that the UK is rapidly approaching that point. The “moron premium” doesn’t help.

So what now? A retreat to inaction is not an option. History won’t allow it. You might imagine, therefore, that anyone thinking constructively would begin to consider other forms of crisis-management that do not involved loading up the public balance sheet with more liabilities.

The main options are precisely those that Gopinath lists:

  • Price controls to prevent opportunistic profit-taking and gouging.
  • Nationalization to ensure investment and capital discipline.
  • Coordinated action by central banks and treasuries to ensure that expanded borrowing can be met without bond market panic.
  • Financial repression - negative real interest rates - to ensure that the financial legacy burden is distributed in a socially acceptable way.

But that, of course, is not Gopinath’s point. She is worried. Her last line is somewhat ambiguous. “None of this would be good for the economy … “. Why it would be bad news, she never spells out. In fact, of course, it depends. It depends on the setting and how the specific policies are implemented.

But what Gopinath actually focuses on is the question of expectations:

None of this would be good for the economy with synchronised sell-offs across stocks and bonds as markets realise that the backstop they were counting on is no longer there.

So the most urgent problem as far as Gopinath is concerned is the one that she started with: cognitive dissonance. Financial markets are priced for the continuation of a generous and ad hoc crisis-management regime. If you follow Gopinath’s argument and the urgings of those of us in the progressive camp, this regime cannot long continue. The risk is that when the penny drops, we are in for a nasty financial-market shock. Simultaneous selling of stocks and bonds, the unhinging of the market’s own balancing system, would be bad news.

The answer from the left is presumably “prepare, prepare, prepare”. The crisis management and the political constraints under which it must be conducted are not of our own choosing. It is a matter of realism to acknowledge the challenges ahead and to consider all the possible policy options. One cannot expect financial markets to welcome such a horizon. But nor would they actually want governments that simply ignored emergencies and let crises unfold. And it is unclear whether they would actually welcome a serious effort at fiscal consolidation. Did the eurozone prosper in the 2010s? Hardly! If anyone has a better idea for redressing the current fiscal imbalances than a sustained dose of financial repression (with inflation settling at 3 percent or more and financial regulations in place to prevent avoidance), let them make the case. In a world of second- and third-bests, you have to be willing to consider every option.

Perhaps this is a moment to return to the discussion five or six years ago to which Gopinath herself was a contributor, in which analysts associated with the IMF and the BIS attempted to lay out a new integrated framework for monetary, exchange rate, and financial stability policy. What the IMF and the BIS were then trying to come to terms with was the ramifying impact of the 2008 crisis and its aftermath. The shock to financial stability from the US and Europe and the ensuing wave of central bank intervention (QE) had left major emerging markets facing volatile capital flows. hey had responded creatively and effectively by stretching the boundaries of the Washington consensus. They adopted previously anathematized heterodox policy measures to respond both to immediate shocks and persistent structural features of the dollar system. And the response of the IMF and the BIS was, somewhat surprisingly, to embrace this reality and to seek to rationalize it.

As Claudio Borio put it in June 2019 for the BIS:

Emerging market economies (EMEs) have adopted inflation targeting but combined it with FX intervention rather than free floating - in contrast to what standard textbooks would prescribe. Practice has moved ahead of theory. Moreover, EMEs have complemented such monetary frameworks with macroprudential measures. The choice reflects EMEs’ high sensitivity to capital flows and exchange rate fluctuations. This gives rise to potential policy trade-offs: it can make it harder to stabilise output and inflation at the same time and to reconcile macroeconomic stability today with macroeconomic stability tomorrow. FX intervention and macroprudential measures can improve these trade-offs. The frameworks have served EMEs well, although more work is needed to put the frameworks on a stronger analytical basis and to develop their implementation. The direction of travel is clear. And theory needs to catch up.

In 2020, Gopinath and her co-authors doubled down:

Our work is motivated by two observations. First, the empirical evidence is inconsistent with some of the assumptions underlying the Mundell-Fleming framework regarding both trade and finance. Many emerging markets have dollar invoicing shares above 80 percent. This empirical fact implies that we need to consider a dominant currency pricing paradigm where export prices are sticky in a dominant currency, which is most often the dollar and in some cases the euro. Similarly, on the financial side, a vast literature establishes an array of imperfections in international and domestic capital markets. Foreign currency borrowing is prevalent, and generates a link between the exchange rate and the macroeconomy through currency mismatches and external borrowing constraints. Financial intermediaries operating in foreign exchange markets generally have limited appetite for taking on emerging markets’ currency exposure and hence, the uncovered interest parity condition breaks down. Those countries which intervene heavily in foreign exchange markets during depreciation episodes tend to be the countries where balance sheet concerns prevail, and where financial markets are not deep enough to provide hedging opportunities. In addition, imperfections in domestic financial markets, particularly in the housing sector, are well-established for both emerging and advanced markets. The second observation is that many small open economies adopt more eclectic approaches than standard interest rate se‹ing and ƒloating exchange rates to cope with shocks. For example, during the ongoing COVID-19 epidemic, many emerging markets faced capital outƒlow pressures and used multiple tools to achieve macroeconomic stabilization, with some variation across countries. While most countries lowered policy rates and eased macroprudential regulation, some complemented it with sales of foreign exchange reserves to lean against the depreciation. At the same time, a few countries relaxed their restrictions on capital inƒlows. Given their frequent and often heterogeneous use, one of the objectives of this paper is to better understand these alternative tools: what they do, how they interact, the trade-offs involved, and the characterization of policy counterfactuals. In other words, this paper aims to establish under what conditions the standard prescription of ƒlexible exchange rates still holds, and when it may instead be optimal to rely on other tools. Doing so requires going beyond the Mundell-Fleming framework. the novelty of our analysis is that we develop an integrated model that jointly considers the role of monetary policy, capital controls, foreign exchange intervention, and macroprudential regulation in small open economies.1 We characterize the use of these policy instruments as a function of shocks and both real and nominal frictions. … the objective of our analysis is then to map the combinations of shocks and country characteristics to the optimal policy mix, listed in the box at the bo‹om of the diagram.

Or as Gopinath and her co-authors put it in an accompanying blog post:

“Our analysis suggests that there is no “one-size-fits-all” response to capital flow volatility, nor is it a case of “anything goes” or that all policies are equally effective.”

This is the pragmatic spirit with which we should also approach our current moment.

Personally, I am hesitant about price controls. I can see a role for targeted nationalization, or at least the thresat of such measures. I see no good alternative to both financial repression and increased central bank-treasury coordination. Others may disagree. In any case, this is not a moment to give preemptive obedience to the diktat of financial markets or to anathematize the “Four Horsemen of Heterodoxy”. This is a moment for constructive and empirical analysis to figure out what combination of policies offers the best chance of conducting progressive and democratic economic policy in the face of huge shocks repeatedly impacting the world economy.

I love writing the newsletter. If you enjoy it too and fancy buying me a coffee once a month, you know what to do. Click below!

Subscribe now

Claude’s Response

This is the prescriptive companion to Chartbook 447, and reading them as a unit is the productive move: 447 diagnoses the bliss-trade structure; 448 says the political-economy response should be to make Gopinath’s feared future actually arrive on terms that work for a progressive distributional politics rather than against it. The central analytical maneuver — taking the IMF’s own integrated-policy-framework pivot (Borio 2019, Gopinath et al. 2020) as evidence that “Practice has moved ahead of theory” — is rhetorically powerful because it grounds Tooze’s argument in the institutional record of the very organizations that supposedly police the Washington consensus. The four-horseman framing is also disciplined in a way Tooze’s polemical pieces sometimes aren’t: he separates the four measures into distinct policy instruments, gives his own ranking, and explicitly invites disagreement on the ordering.

The piece’s weaker move is the assumption that the political coalition for any of these measures exists in any major Western polity right now. Tooze acknowledges this implicitly — the Starmer government is the example of what happens when the political will fails — but the piece’s “prepare, prepare, prepare” close treats the absence of preparation as the load-bearing problem when the absence of any plausible political vehicle is the much harder problem. The IMF/BIS analogy also has a limit: emerging markets adopted FX intervention and macroprudential measures because they had the institutional capacity (state-owned banks, FX-reserve buffers, capital-account restrictions already in place) and the political legitimacy to deploy them. Advanced economies with neoliberal-era financial sectors don’t have either, which means the “integrated policy framework” doesn’t transfer down without political conflict the piece doesn’t engage with.

The most useful project-level finding from this pair (447 + 448): Tooze has moved from describing the post-1995 consensus dissolution (the May 2 synthesis’s frame) to advocating for what should replace it. The advocacy is open and labeled as such — this is Tooze the progressive macro-political-economist, not Tooze the historian. The shift matters because it tells readers next month what frame to expect when Tooze covers any new fiscal-crisis episode. Pair this with the Voldemort-on-Threadneedle piece (Chartbook 449) for the UK-specific instantiation of the same argument: the Bank of England’s silence is the structural enabler of the Starmer-charade Tooze names here.

Back to summary

Monopoly Round-Up: What Challenging a Bowling Monopoly Says About America

Matt Stoller · 2026-05-11 · Original →

Lots of monopoly news, as usual. Big law gets snagged in an insider trading conspiracy, the politics of pricing heat up, and cabinet member Sean Duffy is doing a seven month road trip reality TV show sponsored by companies he regulates.

But before getting to all of that, I want to go hopeful. On Thursday, I co-authored a New York Times oped with the actor Mark Ruffalo on how artists are responding to the Paramount-Warner merger. They are afraid of retaliation, but they are banding together in solidarity to oppose it nonetheless. What I’m noticing, whether it’s Hollywood standing up, or the Colorado legislature just passing a bill banning surveillance pricing, or Oregon chasing corporate ownership out of medicine, we are seeing that good populist politics is bubbling up.

And with that in mind, let’s take a look at an antitrust lawsuit filed a few days ago against a private equity backed bowling monopolist called Bowlero. It may seem like a small fry, since the company is worth just a few billion dollars. But the legal conflict actually brings to bear many parts of our current political struggle. This fight is, in a nutshell, about who will control American culture, and whether we will remain, as Senator Chris Murphy notes, a lonely society, or whether we can rebuild our social and community centers.

Let’s get into it.

Bowling is classic Americana, a quintessential middle class experience, featured in classic movies like Kingpin and The Big Lebowski. 70 million Americans bowl annually, and there are a million people in 28,000 bowling leagues. Said one bowler, “The bowling alley felt to me like the last egalitarian, fun, middle-American thing.” These are community places, centers opened early to allow seniors to bowl, or to let bowling teams practice.

At the peak of bowling mania in the 1950s and 1960s, some alleys offered day care services “so parents could enjoy their game with a bit more peace of mind.” Bowling was also a field of study; sociologist Robert Putnam wrote his famous book Bowling Alone to describe changes in the American experience, using bowling leagues as the classic mechanism to create different forms of social capital.

Yet, like similar experiences, such as skiing, cheerleading, youth sports, movies, and video games, it is being rolled up by financiers, and changed from a community activity for the middle class to an upscale corporatized asset. This episode starts, as many of these stories do, in an ugly moment. In 2012, just after the financial crisis, private equity firm Cerberus and Credit Suisse bank helped supercharge a company called Bowlero, with $450 million in financing. The CEO was a man named Tom Shannon. Over the next fourteen years, Bowlero engaged in dozens of bowling center acquisitions across the country, including the brand Lucky Strike, as well as purchasing the Professional Bowler’s Association. Lucky Strike bowling center in Beverly HIlls.

This rollup was intended to change bowling from a respected sport and middle class activity to something more akin to an extractive nightclub experience. as Amos Barshad of the Lever observed, the people behind the acquisition didn’t even like bowling. “I don’t think anyone takes bowling seriously,” Shannon told Bloomberg in 2011. “Why would you?”

Today, Bowlero owns 360 bowling centers and takes in 35% of the total industry revenue. For frame of reference, the next biggest competitor has just 64. And in all but four markets, its acquisitions led to significant market share increases. Take Northern Virginia, where its market share increased from 34% to 95%. Bowlero controlled the centers in blue, it bought the centers depicted in orange in 2021. This map is from the complaint.

Bowlero has seized dominant shares in markets such as Los Angeles, San Francisco/Oakland, Santa Rosa, Seattle, Phoenix, Denver, Appleton, Milwaukee, Chicago, Grand Rapids, New York, Philadelphia, Baltimore, Richmond, and Ft Myers.

One result is higher prices. For instance, the complaint reads, “some reports show a year after Bowlero acquired Northrock Lanes, the cost of lanes, food, and beverages had tripled at this bowling center.” They also implemented dynamic pricing. As Bowlero President Lee Ekster put it, “[o]ur goal is to fill the centers on the weekends at the highest price we can” and “ultimately dynamic pricing allows you to do that.”

There is also a fundamental change in the quality of the sport. It cut less profitable hours; Lucky Strike L.A. doesn’t even open until 4pm on the weekdays. Bowlero centers feature loud nightclub-style music, with shoddy service. As the Lever noted in 2024, “The bowlers say prices have gone up. They say the pinsetters keep breaking down, and since there are no mechanics on site.” Then there are the constant attempts to upsell customers on food and drinks, and the dive into gambling through Bowlero’s MoneyBowl app. Here’s the complaint:

For instance, Bowlero launched its MoneyBowl app in 2022. MoneyBowl solicits bets from customers on various bowling outcomes (e.g., bowl a certain score) and provides odds-based payouts or, in states in which gambling laws prohibit such activity, coupons to be redeemed within the Bowlero center. Bowlero estimated to market analysts that successful implementation of MoneyBowl would increase games played by customers. And, because MoneyBowl is linked to each lane’s scoring system, it is constantly tracking customers and providing what one market report called “an attractive opportunity to learn more about the customer.”

There are also ways that Bowlero uses its scale to disadvantage rivals. The company gets special pricing from the small number of companies who provide bowling infrastructure, like lanes, pinsetters, and lane management software. It has deals with Kegel, the leader in lane conditioning oil, so it gets better prices than rivals. And a major marketing channel is the Professional Bowler Association, which it owns, hosting PBA tournaments at its own centers while charging rivals significant amounts to host.

And the plan isn’t to stop just at bowling. Since 2024, Shannon has been pursuing the acquisition of water and amusement parks. “The playbook,” he said, “is the same as it has been for bowling.” And of course, CNBC’s Jim Cramer loves the company. But that’s not the end of the story. In 2024, The Lever, an independent reader-funded outlet did an in-depth investigation of Bowlero, likely prompted by reader complaints. “For the most part, Bowlero doesn’t build its own centers,” said the piece. “Instead, it purchases existing ones and makes them over in the Bowlero style: dim lights, loud music, expensive cocktails. At Bowleros, bowling isn’t bowling. It’s “upscale entertainment.”

Then this week came the class action complaint. The lawyers are primarily using the Clayton Act, but also a host of state laws against unfair practices. What they are asking for are not just the standard plaintiff lawyer request, which is to say, money. They want a court-ordered breakup of Bowlero to unwind what they say are its legion of illegal acquisitions, an end to favorable terms from suppliers, an injunction against further mergers, and damages for bowlers. And they are seeking a jury trial, meaning they want to present this case in front of normal Americans.

And these aren’t just any old lawyers, they are part of a new generation of legal talent. The lead is Catherine Simonsen, who did merger work at the Federal Trade Commission under Khan. Before that, she was a leader in the Antitrust Section of the California government. The firm, Simonsen Sussman, is part of a new constellation of law partnerships using creative interpretations of the antitrust laws to get relief.

So there we go. It’s an ugly time, but the anti-monopoly argument is simply getting stronger and deeper. And I think that says something about how the argument over market power explains the world most of us see. Ultimately, that’s why we’re going to win.

And now, for the rest of the news round-up. This one’s got a bunch of insane stories. Oligarchs say that asking for higher taxes on the rich is a form of racism, the Secretary of Transportation is taking a seven month road trip to do a reality show sponsored by companies he regulates, and signs of recession keep bubbling up. Plus, Trump invites the Fortune 500 CEO class on his trip to China, and big corporate law firms run by Yale law grads got caught in a massive insider trading scandal around mergers.

Read on for more…

Keep reading with a 7-day free trial

Start trial

A subscription gets you:

Claude’s Response

The Bowlero case is genuinely interesting evidence for Stoller’s “Khan-trained-lawyers-going-private” thread — Simonsen’s resume and the firm’s structure are real markers of an institutional shift, and the case is plausibly winnable on its specific facts (the 34→95 Northern Virginia jump and the Lucky Strike+PBA tying arrangement give the plaintiffs concrete anchors that don’t depend on contested antitrust theory). The civic-recovery framing also has real bite: bowling alleys are declining as third places, and the operational details (4pm weekday opens, broken pinsetters, nightclub music) describe a real consumer-experience degradation that has a recognizable cause.

But two cautions matter, and the second is structural.
