One thing that I have gotten better at over time has been abiding those feelings and then recognizing that they will change. And that they will change more profoundly if I let them sit there.

But I certainly have not found warmth for them. I’ve become better at attending to them. In some places, you talk about sometimes noting feelings like that as a bell to pay attention to.

And I’ve gotten a little bit better at that. I have a physical relationship to uncertainty, which, when I feel it, I now feel that it is something that I should look at as opposed to trying to get rid of. But I have a lot more trouble when people say ——

Extend unconditional love to it.

Yes.

What about just a gesture, like touching it with your hand?

That does help me. I do that.

In other words, get away from the concept and words altogether, and just put your hand there. That can be very powerful to just do that.

Sometimes people just express affection for themselves by maybe touching the top of their head or — I don’t want to get too corny with this — but some sort of sense of being OK with yourself.

This is such a funny question I have to ask: How do you help people learn to feel what they’re feeling in their body?

It has taken me many years of therapy and meditation to even realize that I often wasn’t feeling what was happening in the body, that I didn’t have awareness of it. I was reacting to it. It was there.

Right. Right.

I had a therapist who really helped me work with this. What she realized about me was that the way I would talk about something and the way I would feel about it were very different.

When I was talking about something, she would say: Stop. Tell me the same thing, but have your hand on your stomach. Tell me the same thing, but have your hand on your heart.

It was a very powerful practice because the feeling would start to come into what I was saying.

Yes, OK. So you’re saying that for you, the physical gesture is actually very important.

It certainly helped me, yes.

I found exactly the same thing,

And that’s where I’ve come to find out that if people just use gestures, it helps a lot to soften up the situation. Touching the heart or touching where the contraction is. Put your hand where that is and have a sense of that hand being friendly.

And the heart seems to be the one that really gets to people. You could be on the street, and then someone, for some reason, they like something you just did or something, and they’ll just sort of do this — touch their hearts. I find it such a sweet thing, a way to communicate with people you don’t know on the street.

Why do you think it’s so hard to feel what we’re feeling?

A lot of times it’s trauma-related, that people close down at a young age around something or other. It’s like trying to open up a floodgate, and maybe people are scared, for one thing, to open up that floodgate.

And actually, it’s not such a great idea to open up a floodgate. It’s more a good idea to put a little hole in it so that the whole thing is a gradual opening.

So let’s say you’re there, and you’re feeling how you’re feeling. Maybe you don’t like how you’re feeling, but you’re at least there with it.

You’ve got a line that I find very evocative. You said, “I once asked the Zen master Kobun Chino Roshi how he related with fear, and he said, ‘I agree. I agree.’”

Yes, it was such a beautiful answer. It was sort of shorthand for this whole thing that we’re talking about.

I stress the warmth and the friendliness of it because people seem to need that a lot. But the fundamental thing is, if you’re saying: What are we actually trying to do here? — it’s agreeing rather than disagreeing, accepting rather than rejecting, staying with rather than running away.

What are some other ways we could say it?

Allowing?

“Allowing” is a good word, yes. Allowing rather than disapproving or criticizing.

What I like about this approach, and what seems to be attractive to people, is that it doesn’t matter where you are in the process, you can make friends with that.

For instance, it’s very common for people who have low self-esteem — which is many, many people — that they hear a meditation instruction, and then it’s just another thing to beat themselves up for, because they think: I could never do that.

So if I had an opportunity to work closely with someone, I would just say: Well, then, let’s just work with what happens in your body when you feel like you’re a loser, or you feel like you can never get it right. Let’s get at what it feels like physically to feel like: I’m always messing up. Or: I’m inadequate. Or: There’s something fundamentally unlovable about me.

Somehow, this gets right to the core of a lot of the dysfunction that they might be feeling.

Getting back to the original thing: I think we all need a lot of help to start to agree with what’s happening with us, rather than feel that it’s because it’s uncomfortable that it has to be rejected. Everybody needs a lot of time and willingness and intention to be able to hold more discomfort, to hold more pain, really.

It is still taking me a really long time to realize that what I’m trying to do when I meditate is not to change how I’m feeling.

I started meditating because I had, and have, a fair amount of anxiety and stress. I began really seriously when I was starting a company. For years and years, I was there in a practice of trying to feel differently than I felt.

I do think it is a very subtle and difficult shift, and one I’ve only begun to recognize needs to be made, to get to this place of agreement — that how you feel might change, but you’re not trying to change it. You’re trying to be in a space of accepting how you feel.

I believe you work with Will Kabat-Zinn.

Yes.

The stress reduction program created by his father, Jon Kabat-Zinn — one of the premises is he says to people: You just have to give up the idea that this is going to help you in any way. You have to give up the idea that there’s a goal here. We’re just going to be mindful of what’s happening for its own self.

That’s such a hard idea to give up. [Chuckles.]

But he must have a lot of success doing it, right? Because it’s in all the hospitals and everything.

But that is a very important part of it: You’re not trying to improve.

These are people with severe back pains, mostly, who no doctors could help. All the exercises are for themselves alone, and they’re not trying to get rid of it. I’m sure it’s very hard. But let’s just say it helps to be introduced to the idea.

People sometimes get kind of fascinated by the idea that there’s an alternative to trying to get rid of it. Why? Because they’ve spent however many years they’re alive trying to get rid of it, and it hasn’t helped. So let’s try something different.

What attracted you to this side of it? If you go through your book titles — “When Things Fall Apart,” “Comfortable With Uncertainty,” “How We Live Is How We Die,” “A Different Kind of Freedom,” “Welcoming the Unwelcome” — there’s been a real attraction for you in this idea that it’s going to hurt sometimes.

[Laughs.] I know. Yes, that’s true.

And: Let’s be OK with it hurting sometimes.

My teacher Chögyam Trungpa Rinpoche always used to say: “Lean into the sharp points.” And that’s a great phrase, I think. It expresses what we’re trying to say here about leaning in rather than pulling back.

Sometimes it’s just really physical. You have the idea: OK, this is really hurting.

And so some people would say: I lean into it.

Other people would say: I stop resisting.

For me, that’s what it is.

I’ve learned that anything unpleasant, I can feel that I’m resisting. I don’t want it to happen. And then I just go through this process — which I’ve done so many times now that I can actually do it — of relaxing with it. Physically not resisting. Unknotting the stomach.

Walk me through that process. I believe you have back pain?

I do, yes.

We were talking about back pain a minute ago, too.

When you’re in pain, what do you do? Some part of you must not want to feel the pain. What then happens in your mind and body?

I stand up, I stretch, I do physical therapy, things like this.

So you’re not just accepting it and letting the pain be there. You are trying to change it, too.

I’m doing those smart things, or what the doctors recommend. [Chuckles.] And those things are really helpful. I’m a big physical therapy fan around physical pain.

But the attitude is the main thing. So I’ve given up the idea that maybe it’s all going to go away, and I live more with the idea of: This is what I’m going to be living with for the rest of my life.

That’s a whole different, more relaxed attitude about it. You do the physical therapy and things like that, but the attitude is, we might say: agreeing. You might say: Making friends with.

But for me, what I catch is when I resist, when I don’t want it. I can feel that physically. And then I lean in.

What does “lean in” mean?

I’ll give an example. I don’t know if it’s going to answer your question.

Chögyam Trungpa once gave a talk, and the topic was collaborating with reality.

He gave an example that I was very familiar with, living in Nova Scotia in the wintertime, of walking in the winter when the snow and sleet is coming in your face, and it’s extremely unpleasant, and your whole body is as if you’re in the dentist’s chair. You’re just tensing up.

Leaning in means you physically stop resisting what’s happening, and you relax with it. The thing is that the contrast is so great between resisting and then relaxing that somehow it’s not that hard to do.

Because it’s so tangible, this resisting thing. I can feel everything in me is pushing away, and that’s fruitless. It’s not going to help at all. Whereas, when I just let it be what it is and stop tensing against it, then it becomes totally fine.

I think about this when I walk home with my kids. Half of the days of the week I do pickup. I have a 4-year-old and a 7-year-old. We live in New York, and it rains ——

Like yesterday.

And when we walk home in the rain, what happens is I’m there trying not to get wet, and they’re like: Puddles!

They’re trying to jump in every puddle, and if they have their rain boots on, that’s great, and if they don’t, I’m like: Don’t get your shoes wet!

But I’m often tensed up against getting wet, and I’m going to get wet one way or the other, and they’re playing in the rain. They’re collaborating with reality.

That’s right.

And I’m resisting reality. I don’t want to get wet, and they’re like: There’s so much water here. That’s so fun!

It is a subtle shift in mentality, I think. For the kids, it’s natural, and then somehow we lose it as we get older, it seems. But then, you can kind of go back. You can begin to be more joyful about what’s happening.

I just had this experience with the sleet and everything. I always use this image — it did feel like I was in a dance with the storm. There was something very joyful about it.

Funny — I think that’s what it was. It became sort of funny. I felt like I was in a New Yorker cartoon or something. But it’s more like your kids with the puddle that becomes enjoyable rather than a battle.

“Struggle” is a helpful word. You find yourself struggling, and basically, you pause, and you find your way to not struggle.

You have a line that I think is interesting, where you say, “When we resist change, it’s called suffering.”

I often find Buddhist teachers make this distinction between suffering and pain. I’d love for you to talk a bit about that, the difference.

Pain is: You put your finger on the burner, and there’s pain — you pull away.

And there are many examples — I have back pain, or whatever it is. So that’s pain. It’s direct experience.

Then there’s suffering, which is all the story lines that we lay on top of it. I call that unnecessary suffering, actually. Suffering in this case is optional because it’s based on the story lines you’re telling yourself.

People are curious. We talk about back pain — spiritual discussions about back pain. [Chuckles.]

But one of the things people are curious about is how to be with the experience without all the story lines. Because they’re saying to themselves things like: Oh, this is going to get worse, and I’m going to be disabled. Or: I’m not going to be able to do my work because of this. Or all sorts of disaster scenarios that are causing them so much suffering. That’s optional, that part.

I take this as a very important part of Buddhism, generally, and of your teaching, in particular, — working with this layer of resistance to what’s happening.

I struggle with this a tremendous amount, and it’s something I’m trying to work on, where I’m in a situation that exists. It’s not a situation that at this point I can change. I have created the schedule. I’m going to the thing. I have back pain, too — I’m feeling the back pain.

Or there’s something in the future that I’m worried about happening, but it may not happen. I was just doing a governor’s forum in California, and I was worried on the flight out that I was losing my voice. I didn’t end up losing my voice, but I worried about it a lot.

There’s this layer of experience that for me is resisting — trying to make it different than it is, when I can’t.

On the one hand, I think I’ve become more attentive to how much suffering comes out of that. But I’m curious, again, in a very physical or tactical way, how you drop that layer. Because for me, the impulse to try to solve every problem, or to treat every moment like a problem to solve or to perfect, is very deep and reflexive.

Though would you say it’s possible to keep it going if you don’t keep feeding it with a story line? Is it dependent on a story line?

When you say to keep feeding it — to me, I am not feeding it. The story line feeds itself.

It takes an enormous amount of mental energy for me not to have worried thoughts feed themselves. I don’t want to be thinking about this; I’m not trying to do it. [Chuckles.]

Worried thoughts do feed themselves, absolutely.

There is this part of “Another Kind of Freedom” — which is my commentary on Trungpa Rinpoche’s book — which may have had a big effect on me. He talks about there being nothing wrong with — in this case it was negativity — but let’s just say, nothing wrong with back pain or nothing wrong with worrying about the future. Nothing wrong.

But the problem is what he called negative negativity. On top of worrying, there’s judgment about worrying, and it goes way down the rabbit hole, right? I think in your case, like on the airplane, it would be almost like meditating, getting back to meditation.

I don’t know what you do when you meditate exactly, but do you have an object of meditation often?

No, I tend to do noting. I continuously speak either aloud or mentally about what I’m aware of at that moment and from which sense.

So I’m aware of looking at you. I’m aware of hearing the sound as you affirm what I’m saying. I’m aware of feeling my fingers touch each other right now.

Just letting everything come into awareness, but doing nothing about it. [Laughs.]

Right. I think I could work with noting, too. But just in terms of more familiar ground for me, if what you did was say: OK, I’m going to gently note or be aware of my breath going out and coming in. And my intention here is to just, as much as possible, stay fully present with the breath going out and the breath coming in. Nothing forced, just natural breathing.

So then what happens is, the worried thought is like a magnet. It’s very seductive, like the sirens calling you. It keeps pulling you off. So fine, that’s what happens. So then we just keep coming back to being present with the breath going in and breath going out, and then it pulls you away again.

You’re training in noting that you’re going off and coming back. So you interrupt it, I guess you could say. You begin to get the hang of what it feels like to not continue with the story line.

And then you might find that by the time you land in San Francisco, or wherever you’re going, that there’s been a shift in your anxiety level, a shift in your obsessive thinking part, and that you’re more ready to just go in, without hope and fear, into the situation.

You’re in a different place with the whole thing, because you’ve stayed so present with what’s going on.

I’m very interested in the difference between the energy of doing something and the energy of just allowing something to be there.

To me, a lot of the exhaustion from worrying is trying to think about: Well, what can I do about it? Do I need to be sucking on a throat lozenge? When I get there, should I see a doctor?

The trying to solve it versus: It’s there. The thoughts are there. I might lose my voice.

And this is true in a lot of areas of my life. And of course, there are things in life that we want to change. You do physical therapy for your back. I work in politics. I’m trying to effectuate change, not just allow things to be the way they are.

And on the other hand, with how much I’ve trained the energy of trying to change things and solve problems and act and optimize — it has made me realize how untrained, for me, and how unfamiliar the energy of just letting things be is.

I’m sure it’s very unfamiliar. But are you attracted to it?

Yes, we wouldn’t be having this conversation if I weren’t attracted to it. [Chuckles.]

Do you find that you can do it sometimes? Just be?

Sometimes.

It is a thing I’m starting to try to learn how to do. It’s been a big shift in my own meditation practice.

I think anxiety comes up a lot — I was anxious about coming over here.

What about it made you anxious?

Coming here? It’s so unknown.

You were uncomfortable with the uncertainty? [Laughs.]

Actually, I didn’t have a story line, particularly. It was just butterflies in the stomach.

My daughter asked me: Well, what are you afraid of?

And I said: I actually don’t know. I’m just having butterflies.

But I wasn’t having a problem with having butterflies. I think that’s what I’m trying to get at. It was just an automatic response. Nothing wrong with it.

It wasn’t escalating into a big story line: Oh, I’m going to be a big flop. Or: He’ll ask me, and I won’t be able to talk.

It didn’t go to any of those places. It just was, I think, we have these old habitual responses to things. Butterflies, no-big-deal butterflies, is what I’m thinking in this case.

In terms of the worry, that’s no big deal, either. But somehow, it escalates and escalates, and that’s when the real unnecessary suffering gets strong and affects you physically.

Just coming back to what it feels like in my solar plexus or whatever, with a feeling of a sense of humor, warmth, no big deal — something more along those lines — you’re interrupting the tendency to escalate. So that you’re actually kind of practicing nonresistance.

We’ve talked so much about the relationship to discomfort. What about the relationship to comfort?

I love it. [Laughs.] This is a really important question.

Let’s talk about comfort as a “comfort zone” — that’s the expression that people use. Are you familiar with that expression? [Chuckles.]

Everybody needs some time with a comfort zone because your nervous system needs it. Swimming in the ocean, all these things that soothe you. Listening to music that you love.

But there’s no growth in the comfort zone. Growth happens where it’s more uncomfortable. We call it a challenge because we’ve come up against our edge a little bit there. You want your edge to expand. In other words, if today your edge is the sidewalk, then by this time next year, you want to be able to walk five blocks or something like that.

Somebody once said to me that the amount of growth you are capable of is a direct correlate of the amount of discomfort you’re willing to tolerate.

Oh, that’s right on. That person was very wise who said that to you. That’s absolutely true.

So I guess what we’re talking about then is, to the degree that we can feel discomfort, to that degree we can grow. And grow means: Let the natural change in evolution happen rather than get frozen in views and opinions that keep you stuck in the same way for your whole life.

Meditation has been coming in and out of this conversation. What is the purpose for you of meditation? What are you trying to practice?

There could be a lot of answers to that question. I think of it as a way to get to know yourself deeply, intimately, fearlessly, with an attitude of friendliness. So a person who, let’s say, goes on a meditation retreat where they do more hours — inevitably, things start floating up. Maybe they think this is all about getting calm and blissful, but then, when they go on the meditation retreat, a lot of painful memories, regrets, flashbacks, all kinds of stuff comes up.

For instance, I once raised my hand with Chögyam Trungpa Rinpoche, and I said: Rinpoche, you’re always talking about making friends with yourself, but I’ve been meditating now for a couple of years, and I think I’m getting a lot of ammunition and proof that I am a pretty messed-up person.

And then he said: OK, so move closer to the feeling of “messed up.” That was his answer.

One of my teachers is called Tsoknyi Rinpoche, and he has this expression, “Being OK with not being OK.” I like that a lot because it’s very pithy. It’s kind of a fearless thing to see your habits, to see your emotional reactivity, to see maybe selfishness, pride, rage, about things that you thought you had worked through and all this kind of stuff.

To me, making friends with yourself is making friends with all of that unresolved stuff. Meditation provides a forum for you to be able to see yourself very clearly. And then the instruction is to agree with what you’re seeing — to not reject what you’re seeing.

What about for someone whose experience with meditation, which I think is very common, is not that they get these fireworks of self-insight, but they just realize they can’t take 10 breaths without their mind running away from them — and they’re bored?

Yes, absolutely. I was jumping ahead a little bit in terms of what I was saying.

One of the things Trungpa Rinpoche says in “Myth of Freedom” is, he has a whole chapter called “Boredom,” and the chapter is about what a wonderful thing boredom is. Why is it a wonderful thing? He says: Because it doesn’t feed the ego at all. There’s nothing about it that feeds the ego.

If you start getting bored, that is an excellent sign that your meditation is progressing. OK, sit through the hot boredom until it becomes cool boredom.

Hot boredom is what we’re familiar with — jumpiness. You want to get out of there.

Boredom has this quality of just wanting to bolt. And cool boredom is: You sit there with a feeling of boredom.

I would hear these teachings on cool boredom. Honestly, I had no clue what they were talking about.

And I use this example: I went to Mexico, where my parents had retired. My father had died. My mother liked to sit inside with all the window shades closed in Mexico, where outside of her door and the windows, it was blazing with color and action, and everything was happening.

And I’m young, so I go there to be with her, and everything in me wants to be outside there. For the first two days, I was so bored and restless, and then I realized: I came all this way to be with my mother. At some point, I just gave up the struggle, and I was just there with my mother.

It was so remarkable because I began to feel like I was sitting on a stage, and every once in a while, the door would open, and a friend would come in. They’d have this conversation, and then the door would close. And then we were in this nothing-happening zone. I just sat there with her, and then she’d start talking, and then that’s what was happening. The whole thing became fascinating.

It feels very similar to what you were saying earlier: The suffering was coming from resistance.

That’s right. The suffering was coming from the resistance. And so I learned — I said: Oh, this is cool boredom. I’m just here with it, and there’s no resistance.

I actually think time is a very interesting dimension of all of this. I mean, everybody feels uncomfortable sometimes. But really, in a way, what we’re talking about here, what you’re talking about, is being willing to feel like that for longer without acting.

You have a line where you say: The opposite of patience is aggression. The desire to jump and move, to push against our lives, to try to fill up space.

You talk about refraining as the method of becoming a dharmic person. And in some ways, I don’t understand any of this as never acting, but as taking a longer space ——

Before acting. Absolutely.

Yes, and that’s been a very important and transformative ——

Distinction ——

And insight for me. I’d be curious to hear more from you on this dimension of time and action.

Along the lines of what I was just saying, it would mean that you’re very patient.

Tell me about how you understand patience.

First, let’s start with impatience — because we might as well start where we are, right? I experience it as restlessness and, like I was saying about boredom, wanting to get out of there, wanting to move, to just get off the hot seat.

Patience would be sitting still with that restless energy. That’s how I would think of patience. Sitting still with the restlessness of the energy, just sitting there with it, like in my mother’s living room. That’s how I experience patience. Again, it’s growing your capacity to hold discomfort. Patience is part of it — it would be a necessary tool, you could call it.

Do you think that, as a general capacity, has weakened?

I’m thinking of something you wrote that I think about a lot: “Refraining is very much the method of becoming a dharmic person. It’s the quality of not grabbing for entertainment the minute we feel a slight edge of boredom coming on. It’s the practice of not immediately filling up space just because there’s a gap.”

We didn’t use to have the ability to fill the space of every gap. You were sitting in traffic, and there wasn’t a lot to do. You were in line at the supermarket, and there was nothing really to look at.

And now we have the world of distraction at our fingertips. We have AirPods in our ears. And so the daily necessity of sitting with boredom, even, has dissolved.

That’s so true.

And I think it changes us.

It’s so true, and not for the better, I would say. We’re less in touch with the richness of the world.

You were discussing with me earlier about going on the subway without your AirPods and just sitting there, and how rich an experience it was of just being there — the sights, sounds and what was happening, the drama, just the whole experience as being very rich.

Sometimes with students, very often these days, I really encourage them to, one day a week or one morning a week, or take a time when they just go offline, go to the grocery store offline, ride on the subway offline. Be there fully for what’s happening because you’re not engrossed in a movie or a podcast or anything. [Chuckles.]

Let’s not get crazy here.

[Laughs.] I’ll put you out of business, Ezra.

But you know how it is: You go on the subway, or anywhere, and everybody is somewhere else. So I encourage them to be present without their devices.

But I’m trying to be realistic and just, say, do it for this short period of time every Wednesday or every Wednesday morning, or something like that.

My granddaughter, when she was still in college, her teacher said: No devices in the room. And I said: Well, couldn’t you just turn the sound off? She said: No, because it vibrates. So they had to leave them outside.

My attention is different if I can feel my phone in my pocket. So when I have conversations, my phone is here, but it’s not in my pocket right now. It’s on the floor near me. Because my attention to you would be different if it were in my pocket. Even knowing that the sound is off, even knowing I’m not going to check it.

Exactly. That’s what she said. And then she said: I’d never realized how I was training myself to be distracted. That was her vocabulary. Because it was so different being in class without it. Even with it just in her pocket, as you say.

I think everybody can do themselves a big favor by spending some time offline and seeing what that’s like for them. You could say: Well, that’s when you get into being bored. But you could also say: Well, maybe that’s when you get into being more alive.

The subway is such a great example. It’s really, totally fascinating to just be there, because of the people, if nothing else. There’s so much happening, from when you get on to when you get off. Sometimes things you wish weren’t happening, but nevertheless.

To expand on that, we were talking earlier, off the microphone, about one thing I’ve been trying to do for the last month or so, which is just do nothing on the subway, to just be aware of what’s happening around me.

It’s interesting because, a lot of the time, I don’t really love what’s happening around me. It’s a rich experience, but — it’s a boombox, it’s somebody trying to grab my attention with music I don’t really want to be listening to, it’s the screeching of the brakes. There’s just a lot going on.

But dropping the effort of trying to find exactly the right music or podcast or thing on my Kindle to distract myself in the right way, or trying to maintain a kind of hermetic comfort — it’s easier to stop.

That’s been my big lesson from it. It’s not so much that I love every moment on the subway, but I didn’t quite notice how much energy I was expending trying to block it all out.

Do you feel more relaxed?

I do. And then I pick up my kids, and I’m more present with them because I just spent the last 35 minutes practicing being present. As opposed to practicing finding somewhere my mind would rather be.

It’s interesting. I would call that a form of meditation: You’re just present.

It’s interesting because we didn’t use to have all these devices. I grew up before television, even. But now that we have the devices, it’s very helpful, actually, to feel the contrast. And somehow, it’s richer.

You have a lovely line — I think it’s a line somebody told you: Meditation is not a vacation from irritation.

That’s right. The first time I ever went for meditation instruction, that’s what the woman said to me. Later, I saw that it was from “The Myth of Freedom” — that Chögyam Trungpa said that, and she had plagiarized. But anyway, it made a big impression on me. Because that’s right: Meditation is not a vacation from irritation. It’s just another way of saying the same kind of thing.

But I have to say, even though I was introduced to this view, or this attitude, from Day 1, it took me a lot of years to really somehow have it penetrate and get to me — the path of nonresistance. It was in there, the seed was in there, but it wasn’t that I immediately was open to everything.

Narrate that progress for a minute. Because somebody listening to this — here you are, you’re a famed nun, and the idea of moving from: I’ve never meditated — to: Whatever you must be experiencing — seems very intimidating.

How would you describe what the stages of your experience of meditation, or your relationship to it, have gone through?

That’s a difficult question to answer because I’ve never actually given it a lot of thought. But let me go back to remembering the first time I was taught to meditate. I could hardly stay with the breath for two seconds.

But that was like what I was saying earlier: It was a revelation to see how I had no idea that my mind was like that. And instead of being discouraged by that, because of what my teacher at the time was telling me and so forth, I was told: Just expect that. That meditation is not about getting rid of thoughts. There are always going to be thoughts, but you don’t have to follow them for an hour and a half.

In the beginning, my very wild mind — and when I say “beginning,” I don’t just mean the first month, but for a couple of years — maybe five years, I don’t know how long — but of course I kept at it, and I did some long meditation practices. We had these monthlong meditation practices, and I did that kind of thing.

Then what started to happen more was a very important thing — Trungpa Rinpoche used to talk about something he called the gap. Again, I didn’t really know what in the world he was talking about. But he said: It could be possible that at the end of every breath, there’s a gap before you breathe back in again.

He even sometimes gave a meditation: Natural breathing out, and then pause, create a gap, and then come back in. He called that the gap. It was supposedly very profound, but I didn’t have a clue, really, what he was talking about.

Then I was in a meditation retreat, and we had a big fan that was going all the time. The hum of the fan became background noise that was always there. I was sitting there meditating, doing my practice, and the fan was going: Mmm, mmm, mmm. And all of a sudden: Mmm. [Pauses.] Mmm. It went off for just a second.

I said: That’s the gap. That’s what he’s talking about.

Mmm. [Pauses.] Mmm. Then I understood what he meant by gap. He meant there’s all this noise, and then suddenly there’s silence.

It’s sort of like being in a sack, and it’s dark, and then there’s this little slit. And you suddenly realize: Oh, there’s a whole big space out there. It’s sort of like that.

Someone used this example: They were in a room with this teacher in Nepal, and his window was covered with black plastic. He said: Think of that as just all the discursive thoughts, this black plastic. It’s just covering over, and then you just make a pinprick in it, and then the light comes through. It’s like: Oh, there’s a background here to this whole thing.

In a breath.

He said that at the end of every breath, you could pause, and you could experience that gap. The gap of, in the sense of the fan, it was just: The sound was going, and then it stopped. So you could say in terms of chatter, it would be: Chatter, chatter, chatter, chatter, chatter. [Pauses.] Chatter, chatter. [Pauses.] Chatter, chatter, chatter, chatter, chatter.

But you’re not trying to prefer the gap.

You’re just trying to discover that behind it all — say, if the discursive thoughts and emotions and everything are foreground — there’s also a background to the whole thing that you could connect with at any moment. You could call it a stillness. You could call it an openness, a freshness.

So I have a question about this — a question I really struggle with. When I read Trungpa’s books, when I read your books, I feel like there’s a shifting back and forth a little bit between this instruction of: There is no good, there is no bad. It is not better to have mental chatter. It is not better to have spacious mental quiet. It is not better to see the light coming through the pinhole in the black plastic over the chatter of the mind. It is not better to be looking at the black plastic.

There’s this nondualism: Everything is totally fine, and in some ways the same, because it’s all the ground of experience. And maybe it is better. [Laughs.]

So why do we even talk about the gap, then? I guess because it’s there.

But I think I’m asking at the underlying thing that is there. I feel like sometimes there is a conversation there, something that’s about better and worse. Or that nothing is better, and nothing is worse. And then sometimes something seems to be being described that is better.

Yes, you’re absolutely right about that. And that’s where a sense of humor comes in. [Laughs.] And the ability to be OK with paradox and ambiguity, and things not always being so neat and tidy like that.

Because if you know the basic thing, that struggle and polarization — pitting one thing against another, trying to get better, which comes from the place of feeling that you’re not good enough to begin with — that as long as you have the idea that we’re moving in the direction of: You’re OK just the way you are — and that’s a Suzuki Roshi joke.

Suzuki Roshi was a Zen master in San Francisco — he started the San Francisco Zen Center — and he had this expression. He looked out at the audience, at his students, and he said: You are all perfect just as you are, and you could use a little work. [Chuckles.]

And it is sort of like that.

And how do you understand what he meant?

I understand that, fundamentally, we have Buddha nature. That would be the traditional way to say it.

But you could say, fundamentally, everybody has this potential for awakening from the sleep of confusion — kind of glamorous language, but everybody has that potential.

You look out, and you see a roomful of Buddhas. You see a roomful of people who are awake but just don’t realize it. And so you begin to say: OK, I’m one of them, and I want to recognize more of my true nature.

But the thing is, if you want to recognize the true nature by getting rid of the ego, it doesn’t work. The only way to actually have the confusion lessen is to become familiar and intimate with yourself just as you are, which is a lot of confusion and wild-mindedness and boredom and all those things.

If you have a view that there’s nothing problematic with any of that, then you can also understand that your fundamental nature is one of basic goodness. But you’re not recognizing that, and so the work is uncovering what’s already here. It is a paradox.

Earlier in the conversation, you were talking about unconditional love. And what you were saying there, as I was trying to absorb it — the thing that came to mind is how I feel as a parent.

OK, that’s a good example.

I don’t want my children to be any different than they are. In some ways, I absolutely don’t want them to be any different than they are. I love them just the way they are.

Also, I want them to grow. There are ways that they can grow, but it doesn’t come from a desire to change them.

I really do love them as they are, and I want them to grow and learn how to do more math and all the things you do as a person. [Chuckles.] And put on their own pants.

Tie their own shoes.

But they’re both there. It’s funny — it was the only experience I could come to that had both of those in my head.

It’s a good one. You could think of yourself that way. Does that make sense?

Yes, that would be nice. [Laughs.]

You used to be that little. You don’t have to think of yourself necessarily as a little kid, but you could think of yourself as fine just as you are. Yet at the same time, you’re not wanting to harm people with your speech, you’re not wanting to harm people with your actions, you’re not wanting to be so critically minded about everything. It is paradoxical, but the basic view is that there’s nothing wrong here.

Here we’re talking about loving and changing. But the way I want to ask the question is more about the relationship between abiding and acting.

We’ve talked about abiding in difficult emotions, and I think that a question that comes up for people, and that has come up for me, is: You think about your partner, if your partner treats you badly, and you think: OK, I’m having these difficult emotions. I’m going to sit here, and I’m not going to be reactive, and I’m going to work with the texture of them and drop the story line and touch the energy of the emotion.

Yes.

And yet your therapist would say: Well, maybe the story here is important. Maybe this person is not treating you well. Maybe you’re allowing it because you don’t love yourself enough.

I’m not giving an example of my own life here. I’m just using it as an example. But there is something about the dropping of story lines that can become, or at least I fear it can become, also a way to accept situations that shouldn’t be accepted — to deny the responsibility for change.

Yes, I hear you.

How do you think about that?

One I frequently get: women in abusive relationships. And I always say: Get out of there as fast as you can. One woman stood up, and she described it as an abusive relationship with her husband for many years.

And she said: So I try to work with it by just going to the feelings, and all of this. And I said: Forget about all of that. Just get out of the relationship. You need to get out of the relationship and get some distance from it, and that is the most compassionate thing you could do for your husband. And also for yourself.

I said: This is not the time to sit there and contact what it feels like in the body. This is time to get out. Take the kids and go, and just start exploring how you could do that, how you practically could do that.

Go to your mother’s or whatever. And she actually wrote to me later, that woman, and she said that she had followed that advice and thanked me. For some reason, she was willing to just take my word for it. I guess she was probably ready. But she would have been an example of what you’re talking about, where she was just using my instructions, but staying there, being beaten.

It was a crazy situation.

So that’s an extreme situation, of course. But how then do you discern when to act? When should you be acting? When should you be taking the story line seriously? Versus: When should you be abiding, feeling, touching the energy without the story line?

Yes. That’s a really good question. This comes up a lot with protesting injustice in various forms. So that conversation I’ve had with a lot of people, and I always say what they already know. But then we have a discussion about it, and that is that you’re not effective if you’re caught in strong emotions and you’re being carried away by the energy of anger or something like this, that you just can’t be effective.

First of all, you’re not able to communicate. Someone is only going to hear your anger. They’re not going to hear your words. There’s no possibility of change. I say: Try to experiment with ways that actually start to communicate to the heart of the people you’re trying to influence for a change. Try it when you’re angry, try it when you’re not angry. Find out for yourself.

That’s the only way it really lands in the body. So I’m encouraging the people to continue with what they’re doing, but not when they’re caught up in their “kleshas.” That’s a Tibetan word for strong, destructive emotions.

And then, in that process, they might go to the body, feel what they’re feeling, get in touch — as a way of being able to then walk through the door and have the conversation that doesn’t come from that place and is actually curious to hear what they have to say, and is actually open to hearing what they have to say, and isn’t controlled by fear.

It’s more like a willingness to kind of take a leap, I guess you could say.

It’s interesting when you say it isn’t controlled by fear.

One of the things I’ve experienced with some of your work is that there were a lot of actions I was not willing to take because I was afraid of feeling the discomfort, the uncertainty associated with them. It was only when I became less afraid of feeling that, that I could take those actions.

There are many actions I could take to try to avoid those feelings, and I did take them.

It didn’t work, right? Or it did?

It worked in its own way, but yes, there were forms of change and also, even in this work, forms of conversation that were not on the table.

What do you mean: “not on the table”?

That I just wasn’t willing to sit in the discomfort of confronting a certain personal situation or risking a certain outcome or having a certain kind of political conversation across difference, because I didn’t trust my own ability to hold the discomfort of it.

It’s an ongoing process for me, certainly. But getting more comfortable being uncomfortable has opened up a wider range of space in which I can act.

There were actions I wouldn’t even really let myself think about.

That now you can?

That now I can because I’m not as afraid of them.

I think a really good way to go about this is to think: OK, where do I want to be in one year’s time? How about five years? Do I want to be stuck in exactly the same way on this day in 2027? Or on this day in 2027, will I feel that I’m able to sit with discomfort a little bit more than before?

In other words, it’s a growth process, and I think you’re actually changing the DNA in some kind of way. It’s really fundamental what’s being changed.

All these studies now about the brain and how meditation affects it — they have some interesting observations from that, and one of them is that there are grooves in the brain that we experience as habitual patterns, and every time you follow the habitual pattern in the old way, the grooves are getting deeper. And every time you even pause and consider an alternative, it opens up a new neurological pathway.

There’s an opportunity for change in that way, really at the level of your brain. I found that pretty exciting to hear, because it’s so optimistic, in a way.

So you were asking about meditation and what it is, and I was emphasizing, in this case, that it would be seeing what the habits are that you’re stuck in, that you keep making, that grow deeper and deeper, and experimenting with how to open up new ones.

It says that all you have to do is: Just don’t go down the rabbit hole, and don’t do anything else. That opens up new pathways.

Or just sit there with the feeling that is coming up in meditation. For me, some of what’s both so intense and interesting about meditation is just sitting there and not doing anything with what’s going on in my head. Which I find difficult. [Laughs.]

Right. But possible, right?

Very possible. And the more I do it, the better I get at it.

Exactly.

As we come to a close here, let me ask you about a very lovely line in one of your teacher’s books. He writes, “One’s whole practice should be based on the relationship between you and nowness.”

“Nowness” is a word I really love.

I struggle a lot with nowness. And it feels like one of those sentences that has a universe in it.

Yes. Read it to me again.

He says: “One’s whole practice should be based on the relationship between you and nowness.”

And in another place he says: “Let the thread of nowness run through your whole life.”

But you don’t like the word “nowness,” so it doesn’t communicate with you.

No, I do. I just struggle with achieving it.

Oh, I see. [Chuckles.]

It’s a wonderful word.

Well, you are achieving it on the subway without your devices.

But I would be curious to hear your reflection. This can all feel so abstract. What does it mean to have a relationship with nowness?

Basically, that you’re present instead of drawn off — and that being present itself can tune you into a bigger perspective on your life. It’s like what I was talking about with foreground and background.

An example might be the difference between being all caught up in your thoughts and going to the window and looking at the sky. It’s like the astronauts’ experiences — they’re all out there, and they’re having these amazing spiritual experiences just because they’re seeing the Earth from the perspective of vast space.

And the earlier astronauts had the same experience. They said: It’s just this one Earth. Why can’t we just all live on it together? It seems so easy from the perspective of infinity.

And then you get back down on Earth, and right away, all your habitual patterns and things click in. You’re already stuck in struggle and so forth.

So in a way, what it means with nowness is that you begin to have more of that big perspective that puts everything in perspective. And then you just go about your life. But all the time you know at the same time that Earth is a little dot, and you are nothing — tiny in the face of this vast universe that just expands forever and does not have an end.

If you do this practice in a committed way — I mean, you’ve done it for many decades — if what it’s promising is not an end to pain, if what it’s promising is not that you’ll always feel radiant joy or equanimity, what is it promising? What are you trying to achieve? Or what is achieved amid it?

Contentment. Being OK with how things unfold, even if it’s disturbing. In other words, being OK with how things unfold doesn’t mean that you wouldn’t act, but it does mean that you aren’t struggling against what’s happening.

Contentment so deep because you’re not struggling against the unfolding of your life. You’re more letting it unfold and then doing things to fine-tune it or uncover the openness and vastness of your mind, and not being all caught up in the smallness of petty grievances and criticisms and likes and dislikes.

Somehow, then, all of the likes and dislikes and everything just have a lot of room to exist, and there is a sense of less and less separation between you and your experience. And that has a lot of contentment in it.

I can say definitely that I am deeply contented with my life. I have a very good life, and there’s not a lot of horror in it or anything like that. But I do feel it comes not from the outer circumstances but from the meditation practice and working with my mind and knowing that the mind has so much power to make you suffer — or to help you stay awake and alive to your life.

I think that’s a lovely place to end.

Then always our final question: What are three books you’d recommend to the audience?

I recommend “Shambhala: The Sacred Path of the Warrior” by Chögyam Trungpa Rinpoche, which is an excellent book to be reading right now in terms of what’s happening in the world.

And I recommend “Zen Mind, Beginner’s Mind” by Shunryu Suzuki Roshi, which is one of the very first books I had ever read in Buddhism. Have you read it?

Yes.

And I recommend this book called “Enlightened Vagabond,” by Matthieu Ricard. He collected stories about this 19th-century, eccentric Buddhist master — very eccentric. The stories are totally delightful. It’s like every story has a moral, so to speak. But it’s very funny, and the man was a fabulous character.

I love those stories, and Matthieu Ricard collected them over many, many years, hearing them from his teachers. So those are the three books that I recommend.

Pema Chödrön, thank you very much.

Ezra Klein, thank you very much.

Claude’s Response

The conversation is more politically loaded than the genre-frame suggests. Klein chose this guest, in this week, after weeks of writing about the Iran war’s psychological churn, after his Hasan Piker piece on whether Democrats’ cancellation reflex costs them more than the offending statement, after the AI cognitive-surrender essay. The hinge in the transcript is the abide-versus-act exchange: Klein refuses to let Chödrön’s “dropping the story line” become a counsel of withdrawal, and Chödrön — to her credit — does not flinch from the question, conceding directly that women in abusive relationships should leave, and reframing the dharmic position as a discipline that makes effective protest possible rather than as a substitute for it. The political payload is that Klein is consciously sourcing an argument that the emotional reactivity he sees as dominating Democratic politics (Piker discourse, Platner discourse, the cancellation reflex) is itself the obstacle to the political agency the same Democrats want.

The conversation’s strongest passages are the body-mapping ones (the technical work of locating contraction, of “noting” attention) — they are concrete and reproducible in a way that Buddhist popular writing often isn’t. The weakest are the bits where Klein and Chödrön both flirt with paradox-as-evasion: the back-and-forth on whether nondualism is supposed to be normatively flat or actually does prefer some states to others (“And maybe it is better. [Laughs]”) punts on a question that matters for the dharmic ethics, and Chödrön’s “sense of humor and ability to be OK with paradox” is rhetorical retreat from a doctrinal tension. Trungpa Rinpoche himself is name-checked uncritically; for a piece that runs nearly 12,000 words with two careful interlocutors, the absence of any acknowledgment of the documented abuses in the Shambhala lineage (cited extensively in journalism since 2018) is a notable Klein omission — the surrounding piece is so explicit about the danger of accepting situations that shouldn’t be accepted that the absence reads as deliberate or careless rather than ignorant.

For project purposes, the most useful extraction is the closing exchange on contentment-without-cessation-of-pain: “Contentment so deep because you’re not struggling against the unfolding of your life. You’re more letting it unfold and then doing things to fine-tune it.” That is the same shape as the synthesis composer’s recurring distinction between the writers who diagnose without prescribing and the writers who prescribe from a place of imagined leverage — Chödrön is articulating a normative position in which the diagnostic posture is the prescriptive posture. The piece pairs naturally with Klein’s prior-week Rosenblatt podcast on liberality and his earlier AI-cognitive-surrender essay as a three-piece arc on what kind of inner discipline an exhausted liberalism requires.

Back to summary

Why kids don’t go anywhere anymore

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-05-13 · Original →

I do not think of myself as an especially permissive or relaxed parent — I only seem low-key and non-anxious compared to Kate. But I was shocked by this Institute for Family Studies survey of what contemporary American children are and are not allowed to do unsupervised at various ages.

The fact that a quarter of 11-year-olds are not allowed to leave the house unsupervised doesn’t seem all that shocking, until you realize that this means they’re not allowed to play in their yard unsupervised.¹ Leaving the yard is a whole separate category. I’ve also been surprised to see so much skepticism about the idea that nine- and 10-year-olds can be left home alone. We are a family with two parents who both have flexible schedules and just one child, so it’s not like our kid is left home alone all that frequently. But sometimes it’s the logistically reasonable course of action and what’s the problem?

The thing that really makes us outliers, though, is that our child is allowed to “leave the street” in the parlance of this poll and walk to places in the neighborhood.

Yet even though that’s the answer I would give to a pollster (because it’s accurate), I have to confess that in practice, he doesn’t often walk places in the neighborhood without an adult.

One reason for that is just that norms are self-reining and sticky; most people modulate their own behavior in part to match the behavior of peers. And if his friends aren’t allowed to walk around the neighborhood, he won’t do much of that either.

But I do think there’s more than sheer conformity at work here.

Specifically, there are three factors that people who are not currently parents of young children may underrate: Kids today have fewer places to go, street traffic has gotten much scarier, and kids just don’t want to go places as much as they used to.

The supersized vehicle factor

It’s pretty well-documented that parenting styles have become more anxious and defensive despite most kids facing fewer objective threats from their environments. Kids today almost all wear helmets while riding their bikes or scooters. Notwithstanding the 2020 shooting spike, violent crime is generally much lower than it was when I was a kid. Medical care has gotten systematically better.

Parents of course worry about their children’s safety, but in almost every measurable way kids are safer today and logically should be given more, rather than less, freedom.

But there is a huge exception to this:

The share of vehicles on the road that are S.U.V.s or trucks with raised fronts has gotten much larger.

These cars with high front ends are about 45 percent deadlier on average to pedestrians. A separate study finds that they are 44 percent deadlier to adults and 82 percent deadlier for kids. A third indicates that every four inches of additional front-end height increases fatality risk by 22 percent.

If you combine these deadlier vehicles with the fact that there seems to be a structural increase in the number of car accidents (due to drivers distracted by smartphones), then I think it is genuinely the case that the basic act of crossing the street has, in fact, become a lot deadlier for children.

It’s important to remember that safety statistics are partially endogenous. There hasn’t been an aggregate surge in children being run over by cars, but that’s in part because parents have responded to the threat of deadlier cars by becoming much more conservative in their parenting.

The objective hazardousness of bicycling in mixed traffic or of traveling on foot near busy roads has, in fact, gone up.

This is also a big deal because it interacts in an unfortunate way with questions of street design. A classic urbanist talking point highlights that a dense street grid enhances “walkability” in the sense that much more stuff ends up being within walking distance of any given point on the grid. Kids are going to walk more if there is more stuff that they can walk to. The problem is that this kind of street grid also encourages more (and faster) automobile through-traffic, making the streets less safe for kids. There’s really no way to jointly solve this problem on the level of street design.

What’s needed is a world where cars are driving at safe speeds, drivers are not distracted by their text messages, motorists are low enough to the ground that they can see children, and the fronts of vehicles are not designed to kill people. But that’s not the world we all live in.

Staying home has gotten more fun

But there’s another side to these push-pull dynamics.

Kate and I allow our son to leave the house, the yard, and the street. This means, in practice, that he sometimes walks to or from his school or the adjoining playground without parental supervision. If he had some burning desire to walk to the bookstore or the fast-casual rotisserie-chicken place or the Sephora or the mattress store or the wine bar that are also nearby, we would definitely let him do that. But he’s never asked to go any of those places.

Pretty recently, he and a friend were playing together at our house and asked if they could take a kite to the playground. We were delighted to receive such a wholesome request!

But this doesn’t happen that often.

Our house, for starters, is much larger and more full of fun stuff than the apartment I grew up in was. It has dedicated spots for kids to hang out in and plenty of toys and games. It also has (of course) digital devices. I played video games a lot when I was a kid, but the games available on today’s Switch are a lot better than the games on my old Super Nintendo. And of course I watched television a lot when I was a kid, especially when I didn’t have any friends around. But thanks to streaming, children today can get highly optimized kid-oriented content 24/7.

That doesn’t necessarily mean it’s all brainrot. Our son’s favorites are the “Nova” and “Nature” documentaries on PBS. He’s actually kind of a science programming snob, and told me he wouldn’t watch the new Netflix dinosaur documentary with me because he doubted it was scientifically accurate.² This is part of what’s good about having a bottomless pit of streaming content: It’s not just an endless parade of dreck — there’s lots of good stuff on there.

The upshot, though, is that children are less likely to get as bored as I was as a kid.

If you stop to think about it for a minute, one thing you’ll see is that this is true of adults as well. People are going to the movies less. They’re going to fewer parties.

Adults are spending more time alone —a trend that is particularly pronounced among the youngest adults, who are most habituated to modern entertainments.

Compared to 20-somethings, I think kids actually spend a large share of non-school time doing playdates, or sports and other group activities, or messing around with their siblings. But the decline in going “out and about” unsupervised in the neighborhood is in part downstream of the larger trend toward everybody doing less stuff because chilling at home has become more entertaining.

Nowhere to hide

An interesting counterpoint to this discourse is an ongoing controversy here in D.C. about “teen takeovers,” where big mobs of teens who informally organized on social media all arrive in the same location and generally get up to trouble.

This isn’t just a little mischief; there have been assaults and gunfire at these takeovers, and it’s a real problem for local businesses and residents. It’s also the kind of scene that’s going to make a lot of parents of teenagers want to restrict their kids’ freedom so they don’t get caught up in this kind of thing.

The proximate political argument here is that the mayor wants to extend curfew authority so she can crack down on these gatherings, and the progressive bloc on the D.C. Council is so far stopping her. It’ll be interesting to see what happens over the summer, but I’m pretty concerned things will get out of hand — as far as I can tell, the existing D.C. curfew policies have in fact been effective at keeping people safe in other contexts.

The critics are right to say that in some sense, curfews are just a bandaid, but I don’t really understand dismissing them on those grounds — when I’m bleeding, I happily put on a bandaid! I also think that part of the “root causes” solution has to be an idea that Council progressives really aren’t going to like:tougher penalties for individual teenagers who commit crimes.

Still, I do sympathize with the progressive view that society needs to give teens something to do and not just keep them cooped up at home every night.

Progressives’ main idea is government-funded and government-organized youth activities, which seems reasonable. That being said, “make a fun place to hang out” has traditionally been a private-sector undertaking.

And the private sector is also well-equipped to strike an appropriate balance between allowing enough rowdiness that it’s fun but not so much that it spoils the fun, and they can kick you out without throwing you in jail.

But where would kids go?

I remember spending a lot of time lingering at cheap diners, hanging out at a pool hall, grabbing a slice of pizza, and being very interested in coin-operated arcade games. And there’s just way less of this stuff than there used to be. Even as a young adult living in the same D.C. neighborhood I live in now, I used to go with friends to the jumbo-slice place that had “Time Crisis II” and everyone would play that.

But there’s a vicious cycle of kids being less independent, which means fewer “third place” spots counting on teen customers, which means that teens who are out and about are more likely to be making trouble, which leads to more restrictions on teenagers.

Along the lines of the points Caroline already made about autonomous vehicles, I think there are some potential wins here in terms of older kids being able to travel by car safely without their parents. Replacing today’s dangerous vehicles with much safer ones would also help solve the street design paradox and make it viable for more neighborhoods to be both safe for kids and connected enough that there is somewhere for them to go.

But I really do think the decline in everyone leaving the house and doing stuff is easy to underrate as one of the signature trends of our time.

My guess is that this is comparable to how the same forces of progress that spared humans from the omnipresent threat of starvation and physical hardship also generated a huge rise in obesity. We’re all — not just parents and kids, but everyone — going to have to work out our socializing muscles more.

1

To put my cards on the table, we have a teeny tiny rowhouse yard, located on a very low-traffic street, that kids are definitely allowed to play in without adult monitoring and have been ever since they were old enough to walk down the stairs safely. In Maine we have a larger yard where children roam freely.

2

Paleontology Reddit seems to confirm his suspicions.

Claude’s Response

The SUV-front-end-height stat is the load-bearing piece of evidence here and it does the work Yglesias asks of it — it gives the “helicopter parenting is irrational” thesis a genuine empirical wrinkle and grounds his argument in materially changed conditions rather than vibes. The endogeneity point about safety stats is also well-made: aggregate child-pedestrian fatalities being roughly stable doesn’t refute “streets got more dangerous” if parents have responded by keeping kids indoors. This is the kind of disciplined empirical move Yglesias is good at.

The argument gets weaker when it tries to combine three quite different mechanisms (deadlier vehicles, more compelling indoor entertainment, declining third places) into a unified story without acknowledging that they have different policy implications. The vehicle problem points to NHTSA regulation of pedestrian-impact standards and front-end height limits (which exist in the EU and Japan but not the U.S.) — a concrete, achievable, unambiguously-good lever. The “staying home is more fun” mechanism is essentially preference-driven and isn’t really a problem in the same sense; if kids genuinely prefer Switch games to wandering, that’s a different kind of “loss” than streets being too dangerous. The third-places point is structural and ambiguous — many of the venues he nostalgizes (cheap diners, coin-op arcades) had unfavorable economics that the digitalization of leisure simply outcompeted, not policy failures. Bundling these together produces a piece that nods at policy without making a specific demand.

The DC curfew detour is doing factional work that’s worth flagging. Yglesias takes the side of the mayor and explicitly endorses “tougher penalties for individual teenagers” — a position he knows will alienate progressive DC Council members and that maps onto his broader stance against soft-on-crime urban progressivism. There’s an argument for it, but the bandaid metaphor (“when I’m bleeding, I happily put on a bandaid”) is doing a lot of work without engaging the actual progressive objection, which is that selective curfew enforcement reliably produces racially disparate stops and arrests with downstream consequences for the very kids the policy claims to protect. The bandaid analogy works for individual bleeding because the bandaid doesn’t have second-order effects on the bleeder’s life chances; that’s not true of teen criminal-record exposure. The piece is strongest when it sticks with the materially-changed-conditions argument and weakest when it slides into broader generational and cultural diagnosis that the SUV data can’t bear.

Back to summary

How to know what politicians really think

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-05-15 · Original →

Had a few questions about the DC mayor’s race, so I just wanted to aggregate my answers together at the top and say that over and above my serious doubts about Janeese Lewis George’s positions on crime¹ and education,² there is a clear affirmative case for Kenyan McDuffie. I disagree with some of his takes on traffic cameras and historic preservation, but where I really agree with him is his correct diagnosis of the actual specific situation facing the city right now. We in DC were hit unusually hard by Covid because the federal government was much slower than the private sector to resume in-person work.

And then, just when the Trump administration curtailed remote work, it hammered the city’s economy with DOGE. This is having all kinds of downstream adverse consequences for the city’s restaurants and other businesses and taking a toll on working-class people. McDuffie is doing an excellent job of understanding that this is the problem we are facing and that the solution has to be a city government that is focused on reviving private-sector economic growth. These are issues specific to Washington, while George is essentially copy-and-pasting a progressive playbook out of New York or Boston, which are cities facing very different circumstances. I’ve also been disappointed to see many local YIMBY groups endorse as if we are replaying the New York mayor’s race, which featured a socialist who favored upzoning facing off against Andrew Cuomo, a “moderate” NIMBY backed by all the building trade unions.

The actual election that DC is having features two candidates, both of whom are proposing large changes to the comprehensive plan and the city’s land-use framework. George has offered a higher target number of new units of housing as part of a comprehensive pattern of saying yes to every interest group demand from every quarter, but is also proposing many different large regulatory-burden increases that will make new housing investment impossible. McDuffie’s pro-growth housing proposals are grounded in an overall pro-growth platform that I think might actually succeed because, again, he is paying attention to what the actual situation is — job losses, building closures, and capital flight — and thus is trying to solve real problems.

The Digital Entomologist: A couple weeks ago Matt said that politicians who need to BS their voters should let him know their true policy objectives so he can help them. I guess a sort of Irving Kristol’s 4 truths type of thing. Is there a way, in general, to discover a politician’s true policy objectives as just a regular voter who is interested in finding them? Or do you have be in the back rooms and have deep sources?

It is extremely hard to tell what politicians really think, not only because it is generally hard to truly know the mind and soul of another human being, but also because most politicians don’t really think that way. A good politician will have a couple of issues he cares a lot about and works hard on and is very knowledgeable about, but then mostly passes through life addressing things either on the basis of going along with his colleagues or else some specific consideration related to politics in his home state or home district.

I don’t think it would occur to the vast majority of members of Congress to seek out factual information about the climate impact of liquefied-natural-gas exports and develop an accurate opinion about it.

The only possible thing that could result from something like that is you might develop an opinion that gets you in trouble politically. This is why I spend so much more time talking about the groups and their funders than about elected officials. Most politicians most of the time are just triangulating a set of electoral and coalition-management concerns and do not have the disposition to peer deep into the merits. By the same token, this is why governors are systematically more sensible than senators: When you hold a responsible office that forces you to address fiscal tradeoffs and be held accountable for results, you need to start caring more about the merits. But very few political jobs are like this.

So how can you tell? I really wasn’t joking that talking to me and other takeslingers is a good way for politicians to try to convey information to high-information voters about what they really think. Off-the-record chats are good — it’s worth taking seriously the possibility that writers whose judgement you find generally reliable may have access to information you don’t and that you should trust writers like that to some extent when we evaluate politicians.

On-the-record discussion is good, too. Zohran Mamdani recording an interview with a skeptical Derek Thompson where he explained what he liked about the abundance message and name-checked the sewer socialists of early 20th-century Milwaukee was an intriguing choice. Once you start trying to cultivate a reputation as that sort of guy, then if you win you need to try to follow through on that. I think there are real structural limits to how much sewer socialism can be achieved in the context of a blue state with strong public-sector labor unions without engaging in a hard break with conventional progressive politics. But within those constraints, he really is trying to do sewer socialism. Building that kind of political brand creates pressure to deliver.

Contrast that, say, with Tom Steyer, a smart guy who is going out of his way to convey to everyone that he wants to be seen as a yes man for every progressive advocacy group in California.

Last but not least, it’s worth trying to think about different people’s teams to the extent that they have them. On the campaign trail in 2008, Barack Obama criticized NAFTA, which is a smart thing to do if you’re running in Midwestern primaries against Bill Clinton’s wife. But Obama always had a very conventional economic team. This was a specific case I had in mind when I said that if you want to sling some slopulism on the campaign trail, you should also find ways to directly convey to technocratically minded opinion-leaders that you’re a well-informed person. No president has ever governed like a 100 percent textbook free-trader, but Obama always made it clear that he knew what the textbooks said and was going to have an economic policy team that dispensed normal economic advice. Donald Trump, by contrast, has always been very interested in cranks like Peter Navarro.

Of course, you can’t always know a lot about which advisors a politician is relying on. But to me, something like Chris Murphy bashing airline deregulation is a red flag because there’s no particularly obvious reason why a senator from Connecticut would be addressing a policy controversy from the Carter administration.

But Ganesh Sitaraman and Phil Longman have strongly held views on this subject and are both big players at the Vanderbilt Policy Accelerator and the Open Markets Institute, respectively. I am inferring that Murphy is saying this because he’s relying on these intellectual communities. I think those communities’ analysis of this question is wrong.

Johnson: Why does AOC draw so much more hostility from internet leftists than Mamdani, despite broadly similar politics, and despite the fact that he has actually substantively moderated more these four months than she has over six years? Is there another explanation besides gender?

I think the biggest difference is that the one topic Mamdani has really done nothing to demonstrate moderation or pragmatism on is Israel, and Israel is very much the “current thing” on the left. Ocasio-Cortez, by contrast, keeps getting into hot water with elements of the left specifically over Israel. For years, for example, she kept voting in favor of funding the Iron Dome and other defensive weapons systems. She has reversed course on that, but the latest blowup came when she said that she’s not entirely comfortable with the Strange New Respect being accorded on the left to Marjorie Taylor Greene.

This will probably not help AOC out with her enemies, but I thought it was a good take on her part. A lot of Israel critics used to note that Taylor Greene tended to get an undeserved free pass on spreading antisemitic conspiracy theories because she didn’t criticize Israel, while politicians who advocated for Palestinian interests were constantly put under the microscope. Taylor Greene ended up eventually flipping and becoming stridently anti-Israel. But given the fact that she was talking about Jewish-weather-control conspiracies years before she developed concerns about American military aid to Israel, this seems like a pretty clear-cut case of someone who is hostile to Israel because she doesn’t like Jews rather than because of her belief in the universal value of human rights.

The fact that AOC sees this distinction while her critics like Ryan Grim do not I think shows that Israel just does not play that central of a role in AOC’s worldview, whereas to Grim and many contemporary leftists this is the critical moral issue of our time.

I see Mamdani as almost the exact opposite of AOC in this regard. He showed up on campus at Bowdoin and co-founded a Students for Justice in Palestine chapter. That’s the issue that got him interested in politics. And at the time he was in school and when he graduated, the only place on the American political spectrum where anti-Zionist views were welcome was on the far left. But that’s a little bit incidental. If you go to Turkey or Iraq or some other Muslim-majority country, it’s not as if every single person who has stridently anti-Israel views also has left-wing opinions on climate change or transgender issues or economics. So as Mamdani has had to focus more and more of his practical attention on public policy issues facing New York City, he’s found a good measure of pragmatism while staying core to his original views on Palestine.

The other (but related) problem for AOC is that fundamentally left-wing people are extremely interested in what you can achieve by winning primaries in safe blue areas. There have been about 1,000 times as many articles written by progressive journalists about Graham Platner than about Roy Cooper because progressives just sincerely do not find beating Republicans in red-leaning areas to be an interesting topic. The problem with this worldview is that you can’t actually achieve very much by winning primaries in safe blue areas. AOC knocking off a member of Democratic Party leadership in a 2018 primary bid was fascinating, but essentially nothing changed as a result of that. The correct thing for left-wing people to do would be to notice that if you care about things like progressive taxation and giving poor people health care, electing AOC types in safe districts doesn’t accomplish anything while electing people like Marie Gluesenkamp Perez and Vicente Gonzalez in red districts accomplishes a lot.

But rather than revisit the premise, people are getting restless with AOC. If she’d run for mayor in 2021 or challenged Schumer in a 2022 primary or was running for governor in 2026, then she’d be exciting to left-wing people who love blue-on-blue primaries. The longer she stays in the House, though, the more she’s just experiencing convergent evolution to every other House member — writing bills, hoping to retake the majority, gaining seniority — which I think is great but is fundamentally boring to progressives who only care about blue-on-blue primaries. If she spends the summer hinting about a presidential campaign and announces soon after the midterms, she’ll be the left’s factional champion and be exciting again. But if she takes a pass and dedicates her life to being an effective and responsible member of the House, people will keep coming up with reasons to get mad at her.

Brian: Recently, Obama said, “What I’m more interested in for Democrats is, do you know how to just talk to regular people like we’re not in a college seminar? Can you talk in plain English to folks?” Obama has made similar comments at various points in his post-presidency. Am I right to say that Obama’s comments chiding Democrats have generally fallen into the category of the “dog-whistle moderation” that you criticize? Am I right to think that Obama has appeased the Democratic Party too much and squandered his unique credibility with the Democratic Party in an effort to maintain his popularity?

I wouldn’t say he’s “appeased too much,” but I agree with your take that he’s engaged in too much dog-whistle moderation. I think this is clearly a take that is meant to have the upshot “Democrats should be more moderate” but, as with his various invocations against cancel culture, it lacks specificity.
