Beyond that, it even lacks a recognition of tradeoffs. Something Obama said to a group that I was part of once back before he won the 2008 nomination was that if Lyndon Johnson had run in 1964 on overturning miscegenation laws, he might not have been in a position to appoint the Supreme Court justice who helped overturn them. Obama flip-flopped on marriage equality before the Obergefell decision came down, but he nominated Justices Sotomayor and Kagan back when he was officially opposed to gay marriage. In both of those cases, the politically expedient position was also right on the merits.

In the case of Democrats’ post-Obama leftward pivot on teacher compensation, I think they’ve landed on a position that is both worse politically and also worse on the merits. Obama’s second-term executive actions on immigration were a calculated political risk that backfired and left immigrants worse off than they were before.

I don’t want to see Obama try to dictate the party’s policy positions on everything, but I think some honesty with donors and staffers and elected officials about the kind of tradeoffs he wrestled with would be more constructive than this kind of vagueness. I’m sure Obama was often frustrated by people like Mary Landrieu and Byron Dorgan, but his ability to achieve things was downstream of their presence in the Senate. And they didn’t win those races with “vibes” — they were genuinely more moderate (or shmoderate, if you prefer) than he was. And while he personally was a progressive factional favorite in the 2008 primary thanks to Iraq, by 2008 “invading Iraq was bad” was a mainstream consensus position. He was constantly attentive to the actual contours of public opinion. Not just in terms of using relatable language, but in terms of the substance of what he said.

John Freeman: A few weeks ago you wrote that American prisons are too violent and should be relatively calmer places. What would paths to achieving this look like?

Arnold Ventures and the state of Pennsylvania are funding an experimental Nordic-style prison to try to assess whether this more expensive, more humane mode of incarceration leads to less staff turnover (which would reduce the actual cost of implementation at scale) and better recidivism outcomes. If it performs on recidivism, then there will be a strong case for expanding the model and hopefully we will see elected officials willing to follow the evidence.

Ed: Since you got into reading novels, can you pass on a question to your father, Rafael Yglesias, about what’s wrong with contemporary fiction?

Last summer he posted an interesting comment in response to this substack:

“I’m tempted to say it’s because they don’t read my novels but self-pity is no substitute for analysis, although it’s often a starting point. My real belief is that the widespread study of literature in academia planted the seeds of its decline in a variety of ways, culturally and in the marketplace …. A beautifully written work that has no story isn’t a successful literary work and for a long time now they have been hailed as such.”

Henry Oliver responded:

“I agree with all of this but it ought to be possible for it to survive outside academic study. Lots of writers didn’t study Eng Lit.”

Your father responded:

“I think the impact on the gatekeepers and readers is more significant. People believe for a novel to be serious it shouldn’t have a plot, the language should be a struggle to read easily, and it should refer to other literary works, blow up genres and so on. They are challenging to read in every way but emotionally, and unlikely to sell. So yes literary writers can avoid academic approaches to writing by writing for TV and movies but that doesn’t help the novel thrive as a dramatic form that engages the reader with their own lives in a serious way … the main issue for me has been the separation of a belief in story from elegance of language. And also a lack of awareness that’s there’s a distinction between plot and story that is crucial to understanding what once made literary fiction popular, apart from the obvious lack of competition.”

Henry Oliver responded:

“can you say more?”

But your father hasn’t responded yet. It would be interesting to hear him flesh out these thoughts.

“Distinction between plot and story”? Interesting.

Rather than ask him, I’m going to just try to prove that I’ve listened to my dad over the years. Plot versus story I think is easiest to understand in terms of very lowbrow books that are heavy on plot and light on story. Every Jack Reacher adventure has a plot. He shows up in a town, something happens, he gets sucked into a caper, and a bunch of events occur. These books are designed to be exciting so there’s a lot of bang-bang-bang events.

Early Reacher novels also have the outline of a story. Reacher is discharged from the Army, wandering the country and trying to figure things out. He avenges his brother in the first book. In the third book, he reconnects with his mentor and his mentor’s now-adult daughter. In the fourth book, he’s dating Jodie Garber and is considering establishing a permanent place of residence as a civilian. If he’d done it and settled down with Garber and started a family, that would be a proper story with an arc. The problem is that it would either need to be the case that the story of Jack Reacher ends with him settling down, or else that it continues and becomes a very different kind of story about marriage and parenting rather than about beating up criminals with turkey-sized hands. The basic logic of commerce is for Jack Reacher to keep having Reacher-like adventures, so he and Garber break up, he never has another even slightly serious relationship, and nothing about the shifts in American society or the passage of time seem to impact Reacher at all.

Network-television police procedurals have plots, but most of them have very little in the way of story. Law & Order took this to an extreme, giving us detectives and assistant district attorneys who have no life outside the case of the week. The Sopranos is a multigenerational story.

Anyways, I think my dad’s take is that as literary fiction got more influenced by academia and more divorced from the commercial sphere, people not only learned that plot-centric books are lowbrow but also lost sight of the fact that classic works of literature have stories. Crime and Punishment is not a gritty thriller à la Michael Connelly, but there is a crime. There is punishment. Stuff is happening. It’s not a writing exercise. People debate the merits of different translations, but one’s appreciation of the book will ultimately hinge on one’s appreciation of the story. Not coincidentally, Dostoevsky worked at a time when even highbrow novels were meant to be commercially successful mass entertainments. It wasn’t a thing that had developed its own niche economy of prestige, grants, prizes, and workshops totally divorced from breadth of readership.

BK: Should Disney reboot the Star Wars timeline after Return of the Jedi?

To nerd out a bit, there used to be a Star Wars Expanded Universe composed mostly of books and comics that had a fairly coherent canon. The best-regarded of these properties was a trilogy of novels featuring Grand Admiral Thrawn as the main antagonist (he led what remained of the fallen empire’s forces) and set about five years after the conclusion of Return of the Jedi. These books had Luke and Han and Leia as central characters and also introduced a new hero, Mara Jade.

The most logical thing to do for a sequel trilogy would have been to adapt these books into films. But due to the chronology, that would have required recasting the central three characters from the original movies. Disney instead elected to re-use the original actors, which required a whole new plot set much further into the future. But they didn’t want to make a movie just about senior citizens (and I think Harrison Ford had limited interest in doing extensive work) so they also wanted to introduce three new characters. Thus they decided to de-canonize almost the entire Expanded Universe (now rebranded as “Legends”) with the exception of the Clone Wars animated series and make what became Episodes VII, VIII, and IX. Unfortunately, while Episode VII seemed like a promising-but-unoriginal launch to a new trilogy, the next two movies are a mess.

So I think a popular idea among old fans of the Thrawn books is to throw out the sequel trilogy, reboot the canon, recast the parts, and do the Thrawn story.

I am all for this with one significant caveat. Adapting novels into movies is a non-trivial task. And when I look at the whole mass of Star Wars content that’s been produced for film and television since Return of the Jedi, it is mostly bad. Even the stuff that’s kind of good like Rogue One and The Force Awakens and the first season of The Mandalorian is actually pretty bad. Andor has shown that it’s not impossible to make actually good content set in the Star Wars universe, but that show has no Jedi, no Sith, and no lightsabers — it’s this very deliberate effort to wield the commercial appeal of the Star Wars brand to tell a story that is really very disconnected from the main conceits of the Star Wars franchise.

This all just kind of makes me think that if they made a Thrawn series of movies, they would probably be bad just because the people calling the shots don’t seem to be very good at making things that are good. Even Andor, though I like it, is far from my favorite thing that Tony Gilroy has done, so I don’t particularly want them to “put him in charge and fix Star Wars” or whatever — he’s working on a movie called Behemoth and I’m looking forward to seeing it when it’s done.

bulb5: Does Adam Hamilton running for Senate as a Democrat in Kansas have a chance? I’ve been a fan of him for a long time and was inspired by seeing him run, but I wonder if he should have tried the Dan Osborn independent route instead. But Kansas does have a Democratic governor, so they’re not as deep red as you might think.

He absolutely has a chance — it’s just not a very high chance. But I am incredibly supportive of what he is trying to do.

I understand the independent play and I like the people who are trying to run it, but fundamentally I’m skeptical of the strategy. The problem with the independent strategy is that it requires a level of de facto cooperation from the Democratic Party that I think tends to undermine its effectiveness. Saying “I am a Democrat because of XYZ, but I understand that lots of people in this state don’t like Democrats because of ABC and I want to specifically reassure you that I don’t agree with ABC and intend to be an independent voice” is a harder path in some respects but in other ways, I think, an easier and more comprehensible one. If you’re going to do it, you should say you want to be a Democrat in the style of Laura Kelly or Joe Manchin. Say you want to form a cross-party caucus with Susan Collins and Lisa Murkowski and break down the leadership-dominated congressional rules that enforce partisan conformity. Say things that the median voter in Kansas agrees with.

John From FL: What are your thoughts prosecutorial discretion? (Note: not the “too few resources so have to pick the biggest crimes to go after”, but instead “I don’t like these laws so will not enforce them”).

On the one hand it is usually a choice made by an elected executive (District Attorney, President, Governor), so there is some link to an election. But on the other hand, it seems anti-democratic since it diminishes or eliminates the role of the legislature in crafting laws, expecting them to be faithfully executed?

I think this illustrates that trying to assess things based on how “democratic” they are is not that productive. The tradition in the United States is that each county has its own separately elected prosecutor, so prosecutorial divergence can diverge quite a bit from place to place even though the formal criminal code is the same. This doesn’t make a ton of sense to me as a system relative to having a state prosecutorial agency staffed by civil servants and headed by an attorney general who is appointed by the governor. But I think most people would say my proposal is “undemocratic” and that having more elected offices is “more democratic.” That “democratic” system just naturally has the consequence that discretion will be wielded in a non-uniform way, with some jurisdictions behaving in ways that the legislature does not favor.

1

She was an enthusiastic police defunder whose idea of tackling a crime hotspot in her district was to ask drug dealers nicely to stop shooting people while the problem was eventually resolved by a major federal interagency task force arresting a bunch of people.

2

She uses coded language about this but wants to dismantle the city’s successful performance pay system for teachers.

Claude’s Response

This is one of the more useful Yglesias mailbags because the framing question — how can a voter who isn’t in back-room conversations actually infer what a politician thinks — has a real answer that he develops well. The staffing-and-intellectual-community heuristic is genuinely portable; once you accept that most politicians are coalition-management triangulators rather than policy-formers, looking at who they hire and which think-tank ecosystem they cite becomes the cheapest source of signal a voter has. The Murphy/airline-deregulation example is especially clean: a senator from Connecticut has no Connecticut-specific reason to be litigating Carter-era policy, so the position must be coming from somewhere, and Yglesias can name the somewhere (Vanderbilt Policy Accelerator, Open Markets) with reasonable confidence. This connects directly to his Spirit/anti-monopoly mega-essay published the same week — the airline-deregulation question is doing diagnostic work because it tells you which intellectual community a Democrat is plugged into.

The AOC/Mamdani analysis is the most interesting piece of the mailbag because it actually disrupts a discourse default. The standard reading — that AOC catches more flak because of gender — is one Yglesias takes seriously enough to engage and reject in favor of a specific structural alternative: the contemporary left has organized itself around Israel as its central moral commitment, AOC’s willingness to make non-Israel-instrumental judgments (the Greene example) signals that the issue doesn’t have that load-bearing weight in her worldview, and Mamdani by contrast has Israel as the constitutive issue of his political formation. That’s a sharper claim than the gender one and it has predictive content (AOC’s standing with the left will keep eroding the longer she remains a normal House member; Mamdani’s will hold as long as he doesn’t moderate on Israel, regardless of how much he moderates on city policy). Whether this is right is a separate question — there’s a perfectly reasonable response that gender and Israel are both operative and the analytical move of picking one is itself a discursive choice — but it’s a non-trivial argument worth engaging.

The McDuffie endorsement is mostly DC-inside-baseball but worth noting because of how the analysis-by-staffing heuristic plays out in reverse: Yglesias is essentially saying that George is running on the wrong cognitive map (copy-pasting NYC/Boston progressive policy onto a city facing a fundamentally different shock), which is exactly the kind of intellectual-community-source-mismatch the epistemic-method section diagnoses. He’s modeling the move he’s recommending.

The fiction segment is the weakest part for Crosscurrents purposes — interesting but disconnected — though the plot/story distinction is genuinely useful and the underlying claim (that academia-mediated literary culture lost the ability to produce commercially-engaged storytelling that earlier literary fiction took for granted) is the kind of cultural-criticism move Yglesias usually doesn’t make and that fits within a broader recurring Crosscurrents-cluster discussion about cultural mediation institutions (cf. Scholar’s Stage on academia, Tooze on think-tank intellectual capture). The strongest section overall is the politicians-epistemics piece, which earns the headline; the surrounding mailbag is a useful but incidental tour.(https://substack.com/img/email/publish-button@2x.png)](https://substack.com/redirect/2/eyJlIjoiaHR0cHM6Ly9zdWJzdGFjay5jb20vc2lnbnVwP3V0bV9zb3VyY2U9c3Vic3RhY2smdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fY29udGVudD1mb290ZXImdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPWF1dG9maWxsZWQtZm9vdGVyJmZyZWVTaWdudXBFbWFpbD10d2lkbGFuZEBnbWFpbC5jb20mcj0xMjVldnoiLCJwIjoxOTcyMTA3NTEsInMiOjE1OTE4NSwiZiI6ZmFsc2UsInUiOjY0MDc3OTgzLCJpYXQiOjE3Nzg4Mzk2NDYsImV4cCI6MjA5NDQxNTY0NiwiaXNzIjoicHViLTAiLCJzdWIiOiJsaW5rLXJlZGlyZWN0In0.vR7H035fcgaSDotV72yfhi8J0nfKLcAWjOsBHGtl1gk?)

Back to summary

Dwarkesh in the Datacenter

Alex Tabarrok · 2026-05-17 · Original →

Dwarkesh tours one of Jane Street’s datacenters. It’s extraordinary how much compute goes into finance. (I once predicted that the finance AIs would be the first to become conscious, since they have the most compute.) More generally, however, this is a peek inside the remarkable economics, technology and physics of a datacenter. Did you know the electrical signal in a copper wire can travel faster than light in fiber…and that matters! Amazing.

The post Dwarkesh in the Datacenter appeared first on Marginal REVOLUTION.

Claude’s Response

This is Tabarrok in shortpost-curator mode, doing the MR thing where the value is the surfacing rather than the commentary. The post intersects this week’s broader datacenter / AI-infrastructure / data-center-political-economy cluster (per last week’s Heatmap $41.7B cancellation finding and Tonya Nickol’s Trenton Ohio dispatch) at a tangent — it’s about the physical/financial reality of compute infrastructure rather than the political-economy contest over siting it. Worth a one-line mention in the synthesis if the AI-infrastructure thread continues to develop, but probably not a load-bearing pick on its own.

The substantive observation about copper-beating-fiber for short-distance latency is genuinely interesting and underappreciated in popular AI discourse, where “more compute” and “more bandwidth” tend to get collapsed into a single abstraction. The Jane Street tour itself (which Tabarrok links to but the analyst hasn’t seen) presumably contains the operational detail that would make this a real contribution; Tabarrok’s post is a pointer.

The “finance AIs would be the first to become conscious” line is the kind of Tabarrok aside that operates as an in-group wink — the literal claim is silly (consciousness isn’t proportional to compute), but the implied claim (that AI-discourse focus on frontier labs systematically misses where the largest deployed AI workloads actually live) is sharper than the literal line. Not Straussian in the disciplined project sense — the conclusion is stated directly enough as an aside — but the rhetorical compression is real. The post is mainly useful as a continuity marker on the AI-infrastructure thread and doesn’t independently warrant deep treatment.

Back to summary

Hayek in Jacobin

Alex Tabarrok · 2026-05-16 · Original →

Here’s something I never expected to write: Jacobin, the magazine of the DSA-aligned left, has a good article on central planning. In an interview, Vivek Chibber lays out essentially the Mises–Hayek–Kornai critique of central planning. Information problems, incentive problems and the consequent failures are laid bare. Moreover, Chibber refuses to lay the blame at the feet of Stalin, poverty, or the Russians. Nor does he wave hopefully at supercomputers and AI, as is fashionable today on the planning-curious left:

The dilemma is this. There is a problem of information. Supercomputers will in fact help process information better. But if the information coming in is junk, and if that junk is built into the system because of the incentives that operators have in workplaces to lie, you will not have a planning system that can be put on its feet through the advent of computers or artificial intelligence or anything like that. I don’t see any reason to think that that strategic misalignment of incentives is simply there because of Russian backwardness or poverty.

Even the pedestrian is shocking coming from Jacobin:

Normally in capitalism, what do managers do? They want to make profits. The way to make a profit is by trying to sell, at the lowest price possible, the best-quality good that you can.

A vivid conclusion:

Melissa Naschek: What do you think leftists should learn from the failure of fully planned economies?

Vivek Chibber: What they should learn is that the burden of proof is on us, on the Left, if we want to continue with this slogan of replacing the market with the plan. The burden of proof is on us to show that it can work. You might say that along with this ought to come a kind of humility about facts and about the world.…it would be criminally negligent to ignore the experience of decades upon decades of planning and say to yourself, “Well, that wasn’t what my vision of socialism is, so I’m going to ignore it.” Because if you do that, I can guarantee 100 percent you will end up repeating many of the mistakes and falling into the same dilemmas that the planners did.

I could offer critiques. Stalin was not an impediment to central planning but a consequence of it. And to warn that ignoring the experience of central planning risks repeating “the same dilemmas that the planners did” is a bloodless way to describe dictatorship, famine, and mass murder. But that would be churlish. Let me end instead by saying that I agree with this:

If we’re actually serious about changing the world, people on the Left … should be the most remorseless and the most merciless when it comes to facts.

Replace “people on the Left” with “we” and the line is exactly right.

The post Hayek in Jacobin appeared first on Marginal REVOLUTION.

Claude’s Response

This is Tabarrok in across-the-aisle mode, surfacing a piece from the DSA-aligned left that runs against the standard left-rationalist priors and operates as evidence that the post-Cold-War socialist-calculation debate is in a different place than the standard right-wing framing suggests. The choice of what to highlight is itself the move: Tabarrok could have framed Chibber as ideologically captured, found the holes in Chibber’s argument, or used the piece as ammunition against the broader left. Instead he treats it as a serious engagement with the calculation problem and the substantive critiques are kept brief and labeled as churlish.

The substantive content of the post depends on accurate representation of Chibber, and the excerpts cited do read straight — Chibber does seem to be making the Mises–Hayek–Kornai argument in good faith, naming the strategic-misalignment-of-incentives mechanism specifically (not just the information problem), and refusing the AI escape hatch that’s become the standard contemporary planning-curious move. That’s a genuine and underreported development in left intellectual discourse, and Tabarrok is right to flag it. The piece pairs naturally with the “lumpen left” Shullenberger/Compact piece from last week’s discovery cluster (which diagnosed the left’s drift toward Frankfurt-conspiracy thinking) — Chibber-via-Tabarrok represents the opposite, more analytically serious left current that’s also active.

The two embedded critiques are intellectually serious. The Stalin-as-consequence-not-impediment-of-planning point is a real challenge to Chibber’s framing: if planning’s information and incentive problems generate the political pressure that produces dictatorship, then the failure modes Chibber wants to learn from are inseparable from the regime types that produced them, and the lesson is harder than Chibber wants it to be. The “bloodless” complaint about Chibber’s “same dilemmas” phrasing is fair but Tabarrok labels it as churlish, recognizing that holding Chibber to a higher rhetorical standard than other commentators would be selectively applied.

The genuine intellectual question Tabarrok leaves untouched: if Chibber’s diagnosis is right that information problems are the binding constraint, what does that imply for the contemporary debate over algorithmic / platform governance, where private firms (Amazon, Walmart, ride-share platforms) are running large-scale planned coordination internally with much better data infrastructure than Gosplan ever had? Cosma Shalizi has been raising this question for years from the same Hayek-respecting left position; the synthesis composer might want to note that Chibber’s argument either applies to or excludes platform capitalism’s internal planning, and the answer determines whether the calculation critique is a knockout against socialism or a more limited critique against state-run economies specifically. Worth carrying forward as a productive frontier in the left-economics discourse the project tracks intermittently via Bruenig, Stoller, and Chibber-via-MR.

Back to summary

One way to benefit adolescents

Tyler Cowen · 2026-05-15 · Original →

Have school start later:

We examine the impact of California’s Senate Bill 328 (SB 328), the first statewide mandate requiring later school start times for middle and high schools, on adolescent sleep, mental health, and academic outcomes. Using difference-in-differences and eventstudy designs across five data sources, we find that SB 328 increased the share of students sleeping at least 8 hours per night by 13%, meeting the CDC-recommended minimum for this age group. Average mental health effects are imprecisely estimated, but boys show significant reductions in sadness, hopelessness, and suicidal ideation, and Hispanic students, who experienced the largest sleep-timing shifts, show parallel reductions in difficulty concentrating; together these patterns are consistent with a dose-response relationship between sleep improvement and mental well-being. Math and English scores in grade 8 improved by approximately 0.08–0.10 standard deviations, with the largest gains among Hispanic and economically disadvantaged students. A within-state analysis using teachers’ commute arrival times as a proxy for pre-policy school start times corroborates these findings, and shows academic gains accumulating over 2023–2025 alongside a suggestive decline in high school dropout rates. The absence of effects on chronic absenteeism rules out an attendance-driven mechanism, pointing instead to the direct cognitive benefits of aligning school schedules with adolescents’ biological rhythms.

That is from a new NBER working paper by Jialu (Gloria) Dou, Rania Gihleb, Osea Giuntella & Jakub Lonsky.

The post One way to benefit adolescents appeared first on Marginal REVOLUTION.

Claude’s Response

This is Cowen in econometric-curator mode — surfacing a clean policy-evaluation paper with effect sizes that are credible and modest, on a policy intervention that’s unusually well-suited to causal identification (a single statewide mandate creating a sharp temporal discontinuity with a within-state proxy for the counterfactual). The title is the closest thing to Cowen’s editorial position and it reads as straightforward endorsement: the policy works, it’s cheap, the effects are heterogeneous in pro-equity directions (Hispanic and economically disadvantaged students gain most), and the mechanism is biologically rather than institutionally mediated, which makes the result more portable across jurisdictions.

This is not a Straussian post by the project’s discipline — the surface argument (“school should start later”) matches the operative argument, the effect sizes are reported transparently, and there’s no rhetorical understatement obscuring a different conclusion. The disciplined application of the Straussian label per feedback_dont_apply_straussian_label_reflexively.md rules this one out. It’s a direct policy endorsement via curation.

Two genuine substantive notes for the synthesis composer. First, the effect size on test scores (0.08-0.10 SD) is small in absolute terms but unusually well-priced — most cheap interventions show effects of this size only on the most well-measured outcomes, and the paper finds them across math, English, and a suggestive dropout decline. The mental-health subgroup effects (boys, Hispanic students) are noisier but biologically consistent. Second, the policy implication is straightforwardly liberal-coded (state mandate overriding local school district scheduling preferences to produce measurable improvements in adolescent welfare) — Cowen surfacing it without methodological qualifiers is a small signal that he’s reporting the result on its scientific merits rather than running it through his usual market-deference filter. The piece is appropriate to mention briefly in the synthesis if there’s a “policies that actually work” or “small interventions with measurable effects” thread, but doesn’t independently warrant deep treatment. The most analytically interesting follow-up question — why states haven’t adopted this when the cost-benefit is unusually favorable — Cowen doesn’t engage, which is the closest the piece comes to leaving something on the table.

Back to summary

Philosophical Ideas Behind Their Time

Alex Tabarrok · 2026-05-15 · Original →

Justin Weinberg at Daily Nous riffs off my post, Ideas Behind Their Time, to ask for philosophical examples. He nominates Gettier problems–i.e. counterexamples to the idea that knowledge is simply “justified true belief” as a possibility. The classic Gettier paper is from 1963. Wikipedia notes that the Indian philosopher Dharmottara has some clear examples c770 AD but as an element within the Western tradition the idea does seem behind its time.

I would nominate the following as philosophical ideas behind their time:

Hume’s is/ought distinction: the idea that you cannot derive a normative conclusion from factual premises.

Hume’s problem of induction: past regularities do not rationally guarantee future regularities.

Rawls’s Veil of Ignorance: the principles of justice should be derived without knowing one’s own particularities of class, race, gender and so forth. Seems obvious as an idea.

The Trolley Problem: similar ideas can be found earlier but the clean distinction between killing and let die or more generally omission and commission could have come much earlier. One might also think of the Prisoner’s Dilemma in this category of ideas or constructs that cleanly isolate an otherwise present but opaque idea.

The analytic/synthetic truths distinction: some things are true by definition, others are empirical. Obvious and it can be found before say Kant, yet a clear earlier statement would have resolved many issues and seems well within say Aristotle’s capability.

Aumann’s Agreement Theorem, technically, this requires Bayesian machinery and is difficult to formulate with precision, so I would not say the actual theorem was behind its time. But the underlying idea—that disagreement itself, not merely the arguments offered, should cause one to question and refine one’s own beliefs—could have been developed in Athens.

I’d also nominate a package of ideas like abolitionism, equal rights for women, and religious toleration–each of these is tendentious as examples yet the basic package seems fairly obvious as a category and yet late. (Perhaps if the veil of ignorance had been thought of earlier so would these ideas!) Note, that I am not arguing that abolitionism or equal rights for women could have happened much earlier only that these ideas were behind their time–the ideas were morally obvious even if not institutionally feasible.

Note also that I am not arguing that these ideas are all correct, only that they were philosophical ideas behind their time. More examples?

The post Philosophical Ideas Behind Their Time appeared first on Marginal REVOLUTION.

Claude’s Response

This is Tabarrok in intellectual-history-recreational mode — a piece that’s mainly fun and doesn’t carry much analytical weight for the broader synthesis, but is interesting as a case study in how the “ideas behind their time” frame operates and what it conceals. The frame implicitly assumes that ideas can be evaluated on their internal logical structure independent of the institutional, material, and cognitive scaffolding required to formulate them — which is itself a contestable view, and one that the comments section on MR sometimes pushes back on with the “but you needed concept X first” objection.

Three observations the synthesis composer might find useful if AI / philosophy / intellectual-history threads come up elsewhere in the week. First, Tabarrok’s selection skews heavily toward Anglophone analytic philosophy with one Indian outlier (Dharmottara) — there’s no engagement with whether comparable ideas emerged earlier in classical Chinese (Mohist consequentialism, neo-Confucian rectification of names) or Islamic (Mu’tazila on free will and divine command) traditions, which would either reinforce or complicate the “behind its time” judgment depending on what counts as “available.” Second, the moral-package nomination (abolitionism, women’s equality, religious toleration) carries the largest implicit cost: Tabarrok’s “morally obvious even if not institutionally feasible” formulation lets readers reach the conclusion that the institutional infeasibility was somehow detachable from the moral non-obviousness, which the actual historical record contests pretty sharply (slavery’s moral status was contested in antiquity and the contest was partly about how the moral status interacted with the institutional dependence, not just downstream of it).

Third, Aumann’s Agreement Theorem framing is the most analytically interesting and the most underdeveloped. The underlying intuition — “disagreement itself, not merely the arguments offered, should cause one to question and refine one’s own beliefs” — is, as Tabarrok says, available without Bayesian machinery. But it’s worth noting that the intuition was actively resisted in classical thought (Socratic elenchus is built on the opposite premise: that disagreement is generative of new beliefs through argument, not a signal that one’s own beliefs need revision based on the bare fact of disagreement). The Aumann intuition presupposes both interlocutors share a prior and have common knowledge of each other’s rationality — those are nontrivial preconditions and may be what was missing, not the bare intuition. Tabarrok’s framework has a tendency to underweight these scaffolding conditions, which is why “ideas behind their time” can sometimes read as “ideas that look obvious once you have the modern conceptual furniture they helped install.”

The piece has no fundamental flaw — it’s a curated list, transparent about its own limits (“not arguing these are correct, only that they were behind their time”) — but it’s a low-priority pick for the synthesis. Worth mentioning only if a “what counts as intellectual progress” or “AI / intellectual genealogy” thread coalesces around other pieces in the week.

Back to summary

Hollywood Isn’t Dead Yet

The Free Press · 2026-05-15 · Original →

Hollywood Isn’t Dead Yet

In the new season of ‘Euphoria,’ Sydney Sweeney comes out on top. Plus: I talk to screenwriter Allan Loeb about his appearance in the show. This week in pop culture, like most weeks, began with a meltdown over Sydney Sweeney. (Illustration by The Free Press, images via Getty and HBO)

Welcome to the Second Thought newsletter, my weekly tour of what’s going on in the zeitgeist: This week in pop culture, like most weeks, began with a meltdown over Sydney Sweeney. This time, it was about her character on the show Euphoria, the HBO series that mirrors Sweeney in at least two ways: First, people never fail to get riled up over what it shows, and says, or what they think it might be saying—and second, they simply cannot look away.

On Tuesday, I wrote a piece about how, in the season of the show that’s currently airing, Sweeney’s character, Cassie, plays an aspiring OnlyFans star who is willing to do anything, pretty much, for money and attention. That includes donning a ridiculous baby costume (though more often she’s donning nothing at all), and fulfilling the requests of her thousands of male followers: for her to humiliate them, eat hot dogs in front of a camera for them, or simply whisper their name. Sydney Sweeney in season 3 of Euphoria (HBO)

For taking on the risqué role within a role, Sweeney, and the show’s creator, Sam Levinson, have been accused, on the one hand, of sexualizing infancy and making normal people “recoil,” to quote Megyn Kelly; and on the other, of portraying porn stars in a way that is unflattering, and “absurd,” to quote a performer called Sydney Leathers. So what I’m hearing is: The show is inflaming both sides of the culture war, and asking big questions—about empowerment, and selling ourselves, and if there are any taboos left when it comes to baring our bodies for a buck.

This season of Euphoria is very different from the last two. The ensemble cast has graduated high school, and they’re now in their early twenties, navigating young adulthood. But they’re wrestling with the same demons that they were when they were teenagers, which, in Cassie’s case, is a bottomless, almost violent desire to be adored. You can read my whole column on the show, and how bits of Cassie’s storyline mirror Sweeney’s own life, here: And while I couldn’t get her to do an interview for the Second Thought podcast this week, I got the next best thing . . ..

Allan Loeb Bets on It

My friend Allan Loeb, in addition to being a very successful Hollywood screenwriter, and a lifelong compulsive gambler, has appeared in two seasons of Euphoria, including this latest one. His is a small cameo—he plays Gabe Finkelstein, who this season is shown for a few seconds demanding a refund for a botched lap dance—but I’m told there are no small parts, only small actors.

Allan has spent much of his career penning scripts for movies like 21, and Just Go with It. So obviously we had to speak about AI, which everyone in Hollywood seems to consider the grim reaper that will finally bring their industry to its knees. Turns out, Allan is the exception: He also has a sunny view of AI. “We don’t know what we don’t know,” he told me. “But I think the rumors of our demise are greatly overstated.”

Allan is also, in his spare time, an on-off compulsive gambler. You may recognize his name from his big essay about obsessive betting that ran in these pages.

“The manifestation of numbers into money is just fascinating to me, especially in the stock market and sports betting,” Allan tells me. He’s always had an obsession with what he calls the “domestication of randomness.”

I was struck both by his honesty—he calls the 10 or so years he spent betting on baseball “embarrassing” and “pathetic”—and his optimism. Allan thinks popular betting apps like DraftKings and FanDuel could actually benefit the compulsive gambler, who might otherwise get themselves in trouble with shady bookies, like he did.

For nuggets of wisdom—and some warnings—from Allan, plus a dissection of the big stories of the week, including hantavirus, and Billie Eilish’s new movie, watch the latest episode of Second Thought below.

What Else I’m Thinking About

What’s that Tolstoy line? That while“all happy families are alike; each unhappy family is unhappy in its own way”? I thought about that as I read this seemingly standard New York Times Vows story: She works in fashion design, him in tech. They met at a party, then again on a dating app a few years later, then fell in love. Then, 44 days before their wedding, she discovered his pill addiction. Then, she discovers that she’s pregnant. Read it for a lesson in divine timing and second chances.

For most of us who live here, being a New Yorker means being on the subway every day. Most times, your trip is totally fine—except for, maybe, someone playing TikToks at full volume, which is its own nightmare—but there’s always the thought in the back of your mind that your morning commute could erupt into something genuinely dangerous. That’s what happened to one 23-year-old woman, who was violently attacked on the train last month. She refused to prosecute her assailant—because, as she put it, “I don’t want to put another black man in jail.” This week, that man allegedly killed someone—and my colleague Kat Rosenfield wrote about the worldview that preaches empathy but hides something far worse.

Against type, I haven’t paid much attention to the high-stakes trial pitting Elon Musk against Sam Altman. It seems like a squabble over accounting statements—even though I know it’s more important than that, and likely, filled with juicy gossip. I perked up this week when OpenAI president Greg Brockman’s private diary was made public. Ben Cohen wrote about the legal fallout from the diary, and about how weird it must be to have your most private thoughts and wishes used against you, and be made available for everyone to see. Brockman said it’s painful, but he’s not ashamed!

Speaking of tech titans: This week, Jeremy Stern of Colossus interviewed Scott Wu, the “nerd emperor” and founder of AI company Cognition.It’s a story about how old-school geeks like Wu, who Stern calls the “Michael Jordan of competitive math and programming,” didn’t just become cool—they became kings of the world.

And, if you’re looking to raise an AI emperor of your own, you better start as early as you can. For a look into that future, read my colleague Maya Sulkin’s deeply reported dispatch from San Francisco’s new AI academy, Alpha School. The Free Press is for free thinkers. Send this to someone who values open debate and curiosity.

Claude’s Response

Weiss is doing a recurring Free Press newsletter format — a personal lede plus a podcast plug plus a “what I’m reading” list — and that genre constraint dictates what she can deliver. As a Second Thought roundup, the piece is fine; as an argument for the proposition that “Hollywood isn’t dead yet,” it doesn’t actually argue. The headline claim would require some engagement with the underlying decline thesis (collapsed theatrical revenue, the writers’-and-actors’-strike production gap, IP-franchise exhaustion, the streaming-economics bust), and there is none. What Weiss offers instead is: one HBO show is generating culture-war discourse, therefore the medium is alive. That’s a category error — Euphoria is a single Sam Levinson production (Sweeney and Levinson are the named principals), and its capacity to provoke Megyn Kelly tells us nothing about the aggregate health of film and television as industries. Loeb’s “rumors of our demise are greatly overstated” line is doing similar work: a sunny anecdote from one working screenwriter is not evidence against the AI-displacement worry Weiss concedes “everyone in Hollywood” is having.

Where the piece does have a point worth holding onto, it’s buried in the Cassie-as-OnlyFans frame: that the cultural argument over Euphoria is structurally the same on both poles (Kelly says it sexualizes; Leathers says it under-glamorizes sex work) and that “asking big questions about empowerment, and selling ourselves, and if there are any taboos left” is the actual cultural function of mass entertainment. That’s a defensible reading but it’s a defense of one show, not of Hollywood. The Allan Loeb section is the most reportorially substantive — a recovering compulsive gambler arguing that DraftKings and FanDuel are less harmful than the shady-bookie alternative is a non-obvious harm-reduction case worth more than the Euphoria peg got — but the piece can’t decide whether it wants to be a column about Sweeney or a podcast plug for Loeb, and so does both insufficiently.

The Kat Rosenfield reference (a subway-attack survivor declining to prosecute “because I don’t want to put another black man in jail,” whose alleged attacker subsequently killed someone) is doing heavier lifting than its one-paragraph treatment suggests — it’s the genuine empathy-as-cover-for-harm case Weiss alludes to, and it would have been a more honest week’s column than the Hollywood-isn’t-dead frame. As an item in the Second Thought zeitgeist tour the piece works; as anything more, it’s a headline written for a different essay.

Back to summary

What Los Angeles Has Become

Meghan Daum · ” · Original →

Spencer Pratt, the former reality-TV star and aspiring mayor of Los Angeles, recently spoke with me for a podcast. We met in front of the Airstream trailer that sits where his house did before it burned down in the Palisades Fire in January of last year. He was excited to share his ideas, if not always able to complete his thoughts, about what he’ll do when he’s in charge. For starters, he’ll clear the drug-ravaged homeless encampments of downtown, bring in developers from all over the world, and use 3-D-printing technology to build “an entire art deco, vibed-out affordable housing.” On the issue of bike lanes, a pet cause of the YIMBY voters who are backing one of his main opponents, Pratt says he’ll do them one better.

“I’m going to have bike tubes through the sky!” he says. “You know, like it’s endless possibilities when you enforce a law and you get rid of the zombies.”

Zombies is Pratt’s term for the tens of thousands of people who live in depraved conditions on L.A.’s streets, many of them addicted to drugs that leave them profoundly incapacitated and sometimes violent. It’s not a nice word to call someone who’s fallen into a bottomless abyss of hallucinations and thrashing self-destruction. But anyone living here knows exactly what he means.

For anyone not living here, it’s nearly impossible to comprehend what has become of the place. It’s not just that, in January of 2025, wildfires destroyed more than 16,000 structures and engulfed nearly 40,000 acres across the county. Apocalyptic as the fires were, they are not the main story and never really were. The main story is one of a city seemingly annihilating itself. Potholes crater the roads. Street lights, stripped of copper wire by organized-theft crews, are out across the city.

[Conor Friedersdorf: Spencer Pratt and the temptations of populism]

Vector-borne diseases such as typhus are breaking out at record levels, the result, at least in part, of 45,000 people (a low estimate, and closer to 75,000 if you include the whole county) living in squalid encampments on sidewalks. Fueled by fentanyl and a new, psychosis-inducing form of methamphetamine, street homelessness is no longer confined to the 50 hellish blocks of Skid Row in downtown Los Angeles. It’s now a city- and countywide humanitarian crisis that lives on freeway ramps, underpasses, parks, the banks of the desiccated L.A. River, the steps of public libraries, and the alleys behind homes and businesses. Undeterred—and unprotected—by laws that are no longer enforced, profoundly sick humans stumble blindly into traffic, defecate in plain sight, wave machetes in the air, threaten violence to passersby, and leave dogs tied to junk-filled shopping carts while they slump on the sidewalk in a fentanyl stupor.

It’s possible to live here and not fully comprehend the scope of it. Not just by dint of wealth, though that helps, but because the region is carved up into separate jurisdictions with seemingly no logic. Pacific Palisades, where almost every structure north of Sunset Boulevard burned to the ground, is part of Los Angeles, but its neighbors up and down the coast, Malibu and Santa Monica, are their own jurisdictions with their own city officials. Inglewood, an independent municipality whose mega sports stadiums have lifted it into affluence, sits adjacent to South Central, where the 1992 riots that follow the Rodney King beating still haunt the streets. Trendy northeast L.A. neighborhoods abut Glendale and Pasadena, cities with their own micro-cultures and tolerance levels for street camping and open drug use. To live in greater Los Angeles is to embrace the arbitrariness of it all. Your neighbor one block over might have an entirely different mayor, police force, and fire department than you do. This is usually immaterial, the kind of thing most people don’t notice until there is a very real reason to notice it, at which point it matters quite a bit.

Such is the setting of 2026 L.A. mayor’s race. On June 2, Los Angeles will hold a nonpartisan primary in which the top two vote-getters, regardless of party, advance to a November runoff. (If someone gets more than 50 percent in the primary, they win right there and then.) Incumbent Mayor Karen Bass, a career politician with deep ties to the city’s progressive-nonprofit world, would probably have skated through a couple of terms if not for the force majeure of January 7, 2025. On that day, she happened to be attending a presidential inauguration in Ghana even though fire-weather warnings had been issued before her plane left L.A. Among her campaign promises in 2021 was never to travel abroad during her time as mayor.

More than a dozen mayoral candidates are on the ballot, including a few unlikely sorts who seemed at first to be running almost as a joke. Until mid-April, Bass’s chief rival was presumed to be Nithya Raman, a city-council member who chairs the city’s Housing and Homelessness Committee and who identifies as a democratic socialist.

Then one of the jokes got serious: Pratt. A lifelong resident of Pacific Palisades, Pratt lost both his own house and his parents’ house in the fires. Shortly thereafter, he found himself in a new kind of reality show. Part community advocate, part self-appointed investigator, he ranted on TikTok about municipal negligence and alleged cover-ups, filed a lawsuit, and testified at a Senate hearing about the failed fire response. On the one-year anniversary of the fires, he took the podium at a rally called They Let Us Burn and announced his candidacy for mayor. “Business as usual is a death sentence for Los Angeles,” he told a crowd of about 1,000, “and I’m done waiting for someone to take real action.”

Spencer Pratt watches the wildfire as it approaches his house on January 7, 2025 in Pacific Palisades, California. (MEGA / GC Images / Getty)

Pratt is a remnant of the last era of television as something that was actually watched on a television. The designated villain on the MTV series The Hills, which ran from 2006 to 2010, he was of the generation of reality stars for whom mere reality wasn’t enough. Audiences demanded drama—love triangles, career sabotage, family histrionics—and cast members had to supply it while maintaining the premise that it was all true. Therein began a mass erosion of the fourth wall. Tabloids reported on storylines as if they were real-life scandals, a central one being the tumultuous relationship between Pratt and his girlfriend-turned-wife, Heidi Montag, who were famously accused of leaking a sex tape belonging to their castmate Lauren Conrad.

After the show ended, Pratt continued his commitment to notoriety, publicly blowing his fortune on Birkin bags, designer suits, a crystal collection, and, as he wrote in his memoir, The Guy You Loved to Hate, “tens of thousands of rounds of ammo stacked in the closet next to my Armani.” The book, published a few weeks after Pratt announced his candidacy, is a frenzied romp of self-incriminations and about as far from campaign literature as you can get.

The notion of Pratt being the leader of the second-largest city in America is random and absurd. But Los Angeles is itself random and absurd. Built in a waterless basin on top of two major fault lines and fringed with chaparral ready to burn at a moment’s notice, it’s a city that never really should have been here in the first place. And although it flourished magnificently in spite of itself, it is now, in many ways, less here than it has ever been in its entire history. As of January, rebuild permits had been issued for roughly one in five of the homes destroyed across the region. The slowly-then-all-at-once downfall of the film and television industry has gone from something everyone talks about to something actually happening to everyone. Locals all seem to know at least one art director or costume designer leaving the business and going back to school to become a therapist.

As work disappears, the average monthly rent hovers around $3,000 and the average home price is just under $1 million. What that has to do with the number of people sleeping in urine-soaked clothes on the sidewalk is a question that can be debated in good faith. But it is by now a truth almost universally acknowledged that the man waving around a machete on Venice Beach is not doing so because he can’t afford an apartment. He is doing so because his situation requires serious professional treatment, and he should get it whether he wants it or not. Even Mayor Bass recognizes this. “Just putting someone in a house is not enough,” she has said. “There needs to be health care and other social-services support.” Fair enough. But she’s had four years to implement that policy and hasn’t come close.

This sort of fecklessness from politicians, alongside ineptitude, waste, and possibly fraud from the nonprofit sector—a federal audit found the city’s accounting of $2.3 billion in homeless services so opaque that auditors couldn’t track what had actually been spent as intended—is why many Angelenos see no solution other than a factory reset.

After a great deal of initial skepticism, I have landed in that camp. If I thought any other candidate was viable, I would not be entrusting a city I love to a guy from The Hills. But when the moderate Democrat I’d have otherwise chosen couldn’t raise his polling numbers above the low single digits, I crossed the Rubicon into the surreal. It helped that I have never seen an episode of The Hills.

What you will frequently hear about Pratt, including from me, is that even if he accomplished very little of his agenda as mayor—not an unlikely scenario in a city where the mayor shares power with 15 council members and has no authority over the county agencies that run most social services—his mere willingness to acknowledge reality and enforce existing laws would be an improvement over the status quo.

Pratt is a registered Republican (he told me all of his friends are Democrats except one he hadn’t seen since ninth grade), but the mayoral seat is nonpartisan, and he’s running on local issues that cut across party lines. His plan for the homeless is, depending on your point of view, simplistic or seductively simple: get them off the streets and into appropriate treatment, by force if necessary, and do away with the nonprofits that stay in business by perpetuating the cycle. He’s also won over some voters by talking about an issue so disturbing, it all but goes ignored: the horrific abuse of dogs on Skid Row, which rescue groups have said are being bred, sold for drugs, and tortured by addicts and dealers on the streets. Pratt has made the issue central to his platform. “People that are torturing dogs, these are monsters,” he told me. “They are going to jail with Mayor Pratt. Under the jail.”

Pratt told me he believes he can win on dog lovers and safety-concerned moms alone. Whether enough of those moms and dog lovers are registered voters in the actual city of L.A. and not Burbank or Calabasas or any one of countless not-in-L.A. parts of L.A. is anyone’s guess. Less than 15 percent of L.A. voters are Republicans. Rick Caruso, a housing developer who spent $100 million on the 2022 race, switched his registration to Democrat before running but still lost to Bass 55 to 45 percent. Although many Angelenos I’ve talked with now kick themselves for not voting for Caruso, a sizable portion are so unwavering in their hatred of Donald Trump that anyone bearing the slightest resemblance to that other reality star is a hard pass.

[Read: California’s Blue Armageddon]

Fittingly, given his background, it could be argued that Pratt’s message is almost a side note to the medium. The short videos that were instrumental to Zohran Mamdani’s mayoral win in New York already look like vintage films compared with the avalanche of content generated by and for the Pratt campaign. The official campaign ads are notably polished, sincere, and stylish. At the same time, independent creators are using AI to make their own pro-Pratt videos, putting made-up words into real people’s mouths and throwing out all pretense of convention or decorum. Gleefully unwoke and unapologetically crude, they roll out almost day by day, heralded by fans—and maybe sometimes bots—as “the best campaign ad ever made.”

The campaign is rewriting the rules about what is allowed and therefore what is possible. It’s also a bit of a trick mirror, given that selling Pratt as a serious adult requires selling the idea that his previous incarnations weren’t really him but rather an extended piece of performance art. In a cruel electoral irony, some of the people most derisive about Pratt’s mayoral effort are deeply invested Hills viewers who hate the embellished character he created in his own name and can’t comprehend that it’s anything but real.

One of Pratt’s refrains is “I keep being called a reality star, but I’m the only one living in reality.” In a city fed up with leaders who tell constituents that problems happening before their eyes aren’t happening at all, that message lands, as they say in Hollywood. Even if Pratt never becomes mayor, it’s possible that simply saying the truth out loud will make him a bigger force for change than whoever does.

Claude’s Response

Daum is a careful essayist whose register here is autobiographical-confessional (“after a great deal of initial skepticism, I have landed in that camp”), which is the genre’s honest mode but also its scope-limiting one. The piece’s strongest contribution is the dense factual texture: the 45–75K encampment number, the federal-audit finding that $2.3B in homeless services was un-auditable, the 1-in-5 Palisades rebuild-permit number, the typhus return, the copper-stripping of streetlights. Each of those is a discrete material fact that anchors the otherwise impressionistic “L.A. is annihilating itself” claim, and they’re worth the read on their own as a snapshot of where civic-services delivery has gone in a major Democratic city.

The structural-claim layer is weaker. Daum’s case for Pratt is not really that he’d be a good mayor — she concedes the structural reality that “the mayor shares power with 15 council members and has no authority over the county agencies that run most social services,” meaning even a competent mayor on Daum’s preferred platform would be heavily constrained — but that his rhetorical willingness to “acknowledge reality” is itself the change-vector. This is the same logic that animates the Pratt campaign’s AI-generated content stream Daum mentions admiringly: the medium is the message, performance of unmediated honesty substitutes for the harder argument about what would actually work. It’s a recognizably anti-managerial frame (compare to the European center-exhaustion cluster from last week — Reform UK, Tisza, Magyar — where insurgent vehicles win on the same proposition that “the institutional class is incapable of solving the problems voters have”), but in the L.A. context the gap between Pratt’s diagnostic accuracy and his actual capacity to govern a city of 4 million is structurally larger than Daum lets register.

The piece also avoids the harder substantive question its evidence raises: why did the $2.3B in homeless services become un-auditable? Daum gestures at “ineptitude, waste, and possibly fraud from the nonprofit sector” but doesn’t engage the political-economy mechanism (the L.A. homeless-industrial complex is structured around providers whose continued existence depends on the problem persisting, and the city council is electorally dependent on those providers’ workforce). A more rigorous version of Daum’s argument would either name that mechanism and propose how a Pratt administration would actually break it, or concede that the mechanism is structurally unbreakable through the mayor’s office and the Pratt vote is therefore expressive rather than instrumental. Daum is doing the expressive read but is reluctant to admit it as such.

The piece is best treated as L.A. ground-truth dispatch rather than a structural analysis. It pairs naturally with the Brooks essay’s “public trust collapse” framing — Brooks asks why elite institutions have lost public confidence; Daum supplies the operating-layer answer in one specific city. It also pairs with last week’s european-center-exhaustion cluster as a U.S. local-level instance of the same dynamic.

Back to summary

Why Is Susan Collins Shaking?

Ken Klippenstein · 2026-05-11 · Original →

Senator Susan Collins

Subscriptions are declining - please become a paid subscriber so I can keep doing this work!

With a single tweet last week, I forced virtually every major media outlet to finally report on 73-year-old Maine Senator Susan Collins’ visible and worsening tremor.

But they’re still not telling the truth.

After 78-year-old Gov. Janet Mills dropped out of Maine’s U.S. Senate race, I was curious who upstart Graham Platner would be facing. Watching Collins’ video announcing her sixth-term bid, I noticed the senior senator visibly shaking despite some clever camera cuts. When I checked the coverage, I was amazed no one had ever reported on her tremor.

So I tweeted the video clip, saying she was vibrating like an electric toothbrush. I’d like to apologize for that. On reflection, she shakes more like a chihuahua in the snow.

My tweet went so viral that within days, Collins was compelled to respond.

Here’s what Collins told a local NBC affiliate she granted an exclusive interview: “What I have is an extremely common condition that is called a benign essential tremor.” The outlet, News Center Maine, used the same language in its headline: “Sen. Susan Collins reveals medical condition: benign essential tremor.”

There’s just one problem. The condition isn’t actually called that. It’s called “Essential Tremor.”

Movement-disorder neurologists dropped the word “benign” from the name over a decade ago — and for good reason. The condition coincides with a 300 percent increased risk of dementia and a 45 percent increased risk of death. For many people the condition is debilitating, with 85 percent of patients seeking treatment reporting “functional disability” in daily life tasks like eating and drinking. Even sleep often becomes difficult. As a result, about 80 percent of patients screen positive for signs of clinical depression — the same rate as found in Parkinson’s cases.

In other words, Collins isn’t being honest; but the local media amplified her framing anyway, too lazy (or cushy) to independently check. And then the New York Times and other national outlets did the same.

Here’s the real story.

In 2011, two prominent neurologists working on the disorder — Elan D. Louis of Columbia (now at UT Southwestern) and Michael S. Okun of the University of Florida — published a paper titled, “It is Time to Remove the ‘Benign’ from the Essential Tremor Label.”

The old picture of essential tremor as a mild, single-symptom nuisance is wrong, they argued. Newer evidence has recast it as a progressive disorder linked to cognitive decline, mood disturbance, gait problems, hearing deficits, and even increased mortality. Calling it benign, they wrote, “is a mischaracterization of the disease course, and could prove misleading.”

Quoting from the paper:

“Over recent years, the older view of ET as a simple, monosymptomatic condition has been challenged, as new data have emerged painting a picture of a progressive disorder associated with several co-morbidities, and possibly even an increased risk of mortality.”

Today, medical institutions use the term “Essential Tremor.”

So how did countless national news outlets end up parroting a retired medical term? Because the local outlet she chose to give the exclusive to didn’t question it. And this wasn’t the first time.

The reporter behind the story, News Center Maine’s Phil Hirschkorn, got another campaign exclusive from Collins in which he somehow forgot to do his homework.

“Sen. Susan Collins says if she wins in November, it’ll be her last term,” another Hirschkorn exclusive from earlier this year reads.

What the reporter neglected to mention anywhere in the story is that Collins made this pledge before. During a televised debate during her first campaign for Senate in 1996, candidate Collins looked into the camera and said:

“I do support term limits and I have pledged that if I’m elected, I will only serve two terms, regardless of whether a term limits law or constitutional amendment passes or not. Twelve years is long enough to be in public service — make a contribution and then come home and let someone else take your place.”

Now Collins is running for her sixth term, after 29 years in office.

In other words, Collins made and violated the exact same promise she’s making now — something Hirschkorn should have at least mentioned in his article (or better yet, pressed her on).

To back up Collins’ “benign” framing, Hirschkorn quoted Dr. Rees Cosgrove, Chief of the Division of Functional Neurosurgery at Mass General Brigham. He said essential tremor is “not at all” incapacitating, that it isn’t associated with “cognitive decline or memory decline,” and — when asked whether it’s a physical condition without a mental component — replied, “Absolutely.”

Cosgrove is a real expert. He performs focused ultrasound treatments that target the part of the brain causing the tremor and destroys it. But in his own publications going back decades, I cannot find a single instance where he calls it “Benign Essential Tremor.” He calls it “Essential Tremor.” Because that’s what it’s called.

On a Mass General Brigham page detailing the procedure, Cosgrove says of patients: “This is the operation that makes grown men cry. They’re so emotional and they can’t believe that their hand, which has been shaking for years, isn’t shaking anymore.”

In a video interview, Cosgrove describes a grown man crying with relief after the treatment.

“One man who had had tremor since university days, and, uh, he was never really able to be fully employed at the level because he was socially so, uh, cognizant and reticent about being in public because he had such severe shaking… as he wrote his name, he started crying because he said, ‘You know, I haven’t been able to do that in 20 years.’”

But not at all incapacitating! What?

Essential Tremor also gets worse with age. Cosgrove’s clinical page lists “at least 70 years old” among the criteria for surgical intervention. Collins is 73. He also lists current medication use as a criterion. Collins has confirmed she takes medication for the condition.

In Collins’ statement to the AP, picked up by the Times and everyone else, she took a swipe at me.

“The tremor is occasionally inconvenient, and sometimes the subject of cruel comments online, but it does not hinder my ability to work,” she said.

You know what I call cruel? A sitting U.S. senator, facing a tough reelection, throwing millions Americans living with Essential Tremor under the bus to control a news cycle. Many of those people cannot work. Some sit in Dr. Cosgrove’s surgical suite and weep when their hands stop shaking for the first time in decades.

But that’s not how decorum works. “Cruel” is what little people get called; for the powerful like Collins who make decisions that result in layoffs, in cuts to public services, and in wars, she’s just being clear-eyed, hard-nosed, unsentimental.

It’s possible that Collins is one of the lucky ones who has a good prognosis. But how could she know for sure? And isn’t it the job of journalism to question and give the voters the opportunity to decide what they make of it? Instead, virtually every major outlet picked up News Center Maine’s “benign” framing — which is to say, they picked up Collins’ framing.

The New York Times’ headline reads, “Susan Collins Says She Has Long Had a Benign Tremor.”

NBC News at least put scare quotes around it: “Sen. Susan Collins reveals ‘benign’ tremor, says it hasn’t affected her job or how she feels.”

The Associated Press wire moved with “benign essential tremor” in the lede, and outlets from Fortune to ABC and even People Magazine ran with it.

All of these headlines are a subtle way of telling the public to stay in its lane, that you’re not authorized or credentialed enough to care about this issue.

Now some of you wince at the language I used to describe Collins’ tremor. Fine. But can you argue with the results? The announcement video had been on YouTube for three months — about as glaring a tableau of the gerontocracy as you could find anywhere — and not one major outlet had touched it. Until I posted the word “toothbrush.”

On the Breaking Points show, conservative commentator Emily Jashinsky noted that questions about Collins’ health had been circulating among Republicans for years before I broke the silence. Years. That’s the kind of logjam the media could break if it just spoke plainly about things.

The Times, hilariously, acknowledges this in its prim and proper way, linking to my post but refusing to quote it. The Times says:

“Her remarks came amid mounting online scrutiny — pushed in particular on the left — of the shakiness that is often detectable when Ms. Collins speaks, and questions about whether it has worsened with time.”

Questions about whether it has worsened with time? Anyone with access to YouTube can pull up Collins from ten years ago and Collins from last month and answer that question in about 30 seconds. The condition is well-documented as progressive. But the Times can’t just say what it plainly sees. In old school journalism, what I call Journalism 1.0, stating the obvious is considered “editorializing.”

Then there’s “pushed in particular on the left.” That little label reduces the scrutiny to partisan sniping. It suggests this is just “left” criticism, a tribal food fight. I’ve been writing about gerontocracy for years, and I’ve been just as blunt about Joe Biden’s decline and plenty of other Democrats. I don’t need to launder that through quotes from operatives or experts. I just say it.

That’s the core problem. The straitjacket of Journalism 1.0 is that reporters are not allowed to describe reality in their own words. They must route everything through someone else’s mouth. So they go hunting for the most “authoritative” voice they can find — which, in this case, turns out to be the politician with the strongest incentive to minimize the story. And so we get “benign.”

Here is how that works in practice:

  1. A senator hands a friendly local outlet her preferred language.

  2. The local outlet prints it.

  3. The networks pick it up from the local.

  4. The wires pick it up from the networks.

  5. The national papers pick it up from the wires.

At no point does anyone in this chain stop and ask whether the word is medically accurate, or even consistent with the facts in front of them.

That is Journalism 1.0 in a nutshell. And clearly, newsrooms have learned nothing from the Biden episode.

The stakes here are not small. Collins is in one of the most closely watched Senate races in the country. She’s up against 41‑year‑old Democrat Graham Platner, a combat veteran who has been open about his own health — chronic pain, PTSD, a 100 percent VA disability rating — while continuing to work as an oyster farmer. Collins, by contrast, said nothing about a condition she now admits she has had for her entire 29 years in the Senate.

That is an actual story. And the only reason it is being told at all is because some punk outside the DC cocktail circuit had the audacity to type the word “toothbrush.”

Claude’s Response

The medical-terminology finding is real and tightly sourced. Klippenstein cites the actual 2011 Louis-Okun paper and the actual Mass General Brigham clinical page for Cosgrove, and shows that Cosgrove’s own published writing uses “Essential Tremor” without “benign.” The mortality, dementia, and disability figures are from peer-reviewed PubMed sources he links. This is the kind of small, checkable, falsifiable evidence that survives skeptical reading — and it’s enough to support the operative claim that “benign” is not what the medical community calls the condition. The piece also avoids overreach in a way that matters: Klippenstein does not claim Collins has dementia, does not claim her tremor is debilitating her specifically, and explicitly acknowledges she may be “one of the lucky ones who has a good prognosis.” His point is that voters should be able to assess the question, and the press’s source-laundering forecloses that.

The 1996-term-limits broken-promise finding is the meta-track’s strongest evidence that Hirschkorn’s beat coverage of Collins is structurally compromised — the prior pledge is a matter of public videotape from a campaign debate, and a reporter writing about Collins’ “last term” pledge who doesn’t mention she’s broken the exact same pledge before is not doing journalism in any defensible sense. This is a clean institutional-failure finding.

The piece’s weaker move is the gerontocracy-framing generalization. Klippenstein wants the Collins-Biden parallel to do work it doesn’t fully do: Biden’s decline was cognitive and observable in real time, while Collins’ tremor (per her own description and Cosgrove’s testimony for unrelated patients) is a motor condition that doesn’t necessarily affect cognition. The two stories are structurally similar in the press behavior (source laundering, defensive deference), but the underlying medical facts are different in important ways Klippenstein doesn’t quite separate. The “Journalism 1.0” rhetorical frame is useful but rhetorically convenient — it lets him cast every news outlet’s caution as cowardice rather than as the result of a more complicated trade-off between editorial autonomy and source dependency that the legacy press faces. Pieces like this one are precisely what the trade-off enables: an independent journalist with no institutional reliance on Collins or her staff can describe what he sees, while the AP correspondent who needs Senate access cannot. The right reading isn’t that legacy press is broken and Klippenstein-style independent journalism is the answer; it’s that the two operate in different institutional positions with different failure modes, and the Collins case is one where the independent position is clearly producing the better factual coverage.

A useful pair for the synthesis: this piece against the broader theme of how the press handles politicians’ health and decline (the Biden cover-up post-mortems from late 2024, the Feinstein coverage of 2023, the John Fetterman-stroke coverage of 2022-23). The pattern Klippenstein names — “describe reality only when quoting an authoritative source who’ll describe it for you” — is real and recurring, and his cataloging it as a structural feature rather than a series of one-off failures is the most analytically useful contribution of the piece.

Leave a comment

— Edited by William M. Arkin

Back to summary

What I Told Students At the College of William and Mary

Francis Fukuyama · 2026-05-15 · Original →

Will you be in London on Sunday, September 6? Yascha Mounk will be interviewing Francis Fukuyama about his life and thought to mark the publication of his memoir In the Realm of the Last Man at the Sekforde at 5pm. Find out more and book tickets here. (Paying subscribers can access a code for free tickets here.)

William III and Mary II, King and Queen of England, Scotland, and Ireland, c1689.(Photo by Historica Graphica Collection/Heritage Images/Getty Images.)

Today, it was my pleasure to speak at William & Mary’s 2026 Commencement ceremony. The namesakes of this college played an underappreciated role in the birth of liberalism, so I’m delighted to share my remarks and their story with you now.

—Frank.

Chancellor Gates, Rector Poston and members of the Board, President Rowe—thank you for the beautiful video and introduction and for the honor from this distinguished university.

To the students, faculty, parents, relatives, and friends of the graduating class of 2026, I am very honored to be able to speak to you on this day. This year is part of William and Mary’s “Year of Civic Leadership,” as well as the 250th anniversary of the signing of the Declaration of Independence. I’m grateful for the introduction, and want to add one small biographical note.

Back in 1989, I published an article entitled “The End of History?” with a question mark at the end. I don’t want to spend any time today explaining the meaning of that phrase, which was not mine but that of the German philosopher Hegel. Suffice it to say the article set off a firestorm of discussion about democracy and world politics. It eventually led to my getting my first academic appointment at George Mason University. I was told by my sponsors that I had received a huge number of references in the citation index that is used to evaluate the work of scholars.

What they didn’t say was that probably 99 percent of those citations said I was completely wrong.

With your permission, I would like to talk to you a little about history and the role that your college’s namesakes played in it.

William of Orange was a Dutch prince, married to Mary Stuart, the young daughter of King James II of England. William was invited by a group of English nobles to help overthrow Mary’s father. This was the culmination of a series of events starting in 1688 known as the Glorious Revolution.

At the heart of the Glorious Revolution was a crisis over constitutional authority. The Stuart kings Charles I and James II had insisted on their right to unilaterally impose taxes and make laws without the consent of Parliament. This led to an English Civil War, the beheading of Charles, an interregnum under Oliver Cromwell, and finally the restoration of the Stuart dynasty.

Subscribe now

But the principle of parliamentary supremacy had not been fully established by the late 1680s. The Catholic King James refused to consult with Parliament on a host of policy issues, and he appointed Catholic officers to key posts in the army. Fear that he would return England to Catholicism led to an uprising, James’s abdication, and his replacement by William and Mary.

The Parliamentary side in the Glorious Revolution rallied under the banners of two principles: “no taxation without representation,” and, more broadly, legitimate government coming from the “consent of the governed.” William and Mary ascended to the throne with the understanding that hereafter, English monarchs could no longer operate with absolute authority, but needed to get the consent of Parliament first. The philosopher John Locke had accompanied Mary Stuart back to England from the Netherlands, and wrote his Second Treatise of Government that set these principles in a universal context.

The American Founding Fathers read John Locke. Thomas Jefferson studied John Locke while a student at William and Mary College, and incorporated the principles of “consent of the governed” and “no taxation without representation” into the Declaration of Independence.
